glossary.def: The opening of the vocal folds.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: The opening movement of the vocal folds away from the midline. The vocal folds abduct during quiet breathing to allow air to pass freely through the glottis.
glossary.source: Lecture 2.2 — Ch. 3, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def: Usually considered the speech pronunciation and inflections used by nonnative American English speakers (foreign accent). Like dialect, an accent is a difference, not a disorder. A disorder must occur in the speaker's primary language to be classified as such.
glossary.source: Ch. 4 PDF — Prof. Handy, SJDC

glossary.def: Damage to hearing from a transient, high-intensity sound.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: A disorder that begins after an individual has developed normal communication abilities, such as a hearing loss from loud noise exposure or a speech, language, or cognitive disorder caused by a traumatic brain injury.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: Tasks performed in a typical day that allow independent living in the home, community, and work and school environments, such as dressing, hygiene, and self-feeding, with more advanced ADLs including cooking, performing household chores, doing laundry, telephoning, using transportation, shopping, and managing personal finances.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: Intense and of short duration, usually referring to a disease or injury.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: A hospital where patients are treated for brief but severe episodes of illness, injury, trauma, or during recovery from surgery.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: An abrupt, intense, and usually relatively brief inflammation of the mucous membrane lining in the larynx, accompanied by edema of the vocal folds with hoarseness and loss of voice that is often caused by severe vocal abuse.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: The ability to act as independently and responsibly as other people of the same age and cultural background; includes conceptual skills (e.g., language and literacy, time, and number concepts), social skills (e.g., interpersonal skills, social problem solving, and the ability to follow rules and obey laws); and practical skills (e.g., personal care, occupational skills, basic healthcare, travel/transportation, schedules/routines, safety, use of money, use of the telephone).
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: The insertion of a sound or sounds not part of the word itself, such as animamal for animal.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: An articulation error in which an extra sound is inserted into a word that does not belong there. Example: 'animamal' for 'animal'; 'suh-poon' for 'spoon' (schwa /ə/ inserted between /s/ and /p/).
glossary.source: Quiz answer key — Ch. 5 / Lecture 4.2, Kidwell

glossary.def: The closing of the vocal folds.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: The closing movement of the vocal folds toward the midline. The vocal folds must adduct to produce voice. Subglottic air pressure builds, forces the folds apart, and Bernoulli's law causes them to snap back together, creating vibration.
glossary.source: Lecture 2.2 — Ch. 3, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def: Lymphoid tissue found on the pharyngeal wall in the nasal-pharyngeal region. They begin to develop at about 6 months of age and may continue to grow and possibly interfere with the Eustachian tubes. When enlarged, adenoids may block the passage of air from the nasal cavity into the pharynx, preventing normal nasal breathing. Sometimes surgically removed to allow nasal breathing and help prevent middle ear infections.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: A broad syndrome first described in 1845 and accepted as a true disorder in 1902. Defined by the APA (DSM-5, 2013) as problems in three primary areas: inattention, impulsivity, and hyperactivity. Occurs in approximately 3–5% of the population/classroom (worldwide prevalence ~5.3% of children; U.S. parental reports ~9% of 4–17 year olds). More common in boys. Average age of first diagnosis: 7 years (physician), 6 years (psychiatrist/psychologist). SLPs cannot diagnose ADHD but must be aware of it and make accommodations in assessment and therapy.
glossary.source: Lecture Ch. 9, Kidwell / Ch. 9 PPTX

glossary.def:
SLPs CANNOT diagnose ADHD. Diagnosis is made by physicians (typically pediatrician, psychiatrist, or neurologist) and/or psychologist or neuropsychologist using parental and teacher behavioral rating scales. First indications for evaluation: behavioral problems at home, poor schoolwork, learning difficulties.

SLP ASSESSMENT ROLE: (1) observe interactions with peers, authority figures, parents/family in natural and testing settings; (2) interview parents/caregivers about development; (3) interview child to evaluate self-awareness; (4) formally and informally evaluate speech and language (articulation, phonemic awareness, vocabulary, semantics, syntax, fluency); (5) evaluate narrative ability; (6) assess social communication/pragmatics; (7) assess planning, organization, and attention to detail.
glossary.source: Ch. 9 PPTX / Lecture Ch. 9, Kidwell

glossary.def: ADHD frequently co-occurs with other disorders (comorbidity). Associated disorders include: auditory processing disorders, learning disabilities, behavioral disorders, anxiety, depression, autism spectrum disorders, traumatic brain injuries (TBI), motor development problems, sleep problems, oppositional defiant disorder, and speech and language delays or impairments. Children with speech and/or language disorders have higher rates of ADHD signs/symptoms than children without speech/language disorders. SLPs may need to make referrals to other specialists for assessment and treatment.
glossary.source: Lecture Ch. 9, Kidwell / Ch. 9 PPTX

glossary.def: ADHD treatment uses a TEAM APPROACH. Components include: helping the child and family understand ADHD and its treatment; behavioral management; stimulant medication (pattern of undertreatment noted); classroom environmental modifications to facilitate learning; appropriate speech and language goals. SLP-specific goals: help child and family understand ADHD; develop language and study skills; support teachers. SLPs must make accommodations in communication assessment and therapy for children with ADHD.
glossary.source: Ch. 9 PPTX / ADHD_APD.pdf / Lecture Ch. 9, Kidwell

glossary.def:
Key behavioral differences between ADHD and APD:
APD: difficulty hearing in background noise, difficulty following oral instructions, poor listening skills, poor auditory association skills — the HEARING/PROCESSING is the core problem.
ADHD: inattentive, hyperactive, hasty/impulsive, interrupts and intrudes — the ATTENTION/BEHAVIOR regulation is the core problem.
Important: both disorders may produce similar-looking behaviors in the classroom, making differential assessment critical. They also commonly co-occur.
glossary.source: Lecture Ch. 9, Kidwell / Ch. 9 PPTX

glossary.def: Some children with SLI/LLD continue to have impairments into adolescence and beyond. Adolescent RECEPTIVE problems: weak vocabulary, difficulty with figurative/abstract language and words with multiple meanings, trouble following directions and understanding lectures, poor listening skills, misinterpreting facial expressions, difficulty with rapid speech. Adolescent EXPRESSIVE problems: incomplete/inaccurate/inappropriate communication, weak vocabulary, use of low-content words ('things', 'stuff'), simple syntax, verb tense errors, violating social distance rules — may be perceived as 'abrasive.' These impairments impact education, employment, and relationships.
glossary.source: Lecture Ch. 7, Kidwell, SJDC

glossary.def: Relating to, arising from, or influencing feelings or emotions; affect is revealed by facial expressions, body posture and gestures, tone of voice, and choice of words.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: Impairment of grammar and syntax and the ability to produce words in their correct sequence and with all necessary morphemes.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: An acquired disorder of writing resulting in difficulty writing letters, words, and sentences.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: The transmission of sound to the inner ear through the external auditory canal and the structures of the middle ear.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: Impaired ability to read as the result of neurological damage.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: ALEXIA (reading impairment in aphasia): difficulty comprehending written material, difficulty recognizing words by sight, inability to sound out words, substituting associated words, difficulty reading functor words. AGRAPHIA (writing impairment in aphasia): difficulty writing or copying letters/words/sentences, writing single words only, substituting incorrect letters or words, spelling or writing nonsense syllables, writing run-on sentences that do not make sense, writing sentences with incorrect grammar.
glossary.source: Ch. 15 PPTX

glossary.def: A slight variation in the way different people produce individual phonemes, which can be affected by the initial, middle, or final position of a word, or by what sounds precede or follow an individual phoneme. Example: the /b/ in 'lab' vs. the /b/ in 'boy' sound slightly different — both are allophones of /b/.
glossary.source: Quiz answer key — Ch. 5 / Lecture 4.2, Kidwell

glossary.def: Letters and combinations of letters represent speech sounds; speech can be turned into print; and print can be turned into speech.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.term: ALS (Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis / Lou Gehrig's Disease)
glossary.def: A progressive neurodegenerative disease affecting both upper motor neurons (UMN) and lower motor neurons (LMN), resulting in MIXED dysarthria (flaccid + spastic). Causes progressive weakness and paralysis of all speech subsystems. Speech deteriorates over time from mild dysarthria to anarthria (complete loss of speech). AAC is eventually required. Always progressive — no cure. One of the four neuromuscular disorders most commonly associated with dysarthria.
glossary.source: Lecture Ch. 17, Kidwell / Ch. 17 PPTX

glossary.def: The bony ridge of the upper gum line just behind the upper front teeth. Surrounds the hard palate. An important landmark for articulation — many consonants (e.g., /t/, /d/, /s/) are produced with the tongue contacting the alveolar ridge.
glossary.source: Lecture 2.2 — Ch. 3, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def: The spongy tissue of the lungs where gas exchange takes place. Walls of alveoli are one cell thick and porous, allowing rapid transfer of fresh oxygen into the capillary bed surrounding the alveoli, and of carbon dioxide from the capillary bed into the alveoli to be exhaled.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: Tiny air sacs at the ends of the bronchioles in the lungs where gas exchange occurs — oxygen enters the bloodstream and carbon dioxide is removed. During the respiratory cycle, outside air flows into the alveoli.
glossary.source: Lecture 2.2 — Ch. 3, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.term: Alzheimer's Disease — Stage I: Mild (Forgetfulness Stage)
glossary.def: Early-stage Alzheimer's — signs may be subtle and difficult to recognize. The individual is often frustrated or angry at their increasing impairments. Features: IMPAIRED WORKING MEMORY; difficulty completing FAMILIAR TASKS; difficulty remembering COMMON NAMES; misplacing items in INAPPROPRIATE places; sudden unexplained changes in MOOD or BEHAVIOR; showing indifference to PERSONAL APPEARANCE; DISORIENTATION to person, place, time, and purpose; becoming LOST while driving on familiar streets. At this stage, family members may be in denial about the severity of the condition.
glossary.source: Ch. 16 PPTX; Kidwell lecture notes

glossary.term: Alzheimer's Disease — Stage II: Moderate (Confusion Stage)
glossary.def: Middle-stage Alzheimer's — more apparent signs and symptoms. Family members typically bring their loved one to the doctor and diagnosis is often made at this stage. The most difficult stage because personal safety is a major concern requiring constant monitoring. Features: INCREASED loss of working memory; significantly DIMINISHED VOCABULARY; conversation increasingly EMPTY and MEANINGLESS; loss of READING and WRITING ability; difficulty with SKILLED MOVEMENTS (tying shoelaces, using utensils); AGGRESSIVENESS, outbursts of anger, or withdrawal; INAPPROPRIATE PUBLIC BEHAVIOR; HALLUCINATIONS or DELUSIONS. Inability to ensure safety and hygiene often leads to nursing home placement.
glossary.source: Ch. 16 PPTX; Kidwell lecture notes

glossary.term: Alzheimer's Disease — Stage III: Severe (Terminal Stage)
glossary.def: Late-stage Alzheimer's — the individual can no longer think, reason, or communicate. NOT usually a good candidate for speech therapy at this stage. Features: LITTLE OR NO MEMORY; difficulty speaking and understanding even simple sentences and individual words; LITTLE OR NO EMOTIONAL EXPRESSION; MINIMAL VERBAL COMMUNICATION; difficulty RECOGNIZING OTHERS and sometimes oneself in a mirror; needs TOTAL ASSISTANCE with all ADLs and personal care; difficulty CHEWING and SWALLOWING resulting in weight loss, dehydration, and high risk for ASPIRATION PNEUMONIA. Often referred to as 'the long goodbye' due to the protracted nature of the disease progression.
glossary.source: Ch. 16 PPTX; Kidwell lecture notes

glossary.def: Also called DAT (Dementia of the Alzheimer's Type). A neurological disease causing PROGRESSIVE dementia with decline in intellect, memory, communication, and personality. Impairments must be severe enough to interfere with daily functioning. MRI may reveal CORTICAL ATROPHY, but a definitive diagnosis requires BRAIN AUTOPSY (post-mortem). Divided into three stages: STAGE I (Mild/Early/Forgetfulness), STAGE II (Moderate/Middle/Confusion), STAGE III (Severe/Late/Terminal). Stages may OVERLAP and there is no exact timing for each stage. Preceded by PRECLINICAL Alzheimer's disease (brain changes without clinical symptoms).
glossary.source: Ch. 16 PPTX; Kidwell lecture notes

glossary.def: A neurological disease that causes progressive dementia that usually begins after age 65 and in many cases is related to genetic factors that cause microscopic changes to both gray and white matter, resulting in cerebral atrophy, and is characterized by decline in memory, intellect, disorientation, delusions, personality changes, and communication impairments.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: Background sounds that are present in an environment or location, such as wind, water, birds, crowds, office noises, traffic, and so on.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: A manual system of communication commonly used by members of the Deaf culture in the United States; sometimes referred to as Ameslan.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.term: Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS; Lou Gehrig's disease)
glossary.def: A rare, rapidly progressive degenerative disease of motor neurons that control movement of all muscle systems, including the speech systems.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: A complete or near complete loss of the ability to articulate speech.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: The study of the physical structures of the body — the parts themselves. In SLP, anatomy refers to the specific structures (bones, muscles, cartilages) involved in speech, language, hearing, and swallowing.
glossary.source: Lecture 2.2 — Ch. 3, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def: A localized dilation of a blood vessel wall, resulting in the thinning of the wall at that site and increasing the likelihood of hemorrhaging, especially with high blood pressure.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: Anomia = consistent inability to recall the appropriate word to identify an object, person's name, or numbers. A deficit of EXPRESSIVE language; receptive language (understanding) is not impaired. THREE TYPES: (1) LEXICAL ANOMIA — patient knows how to use an object and can select it from a group but cannot name it; may have selective impairment for specific categories (e.g., animals or colors). (2) PHONOLOGICAL ANOMIA (Conduction Aphasia) — patient knows the word but selects wrong sounds; long words especially difficult. (3) SEMANTIC ANOMIA — meaning of words becomes lost; patient cannot select the correct object from a group even when given the name.
glossary.source: Handy Handout #435; Ch. 15 PPTX

glossary.def: SLP goals for anomia treatment: preserve existing language skills, restore lost skills, and discover new ways of communicating. Ultimate goal: increase INDEPENDENCE. Treatment activities: using writing or gestures to cue word retrieval; flash cards with pictures and words; repeating words back to therapist; computer programs for speech/reading/writing. WORD RETRIEVAL CUEING STRATEGIES: giving first sound or first letter; asking questions about the word's meaning; pointing to a corresponding picture from a choice; structured fill-in-the-blank sentences ('I don't want salt, I want ___'). CAREGIVER CAUTION: providing words for the patient makes conversations easier but creates a cycle of dependence — patience and positive reinforcement are essential.
glossary.source: Handy Handout #435

glossary.def: A fluent aphasia characterized by a persistent, severe word-retrieval problem (anomia) while other areas of language may be relatively intact. The most common residual symptom of people who have had any type of aphasia — word retrieval difficulties often continue after other symptoms have been rehabilitated. Features: good auditory comprehension, good repetition, circumlocutions, use of generic fillers ('you know,' 'like,' 'uh'), nonspecific nouns and pronouns ('thing,' 'they'), speech full of vague expressions of frustration. Fluency: +, Comprehension: +, Repetition: +, Naming: −.
glossary.source: Ch. 15 PPTX; Kidwell lecture notes; Handy Handout #435

glossary.def: ANOSOGNOSIA: a condition in which a patient is unaware of or lacks insight into their own deficits — they do not recognize that they have impairments. Common in right-hemisphere syndrome. Makes treatment challenging because the patient does not perceive a need for change. APROSODIA: reduced or absent variation in prosody (pitch, rate, rhythm, stress) in communication — speech sounds flat and monotone; communication is devoid of emotion. Associated with right-hemisphere damage. May also manifest as FLAT AFFECT — reduced emotional expression overall.
glossary.source: Ch. 16 PPTX; Kidwell lecture notes

glossary.def: A complete absence of oxygen supply to the brain, causing brain damage. Can result from lack of oxygen at birth, stroke, exposure to toxic chemicals or fumes, or cessation of breathing. A cause of acquired neurological communication disorders.
glossary.source: Lecture 2.2 — Ch. 3, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def: A complete lack of oxygen to the brain. Relatively rare but causes devastating neurological disorders. Distinct from hypoxia. Can occur perinatally (e.g., cord wrapped around neck during delivery). Results in significant communication and cognitive impairments.
glossary.source: Quiz answer key — Ch. 5

glossary.def: Lack of oxygen in the brain. Specifically caused by asphyxiation (e.g., near-drowning, choking) or inadequate blood circulation (e.g., heart attack). Results in unconsciousness and death of brain tissue. A leading cause of acquired neurological communication disorders.
glossary.source: CSD Ch3 PP (Fogle 3rd ed.)

glossary.def: General principles for AOS assessment (Evidence-Based Practice): evaluate speech and oral mechanism functioning. Tools: Apraxia Battery for Adults, 2nd ed. (Dabul, 2000); Quick Assessment of Apraxia of Speech (Tanner & Culbertson, 1999); Test of Oral and Limb Apraxia (Helm-Estabrooks, 1992). Differential diagnosis from dysarthria and aphasia is essential. Note: AOS may be impossible to confirm in severe cases — the person may be able to say nothing on command.
glossary.source: Ch. 17 PPTX — Fogle/SJDC

glossary.def:
Characteristics of Apraxia of Speech (not all need to be present for diagnosis):
1. Articulation errors NOT caused by muscle weakness or paralysis
2. Highly VARIABLE/inconsistent articulation errors (hallmark)
3. Sound errors more often SUBSTITUTIONS than distortions, omissions, or additions
4. CONSONANT errors more common than vowel errors
5. Errors most often on INITIAL consonants
6. CONSONANT CLUSTERS in error more than single consonants
7. Errors INCREASE with increasing word length (sounds and syllables)
8. GROPING — trial-and-error searching for articulatory placement
9. Front-of-mouth sounds (b, d, z) more likely correct than back sounds (k, g, ch, sh)
10. 'Islands' of fluent, error-free words/phrases among effortful speech
11. Adequate isolated movements but overtaxed during complex speech
12. Disfluencies (stutter-like speech)
13. Automatic speech typically intact; volitional speech impaired
glossary.source: Lecture Ch. 17, Kidwell / Ch. 17 PPTX

glossary.def: Primary goal: maximize the effectiveness, efficiency, and naturalness of communication. General principles (Evidence-Based Practice): drill (intensive repetitive practice) is most effective; start with simpler/shorter speech and increase complexity; multimodal approach (mirrors, tactile, visual, auditory cues); use of key phrases and islands of fluency; errorless learning and motor learning principles. Individuals often recognize errors and try to self-correct — the harder they try, the more difficult production becomes. AAC may be needed for severe cases.
glossary.source: Lecture Ch. 17, Kidwell / Ch. 17 PPTX

glossary.def:
SLPs are OFTEN THE FIRST to recognize APD. However, SLPs CANNOT diagnose APD — ONLY AUDIOLOGISTS can diagnose APD, using a sound-treated booth to assess central auditory functions at the CORTEX level.

SLP ROLE: screen for APD and make referrals to audiologists. APD screening tools available to SLPs: SCAN-3:C (Test for Auditory Processing Disorders for Children; Keith, 2009); Auditory Skills Assessment (ASA; Geffner & Goldman, 2010); Differential Screening Test for Processing (DST-P; Richard & Ferre, 2006). Further assessment: TAPS-3, DTAP, comprehensive language evaluation.
glossary.source: Ch. 9 PPTX / Lecture Ch. 9, Kidwell

glossary.def:
Signs that a child may have APD (Ch. 9 PPTX / Table 9-1):
• Difficulty understanding speech in adverse/noisy listening environments (auditory figure-ground difficulty)
• Misunderstanding messages; responding inconsistently or inappropriately
• Frequently asking for repetition ('What?' or 'Huh?')
• Difficulty attending and avoiding distraction
• Delay in responding to oral communication
• Difficulty following complex/multi-step auditory directions
• Reduced musical and singing skills
• Associated reading, spelling, and learning problems
• Unusual reactions to sudden or loud sounds
• Difficulty remembering names, dates, times
• Difficulty identifying direction of sound from another room
• Poor relationships with family and peers; few friends
• Slow or inappropriate responses; appears confused in conversations
glossary.source: Ch. 9 PPTX / Lecture Ch. 9, Kidwell

glossary.def:
Speech and language problems associated with APD:
• Auditory figure-ground problems (difficulty in noisy environments)
• Difficulty recognizing subtle differences between sounds in spoken words
• Speech, reading, spelling, and writing problems
• Difficulty following conversations, answering questions, and understanding jokes
• Slow, delayed, and inappropriate responses to questions and communications
• Difficulty following multi-part directions; often says 'What?' or 'Huh?'
• Difficulty remembering information
• Difficulty processing rapid speech
glossary.source: Ch. 9 PPTX — Fogle/SJDC

glossary.def:
APD treatment is TAILORED TO THE INDIVIDUAL using a MULTIDISCIPLINARY (TEAM) approach. Three-component approach:
1. ENVIRONMENTAL MODIFICATIONS — reduce background noise, preferential seating, classroom acoustics improvements
2. PERSONAL FM SYSTEM — assistive listening device that transmits the teacher's voice directly to the child's ears
3. COMPENSATORY STRATEGIES — teach the child strategies to manage in difficult listening situations

Direct remediation and auditory training may include: phoneme discrimination, phoneme-to-grapheme skills, temporal gap discrimination, temporal ordering/sequencing, pattern recognition, localization/lateralization, recognition of auditory information in background noise.
Treatment must be FREQUENT and INTENSE. Computer software supplements traditional interventions.
glossary.source: Ch. 9 PPTX — Fogle/SJDC

glossary.def: An acquired language disorder in adults resulting from brain damage (e.g., stroke or brain injury). Affects a person's ability to speak, understand, read, and/or write. Distinct from developmental language disorders present since childhood.
glossary.source: Lecture 1.2.1 — Chapter 1, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def: An acquired language impairment resulting from neurological damage in the absence of sensory, motor, intellectual, or cognitive impairments. A language processing deficit that may affect all input modalities (auditory, visual, tactile) and output modalities (speaking, writing, gesturing). Most SLPs label patients as having 'aphasia' though technically most have 'dysphasia' — a partial rather than complete loss of language. More than 100,000 people in the U.S. get aphasia each year; affects 1 in 250 people — more common than cerebral palsy, Parkinson's disease, or muscular dystrophy.
glossary.source: Ch. 15 PPTX; Kidwell lecture notes; Handy Handout #177

glossary.def: Comprehensive assessment process: (1) careful review of medical chart; (2) interview patient and family to verify history; (3) formal assessment of receptive and expressive language. RECEPTIVE abilities assessed: hearing acuity, visual acuity, pointing to named objects/pictures, answering simple to complex questions, following simple to complex spoken directions, following conversational speech. EXPRESSIVE abilities assessed: confrontation naming, responsive naming (completing phrases), word fluency, repetition, sentence formulation, discourse, picture description, nonverbal communication. READING and WRITING abilities also formally assessed.
glossary.source: Ch. 15 PPTX; Kidwell lecture notes

glossary.term: Aphasia Treatment: Restorative vs. Compensatory Approaches
glossary.def: Two primary treatment approaches: RESTORATIVE — aims to improve the underlying impaired process; attempts to retrain damaged skills and restore pre-injury function. COMPENSATORY — teaches strategies to cope with persistent language deficits; focuses on daily functional communication (e.g., teaching gestures or use of a communication board when word-finding does not improve). Most programs use a combination. Overall goal: improve QUALITY OF LIFE and FUNCTIONAL OUTCOMES. Factors affecting treatment: age, prior language/literacy skills, nature and size of lesion, severity, timing, length, intensity, appropriateness of goals, and family involvement.
glossary.source: Ch. 15 PPTX; Kidwell lecture notes

glossary.def: A complete loss of voice followed by whispering for oral communication that typically has psychological causes such as emotional stress.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: An applied science devoted to developing procedures that produce measurable changes in behavior using principles of operant conditioning, classical conditioning, and social learning theory (formerly, behavior modification).
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: A deficit in the neural motor planning or programming of the articulatory muscles for voluntary movements for speech, in the absence of muscular weakness, that primarily affects articulation and prosody. Most common cause: STROKE with damage to or around Broca's area. Distinguished from dysarthria because muscle weakness is NOT present. Distinguished from aphasia because the language system is intact — the person knows what they want to say but cannot execute the motor plan.
glossary.source: Lecture Ch. 17, Kidwell / Ch. 17 PPTX

glossary.def: Difficulty recognizing, interpreting, and conveying prosody (rhythm, pitch, stress, intonation) that is usually secondary to neurological impairment.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: A common disorder characterized by thickening, hardening, and loss of elasticity of arterial walls as a result of buildup of atherosclerotic plaque, leading to decreased blood supply.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: The process of shaping sounds into recognizable words using the articulators. Occurs when parts of the mouth touch or interact with each other. The primary articulator is the tongue; others include the lips, teeth, cheeks, and palate.
glossary.source: Eugene Speech Language Therapy — How We Speak (YouTube, 11xf89bXaCg)

glossary.def: The fourth of four speech production processes. Modifies sounds entering the oral cavity using jaw, lip, and tongue movements to produce specific speech sounds. Example: /m, p, b/ are produced with both lips together; /k, g/ require the tongue to rise at the back of the mouth.
glossary.source: Lecture 1.2.1 — Chapter 1, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def:
The evaluation procedure to determine if a child has an articulation/phonological disorder. Components:
1. Caregiver questionnaire/interview (background)
2. Establish rapport with child
3. Hearing screening (required — hearing is most crucial influence on speech)
4. Language screening (co-morbid disorders are common)
5. Spontaneous speech sample (best case scenario — analyze intelligibility, error types, consistency)
6. Oral motor evaluation (examine anatomy and physiology of articulators)
7. Structured standardized articulation assessment (standard scores, percentiles — required for school eligibility)
8. Phonological error pattern analysis if indicated
9. Stimulability testing
glossary.source: Lecture 4.2, Kidwell, SJDC

glossary.def: A speech disorder in which a child cannot correctly produce speech sounds, most often involving improper tongue placement. Categorized as a speech impairment along with phonological and motor speech disorders.
glossary.source: Lecture 1.2.1 — Chapter 1, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def: Incorrect production of speech sounds because of faulty placement, timing, direction, pressure, speed, or integration of the movements of the mandible, lips, tongue, or soft palate. Most common communication disorder in children. May occur as an isolated problem or alongside language disorders, cognitive delays, brain injuries, or orofacial anomalies.
glossary.source: Quiz answer key + Lecture 4.2, Kidwell, SJDC

glossary.def: The structures of the mouth that shape sounds into speech. The main articulator is the tongue. Other articulators include the lips, teeth, cheeks, and palate.
glossary.source: Eugene Speech Language Therapy — How We Speak (YouTube, 11xf89bXaCg)

glossary.def: Two small pyramid-shaped cartilages in the larynx. The vocal folds attach posteriorly to the arytenoids. Their rotation and movement control abduction (opening) and adduction (closing) of the vocal folds.
glossary.source: Lecture 2.2 — Ch. 3, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def: The primary professional organization for speech-language pathologists and audiologists in the United States. ASHA establishes the Scope of Practice and Code of Ethics for both professions and maintains Certificates of Clinical Competence (CCC-SLP and CCC-A).
glossary.source: Lecture 1.2.2 — Chapter 2: The Professionals, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.term: ASHA Essential Skills for Dysphagia Clinicians
glossary.def: ASHA (2002-2007) outlined six essential skills for SLPs providing dysphagia services: (1) Identify people at risk for dysphagia; (2) Conduct and interpret CLINICAL ASSESSMENTS of oral-pharyngeal and respiratory functions related to feeding; (3) Conduct and interpret INSTRUMENTAL-BASED EVALUATIONS of swallowing; (4) Develop INTERVENTION STRATEGIES (safe feeding recommendations, swallowing precautions, therapeutic interventions); (5) Document care and discharge planning; (6) Provide EDUCATION, COUNSELING, and TRAINING to patients and all relevant individuals (family and health professionals). Beyond academic coursework, considerable hands-on training during hospital internships and professional workshops and seminars is necessary to become competent to work independently with dysphagia patients.
glossary.source: Fogle, Ch. 18

glossary.def: Material (food, liquid, saliva, etc.) penetrating the larynx and entering the airway below the true vocal folds; food or liquid entering the lungs rather than the stomach after the swallow.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: An acute inflammation of the lungs caused by foreign material (e.g., food or liquid) entering the lung tissue and resulting in infection; alveolar and bronchiole congestion with substances discharged from the alveolar walls as a protection against foreign material.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: ASPIRATION: Material (food, liquid, saliva, etc.) penetrating the larynx and entering the airway BELOW the true vocal folds — food or liquid entering the lungs rather than the stomach after the swallow. Risk: aspiration pneumonia. SILENT ASPIRATION: Penetration of food or liquid (including saliva) into the larynx and passing below the vocal folds WITHOUT a protective cough or choking occurring. Particularly dangerous because the patient has no normal sensation in the laryngeal region and does not react to protect the airway. Must be identified through bedside evaluation (checking for adequate protective cough) and confirmed through instrumental evaluation (MBSS or FEES).
glossary.source: Fogle, Ch. 18

glossary.def: Amplification systems, other than conventional hearing aids, that are designed for specific listening situations (e.g., personal amplifiers, telephone amplifiers, personal text telephones, and personal FM systems).
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: A common disorder characterized by yellowish plaques of cholesterol and other lipids (fats) in the inner layers of the walls of arteries; often associated with tobacco use, obesity, hypertension, diabetes mellitus, and aging.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: A clinical doctoral degree required to practice as an audiologist, alongside a Ph.D. Both degrees require completion of a Clinical Fellowship Year, national examination, and state licensure.
glossary.source: Lecture 1.2.2 — Chapter 2: The Professionals, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def: A standard graph used to record pure-tone hearing thresholds with air- and bone-conduction thresholds graphed by frequency in hertz (Hz) and hearing level (HL) in decibels (dB).
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: A licensed professional who identifies, evaluates, diagnoses, and treats disorders of auditory and vestibular function. Requires a Ph.D. or Au.D., completion of a Clinical Fellowship Year, passage of a national exam, and state licensure. Determines type and extent of hearing loss and assesses amplification needs.
glossary.source: Lecture 1.2.2 — Chapter 2: The Professionals, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def: Support personnel who perform duties directed and supervised by a licensed audiologist. Requires a high school diploma and competency-based training. Assists with routine tasks under direct audiologist supervision.
glossary.source: Lecture 1.2.2 — Chapter 2: The Professionals, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def: An electronic device designed to measure the sensitivity of hearing of pure-tone frequencies (hertz, Hz) and intensities (decibels, dB); sounds are delivered to the ears either through earphones or free field in an audiometry booth.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: An electrophysiological response to sound that consists of five to seven identifiable peaks that represent neural function of auditory pathways.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: The understanding of spoken messages, as distinguished from producing messages.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: A therapy approach that trains a child's ability to perceive and identify differences between sounds — specifically between the target (correct) sound and the child's error production. Used as a foundational step in many articulation therapy approaches before production is targeted. Sometimes called 'ear training.'
glossary.source: Ch. 5 slides — Fogle/SJDC

glossary.def: A hallmark APD problem — difficulty understanding and attending to a primary auditory signal (the 'figure,' e.g., a teacher's voice) in the presence of background noise or competing sounds (the 'ground'). The person cannot separate the important auditory signal from irrelevant background noise. Results in difficulty following instruction in classroom settings, group conversations, and any noisy environment.
glossary.source: Ch. 9 PPTX / Lecture Ch. 9, Kidwell

glossary.def: The perceptual processing of auditory information in the CENTRAL NERVOUS SYSTEM (CNS) and the neurobiological activity that underlies that processing (ASHA, 2005). What the brain does with the information we hear — including: (1) understanding what was heard; (2) remembering it; (3) maintaining the sequence of information; (4) combining visual information with the auditory; (5) effectively using the information. The EAR transfers sound waves → fluid waves → electrochemical signals → to the brain. HEARING actually occurs in the BRAIN, not the ear.
glossary.source: ADHD_APD.pdf / Ch. 9 PPTX / Lecture Ch. 9, Kidwell

glossary.term: Auditory Processing Disorder (APD) / Central Auditory Processing Disorder (CAPD)
glossary.def: Difficulty using auditory information to communicate and learn, WITHOUT the presence of a peripheral hearing loss (ASHA, 2005). The ear and peripheral hearing may be intact — the problem is in the CNS's processing of what was heard. Also called Central APD (CAPD). Kidwell's view: APD is a type of RECEPTIVE LANGUAGE DISORDER. APD may negatively impact language, learning, and reading. Characterized by 'paucity' (limited) research. APD, ADHD, learning disorders, psychological disorders, and psychophysiological disorders may all interact.
glossary.source: Lecture Ch. 9, Kidwell / Ch. 9 PPTX

glossary.def: Aural rehabilitation methods designed to optimize use of residual hearing by structured practice in listening, altering the environment, and use of hearing aids to increase sound awareness, sound discrimination, identification of words, and comprehension of spoken messages.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.term: Augmentative and Alternative Communication (AAC) / Assistive Technology (AT)
glossary.def: Any approach designed to support, enhance, or supplement the communication of individuals who are not independent verbal communicators in all situations. Augmentative approaches: ENHANCE conventional forms of communication. Alternative approaches: REPLACE conventional forms. Includes electronic (high-tech: speech-generating devices) and non-electronic (low-tech: communication boards) systems. Requires a multidisciplinary team. Selection techniques: direct (pointing) or scanning.
glossary.source: Ch. 5 slides — Fogle/SJDC

glossary.def: Intervention for people who have not developed listening, speech, and language skills, and may include diagnosis of communication and hearing-related difficulties, speech perception training, speech and language therapy, manual communication, educational management, and patient and family counseling. Sometimes used synonymously with aural rehabilitation.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: A broad term that involves intervention aimed at minimizing and alleviating the communication difficulties associated with hearing loss, including identification and diagnosis, patient and family counseling and education, selection and fitting amplification devices, communication training strategies, speech perception training, speech-language therapy, promotion of literacy, and educational management.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: A lifelong complex behavioral syndrome that appears by 3 years of age, with children having a markedly absent interest in social interactions and relationships, severely impaired communication skills, and repetitive, stereotyped movements, combined with restricted interests that are often obsessive or fixated.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: Over-learned sequences of words that can be recited without much conscious thought, such as counting to 10; saying the days of the week, months of the year, and the alphabet; and singing some songs.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: In AOS, automatic speech (well-learned, overlearned sequences) is typically preserved while volitional/purposeful speech is impaired. Examples of preserved automatic speech: counting to 10, reciting days of the week, singing familiar songs, producing common phrases ('thank you', 'I don't know'), and even cursing. However, if you ask the person to say 'nine' on demand — even right after counting to eight — they may not be able to do so.
glossary.source: Lecture Ch. 17, Kidwell, SJDC

glossary.def: Respect for the patient's right to determine his or her own choices in making healthcare decisions.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def:
Behaviors developed by people who stutter to AVOID stuttering — a core component of covert reactions. Types of avoidance:
• WORD-LEVEL: avoiding feared words; substituting non-feared synonyms; some develop large vocabularies specifically to circumvent feared words
• SITUATION-LEVEL: not speaking in class, not asking questions, refusing oral presentations, avoiding the telephone, avoiding social situations
• PERSON-LEVEL: avoiding people who are the most difficult to speak with
Important: avoidance behaviors reduce short-term anxiety but REINFORCE and WORSEN stuttering long-term — maintaining the anxiety-avoidance-stuttering cycle.
glossary.source: Lecture Ch. 12, Kidwell, SJDC

glossary.def: The long, slender projection of a neuron that carries electrical impulses away from the cell body toward other neurons, muscles, or glands. Part of the basic transmission sequence: neuron → axon → dendrite → neuron.
glossary.source: CSD Ch3 PP (Fogle 3rd ed.)

glossary.def: Production of consonant and vowel sounds in the same syllable (e.g., ba-ba, da, ga). Occurs from approximately 3–6 months onward. Key distinction from cooing: babbling MUST include a consonant AND a vowel in the same syllable. Deaf babies may begin babbling but stop by 8 months because they cannot hear themselves.
glossary.source: Lecture 3.2 — Ch. 4, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def: A feeling of fullness or pressure in the middle ear as a result of ascending or descending with changes in altitude. Symptoms may include general ear discomfort, fullness or stuffiness in the ear, difficulty hearing, and dizziness. Prolonged barotrauma may result in ear pain, nosebleed, moderate to severe hearing loss, and in severe cases, rupture of the tympanic membrane.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: Perspective that language is a set of verbal behaviors learned through operant conditioning (B.F. Skinner). Key mechanisms: reinforcement (behavior reinforced immediately after it occurs), imitation, and successive approximation. SLPs apply this through stimulus-response therapy and shaping. Limitation: cannot explain novel sentences or rapid vocabulary acquisition without direct teaching.
glossary.source: Lecture 3.2 — Ch. 4, Kidwell / Ch. 4 PDF — Prof. Handy

glossary.def: A principle of physics (airflow/air pressure) that explains how vocal folds close after being forced open. When air moves quickly between the folds, it creates a drop in pressure that pulls them back together, sustaining vibration.
glossary.source: Lecture 2.2 — Ch. 3, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def: Procedures based on the best available evidence (research and expert opinion); procedures that are accepted or prescribed as being correct or most effective.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: Place of articulation involving both lips coming together. 'Bi' = two, 'labial' = lip. Bilabial sounds include /p/, /b/, and /m/. Example: /b/ — both lips are pressed together, the airflow stops, and the vocal folds vibrate (voiced stop bilabial).
glossary.source: Lecture 4.2, Kidwell, SJDC

glossary.def: The attempt to understand and resolve ethical issues and problems in a healthcare setting.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: The process of becoming aware of various physiological functions of the body using instruments that provide information on the activity of muscles or systems being monitored, with the goal of being able to control or manipulate those muscles or systems voluntarily.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: The primary, life-sustaining purpose of a body structure. For example, the biological function of the lips is to hold food and liquid in the oral cavity; of the vocal folds, to prevent food/liquid from entering the trachea (airway protection).
glossary.source: Lecture 2.2 — Ch. 3, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def: A semipermeable membrane barrier that prevents circulating blood in the capillaries and other blood vessels from making direct contact with the cells of the nervous system, but allows oxygen and nutrients to pass through the membrane to supply the nervous system tissue.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: Transmission of sound to the inner ear through vibration applied to the bones of the skull; allows determination of the cochlea's hearing sensitivity while bypassing any outer or middle ear abnormalities.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: Composed of the pons and medulla oblongata. Connects the brain to the spinal cord. Controls the face, mouth, and larynx for speech production. Also controls vital life functions. Damage can cause severe speech, voice, and swallowing problems.
glossary.source: Lecture 2.2 — Ch. 3, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def: Incomplete closure of the vocal folds during phonation that results in excessive unvibrated air escaping.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: A nonfluent aphasia with lesion in or around Broca's area in the left frontal lobe. Characteristics: effortful, trial-and-error groping of articulators; AGRAMMATISM — impaired syntax; TELEGRAPHIC SPEECH — content words (nouns, verbs) are preserved but functor words (articles, prepositions) are omitted; poor repetition; mildly to moderately impaired comprehension. Patient knows what they want to say but finds it very difficult to produce the words. May be limited to single words or very short sentences (under 4 words). Also features limited vocabulary, clumsy sound formation, and difficulty writing. Fluency: −, Comprehension: +, Repetition: −, Naming: −.
glossary.source: Ch. 15 PPTX; Kidwell lecture notes

glossary.def: Located in the lower posterior portion of the left hemisphere's frontal lobe. The center for motor speech control. Plans, sequences, coordinates, and initiates motor movements of the articulators for speech production. Damage causes Broca's (expressive/non-fluent) aphasia.
glossary.source: Lecture 2.2 — Ch. 3, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def: A region in the left frontal lobe of the brain associated with motor programming of speech. Damage to or around Broca's area is the primary cause of acquired apraxia of speech in adults (typically from stroke). Produces nonfluent, effortful speech with groping behavior. Note: CAS in children is NOT specifically linked to Broca's area damage.
glossary.source: Lecture Ch. 17, Kidwell, SJDC

glossary.def: A malignant neoplasm (new growth) characterized by uncontrolled growth of cells that tend to invade surrounding tissue and metastasize (spread) to distant body sites.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: Firm, fibrous, and strong connective tissue that does not contain blood vessels.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: Assessment of CAS is challenging — a definitive diagnosis may not always be possible. CAS is a diagnosis of exclusion: articulation and phonological disorders must be ruled out first. Components: comprehensive oral mechanism and motor speech exams, sequential/alternating movement repetitions (formerly diadochokinesis), structured assessment tools. Tools: KSPT (Kaufman Speech Praxis Test), STDAS-2, SRT (Syllable Repetition Task), DEMSS. Evidence-based practice guides all assessment decisions.
glossary.source: Ch. 6 PPTX — Fogle/SJDC

glossary.def: Treatment principles for CAS: intensive, individualized therapy; motor learning principles; proprioception; drill (many repetitions); incremental increases in difficulty; multiple modalities (tactile, visual, auditory cues); PROMPTS (Prompts for Restructuring Oral Muscular Phonetic Targets). If the child is highly unintelligible, AAC may be implemented. Lack of progress with traditional articulation therapy is a CAS characteristic — therapy must be CAS-specific.
glossary.source: Ch. 6 PPTX / Lecture Ch. 6, Kidwell

glossary.term: CAS vs. Acquired Apraxia of Speech (Adult AOS)
glossary.def: Adult-acquired apraxia of speech (AOS) can be attributed to damage to or around Broca's area (typically from stroke). CAS in children is NOT associated with a specific lesion site in the same way — CAS etiology is neurological but often idiopathic. Both share the hallmark of inconsistent errors in the absence of muscle weakness, but CAS affects developing children who may never have had correct speech.
glossary.source: Lecture Ch. 6, Kidwell, SJDC

glossary.def: Dysphagia may occur at any age — from newborns to end of life. The same neurological causes of motor speech disorders and cognitive disorders may cause dysphagia. CHILDREN: prematurity and low birth weight, heart disease, conditions affecting the airway, meningitis, encephalopathy, cerebral palsy. ADULTS: cerebrovascular accident (stroke — incidence 13-94% post-stroke), traumatic brain injury (TBI), Parkinson's disease, multiple sclerosis, amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS), muscular dystrophy, Alzheimer's disease. Also: cancer (lingual, oral, pharyngeal, laryngeal, esophageal) and its treatment (surgery, radiation, chemotherapy). Side effects of some medications (e.g., muscle relaxants) may also cause swallowing problems.
glossary.source: Fogle, Ch. 18

glossary.def: The ASHA-issued credential certifying that an audiologist has met all educational, clinical, and examination requirements. Maintained by ASHA.
glossary.source: Lecture 1.2.2 — Chapter 2: The Professionals, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.term: CCC-SLP (Certificate of Clinical Competence — Speech-Language Pathology)
glossary.def: The ASHA-issued credential certifying that a speech-language pathologist has met all educational, clinical, and examination requirements. Maintained by ASHA.
glossary.source: Lecture 1.2.2 — Chapter 2: The Professionals, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def: Comprised of the brain, cerebellum, brainstem, and spinal cord. Communicates nerve impulses from neuron to neuron. All incoming and outgoing messages are generated and processed here. Sequence: neuron → axon → dendrite → neuron.
glossary.source: Lecture 2.2 — Ch. 3, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def: Located at the back and bottom of the brain. Important for coordinating muscle groups and maintaining balance and equilibrium. Damage results in ataxia — uncoordinated, irregular movement including speech (ataxic dysarthria).
glossary.source: Lecture 2.2 — Ch. 3, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def: Either of the two halves of the brain that contains a frontal lobe, parietal lobe, occipital lobe, and temporal lobe.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: A neuromotor impairment caused by widespread damage to the CNS — the most frequent cause of dysarthria in children. Characterized by motor impairment affecting any or all speech subsystems. Associated speech, language, and cognitive problems include: dysarthria, receptive/expressive language delays, cognitive impairments, intellectual disabilities. Associated conditions: hearing/vision impairments, seizure disorders. AAC/AT devices are often used to support communication.
glossary.source: Ch. 5 slides — Fogle/SJDC

glossary.def: A heterogeneous group of nonprogressive, permanent disorders of movement and postural development, often accompanied by abnormal muscle tone, loss of selective motor control, muscle weakness, and impaired balance. Caused by widespread CNS damage. The most common cause of dysarthria in children. Four classifications: Spastic (spasticity), Athetoid (athetosis), Ataxic (ataxia), and Mixed CP. Associated problems: hearing/vision impairment, seizure disorders, cognitive impairments, language delays.
glossary.source: Ch. 6 PPTX / Lecture Ch. 6, Kidwell

glossary.def: The blood circulation system supplying the brain. The brain receives 20–25% of the blood pumped from the heart. Disruption to cerebrovascular blood flow (e.g., stroke) causes anoxia and brain tissue death.
glossary.source: CSD Ch3 PP (Fogle 3rd ed.)

glossary.def: A yellowish or brownish waxy secretion produced by ceruminous glands in the ear canal that protects the ear canal from intrusion by insects.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: Ongoing professional development credits required for audiologists (and SLPs) to maintain licensure and certification after initial credentialing.
glossary.source: Lecture 1.2.2 — Chapter 2: The Professionals, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def: A group of motor speech disorders caused by paresis (weakness), paralysis (complete loss of movement), or incoordination of speech muscles as a result of central and/or peripheral nervous system damage, that may affect respiration, phonation, resonation, articulation, and prosody. Dysarthric speech sounds 'mushy' due to distorted consonants and vowels. KEY DISTINCTION from CAS: dysarthria DOES involve muscle weakness; CAS does NOT.
glossary.source: Ch. 6 PPTX / Lecture Ch. 6, Kidwell

glossary.def: A motor speech disorder — NOT caused by muscle weakness. Affects the planning, programming, sequencing, coordinating, and initiating of motor movements of the articulators. Interferes with both articulation and prosody. The motor planning system is impaired, not the muscles themselves. Inconsistent errors and difficulty with longer/more complex words are hallmarks.
glossary.source: Ch. 5 slides — Fogle/SJDC

glossary.def: A childhood motor speech disorder occurring in the absence of muscle weakness that affects the planning, programming, sequencing, coordinating, and initiating of motor movements of the articulators, interfering with articulation and prosody. Etiology: neurological damage, may be primary or secondary sign of a neurobehavioral disorder, or idiopathic (spontaneous, no known cause). CAS is a diagnosis of exclusion — articulation and phonological disorders must be ruled out first.
glossary.source: Lecture Ch. 6, Kidwell / Ch. 6 PPTX

glossary.term: Childhood Stuttering Prevalence and Natural Variability
glossary.def: Approximately 5% of all children are likely to stutter at some point in their development, usually during the preschool years. It is NORMAL for a child to go back and forth between periods of fluency and disfluency — this can happen for no apparent reason, or when the child is excited, tired, or feels rushed to speak. (Note: this 5% lifetime prevalence differs from the 1% current prevalence cited in Ch. 12 — 5% of children stutter at some point; most recover, leaving ~1% with persistent stuttering.)
glossary.source: 65_Cause_Treatment_of_Stuttering.pdf — Ducworth, CCC-SLP

glossary.def: Of long duration with slow progress, usually in reference to a disease or disorder.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: A persistent laryngitis lasting more than 10 days with inflammation of the mucous membrane lining in the larynx, accompanied by edema of the vocal folds with hoarseness and loss of voice that is often caused by heavy smoking, coughing, allergies and chemical irritants, as well as ongoing vocal abuse and misuse.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: A degenerative brain disease caused by REPEATED mild brain traumas from sports-related concussions. Symptoms begin 8–10 years after repeated mTBIs and include: headaches, mood disorders, cognitive difficulties, speech and language difficulties, aggressive behavior, dementia, and suicidal tendencies. Can ONLY be definitively diagnosed post-mortem via brain autopsy — cannot be confirmed in a living patient. Dr. Bennet Omalu (who worked at the Stockton medical examiner's office) first researched and identified CTE in 2015. The reason professional athletes are now removed from play after concussions and given recovery time.
glossary.source: Ch. 16 PPTX; Kidwell lecture notes

glossary.def: The actual age of a person that is derived from date of birth and expressed in days, months, and years.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: A specific avoidance behavior in which a person who stutters TALKS AROUND a feared word rather than saying it directly — choosing a longer alternate phrase or description to sidestep the word they anticipate stuttering on. Circumlocution is one of several escape/avoidance behaviors driven by fear of specific sounds, words, people, or speaking situations. Other avoidance behaviors include: substituting a different word, delaying a comment, interjecting starter sounds ('um,' 'ah,' 'you know'), covering the mouth, avoiding eye contact, refusing to speak, or using vocal abnormalities (speaking rapidly, in a whisper, slowly, in a monotone, or with an accent).
glossary.source: 306_BehaviorsOfStuttering.pdf — Hodgson, CCC-SLP

glossary.def: A multi-step clinical assessment of dysphagia performed at the bedside without instrumental equipment. Steps: (1) Careful review of patient's medical chart; (2) Screening/interview — patient's perceptions of the problem and assessment of whether patient has a sufficiently strong PROTECTIVE COUGH to clear the airway; (3) Oral-mechanism examination — anatomy and physiology of articulatory structures; (4) Observation of patient swallowing different LIQUID CONSISTENCIES (regular/thin, nectar, honey, pudding) and different FOOD TEXTURES (regular, mechanically altered, puree); (5) Determination of aspiration risk and need for instrumental evaluation; (6) Documentation in medical chart. Referrals are based on: patient appears at risk for aspiration, observed feeding difficulties, or patient is not taking in adequate nourishment.
glossary.source: Fogle, Ch. 18

glossary.def: A supervised clinical experience required of audiologists after completing their degree, prior to full independent licensure. Must be completed in addition to passing a national exam and obtaining state credentials.
glossary.source: Lecture 1.2.2 — Chapter 2: The Professionals, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def: The application of information based on actual observation of a person, combined with objective and subjective data that lead to a decision or conclusion.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: Healthcare, rehabilitation, and educational professionals, such as physicians, nurses, physical therapists, occupational therapists, speech-language pathologists, audiologists, psychiatrists, or psychologists, involved in clinical practice who base their practice on direct observation and treatment of patients and clients.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: CLOSED HEAD INJURY (CHI): the most common type of TBI in the civilian population. The skull is NOT penetrated — damage is caused by blunt force or the sudden deceleration of the head causing the brain to continue moving and strike the skull. Most common civilian causes: motor vehicle accidents and assaults. COUP-CONTRECOUP INJURY: brain is damaged at the SITE of impact (coup/primary) AND on the OPPOSITE side (contrecoup/secondary) as the brain 'flows' and compresses against the other side of the skull. Impact also causes significant SWELLING that may last days or weeks and cause additional damage. BLOOD-BRAIN BARRIER (BBB) may be disrupted.
glossary.source: Ch. 16 PPTX; Kidwell lecture notes

glossary.def: A common phonological process in which part of a consonant cluster (blend) is omitted. Example: 'pider' for 'spider', 'poon' for 'spoon', 'pill' for 'spill', 'pot' for 'spot'. Key distinction: the child will omit the /s/ only when it is part of a blend — NOT when /s/ precedes a vowel (e.g., 'song' and 'same' are produced correctly). One of the most common phonological processes seen in disordered children.
glossary.source: Lecture 4.2, Kidwell, SJDC

glossary.def: A fluency disorder characterized by abnormally fast speech with omissions of sounds and syllables, causing words to be compressed or reduced in length. Disrupts fluent speech.
glossary.source: Lecture 1.2.1 — Chapter 1, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def:
A SEPARATE fluency-related disorder that shares some characteristics with stuttering but is distinct:
• LACKS the repetitions and blocks of stuttering
• Speech is abnormally FAST or IRREGULAR (or both)
• Person lacks normal sound, syllable, phrase, and pausing patterns
• Speech appears DISORGANIZED more than disfluent
• Listener feels the person has something to say but cannot follow what it is
• Also classified as a speech AND language disorder (shares that category with stuttering)
• Incidence is different from stuttering
glossary.source: Lecture Ch. 12, Kidwell, SJDC

glossary.def:
A SEPARATE fluency-related disorder that shares some characteristics with stuttering but is distinct:
• LACKS the repetitions and blocks of stuttering
• Speech is abnormally FAST or IRREGULAR (or both)
• Person lacks normal sound, syllable, phrase, and pausing patterns
• Speech appears DISORGANIZED more than disfluent
• Listener feels the person has something to say but cannot follow what it is
• Also classified as a speech AND language disorder (shares that category with stuttering)
• Incidence is different from stuttering
glossary.source: Lecture Ch. 12, Kidwell, SJDC

glossary.def: The pronunciation of a sound that is affected by the sound preceding and/or following the sound. The influence of one phoneme on another in perception or production.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: The part of the inner ear containing the sensory mechanism of hearing; a spiral tunnel with 2¾ turns about 30 mm long, resembling a tiny snail shell.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: A device that enables individuals with profound hearing loss to perceive sound through an array of electrodes that are surgically implanted in the cochlea and deliver electrical signals to the vestibulocochlear (VIII cranial) nerve, and an external amplifier that activates the electrode array.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: A set of professional standards established by ASHA that all SLPs and Audiologists must follow. Violations can result in loss of certification or licensure. The primary obligation is to put the needs of the client first.
glossary.source: Lecture 1.2.2 — Chapter 2: The Professionals, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def: A normal stage in bilingual language development in which a speaker substitutes words, sounds, or grammatical elements from one language into speech in another language (either automatically or intentionally). Example: 'I cayo here' (cayo = 'I fall' in Spanish). Not a disorder — a natural bilingual behavior.
glossary.source: Lecture 3.2 — Ch. 4, Kidwell / Quiz answer key

glossary.def: Two sounds that differ only in voicing (e.g., /p/ /b/).
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: Two sounds that differ only in voicing — all other distinctive features (place and manner) are identical. Example: /p/ and /b/ are cognates — both are bilabial stops, but /p/ is voiceless and /b/ is voiced. Other cognate pairs: /t/-/d/, /k/-/g/, /f/-/v/, /s/-/z/, /sh/-/zh/.
glossary.source: Quiz answer key — Ch. 5 / Lecture 4.2, Kidwell

glossary.def: The progressive and continuous growth of perception, memory, imagination, conception, judgment, and reasoning. The intellectual counterpart of a person's biological adaptation to the environment. Closely linked to language development — sufficient cognitive function is required for language to emerge and be cultivated.
glossary.source: Ch. 4 PDF — Prof. Handy / Quiz answer key

glossary.def: A disruption to the act or process of thinking or learning that affects communication. Involves deficits in perceiving, memory, reasoning, judgment, and problem-solving. May be developmental or acquired.
glossary.source: Lecture 1.2.1 — Chapter 1, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def: An impairment of attention, perception of stimuli (auditory, visual, tactile, taste, smell), memory, reasoning, judgment, and problem solving.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: TBI may produce a comatose or semicomatose state followed by multiple cognitive impairments: ATTENTION deficits (difficulty maintaining focus), MEMORY deficits (working memory, recent memory, long-term/remote memory), ORIENTATION deficits (disorientation to person, place, time, and purpose), REASONING and PROBLEM-SOLVING deficits, and EXECUTIVE FUNCTION deficits. Executive functions include: abstract thinking, anticipating needs, setting goals, planning strategies, implementing strategies, determining if strategies are working, and regulating emotions and behaviors. When executive dysfunction is present, everyday activities are negatively impacted.
glossary.source: Ch. 16 PPTX; Kidwell lecture notes

glossary.def: Individualized programs focused on specific cognitive areas (e.g., improving memory and language processing) to help brain injured individuals restore abilities or compensate for cognitive deficits to promote independent functioning in daily living.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: A model of counseling designed to help individuals recognize and examine problematic beliefs and replace them with more adaptive and flexible ways of thinking.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: A language problem that exists as a SECONDARY issue to a cognitive problem — the language impairment results from underlying cognitive deficits rather than a primary language disorder. Most commonly caused by: traumatic brain injury (TBI), right-hemisphere brain damage, and dementia. Distinguished from aphasia, where the language system itself is the primary site of damage.
glossary.source: Ch. 16 PPTX; Kidwell lecture notes

glossary.def: A prolonged period of unconsciousness in which a patient has minimal, if any, purposeful responses to stimuli.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: Any means by which individuals relate their wants, needs, thoughts, knowledge, and feelings to another person (Fogle). A basic human need involving two or more individuals. May be verbal or nonverbal. 'We cannot not communicate' — communication occurs in any modality, not just spoken language.
glossary.source: Lecture 1.2.1 — Chapter 1, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def: An apparatus, electronic device, book, or simple board with letters, words, numbers, pictures, and/or symbols used by children and adults with very limited functional verbal communication abilities.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: Defined by Charles Van Riper (1978) as communication that deviates from that of others, calls attention to itself, interferes with communication, and causes distress for the speaker and/or listener. May affect quality of life. The SLP and Audiologist work to improve this quality.
glossary.source: Lecture 1.2.1 — Chapter 1, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def: The channels through which communication occurs. Input modalities: auditory, visual, and tactile. Output modalities: speaking, gesturing, and writing. A disruption to any one or more modalities results in a communication disorder.
glossary.source: Lecture 1.2.1 — Chapter 1, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def: Training caregivers, family members, and communication partners to optimize interactions with persons with aphasia. Strategies: speak slowly; pause between topics; use short utterances; repeat and rephrase; use gestures and facial expressions; paraphrase; use multiple modalities; create equality in conversation. Specific strategies: have the person watch your face as you speak; use natural tone (do not exaggerate or shout); insert short pauses between sentences; if not understood, repeat verbatim first then change a few words; use writing; ask yes/no questions whenever possible; limit figurative language; avoid using numbers. Do not finish their sentences.
glossary.source: Ch. 15 PPTX; Handy Handout #177

glossary.term: Communication Tips for Patients with Dementia
glossary.def: Strategies for communicating with older patients and individuals with dementia: allow EXTRA TIME; minimize visual and auditory DISTRACTIONS; sit FACE TO FACE without a table between you (catty-corner) with good EYE CONTACT; LISTEN without interrupting; speak SLOWLY (but not in an abnormal sing-song way), clearly, and with sufficient loudness; use SHORT SIMPLE words and sentences (rule of 5s) without sounding patronizing; stick to ONE TOPIC at a time; SIMPLIFY and write down instructions; FREQUENTLY SUMMARIZE the most important points; give the person a chance to ask QUESTIONS.
glossary.source: Ch. 16 PPTX; Kidwell lecture notes

glossary.def: The ability to use language(s) and/or dialect(s) — including phonology, morphology, syntax, semantics, and pragmatics — and to know when, where, and with whom to use them. Emerges around 18–24 months with two-word utterances (e.g., requests: 'more milk'; negation: 'no more'; commentary: 'me home').
glossary.source: Ch. 4 PDF — Prof. Handy / Quiz answer key

glossary.def: Existing simultaneously with and usually independent of another condition, impairment, or disorder.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: The presence of two or more conditions, disorders, or disabilities.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: A mild traumatic brain injury caused by a violent jarring or shaking, or blunt instrument injury to the skull that may or may not result in loss of consciousness or seizures, but commonly causes temporary (days to weeks) mild cognitive impairments.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.term: Concussion (Mild TBI / mTBI) and Postconcussion Syndrome (PCS)
glossary.def: CONCUSSION (mTBI): mild traumatic brain injury caused by a blow to the head or violent shaking of the head/upper body. Usually does NOT result in loss of consciousness (LOC), although it may. Those with mild TBI often do not receive rehabilitation but may later notice they cannot perform at prior levels — problems at home and work, relationship deterioration. POSTCONCUSSION SYNDROME (PCS): long-lasting subtle impairments following concussion. Even mild TBI causes long-term structural changes in white and grey matter.
glossary.source: Ch. 16 PPTX; Kidwell lecture notes

glossary.term: Conditions That Increase vs. Decrease Stuttering
glossary.def:
CONDITIONS THAT INCREASE STUTTERING:
• Saying one's own name or using 'I'
• Having to wait before speaking
• Talking on the telephone
• Speaking to an authority figure
• High-stress/high-stakes situations

CONDITIONS THAT DECREASE STUTTERING:
• Speaking to a metronome or beat
• SINGING (stutterers are almost always fluent when singing)
• Shadowing someone's speech (saying what they say, slightly behind)
• Reading in unison with others
• DELAYED AUDITORY FEEDBACK (DAF) — device that delays the speaker's hearing of their own voice; now small enough to fit like a hearing aid
glossary.source: Lecture Ch. 12, Kidwell, SJDC

glossary.def: A fluent aphasia resulting from damage to the arcuate fasciculus — the fibers connecting Wernicke's area to Broca's area. Key characteristic: SIGNIFICANT DIFFICULTY REPEATING multisyllabic words, phrases, and sentences (repetition is impaired despite relatively intact comprehension and fluency). Also features phonemic and verbal paraphasias, some word-finding problems, and comprehension that is functional but may be mildly impaired. Increased complexity of utterance increases difficulty. Fluency: +, Comprehension: +, Repetition: −, Naming: −.
glossary.source: Ch. 15 PPTX; Kidwell lecture notes

glossary.def: A decrease in hearing loudness due to poor sound conduction through the outer or middle ear. Causes include malformation, ear canal blockage, eardrum or ossicle damage, or middle ear infections.
glossary.source: Lecture 1.2.1 — Chapter 1, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def: A disorder that is present at birth.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: Speech sounds articulated by either stopping the outgoing air stream or creating a narrow opening of resistance using the articulators.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: A sequence in which two or more consonant sounds appear together with no vowel separation (e.g., /str/ in 'string', /bl/ in 'black'). Among the last phonological features to develop — most emerge by age 5 along with later-developing sounds (/r, l, s, z, sh, ch/).
glossary.source: Ch. 4 PDF — Prof. Handy / Quiz answer key
