glossary.def: Speech sounds articulated by stopping the outgoing airstream or by creating a narrow opening using the articulators. Described by three distinctive features: Place (where articulators come together), Manner (how the airstream is modified), and Voice (vocal folds vibrating = voiced; not vibrating = voiceless).
glossary.source: Ch. 5 slides — Fogle/SJDC / Lecture 4.2, Kidwell

glossary.def: Vocal fold lesions occurring in the POSTERIOR region where the vocal folds make contact (arytenoid cartilage area). Result from vocal hyperfunction or hard glottal attacks. A functional/organic lesion — among the most frequent benign vocal fold diagnoses. Medical clearance and ENT evaluation are required before SLP treatment.
glossary.source: Lecture Ch. 13, Kidwell / Ch. 13 PPTX

glossary.def: Production of vowel-like sounds (usually /u/ and /oo/) and some consonant-like sounds (/k/, /g/) by infants when feeling comfort or pleasure and interacting with a caregiver. Occurs birth–6 months. Distinguished from babbling because it does NOT combine consonants and vowels in the same syllable.
glossary.source: Quiz answer key + Lecture 3.2 — Ch. 4, Kidwell

glossary.def: A large bundle of nerve fibers connecting the left and right cerebral hemispheres. Allows the two hemispheres to work together to analyze information and produce coordinated, timely responses.
glossary.source: Lecture 2.2 — Ch. 3, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def: The outer layer of brain tissue containing nerve cell bodies (neurons).
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: Shrinkage and wasting away of cortical tissue that is common in dementia.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: An applied social science and a helping, interpersonal relationship in which the clinician's intentions are to assist a person or family member to understand a hearing, communication, or swallowing disorder and its impact on their lives, and ways of preventing, managing, adjusting to, or coping with these disorders.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: Feelings and thoughts such as frustration, anxiety, anger, guilt, hostility, shame, and expectations of difficulty talking, which lead to inhibitory and avoidance behaviors in individuals who stutter.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def:
The internal feelings, beliefs, and thoughts that accompany stuttering. Covert reactions can be SOME OF THE MOST SERIOUS CHALLENGES in therapy and in life. Include: frustration, anxiety, anger, guilt, hostility, shame, and expectations of difficulty talking.

Consequences of covert reactions:
• Avoidance behaviors: not speaking in class, not asking questions, refusing/minimizing oral presentations, avoiding social activities
• Feared words: specific words become feared; person substitutes non-feared words (some develop large vocabularies to avoid feared words)
• Life choices impacted: career selected to minimize communication demands, reduced social involvement, isolation, loneliness

★ THE CYCLE: Negative emotions → more tension → more stuttering → more negative emotions (self-reinforcing).
glossary.source: Fluency_Disorders.pdf / Lecture Ch. 12, Kidwell

glossary.def: 12 pairs of nerves that send messages to and from the brain and muscles of the head, face, and neck via the PNS. Key cranial nerves for speech: V (Trigeminal), VII (Facial), VIII (Vestibulocochlear/Auditory), IX (Glossopharyngeal), X (Vagus), XI (Accessory), XII (Hypoglossal).
glossary.source: Lecture 2.2 — Ch. 3, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def: A ring-shaped cartilage that forms the base of the larynx, sitting just above the trachea. Part of the laryngeal framework along with the thyroid cartilage, arytenoid cartilages, and epiglottis.
glossary.source: Lecture 2.2 — Ch. 3, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def: The first vocalization of a newborn (neonate). A reflexive prelinguistic vocalization that communicates distress or need. The first sound in the sequence of prelinguistic vocal development.
glossary.source: Ch. 4 PDF — Prof. Handy, SJDC

glossary.def: The understanding and appropriate responding to the unique combination of cultural variables—including ability, age, beliefs, ethnicity, experience, gender, gender identity, linguistic background, national origin, race, religion, sexual orientation, and socioeconomic status—that the professional and client/patient bring to interactions.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: A perspective of language development that emphasizes the similarities and differences of people and languages spoken around the world, and stresses that no language or dialect is better than another. SLPs must appreciate CLD and distinguish language differences from language disorders.
glossary.source: Ch. 4 PDF — Prof. Handy, SJDC

glossary.def: The philosophies, values, attitudes, perceptions, religious and spiritual beliefs, educational values, language customs, childrearing practices, lifestyles, and arts shared by a group of people and passed from one generation to the next. A foundational concept in understanding CLD in SLP practice.
glossary.source: Ch. 4 PDF — Prof. Handy, SJDC

glossary.def: Individuals who are deaf or hard of hearing who share a common language (usually manual communication), common experiences and values, and a common way of interacting with each other and with hearing people.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: Ideology, beliefs, and customs shared by many individuals with prelinguistic deafness that may include communication, social protocol, entertainment, art, recreation, worship, and other aspects of culture.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: A basic unit of measure of the intensity (loudness) of sound.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: The first set of 20 teeth. Begin to erupt between 6–9 months of age and continue erupting over 18–24 months. Begin shedding (falling out) between 6–13 years of age to be replaced by the 32 permanent teeth.
glossary.source: CSD Ch3 PP (Fogle 3rd ed.)

glossary.def: Any condition that causes gradual deterioration of normal body functions.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: The act of swallowing. One of the three biological functions of the tongue. A major area of SLP practice — swallowing disorders are treated by SLPs but are not classified as communication disorders.
glossary.source: Lecture 2.2 — Ch. 3, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def: An assistive/treatment device that plays back the speaker's own voice with a slight delay through earphones. Creates a condition in which stuttering is significantly reduced or eliminated — similar to speaking in unison or choral reading. Once large (reel-to-reel), DAF devices are now small enough to fit like a hearing aid. Used as both a treatment tool and a demonstration of how auditory feedback affects speech fluency.
glossary.source: Lecture Ch. 12, Kidwell, SJDC

glossary.term: Dementia Assessment and Treatment: SLP Role
glossary.def: PHYSICIANS and PSYCHOLOGISTS diagnose dementia and Alzheimer's disease — not the SLP. The PURPOSE of SLP evaluation is to determine cognitive, communication, and swallowing FUNCTIONING and to determine if the individual is a GOOD CANDIDATE FOR THERAPY. The SLP cannot cure the condition but uses strategies to MAINTAIN the best communication possible and therefore the best QUALITY OF LIFE. Standardized assessment tools: Arizona Battery for Communication Disorders of Dementia (ABCDD), Communicative Activities of Daily Living 2nd ed., Ross Information Processing Assessment: Geriatric 2nd ed. (RIPA-G:2). Right-hemisphere syndrome assessed with MIRBI-2, RICE-R, RHLB-2.
glossary.source: Ch. 16 PPTX; Kidwell lecture notes

glossary.def: DEMENTIA: degenerative neurological functioning that is more severe than would be expected from normal aging; involves decline in intellect, memory, communication, and personality severe enough to interfere with daily functioning in work or social life. Includes multiple types: vascular disease dementia, multi-infarct dementia, frontotemporal dementia, and Alzheimer's disease. DSM-5 has primary diagnostic criteria. SENILITY: the NORMAL loss of cognition due to aging — NOT a disorder. Key distinction: dementia is pathological and progressive; senility describes normal age-related cognitive changes.
glossary.source: Ch. 16 PPTX; Kidwell lecture notes

glossary.def: The branched projections of a neuron that receive incoming signals from other neurons via synapses. Part of the basic transmission sequence: neuron → axon → dendrite → neuron.
glossary.source: CSD Ch3 PP (Fogle 3rd ed.)

glossary.def: The type, number, and arrangement of teeth in the maxilla and mandible. Includes incisors, cuspids, bicuspids, premolars, and molars. Primary (baby) teeth: 20 total, erupt 6–24 months. Permanent teeth: 32 total, erupt 6–13 years.
glossary.source: Lecture 2.2 — Ch. 3, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.term: Developmental vs. Acquired Speech Sound Disorders
glossary.def: Developmental speech sound disorders: the child has NEVER spoken correctly — the disorder is present from the beginning of speech development. Acquired speech sound disorders: the person LOSES a previously established speech skill due to an accident, stroke, or disease process. Acquired disorders can occur in children but are more commonly seen in adults.
glossary.source: Lecture 4.2, Kidwell, SJDC

glossary.def: A standardized oral-motor task used in CAS/dysarthria assessment — the client rapidly and repeatedly produces alternating syllables (e.g., 'puh-tuh-kuh'). Tests the speed and coordination of articulatory movements. Formerly called 'diadochokinesis'; now referred to as sequential/alternating movement repetitions.
glossary.source: Ch. 6 PPTX — Fogle/SJDC

glossary.def: A specific form of speech and language used in a geographical region or among a large group of people that differs significantly from the standard of the larger language community in pronunciation, vocabulary, grammar, and idiomatic use of words. No dialect is superior to another. A dialect is a difference, not a disorder.
glossary.source: Ch. 4 PDF — Prof. Handy, SJDC

glossary.def: The primary muscle of breathing. During speech and singing, the diaphragm becomes actively engaged — pushing upward while the abdominal muscles contract — to force air out and support longer exhalation needed for speech.
glossary.source: Eugene Speech Language Therapy — How We Speak (YouTube, 11xf89bXaCg)

glossary.def: The primary muscle of respiration. A dome-shaped muscle that separates the thoracic and abdominal cavities. Contracts and lowers during inhalation (increasing thoracic volume). Not under conscious control. Involved in active exhalation during speech and singing.
glossary.source: Lecture 2.2 — Ch. 3, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def: The process of narrowing possibilities and reaching conclusions about the nature of a deficit.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def:
Key differential between AOS and Dysarthria:
AOS: (1) Errors NOT from muscle weakness; (2) INCONSISTENT, variable errors; (3) Substitutions more common; (4) Groping behavior; (5) Errors increase with length; (6) Automatic speech intact; (7) Front sounds better than back sounds; (8) 'Islands of fluency'
DYSARTHRIA: (1) Weakness IS present; (2) Errors consistent across productions; (3) Distortions most common; (4) Sounds 'slurred/mushy'; (5) Affects one or more speech subsystems; (6) Automatic speech also affected in severe cases
Differential diagnosis is made by comparing characteristics and 'ranking possibilities' until the most likely diagnosis emerges.
glossary.source: Lecture Ch. 17, Kidwell / Motor_Speech_Disorders.pdf

glossary.def: A combination of two vowel sounds that occur together within a single syllable, gliding from one vowel to another. Examples: /aɪ/ in 'bite', /ɔɪ/ in 'boy', /aʊ/ in 'bout'. Classified as a special type of vowel sound.
glossary.source: Ch. 5 slides — Fogle/SJDC

glossary.def: Two distinct pitches perceived simultaneously during phonation that is caused by the two vocal folds vibrating under different degrees of tension or vibration of the ventricular folds concurrently with the true vocal folds.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: Any restriction or lack of ability to perform an activity in the manner or within the range considered normal for a human being; the impairment, loss, or absence of a physical or intellectual function.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: Extended verbal exchange on a topic, involving pragmatic skills such as initiation of topics, turn-taking, topic maintenance, and repairs. Develops during the 3–4 year period alongside narrative ability.
glossary.source: Ch. 4 PDF — Prof. Handy, SJDC

glossary.def: A fluency disorder involving unusual repetitions of sounds, syllables, words, or phrases; prolongations of sounds; and/or abnormal stoppages (silent blocks). May include visible tension and struggle behaviors. 'Disfluency' is currently the preferred term over 'stuttering'.
glossary.source: Lecture 1.2.1 — Chapter 1, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.term: Disorders of the Four Swallowing Phases
glossary.def: ORAL PREPARATORY PHASE DISORDERS: Decreased cognition (impaired awareness); impulsivity (dangerously large bites, packing food); weak mandible, lip, or tongue muscles causing poor bolus control. ORAL PHASE DISORDERS: Poor lip seal causing anterior spillage (drooling); poor posterior seal causing premature spillage; weak tongue tip causing discoordinated posterior movement; pocketed food in sulcus (residue/stasis); insufficient tongue propulsive force. PHARYNGEAL PHASE DISORDERS: Delayed swallow response; impaired timing and coordination; weak soft palate (nasal regurgitation); weak laryngeal elevation (epiglottis fails to close — penetration/aspiration); weak vocal fold closure (aspiration); silent aspiration (no protective cough). ESOPHAGEAL PHASE DISORDERS: UEV does not open or stay open long enough; slow or absent esophageal peristalsis (residue, discomfort, infection risk); lower esophageal valve fails to relax (food cannot enter stomach).
glossary.source: Fogle, Ch. 18

glossary.def: The smallest individual difference required to differentiate one phoneme from another in a language. The three distinctive features used to describe consonants are: Place (where articulators come together), Manner (how the airstream is modified), and Voice (whether vocal folds vibrate or not).
glossary.source: Quiz answer key — Ch. 5

glossary.def: An articulation error in which a sound is produced incorrectly but no standard sound symbol precisely represents the error — it is altered or distorted. Example: an interdental lisp on /s/ (tongue protrudes between teeth — sounds almost like 'th' but is neither a perfect /s/ nor a /th/). Another example: distorted /r/ in medial or final position.
glossary.source: Lecture 4.2, Kidwell, SJDC

glossary.def: The most common genetic chromosome disorder that causes lifelong intellectual disability and developmental delays, and may cause some health problems.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.term: DSM-5 Diagnostic Criteria for ADHD (Four Conditions)
glossary.def:
Per DSM-5 (2013), all FOUR conditions must be met for an ADHD diagnosis:
1. Symptoms present for 6 MONTHS OR LONGER
2. Symptoms present BEFORE AGE 12
3. Symptoms present in TWO OR MORE SETTINGS (usually home AND school)
4. Evidence that symptoms INTERFERE WITH or REDUCE the quality of social, academic, or occupational functioning
glossary.source: Lecture Ch. 9, Kidwell / Ch. 9 PPTX

glossary.def: Someone who learns two or more languages at the same time (simultaneously), rather than sequentially. Distinguished from ESL/ELL, where English is learned after the primary language is established.
glossary.source: Ch. 4 PDF — Prof. Handy, SJDC

glossary.def: An assessment approach in which the SLP provides instruction on a skill during the evaluation, then observes whether and how quickly the child can learn from the instruction. Tests the child's LEARNING POTENTIAL rather than only current performance. Particularly useful for children who may be in a borderline range or for whom standardized tests are culturally biased. Once a child begins therapy, the SLP is continually conducting dynamic assessment — observing and adjusting based on the child's ongoing response to instruction.
glossary.source: Lecture Ch. 7, Kidwell, SJDC

glossary.def: Neurological speech disorders characterized by weakness and incoordination in the muscles that control respiration, phonation, resonation, and articulation. Results from neurological damage affecting neuromuscular execution of speech. KEY DISTINCTION from AOS: MUSCLE WEAKNESS IS PRESENT. Dysarthria can range from mild to severe to profound. May co-occur with dysphagia.
glossary.source: Ch. 17 PPTX / Lecture Ch. 17, Kidwell

glossary.def: A group of motor speech disorders caused by paresis (weakness), paralysis, or lack of coordination of speech muscles as a result of central and/or peripheral nervous system damage. Can affect all speech subsystems: respiration, phonation, resonation, articulation, and prosody. Also associated with dysphagia (swallowing difficulty). Most common cause in children is cerebral palsy.
glossary.source: Ch. 5 slides — Fogle/SJDC

glossary.def: General principles (Evidence-Based Practice): differential diagnosis by comparing to AOS and other disorders. Assessment tools: Assessment of Intelligibility of Dysarthric Speech (Yorkston, Beukelman & Taylor, 1984); Dysarthria Examination Battery (Drummond, 1993); Frenchay Dysarthria Assessment (Enderby, 1983); Quick Assessment for Dysarthria (Tanner & Culbertson, 1999). All four speech subsystems must be assessed. Results may be clear-cut or require a differential diagnosis approach — ranking possibilities to arrive at the most likely diagnosis.
glossary.source: Ch. 17 PPTX — Fogle/SJDC

glossary.def: No formal standardized assessment exists for dysarthria in children younger than 12. Assessment is primarily perceptual and clinical, examining: oral motor structure and function, respiration, hypernasality, type and amount of articulation distortions, intelligibility. Tools: Frenchay Dysarthria Assessment 2nd ed. (FDA-2, 2008). Evidence-based practice guides all assessment. All four speech systems must be assessed.
glossary.source: Ch. 6 PPTX / Lecture Ch. 6, Kidwell

glossary.def:
Dysarthria can affect any or all four speech systems:
• Respiratory: low vocal intensity; speech limited to short phrases; frequent pausing to breathe
• Phonatory: breathy voice from unilateral/bilateral vocal fold paresis/paralysis
• Resonatory: hypernasality from soft palate paresis/paralysis
• Articulatory: imprecise, distorted consonants from reduced range of motion (ROM), strength, coordination, and rate of jaw/lips/tongue movement
glossary.source: Ch. 6 PPTX / Lecture Ch. 6, Kidwell

glossary.def:
Dysarthria affects speech through weakness in any or all four systems:
• Respiratory weakness: low vocal intensity; speech limited to short phrases; frequent pausing to breathe
• Phonatory weakness: unilateral or bilateral vocal fold paresis/paralysis → mild to severe breathiness; abnormal voice quality
• Resonatory weakness: unilateral or bilateral soft palate paresis → HYPERNASALITY
• Articulatory weakness: reduced range of motion, strength, coordination, and rate of mandible/lips/tongue → distorted, imprecise consonants affecting intelligibility and prosody
glossary.source: Ch. 17 PPTX — Fogle/SJDC

glossary.term: Dysarthria Therapy — Restorative vs. Compensatory Approaches
glossary.def:
Primary goal: maximize effectiveness, efficiency, and naturalness of communication.

RESTORATIVE APPROACH: focuses on improving the underlying impairment — muscle tone, range of motion of articulators, strength and rate of movement. Appropriate for mild/moderate dysarthria where some restoration is possible.

COMPENSATORY APPROACH: teaches strategies to work around the weakness — rate control, overarticulation, postural changes, energy management. More appropriate when significant improvement is not possible.

Severity guides approach: mild = aim for natural-sounding speech; moderate = aim for intelligibility and efficiency; severe = AAC may be needed.
glossary.source: Lecture Ch. 17, Kidwell / Ch. 17 PPTX

glossary.def: Primary goal: maximize communicative potential. Treatment approaches include: increase physiologic support (muscle strengthening, ROM); develop phonologic and phonetic repertoire; improve vocal loudness, pitch, and quality (LSVT LOUD); prosody training; teach interaction strategies (eye contact, facial expressions); compensatory behaviors (rate control, posture); receptive/expressive language treatment; preliteracy/literacy skills; educate family/caregivers and peers; self-confidence and self-esteem strategies; AAC if needed.
glossary.source: Ch. 6 PPTX — Fogle/SJDC

glossary.def: A developmental motor and/or literacy disorder that affects a child's or adult's ability to write, characterized by messy or illegible handwriting, misspellings, and difficulty with grammar and organizing sentences; note agraphia is a loss of ability to write resulting from injury to the brain.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: A swallowing disorder. May co-occur with dysarthria since both involve the same muscles of the oral mechanism. Children and adults with CP, dysarthria, and other neuromotor impairments frequently have dysphagia in addition to speech impairment. Dysphagia management is within the SLP's scope of practice.
glossary.source: Lecture Ch. 6, Kidwell / Ch. 6 PPTX

glossary.def: Difficulty swallowing that occurs when impairments affect any of the four phases of the normal swallow: oral preparatory, oral, pharyngeal, or esophageal. Dysphagia puts a person at risk for aspiration of food and/or liquid and potential aspiration pneumonia. Although swallowing is not part of the communication process, it is intimately connected to the speech systems (respiratory, phonatory, resonatory, and articulatory). SLPs are recognized by the medical community as the professionals most knowledgeable and directly responsible for the evaluation and treatment of dysphagia — within SLPs' scope of practice per ASHA (2016). Dysphagia is a major area of acute care hospital work for many SLPs.
glossary.source: Fogle, Ch. 18

glossary.def: Dysphagia requires a team approach with the SLP typically acting as the COORDINATOR or HEAD of the team. Team members include: Certified nursing assistants (CNAs) — feeding patients; Dentists — dental appliances and dentures; Dietitians/nutritionists — daily intake, food texture palatability, adequate nutrition; Family members — feeding and care decisions; Gastroenterologists — esophageal/digestive problems and feeding tube insertion; Neurologists — neurological impairments; Nurses — day-to-day medical needs; Occupational therapists — positioning for meals and retrieving food; Otolaryngologists — laryngeal function; Pharmacists — medication side effects on swallowing; Physical therapists — postural problems and wheelchair; Physicians — overall medical management; Pulmonologists — respiratory function and aspiration pneumonia; Radiologists — modified barium swallow studies; Respiratory therapists — respiratory treatments; Social workers — advocacy and insurance; SLPs — assess, diagnose, treat swallowing disorders and recommend safe liquid consistencies and food textures.
glossary.source: Fogle, Ch. 18

glossary.def: Three main treatment approaches for dysphagia: (1) COMPENSATORY TREATMENT TECHNIQUES: Help optimize use of current swallowing abilities; hospital staff and family may be taught these. Include POSTURAL TECHNIQUES (e.g., chin tuck, head rotation to control bolus flow and reduce aspiration risk — easiest for patients with cognitive/communication problems to learn) and FOOD PLACEMENT (placing food on stronger side, limiting bolus size to ~5cc/1 teaspoon). (2) FACILITATION TREATMENT TECHNIQUES: Designed to IMPROVE function of swallowing mechanism; include increasing vocal fold adduction, breath-hold techniques during swallow, and oral-motor exercises to strengthen articulatory muscles. (3) DIET MODIFICATION TECHNIQUES: Modifying food texture and/or liquid consistency using National Dysphagia Diet Task Force (2002) standardized levels. Long-term goal: 'Patient will achieve safe and efficient swallowing to sustain adequate nutrition and hydration orally.'
glossary.source: Fogle, Ch. 18

glossary.def: The most common diagnosis for a voice disorder. The voice sounds rough, raspy, or hoarse. Results from disruptions to normal vocal fold vibration.
glossary.source: Lecture 1.2.1 — Chapter 1, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def: Impaired prosody — abnormal rate, rhythm, stress, and intonation of speech. A key characteristic of both CAS and acquired apraxia of speech. The melodic pattern of speech is disrupted even when individual sounds can be produced. Includes inappropriate stress on syllables and words.
glossary.source: Ch. 6 PPTX / Lecture Ch. 6, Kidwell

glossary.def: A general neurological term for a variety of problems characterized by excessive contraction of muscles with associated abnormal movements and postures.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: An infant's immediate and automatic reproduction or imitation of speech heard from others. A normal prelinguistic vocalization in infancy. (Note: persistent echolalia beyond typical developmental age is associated with autism spectrum disorder.)
glossary.source: Ch. 4 PDF — Prof. Handy, SJDC

glossary.def: Accumulation of excessive fluid in tissue that is associated with inflammatory conditions and results in swelling of the tissue.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: An electronic (battery powered) device used by laryngectomees that produces a vibrated mechanical sound that is held against the neck, with the sound entering the pharynx and oral cavity where it is articulated for speech.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: A specialist in the art of clear and expressive public speech. Alexander Graham Bell's father and grandfather were elocutionists in the 1860s, contributing to the early roots of speech-language pathology and audiology.
glossary.source: Lecture 1.2.2 — Chapter 2: The Professionals, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def: Early skills developed in the preschool years that precede or are presumed prerequisites for later-developing reading and writing skills.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: A sound that a child produces inconsistently — sometimes correctly, sometimes incorrectly. Inconsistent errors on a particular sound may indicate it is emerging and that, with a little therapy, the sound may be established. Closely related to stimulability — both are positive prognostic indicators.
glossary.source: Quiz answer key — Ch. 5

glossary.term: Emotional and Social Effects — Voice Disorders by Age
glossary.def:
YOUNG CHILDREN: most are hyperfunctional (yelling, straining) but are UNAWARE and UNCONCERNED about their voice — they must be emotionally engaged before therapy can be effective. Parent counseling is primary.

ADOLESCENTS: commonly abuse voices by yelling/cheering. Usually lack motivation for therapy until voice problems become CHRONIC. Boys with mutational falsetto fear growing up. Texting may reduce vocal abuse in adolescents by providing voice rest.

ADULTS: typically have more stressors at work or home. Better coping skills → better engagement in therapy and ability to maintain regained voice post-therapy. Quality of life and holistic wellbeing are considerations.
glossary.source: Lecture Ch. 13, Kidwell / Ch. 13 PPTX

glossary.term: Emotional and Social Effects of ADHD
glossary.def: ADHD has significant psychosocial consequences. Comorbid emotional/behavioral conditions include: conduct disorder, oppositional defiant disorder, disruptive mood dysregulation disorder, anxiety, and depression. Social consequences: suspension from school, trouble with police, decreased quality of life, difficulty maintaining relationships. Incarcerated individuals have an estimated 40% prevalence of ADHD — approximately 5 times higher than the general population. Children with ADHD and/or APD often have significant difficulty regulating emotions.
glossary.source: Ch. 9 PPTX / Lecture Ch. 9, Kidwell

glossary.term: Emotional and Social Effects of APD
glossary.def: Research on the emotional/social effects of APD is limited (paucity of research). Individuals with APD may experience similar psychosocial problems as those with language disorders — including poor relationships with family and peers, few friends, frustration from frequent misunderstandings, and decreased quality of life. APD often contributes to academic underachievement which compounds emotional and social difficulties.
glossary.source: Ch. 9 PPTX — Fogle/SJDC

glossary.term: Emotional and Social Effects of Aphasia (Kübler-Ross Stages)
glossary.def: Aphasia has profound emotional and social effects on both patient and family. Patients experience loss of functions, altered appearance, and dependency on others. Social and financial effects include job loss and social isolation as friends may avoid the patient. Elisabeth Kübler-Ross (1969) described FIVE STAGES OF GRIEF relevant to acquired disability: (1) DENIAL, (2) ANGER, (3) BARGAINING, (4) DEPRESSION, (5) ACCEPTANCE. FAMILY SYSTEMS THEORY recognizes that the family goes through stages: passive role during medical procedures → helping role during rehabilitation → adjusting to permanently altered functioning of their loved one.
glossary.source: Ch. 15 PPTX; Kidwell lecture notes

glossary.term: Emotional and Social Effects of Cognitive Disorders
glossary.def: Cognitive disorders profoundly affect both patients and families: TBI — personality changes, loss of skills and emotions, increased anger and isolation; family members experience significant emotional and financial strain. RIGHT-HEMISPHERE SYNDROME — damage to self-image and self-confidence; family members affected. DEMENTIA — called 'the long goodbye' due to the prolonged nature of the progressive loss; patients experience anxiety, agitation, and depression; family members go through a prolonged grieving process. Financial toll of the diseases adds to emotional strain. SLPs must recognize and address the impact on the entire family system.
glossary.source: Ch. 16 PPTX; Kidwell lecture notes

glossary.term: Emotional and Social Effects of Dysphagia
glossary.def: Food and eating have strong emotional significance. Eating is a social function — 'breaking bread' builds and maintains relationships. When dysphagia interferes, patients face serious emotional and social consequences. QUALITY OF LIFE: always the most important concern in patient care; patients have the right to decide (AUTONOMY). Modifying food texture or increasing liquid viscosity changes flavor — patients' complaints that food doesn't taste the same when pureed are legitimate. Patients often decrease consumption of modified foods even knowing they need nutrition. ANXIETIES AND FEARS: embarrassment in front of family/friends during meals; fear of aspiration pneumonia or death; discomfort from suctioning; fear of never eating favorite foods normally again. BIOETHICS: requires SLPs to always respect patient autonomy even when recommending dietary restrictions. Cost of one hospital stay for aspiration pneumonia: approximately $27,000-$28,000.
glossary.source: Fogle, Ch. 18

glossary.term: Emotional and Social Effects of Language Disorders
glossary.def: Language disorders affect children emotionally and socially — impacts that may continue into adulthood. Short-term: difficulty with peer interactions, frustration at not being understood, poor academic performance. Long-term: adverse effects on educational adjustment, academic achievement, career choices, psychosocial functioning, increased probability of psychiatric disorders, and marital/peer relationships. A child who struggles with reading in early elementary may feel like a failure by high school — potentially dropping out, affecting future employment, relationships, and quality of life. Early identification and treatment are critical.
glossary.source: Lecture Ch. 7, Kidwell, SJDC

glossary.term: Emotional and Social Effects of Stuttering
glossary.def:
Stuttering has considerable emotional and social consequences:
• More sensitive temperaments
• ANXIETY about stuttering (primary emotional reaction)
• Low self-esteem and excessive need for social approval
• Habitual avoidance of speaking to deny the problem exists
• Career choices influenced by communication demands — may choose low-communication professions
• Avoidance of people who are the most difficult to talk to
• Self-imposed isolation and loneliness
• Long-term relationships affected if minimal social involvement persists
• PARENTS also affected: guilt, embarrassment, wondering if they caused it

★ Many successful, happy, and famous people stutter — stuttering does not preclude achievement. ('Stutterers Are in Good Company' — Fogle textbook.)
glossary.source: Lecture Ch. 12, Kidwell / Fluency_Disorders.pdf

glossary.def: An uncontrolled and often rapidly changing expression of emotions or mood swings sometimes occurring with neurological impairments such as TBIs and strokes that indicate a loss of inhibitory control, most frequently seen as crying easily for no apparent reason or inappropriate laughter.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: Methods of viewing the velopharyngeal mechanism, vocal tract, or both that use a rigid endoscope introduced intraorally or flexible fiberoptic endoscope transnasally with a camera lens and fiber-optic light source at the tip of the scope.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.term: English as a Second Language (ESL) / English Language Learner (ELL)
glossary.def: Learning English after a child's native (home) language has been established. A child's lack of English proficiency is not a disability — to be classified as a language disorder, the problem must occur in the child's primary (most proficient) language.
glossary.source: Ch. 4 PDF — Prof. Handy, SJDC

glossary.def: A leaf-shaped cartilage at the top of the larynx. During swallowing, it folds down over the laryngeal opening to prevent food and liquid from entering the airway (trachea).
glossary.source: Lecture 2.2 — Ch. 3, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def:
Two categories of secondary behavioral responses to stuttering:

AVOIDANCE BEHAVIORS: used BEFORE a stuttering moment to prevent it from happening — substituting words, circumlocution, delaying speaking, refusing to enter speaking situations.

ESCAPE BEHAVIORS: used DURING a stuttering moment to break out of it — head jerking, eye blinking, leg tapping, throat clearing, and other physical struggle behaviors that help the person force through the block or stutter.

Both types are triggered by FEAR of sounds, words, people, or speaking situations. Both are secondary behaviors — not part of the core stutter — and both can become learned, habitual responses that reinforce avoidance and worsen long-term outcomes.
glossary.source: 306_BehaviorsOfStuttering.pdf — Hodgson, CCC-SLP

glossary.def: The compression of air within the oropharynx and injection of it into the esophagus, followed by the rapid expelling of the air out of the esophagus that causes it to vibrate the upper esophageal valve, which produces a low-pitched, monotone 'voice' that is shaped by the articulators to produce speech.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: The cause of an occurrence (e.g., a medical problem that results in a disorder or disability).
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: A tube lined with mucous membrane that joins the nasopharynx and the middle ear cavity that is normally closed but opens during yawning, chewing, and swallowing to allow equalization of air pressure in the middle ear with atmospheric pressure.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: The foundation and guiding principle of assessment and treatment by speech-language pathologists. Requires that clinical decisions integrate the best available research evidence, clinical expertise, and client/family values and preferences. SLPs must be scientists, humanists, and artists — applying scientific knowledge with individualized, client-centered approaches.
glossary.source: Quiz answer key — Ch. 5

glossary.def: The quality of treatment that is measurable in terms of some form of functional outcomes and maintained in the face of cost-containment efforts. Deemphasizes clinician intuition and unsystematic approaches; requires decisions through a scientific process rooted in examination of clinical research. TREATMENT EFFICACY: the extent to which an intervention can be shown to be beneficial under OPTIMAL (or ideal) conditions. TREATMENT EFFECTIVENESS: the extent to which services are shown to be beneficial under TYPICAL (or real-world) conditions. BEST PRACTICES: procedures based on the best available evidence (research and expert opinion); accepted or prescribed as being correct or most effective. Third-party payers (Medicare, HMOs) want functional outcomes — can the patient be discharged, go home, recover, and return to work — for the least cost in the least time.
glossary.source: Fogle, Ch. 18

glossary.def: A disruption of executive functions that regulate, control, and manage cognitive processes, behaviors, and emotions.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: Disruption of executive functions that regulate, control, and manage cognitive processes, behaviors, and emotions. Many ADHD symptoms are problems with executive function. Executive function is controlled by the FRONTAL LOBE of the brain. People are generally born with this weakness in brain function. Executive function helps with: managing time, paying attention, switching focus, planning and organizing, remembering details, avoiding saying/doing the wrong thing, acting on experience, and multitasking. Can be divided into: ORGANIZATION (gathering and structuring information) and REGULATION (adjusting behavior in response to surroundings).
glossary.source: Lecture Ch. 9, Kidwell, SJDC

glossary.def: A composite of cognitive activities related to goal completion, governed by the frontal lobes. Includes: anticipation, goal selection, planning, initiation of activity, self-regulation/self-monitoring, and use of feedback to adjust future responses.
glossary.source: CSD Ch3 PP (Fogle 3rd ed.)

glossary.def: The process of breathing air out of the lungs. During quiet expiration, the alveolar sacs and thoracic cavity relax, intrathoracic air pressure increases, and air is forced out through the trachea and nose or mouth. Active (forced) expiration for speech requires abdominal muscle contraction.
glossary.source: CSD Ch3 PP (Fogle 3rd ed.)

glossary.def: Difficulty formulating verbal, written, and/or gestural language.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: The ability to produce spoken or written language — how a person verbally communicates their thoughts, wants, and needs. Expressive language delays affect how well a person produces language to communicate.
glossary.source: Lecture 1.2.1 — Chapter 1, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def: The language we produce verbally. Problems in expressive language arise from difficulties in five areas: (1) Articulation/Phonology — reduced intelligibility affects interaction; (2) Morphology/Syntax — grammatically incomplete/incorrect sentences; (3) Vocabulary/Semantics — limited and slow-developing vocabulary, poor concept development, word-finding problems; (4) Metalinguistics — difficulty using language as a tool; (5) Pragmatics — difficulty using language appropriately in social situations.
glossary.source: Lecture Ch. 7, Kidwell / Ch. 7 slides

glossary.def: Acronym for rapid stroke recognition: FACE — ask the person to smile; drooping on one side indicates stroke. ARMS — ask the person to raise both arms; if one arm drifts downward or cannot be raised, stroke may be present. SPEECH — ask the person to repeat a simple sentence; listen for slurred words or trouble speaking/understanding. TIME — if any symptoms are present, call 911 immediately. Time is of the essence. Additional general warning signs: sudden numbness/weakness especially on one side; sudden confusion; sudden visual difficulty; sudden trouble walking or loss of balance; sudden severe headache.
glossary.source: Ch. 15 PPTX; Kidwell lecture notes

glossary.def: The abnormal retardation of growth and development of an infant resulting from conditions that interfere with normal metabolism, appetite, and activity. A postnatal condition associated with neglect, malnutrition, or medical problems that can impact speech, language, and cognitive development.
glossary.source: Quiz answer key — Ch. 5

glossary.def: A second pair of folds above the true vocal folds. They do NOT vibrate during speech. Their function is to close during swallowing to help prevent material from entering the trachea. Also called ventricular folds.
glossary.source: Lecture 2.2 — Ch. 3, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def: Paired, thick folds of mucous membranes with few muscle fibers that lie just above the true vocal folds in the larynx. They do not vibrate during normal voicing but close tightly during swallowing to prevent material from entering the trachea.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: Models of counseling and service delivery that focus on variables regarding the family unit as a whole, where each family member is part of a system, each member affects the others, and the system is interdependent.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: The result of a mother's use of alcohol during pregnancy that may cause a fetus to develop abnormally slowly, have facial abnormalities, and have problems with the central nervous system, including intellectual disability.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: A procedure used to provide visual information about the pharyngeal phase of swallowing using a flexible endoscope passed through the nasal passageway into the nasopharynx.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: An instrumental procedure providing information about the PHARYNGEAL PHASE of swallowing. PROCEDURE: A flexible endoscope with camera and light source is passed through the nasal passage to the hypopharynx (top-down view). Various solid/liquid consistencies are dyed with food coloring for contrast (against saliva/secretions). The SLP observes whether material passes through the UEV, remains in the pharynx, penetrates into the larynx, or aspirates into the airway — with or without attempts to clear the throat or cough. SLPs can view anatomical structures in color, movement of the vocal folds/larynx, and assess for penetration/aspiration events. Unlike MBSS, FEES focuses specifically on the pharyngeal phase and does not involve radiation.
glossary.source: Fogle, Ch. 18

glossary.def: Non-literal language forms including idioms ('raining cats and dogs'), metaphors, similes, and verbal humor. Peak period for learning figurative language is 11–12 years (adolescence). Difficulty with figurative language is clinically significant and affects social and academic success.
glossary.source: Ch. 4 PDF — Prof. Handy, SJDC

glossary.def: Movements that require a high degree of dexterity, control, and precision of the small muscles of the body that enable such functions as grasping small objects, drawing shapes, cutting with scissors, fastening clothing, and writing.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: The continuity, smoothness, rate, and effort in speech production. Fluent speech flows forward without disruption. Fluency disorders are among the most recognized and most researched areas in speech-language pathology — stuttering is commonly the first thing people think of when they think of a speech disorder.
glossary.source: Fluency_Disorders.pdf / Lecture Ch. 12, Kidwell

glossary.def:
Assessment of stuttering in children includes:
• Parent interview (foremost — requires interviewing and counseling skills)
• General speech and language evaluation
• Formal and informal observations in multiple settings (therapy room, classroom, recess, small/large group — variety is essential)
• Analysis of: types of disfluencies, frequency of disfluencies, conditions that increase/decrease stuttering (e.g., emotional topics), physical behaviors, child's covert reactions
• STUTTERING SEVERITY INSTRUMENT-4 (SSI-4): most common formal tool; measures types of disfluencies, rate of disfluencies, physical behaviors, and naturalness of speech
• Diagnosis determines: whether stuttering is normal or abnormal, severity, disfluency rate, types and patterns
glossary.source: Lecture Ch. 12, Kidwell, SJDC

glossary.def:
A structured behavioral approach to stuttering therapy that trains a client to produce fluent speech using specific physiologic techniques:
• Relaxed RESPIRATION during speech
• Relaxed VOCAL FOLDS
• Relaxed ARTICULATORY muscles
• Slow/easy speech and smooth exhalation exercises
• Gradual increase toward natural rate
• Cancellations: stopping after a stuttered word, pausing, and saying it fluently
• Pull-outs: changing from a stutter to a smooth production mid-word
Goal: build entirely new speech pattern. THERAPY EMPHASIS: increasing awareness of muscular tension through relaxation; analyzing and modifying speech; building new speech skills.
glossary.source: Lecture Ch. 12, Kidwell / Fluency_Disorders.pdf

glossary.def:
Two dominant approaches to stuttering therapy:
1. FLUENCY SHAPING (FS): teaches the person to speak with relaxed respiration, vocal folds, and articulatory muscles. Highly structured with behavioral modification. Uses a series of exercises (slow/easy speech, smooth exhalation). Teaches the client to recognize and confront fears, avoidance, and struggle behaviors. Techniques include cancellations and pull-outs. Goal: build fluent speech from the ground up.
2. STUTTERING MODIFICATION (SM): focuses more on COMMUNICATION IMPROVEMENT than on fluent speech. Targets negative forces (fear, avoidance, escape behaviors) for reduction, which creates spontaneous fluency. Develops insight into attitudes, thoughts, and feelings about stuttering.

Both approaches may be used together.
glossary.source: Lecture Ch. 12, Kidwell / Fluency_Disorders.pdf

glossary.def: Aphasia that results from damage to posterior regions of the cortex (i.e., temporal or temporal-parietal lesions) and is characterized by language with normal pauses and relatively normal articulation, but with frequent syntactic errors, paraphasias, and circumlocutions.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: Two broad classification systems for aphasia based on lesion site and speech output characteristics. FLUENT aphasia: lesions posterior to the Fissure of Rolando (central sulcus), often near left temporal lobe; speaking rate, words per phrase, articulation, and intonation may be normal but auditory comprehension is impaired. Includes: Wernicke's, anomic, conduction, and transcortical sensory aphasia. NONFLUENT aphasia: lesions in or around Broca's area in the left frontal lobe; good auditory and reading comprehension but difficulty initiating speech, maintaining rate (often only 50 words/minute), and omission of functor words (articles: a, an, the). Includes: Broca's, transcortical motor, and global aphasia.
glossary.source: Ch. 15 PPTX; Kidwell lecture notes

glossary.term: Form, Content, and Use (Three Components of Language)
glossary.def:
Language can be described by three dimensions:
• FORM: the structure and rules of the language system — includes phonology (sound system), morphology (word structure rules), and syntax (sentence structure rules)
• CONTENT: the meaning conveyed by language — semantics (vocabulary, concepts, word relationships)
• USE/FUNCTION: how language is used in social communication — pragmatics (turn-taking, topic maintenance, eye contact, implied meaning)
A language disorder may involve any one or any combination of these three components.
glossary.source: Lecture Ch. 7, Kidwell / Ch. 7 slides

glossary.term: Four Neuromuscular Disorders Commonly Associated with Dysarthria
glossary.def:
The four neuromuscular disorders most commonly associated with dysarthria in adults (per Fogle/ASHA):
1. Parkinson's Disease (PD) — hypokinetic dysarthria; reduced loudness, monopitch, rapid rate
2. ALS (Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis / Lou Gehrig's disease) — mixed (flaccid + spastic) dysarthria; progressive to anarthria
3. Multiple Sclerosis (MS) — mixed or ataxic dysarthria; variable with relapse/remission
4. Myasthenia Gravis — fatigable dysarthria; worsens with use
glossary.source: Ch. 17 PPTX / Lecture Ch. 17, Kidwell

glossary.def:
The four primary error types in articulation disorders, remembered by the acronym SODA:
• Substitution: one sound replaces another (e.g., 'tat' for 'cat' — /t/ for /k/)
• Omission: a sound is left out entirely (e.g., 'at' for 'bat')
• Distortion: a sound is produced inaccurately but no standard sound replaces it (e.g., interdental lisp on /s/ — almost 'th' but not exactly)
• Addition: an extra sound is inserted (e.g., 'animamal' for 'animal', 'suh-poon' for 'spoon')
Errors can occur in initial, medial, or final position of words.
glossary.source: Lecture 4.2, Kidwell / Ch. 5 slides

glossary.def:
CP is classified by muscle tone and limb involvement:
1. Spastic CP (spasticity): most common type — hypertonicity; stiff, jerky movements
2. Athetoid CP (athetosis): involuntary, writhing movements from fluctuating muscle tone
3. Ataxic CP (ataxia): problems with balance, coordination, and depth perception; unsteady gait
4. Mixed CP: combination of two or more types; most commonly spastic + athetoid
glossary.source: Ch. 6 PPTX / Lecture Ch. 6, Kidwell

glossary.def: A right established under Public Law 94-142 (1975) guaranteeing that all school-aged children with disabilities receive educational services at no cost in an appropriate setting. Remains a significant legal standard today.
glossary.source: Lecture 1.2.2 — Chapter 2: The Professionals, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def: A fold of mucous tissue connecting the floor of the mouth to the midline underside of the tongue. A short or tight frenum (tongue-tie / ankyloglossia) can restrict tongue mobility and potentially affect articulation — though clinical significance varies. An anatomic structure assessed during oral motor evaluation.
glossary.source: Quiz answer key — Ch. 5

glossary.def: A fold of mucous tissue connecting the floor of the mouth to the midline underside of the tongue.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: The number of complete cycles of vocal fold opening and closing per second. Measured in Hertz (Hz). The fundamental frequency is the basic rate of vocal fold vibration. An objective, physical measurement.
glossary.source: Lecture 2.2 — Ch. 3, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def: Essential for cognition, attention, reasoning, judgment, decision-making, and problem-solving. Anterior portion governs personality, character, beliefs, goal-setting, and self-regulation. Posterior portion contains Broca's area for motor speech control.
glossary.source: Lecture 2.2 — Ch. 3, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def: A voice disorder in which a person speaks mostly in a WHISPER despite being physically capable of producing a normal voice during other vocal productions (laughing, coughing, crying). There is nothing physically wrong with the vocal folds. It is primarily an EMOTIONAL problem — typically stemming from a traumatic event or psychological distress causing the person to avoid normal voicing. Also called a CONVERSION REACTION/DISORDER — the psychological conflict is converted into a physical symptom.
glossary.source: Lecture Ch. 13, Kidwell / Ch. 13 PPTX

glossary.def: Forms of behavior that express wants, needs, feelings, thoughts, knowledge, and preferences that others can understand.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: A communication disorder with no identifiable anatomic, physiologic, or neurologic cause. The child has difficulty producing speech sounds, but examination reveals no structural or neurological reason. Contrasted with organic disorder. The cause is unknown. Also called 'idiopathic.'
glossary.source: Ch. 5 slides — Fogle/SJDC / Lecture 4.2, Kidwell

glossary.def: A voice disorder with no organic, physical, or neurological cause — results from EXTREME TENSION in the laryngeal region (muscle tension dysphonia). One of the most common adult voice disorders (20.5% of adults aged 19–60). Voice sounds strained, tense, or pressed. Responds well to voice therapy targeting tension reduction, relaxation, and vocal technique modification.
glossary.source: Lecture Ch. 13, Kidwell / Ch. 13 PPTX

glossary.def: The results of treatment intended to clearly show a person's improved ability to communicate in the hospital setting, at home, in the community, and on the job.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: Words that give order and grammatical structure to sentences rather than carrying primary meaning themselves. Include articles (a, the), prepositions (in, on), conjunctions (and, but), and auxiliaries (is, have). Emerge around 3–5 years. Absent in telegraphic speech.
glossary.source: Ch. 4 PDF — Prof. Handy, SJDC

glossary.def: The baseline rate of vocal fold vibration during normal voicing, as in producing 'ah.' Measured in Hertz (Hz). Average values: adult males ~120 Hz; adult females ~220 Hz. The physical basis of perceived pitch.
glossary.source: CSD Ch3 PP (Fogle 3rd ed.)

glossary.def: American English that is not associated with any regional dialect — the variety heard on radio and television. Derived from British English. Composed of 26 letters representing 45 speech sounds (phonemes). The reference standard used when evaluating articulation and phonological disorders.
glossary.source: Ch. 5 slides — Fogle/SJDC / Lecture 4.2, Kidwell

glossary.def: The end goal of articulation/phonological therapy — the transfer of a correctly learned sound from the therapy room to all words, sentences, and conversations in all settings (home, playground, classroom, etc.). Therapy progresses from isolation → syllable → word → phrase → sentence → spontaneous conversation, always working toward generalization.
glossary.source: Lecture 4.2, Kidwell, SJDC

glossary.def: The most severe form of aphasia — a combination of fluent and nonfluent features with severe impairments of BOTH receptive and expressive abilities. Large lesion affecting multiple language areas. All modalities are severely impaired: auditory comprehension, verbal expression, reading, and writing. Repetition is poor. The patient may retain some ability for self-expression through facial expressions, intonation, and gestures. Apraxia or dysarthria may also be present. Prognosis is often poor due to the severe extent of the damage. Fluency: −, Comprehension: −, Repetition: −, Naming: −.
glossary.source: Ch. 15 PPTX; Kidwell lecture notes

glossary.def: The space between the open (abducted) vocal folds. When the glottis is open, air passes freely for breathing. When the vocal folds adduct and close, air pressure builds below the glottis to initiate phonation.
glossary.source: Lecture 2.2 — Ch. 3, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.term: Gradual Hierarchy Approach — Stuttering Treatment in Children
glossary.def:
A treatment technique for children who stutter that builds fluent speech through a slow, structured hierarchy:
1. Child says SINGLE WORDS in a slow, relaxed way
2. Words are gradually combined into PHRASES and then SENTENCES
Example progression: 'ball' → 'red ball' → 'a big red ball' → 'I have a big red ball.'
This process can take anywhere from a few weeks to six months or more. Goal is for the child to produce fluent speech while learning to SELF-MONITOR. A secondary goal is decreasing secondary characteristics (twitching, blinking, clamped jaw) alongside fluency improvement.
glossary.source: 65_Cause_Treatment_of_Stuttering.pdf — Ducworth, CCC-SLP

glossary.def: The rules of the use of morphology and syntax in a language.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: A hallmark characteristic of CAS in which the child visibly struggles and makes trial-and-error movements (silent posturing) while attempting to find the correct articulatory placement for a sound. The child knows what they want to say but cannot successfully execute the motor plan — the articulators appear to 'search' for the correct position.
glossary.source: Ch. 6 PPTX — Fogle/SJDC

glossary.def: Involvement of the large muscles of the body (e.g., trunk, legs, arms) that require tone, strength, and coordination that enable such functions as standing, lifting, walking, and throwing a ball.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: Habilitation refers to developing communication skills in individuals who never had them (e.g., children born deaf). Rehabilitation refers to restoring communication skills lost due to injury or disease. Audiologists assess potential for both.
glossary.source: Lecture 1.2.2 — Chapter 2: The Professionals, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def: Forceful approximation (closing) of the vocal folds during the initiation of phonation.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: The bony anterior 2/3 of the roof of the mouth. Contained within the maxilla (upper jaw) and surrounded by the alveolar ridge. Forms and fuses during the third month of gestation. Critical structure for resonance and articulation.
glossary.source: Lecture 2.2 — Ch. 3, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def: A 'rough' sounding vocal quality resulting from a combination of hard glottal attacks, low pitch, and high intensity caused by overadduction (hyperfunction) of the vocal folds.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: Any battery powered electronic device designed to amplify and deliver sound to the ear; consists of a microphone, amplifier, and receiver.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: In audiometry, the level at which a stimulus sound, such as a pure tone, is barely perceptible; usual clinical criteria demand that the level be just high enough for the subject to be aware of the sound at least 50% of the time it is presented.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: A common dysphonia that is a combination of breathiness and harshness that may affect loudness (usually decreased), pitch (usually a low pitch with reduced pitch range), and quality (usually decreased 'pleasantness' of the sound of the voice).
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: Use of a single word to express a complete thought. Characteristic of 12–18 month toddlers. Example: 'juice?' said with rising intonation to mean 'Can I have juice?' Early word types include: common nouns (ball, milk, mommy), action words (go, bye-bye), modifiers (hot, pretty), personal-social words (no, please).
glossary.source: Ch. 4 PDF — Prof. Handy / Quiz answer key

glossary.term: Home Strategies for Parents of Children Who Stutter
glossary.def:
Key home strategies (Ducworth, Handy Handout #65):
• Use a SMOOTH, RELAXED RATE when speaking to the child — the most important thing a parent can do
• Speak in SIMPLE, SHORT SENTENCES; pause slightly before responding
• LISTEN without interrupting or finishing sentences
• Make sure the child knows they are being UNDERSTOOD
• SLOW the pace of the household; minimize excitement levels
• When difficulty occurs, it is OK to calmly acknowledge it: 'You had a little trouble getting that out'
• Inform ALL people who interact with the child (family members, teachers) to use smooth, relaxed speech
• Behavioral treatment approaches help children develop more POSITIVE ATTITUDES toward communication

Resources: Stuttering Foundation (stutteringhelp.org); ASHA board-certified fluency specialists (stutteringspecialists.org)
glossary.source: 65_Cause_Treatment_of_Stuttering.pdf — Ducworth, CCC-SLP

glossary.def: A U-shaped bone in the neck that supports the tongue and larynx. The only bone in the body that does not articulate directly with any other bone. Acts as an anchor for tongue muscles and plays a role in swallowing and speech.
glossary.source: CSD Ch3 PP (Fogle 3rd ed.)

glossary.def: Difficulty with the vocal folds closing too tightly or for too long causing the voice to sound tense and strained, and tending to fatigue with use as a result of hypertonicity.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: A pervasive pattern of excessive effort and tension that affects many different structures and muscles in the phonatory system; signs of hyperfunction include a tense-sounding voice and hard glottal attacks.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: A resonance disorder in which too much air flows through the nasal cavity during speech, making the voice sound as if the speaker is 'talking through their nose.' Caused by abnormal palate structure or function.
glossary.source: Lecture 1.2.1 — Chapter 1, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def: Excessive tone or tension of a muscle or group of muscles.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: Difficulty making the vocal folds close strongly enough or long enough for normal phonation that results in a weak, breathy voice that often deteriorates with increasing amounts of vocal use throughout the day.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: Inadequate muscle tone in the laryngeal mechanism and associated structures, including the muscles of respiration; signs of hypofunction include breathiness because of inadequate closure of the vocal folds, weak vocal power, and reduced vocal endurance.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: A resonance disorder in which too little air flows through the nasal cavity during speech, causing nasal sounds to lose their normal resonance. The voice may sound blocked or congested.
glossary.source: Lecture 1.2.1 — Chapter 1, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def: Absence of normal tone or tension of a muscle or group of muscles.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: Hypotonicity: abnormally low muscle tone — muscles are floppy/weak. Hypertonicity: abnormally high muscle tone — muscles are rigid/stiff. Both affect speech production in dysarthria. The type present depends on the site and extent of neurological damage. CP can present with either type.
glossary.source: Ch. 6 PPTX — Fogle/SJDC

glossary.def: Inadequate oxygenation of the blood leading to tachycardia (rapid heart rate) and rapid, shallow breathing that quickly affects the brain and other organs and systems. Less severe than anoxia (complete oxygen absence) but still causes neurological damage. A perinatal risk factor for communication disorders.
glossary.source: Quiz answer key — Ch. 5

glossary.def: Any disorder, disease, or condition that occurs spontaneously or for which the cause is unknown.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: CAS that occurs spontaneously with no apparent or identifiable cause. One of the three recognized etiological categories of CAS (neurological, secondary to a neurobehavioral disorder, or idiopathic). Even when no cause can be found, treatment is still indicated.
glossary.source: Ch. 6 PPTX / Lecture Ch. 6, Kidwell

glossary.def: Any loss or abnormality of psychological, physiological, or anatomical structure or function.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: The rate at which a disorder appears in a population over a period of time. Distinct from prevalence.
glossary.source: Lecture 1.2.1 — Chapter 1, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def: The hallmark characteristic of Apraxia of Speech. Errors are UNPREDICTABLE and VARIABLE — the same word may be produced differently each attempt. Patients may effortlessly produce a complex multisyllabic word on one attempt but struggle with a simple single-syllable word on the next. This inconsistency distinguishes AOS from dysarthria, where errors tend to be consistent and related to specific weakened muscle groups.
glossary.source: Motor_Speech_Disorders.pdf / Lecture Ch. 17, Kidwell

glossary.def: A localized area of necrotic (dead) tissue resulting from lack of blood supply and oxygen to the tissue.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: At rest the ratio is approximately 50:50 (or 40:60). During speech and singing, it shifts dramatically to approximately 10% inhalation and 90% exhalation — a quick short breath in, then a long sustained breath out to support speech.
glossary.source: Lecture 2.2 — Ch. 3, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def: The internal monologue humans use to think, plan, and make decisions privately before expressing thoughts verbally. A uniquely human form of self-directed communication.
glossary.source: Lecture 1.2.1 — Chapter 1, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def: The nearly constant internal monologue a person has with himself at a conscious or semiconscious level that involves thinking in words; a conversation with oneself.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: The process of drawing air into the lungs. During inspiration, the diaphragm contracts and moves downward, the rib cage rises and expands, thoracic volume increases, air pressure decreases, and air flows in through the nose/mouth down the trachea into the alveolar sacs.
glossary.source: CSD Ch3 PP (Fogle 3rd ed.)

glossary.def: A disability characterized by significant limitations in both intellectual functioning (intelligence) and adaptive behavior, and originates before 18 years of age.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: The degree to which speech can be understood by a listener. Speech-language pathologists work with clients to improve intelligibility across all four processes of speech production.
glossary.source: Eugene Speech Language Therapy — How We Speak (YouTube, 11xf89bXaCg)

glossary.def: How well a speaker is understood by a listener. A primary goal of speech therapy is to improve a client's intelligibility through work with the articulators and speech mechanism.
glossary.source: Lecture 2.2 — Ch. 3, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def: How well the average unfamiliar listener (stranger) can understand what a person is saying. Poor intelligibility is a hallmark of articulation and phonological disorders. Intelligibility is assessed as part of every articulation/phonological evaluation through a spontaneous speech sample.
glossary.source: Lecture 4.2, Kidwell, SJDC

glossary.def: The percentage of a child's speech understood by listeners. Developmental benchmarks: 2 years = 75% intelligible; 3 years = 90% intelligible; 4 years = 95% intelligible; 6–8 years = 95–100% intelligible; 9–12 years = speech fully mastered.
glossary.source: Lecture 3.2 — Ch. 4, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def: The physical force with which the vocal folds open and close and the amount of air escaping between them. Measured in decibels (dB). The objective physical measurement underlying the perception of loudness.
glossary.source: Lecture 2.2 — Ch. 3, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def: Muscles located between the ribs (internal and external). Important for both inhalation and exhalation during breathing and speech. Part of the 26 pairs of muscles involved in respiration beyond the diaphragm.
glossary.source: Lecture 2.2 — Ch. 3, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def: A type of distortion error on /s/ (and sometimes /z/) in which the tongue protrudes between the teeth during production, resulting in a sound that resembles 'th' but is neither a correct /s/ nor a correct /th/. Classified as a distortion because there is no exact standard symbol for the altered sound.
glossary.source: Lecture 4.2, Kidwell, SJDC

glossary.def: A group of professionals from various disciplines who work together to coordinate the care of a patient through collaboration, interaction, communication, and cooperation.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: A specially devised set of signs and symbols designed to represent the individual speech sounds of all languages. SLPs use the IPA to write speech sound transcriptions — because standard spelling does not adequately capture how sounds are actually produced. Example: 'cat' is written /kæt/ in IPA, not with the letter 'c'.
glossary.source: Ch. 5 slides — Fogle/SJDC / Lecture 4.2, Kidwell

glossary.def: Variations in pitch on syllables, words, and phrases that produce stress and give emphasis and meaning to utterances. A feature of prosody — changing intonation changes the meaning or emotional intent of a spoken message.
glossary.source: CSD Ch3 PP (Fogle 3rd ed.)

glossary.def: Brief periods of fluent, error-free speech that occur amid otherwise effortful, struggling communication in Apraxia of Speech. Stereotypic phrases (well-rehearsed expressions) may be produced correctly even in severe AOS. For example, a person with severe/profound AOS may be able to say 'hello' or 'I love you' perfectly but cannot produce volitional speech on command.
glossary.source: Ch. 17 PPTX / Lecture Ch. 17, Kidwell

glossary.def: Strings of syllables produced with adult-like stress and intonation that mimic real speech, but contain no true words. The infant sounds like they are speaking a real language — prosody and suprasegmentals are present — but no actual words are used. Occurs 6–12 months.
glossary.source: Ch. 4 PDF — Prof. Handy, SJDC

glossary.def: Continuous but unintelligible speech with various combinations of literal and verbal paraphasias and neologisms, with little or no conveying of information, often with the speaker having little awareness of the problem.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: When a caregiver and infant simultaneously attend to the same object, person, or event. The foundation of early communication and turn-taking. Infants learn to take turns in conversation because they observe when the other person stops and understand it is their turn to 'speak.'
glossary.source: Lecture 3.2 — Ch. 4, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.term: Kidwell's Analogy: APD and Second Language Processing
glossary.def: Kidwell explains APD using a second-language analogy: 'I cannot listen to Spanish radio while talking to someone and then report what was just said. I require careful listening, context cues, a slower rate of speech, and a quiet background to understand Spanish.' A person with APD experiences the same difficulty — but in their PRIMARY language. The ear functions correctly; the problem is that the brain cannot make sense of the incoming signals in difficult or competing listening conditions.
glossary.source: Lecture Ch. 9, Kidwell, SJDC

glossary.def: A socially shared code or conventional system for representing concepts through the use of arbitrary symbols (sounds, letters, gestures) and rule-governed combinations of those symbols (Owens, 2015; Fogle textbook). If you know the code, you understand the message.
glossary.source: Lecture 1.2.1 — Chapter 1, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def:
The work or activity that leads to a clinical decision (the diagnosis). Assessment must be based on valid and reliable measurements. Steps:
1. REFERRAL and SCREENING — parent, teacher, or caregiver refers; SLP screens to determine if full assessment is warranted
2. FORMAL assessment — standardized tests, normative comparison; required for school eligibility (CA standard: 1.5 SD or more below norm on 2+ tests in 1 area)
3. INFORMAL assessment — language samples, observation, dynamic assessment; used especially for culturally/linguistically diverse children
4. DIAGNOSIS and ELIGIBILITY determination
5. Treatment plan and PROGNOSIS
★ Standardized tests are biased — assess in PRIMARY language. If one language is WNL, child is not language disordered.
glossary.source: Lecture Ch. 7, Kidwell, SJDC

glossary.def: An active process in which, from instant to instant, a listener infers the meaning of an auditory message based on the context of the information and long-term stored memory of words and general knowledge.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: An abnormal slowness in developing language skills that may result in incomplete language development.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: The progressive growth of a receptive and expressive communication system for representing concepts using arbitrary symbols (sounds and words) and rule-governed combinations of those symbols (grammar). Involves both receptive (understanding) and expressive (speaking) language.
glossary.source: Lecture 3.2 — Ch. 4, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def: Expressive language that is influenced by cultural and linguistic diversity is NOT a disorder — it is a DIFFERENCE. American English includes many social dialects; no dialect is superior to another. When determining whether a child is language disordered: (1) assess in the PRIMARY language; (2) consider General American English norms AND the child's cultural norms; (3) if even ONE language is within normal limits, the child is NOT considered language disordered. Learning a second language is NOT a disability.
glossary.source: Lecture Ch. 7, Kidwell / Ch. 7 slides

glossary.def: The impaired comprehension and/or use of spoken, written (graphic), and/or other symbol systems (ASHA). The disorder may involve any combination of: (1) the FORM of language — phonology, morphology, or syntax; (2) the CONTENT of language — semantics; and/or (3) the FUNCTION of language in communication — pragmatics/social communication. SLPs treat receptive and expressive language disorders involving form, content, and function in both spoken and written modalities.
glossary.source: Ch. 7 slides — Fogle/SJDC

glossary.def:
These terms should NOT be used interchangeably:
• Language DELAY: implies a slow start — the child is expected to eventually catch up to peers. Parents describe these children as 'slow talkers' or 'late bloomers.' Some children with mild delays do develop normal language. However, most children are more likely to have residual impairments throughout childhood.
• Language DISORDER: the child typically will NOT catch up with peers; language gaps tend to WIDEN over time. Requires SLP evaluation and diagnosis.
The terms are often used incorrectly in everyday conversation — precision matters clinically.
glossary.source: Lecture Ch. 7, Kidwell / Ch. 7 slides

glossary.def: An impairment of receptive and/or expressive linguistic symbols that affects learning and educational achievement. Closely related to SLI — many SLPs consider them the same condition. However, per Fogle: a Learning Disability cannot formally be identified until the child has had the opportunity to learn (read, write, complete oral tasks in school) and is struggling — i.e., school-age diagnosis. Language-learning disabilities run in families. Longitudinal studies show 50%+ of children with SLI pre-K or K develop reading disabilities; 20% more become poor readers.
glossary.source: Lecture Ch. 7, Kidwell / Ch. 7 slides
