glossary.def: An informal assessment tool in which a spontaneous language sample is collected — ideally 50+ utterances — and analyzed for: receptive and expressive content, language form, language use, sequencing of thoughts/stories, and rate of speaking. Open-ended questions/statements elicit more language: 'Tell me about your dog' yields more than 'Do you have a dog?' The sample must be recorded and exactly transcribed for accurate analysis. SLPs analyze MLU, error types, vocabulary, sentence complexity, and pragmatic behaviors from the sample.
glossary.source: Lecture Ch. 7, Kidwell, SJDC

glossary.def:
Evidence-based therapy for language disorders must be: effective, functional, and attainable. Key principles:
• Goals selected based on the child's specific needs
• Normative language data used as a developmental guide
• Progress from SIMPLE to COMPLEX language goals
• Long-term goals: desired end result; short-term goals: incremental steps
• Well-organized and tightly structured sessions, especially for young children
• Flexibility within structure — clinician must adapt in the moment
• Tangible reinforcers may be used (stickers, stamps, bubbles)
• Goals should be functionally meaningful — what will most help the child in real life
glossary.source: Lecture Ch. 7, Kidwell, SJDC

glossary.def: An impairment of receptive and/or expressive linguistic symbols that affects learning and educational achievement and, consequentially, possible occupational and professional choices and success, in addition to emotional and social development.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: A life-threatening organic disease of the larynx requiring medical and surgical management. Approximately 2% of voice disorders; mostly affects males. SMOKING is a major cause of larynx, lung, pharynx, and oral structure cancers. ★ Any person with a voice disorder must be seen by an OTOLARYNGOLOGIST (ENT) to RULE OUT MALIGNANCY before SLP treatment begins. Presbyphonia (age-related voice change) and polypoid degeneration are the most common voice concerns in adults 60+.
glossary.source: Lecture Ch. 13, Kidwell / Ch. 13 PPTX

glossary.def: The anterior (front) point of the thyroid cartilage that is visible and palpable in the neck. More prominent in males due to the larger larynx. Commonly referred to as the 'Adam's apple.'
glossary.source: CSD Ch3 PP (Fogle 3rd ed.)

glossary.def: Surgical removal of the larynx because of cancer; includes the trachea being brought forward and sutured to the skin in the lower midline of the neck to create a permanent stoma.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def:
LARYNGECTOMY: partial or total surgical removal of the larynx — typically for laryngeal cancer. In a TOTAL LARYNGECTOMY: trachea is brought forward and sutured to the neck skin creating a PERMANENT STOMA; pharynx is closed as a separate swallowing tract. The patient can no longer produce vocal sounds.

Three alaryngeal speech options (speech WITHOUT a larynx):
1. TRACHEOESOPHAGEAL PROSTHESIS (TEP): directs exhaled air from the trachea into the esophagus and up through the oral cavity; most natural-sounding option
2. ESOPHAGEAL SPEECH: air in the oropharynx is pushed into the esophagus; expelled air vibrates the upper esophageal valve producing 'burp-like' speech; no device required
3. ELECTROLARYNX (artificial larynx): electronic device placed against the neck; transmits sound through the neck to the pharyngeal and oral cavity; all sounds are voiced
glossary.source: Lecture Ch. 13, Kidwell / Ch. 13 PPTX

glossary.def:
Inflammation of the larynx causing hoarseness. Two types:
• ACUTE (TRAUMATIC) LARYNGITIS: sudden onset — from a cold, allergies, or voice strain at concerts/sporting events. Alcohol worsens the problem by causing dehydration which dries out the vocal folds.
• CHRONIC LARYNGITIS: persistent inflammation from ongoing vocal abuse, smoking, or other repeated causes.
Laryngitis is among the most frequent voice disorder diagnoses. A functional etiology — caused by faulty voice use or abuse.
glossary.source: Lecture Ch. 13, Kidwell / Ch. 13 PPTX

glossary.def: The organ of phonation. Sits atop the trachea and below the root of the tongue. Contains the vocal folds. Composed of the cricoid cartilage, thyroid cartilage ('voice box'), two arytenoid cartilages, and the epiglottis.
glossary.source: Lecture 2.2 — Ch. 3, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def: A disorder in one or more of the basic psychological processes involved in understanding or using language, spoken or written, that may manifest as difficulty with: listening, speaking, reading, writing, spelling, mathematical computations, reasoning, and/or problem solving. NOT diagnosed until school age (when the child has had the opportunity to attempt academic tasks). Often has roots in an underlying language disorder. Approximately 15% of children with LDs also have difficulty with gross and fine motor skills and coordination.
glossary.source: Ch. 7 slides — Fogle/SJDC / Lecture Ch. 7, Kidwell

glossary.def: A legal requirement under Public Law 94-142 that children with disabilities be educated alongside non-disabled peers to the maximum extent appropriate. Remains a significant standard in special education today.
glossary.source: Lecture 1.2.2 — Chapter 2: The Professionals, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def: The dominant hemisphere for receptive and expressive language and speech. Processes rapidly changing information sequentially. Controls movement and sensation on the right side of the body. Contains Broca's area and receives input from Wernicke's area.
glossary.source: Lecture 2.2 — Ch. 3, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def: A person's vocabulary — the total set of words known and used. Between 12–18 months, toddlers develop an expressive vocabulary (lexicon) of approximately 90 words. By 2 years: ~300 words. Vocabulary continues to grow throughout life.
glossary.source: Ch. 4 PDF — Prof. Handy, SJDC

glossary.def: A philosophy and conceptual framework for aphasia intervention that emphasizes COMPETENCE and INCLUSION in activities of daily living (ADLs) and reintegration into the community. Goes beyond impairment-level treatment to address quality of life and social participation. Considered best practice (person-centered care). Focuses on what the patient CAN do and how to support meaningful participation in daily life rather than exclusively targeting the language impairment itself.
glossary.source: Ch. 15 PPTX

glossary.def: Impaired volitional movement of the limbs (arms, hands) that may co-occur with CAS or acquired AOS. A person with limb apraxia cannot perform purposeful limb movements on command — similar to oral apraxia but affecting limbs rather than articulators.
glossary.source: Ch. 6 PPTX — Fogle/SJDC

glossary.def: The scientific study of language. Divides language into five components: phonology, morphology, syntax, semantics, and pragmatics.
glossary.source: Lecture 1.2.1 — Chapter 1, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def: The second most important articulator. Biological function: hold food and liquid in the oral cavity. Overlaid function: shape sounds for speech. Produce 6 movements: opening, closing, rounding, flattening, protruding, retracting. Critical for /p, b, m, w, f, v/.
glossary.source: Lecture 2.2 — Ch. 3, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def: Impaired responsiveness, usually caused by diffuse brain dysfunction or damage to the brainstem and the reticular activating system; duration is often used as a measure of traumatic brain injury severity.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: The perceptual (subjective) correlate of intensity — how loud a sound seems to a listener. Measured in decibels (dB) when objective. Distinct from intensity, which is a physical measurement.
glossary.source: Lecture 2.2 — Ch. 3, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def: A research-based voice treatment approach used in dysarthria therapy that focuses on increasing vocal loudness as the primary target. Based on the principle that increasing loudness through intensive vocal effort improves overall speech system function. Originally developed for Parkinson's disease but applicable to other dysarthria populations.
glossary.source: Ch. 6 PPTX — Fogle/SJDC

glossary.def: Misalignment or incorrect relation between the teeth of the maxillary (upper) and mandibular (lower) dental arches when the dental arches close.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: The lower jaw. Opens and closes the mouth during speech and eating. During speech, jaw movement (how open or closed) is controlled and varies by sound. One of the two primary bones of concern to an SLP.
glossary.source: Lecture 2.2 — Ch. 3, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def: How the outgoing airstream is modified during consonant production. The eight manners of articulation are: Stop (airflow completely stopped), Nasal (air through nasal cavity), Glide (smooth gradual movement), Fricative (continuous turbulent airflow), Lateral/Liquid (/l/ sounds), Affricate (stop + fricative combined), Rhotic (/r/ sounds), Flap (brief tap of tongue).
glossary.source: Ch. 5 slides — Fogle/SJDC

glossary.def: The act of chewing. One of the three biological functions of the tongue. Also involves the mandible, teeth, and facial muscles.
glossary.source: Lecture 2.2 — Ch. 3, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def: The upper jaw bone. Contains the hard palate and upper teeth. One of the two primary bones of concern to an SLP (along with the mandible). Fuses during fetal development; disruption causes cleft palate.
glossary.source: Lecture 2.2 — Ch. 3, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def: The average number of morphemes in a young child's individual utterances. Roughly equivalent to a young child's chronological age (e.g., a 2-year-old has an MLU of ~2 morphemes). Used as a clinical measure of language development. Includes morphemes such as articles (a, the), copulas (is, be), and auxiliary verbs (have, can).
glossary.source: Ch. 4 PDF — Prof. Handy / Quiz answer key

glossary.def: ★ SLPs NEVER treat voice disorders without MEDICAL CLEARANCE from an otolaryngologist (ENT/ear-nose-throat physician). The reason: an SLP cannot determine the cause of a hoarse voice simply by listening — structural, neurological, and organic causes must be ruled out, including malignancy (cancer). The ENT sees the client FIRST and may use: indirect laryngoscopy (laryngeal mirror), endoscopy (rigid or flexible fiber-optic), direct laryngoscopy, and/or biopsy. Only after the ENT examination does the SLP proceed with assessment and treatment.
glossary.source: Lecture Ch. 13, Kidwell, SJDC

glossary.def: A chronic disease of the inner ear characterized by recurrent episodes of vertigo, tinnitus, and sensorineural hearing loss that may be bilateral.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: Metalinguistics = awareness of language as an object/tool — 'talking about language.' Problems include difficulty with: multiple meanings of words, making inferences, figurative language, sequencing, understanding similarities/differences, distinguishing literal vs. figurative meanings, and working with sentence types (declarative, interrogative, imperative, exclamatory, compound/complex). Children with SLI and LLD are particularly challenged in this area as school demands for metalinguistic skills increase with grade level.
glossary.source: Ch. 7 slides — Fogle/SJDC

glossary.def: The ability to think about and talk about language.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: An air-filled chamber located within the temporal bone of the skull; beginning at the inner side of the tympanic membrane and attaching the ossicular chain to the oval window of the cochlea.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: A hearing loss that involves both conductive and sensorineural components simultaneously.
glossary.source: Lecture 1.2.1 — Chapter 1, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def: A dynamic imaging radiographic (X-ray) procedure focusing on the mouth, pharynx, larynx, and cervical esophagus used to examine the process of swallowing.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: Also known as videofluoroscopy swallow study (VFSS). Considered the GOLD STANDARD instrumental evaluation for viewing the physiology of the swallow and determining the presence or absence of aspiration. A dynamic (moving) radiographic (X-ray) imaging procedure — a video recording of real-time movement of the bolus from the mouth to the stomach. Examines all four phases: oral preparatory, oral, pharyngeal, and esophageal. PROCEDURE: SLP and radiologist work as a team in the radiology suite; clinician presents various liquid consistencies mixed with barium and food textures coated with barium (a radiopaque substance that stops X-rays); SLP observes swallowing on video screen; patient also tries various compensatory techniques to identify what allows for safe and efficient swallow. Provides most thorough information on swallowing physiology.
glossary.source: Fogle, Ch. 18

glossary.def: The smallest unit of language that conveys meaning. Can be a whole word or an affix. Example: the '-ed' ending on 'walked' is a morpheme that signals past tense.
glossary.source: Lecture 1.2.1 — Chapter 1, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.term: Morphological and Syntactic Problems in SLI
glossary.def:
Children with SLI are late in developing syntactic structures and using them consistently. Key indicators:
• Low MLU (Mean Length of Utterance) for age — missing morphological markers like plural -s, verb -ed/-ing, possessive 's
• Speak in short, incomplete sentences ('Milk') or simple declarative sentences ('Me want milk')
• Incorrect sentence order: 'Why you not play with me?' or 'Car fast go'
• Incorrect grammar: 'Those childs goed to the park' or 'Her is stand in the water'
• Interferes with ability to convey messages even when vocabulary is adequate
glossary.source: Lecture Ch. 7, Kidwell / Ch. 7 slides

glossary.def: The study of the way words are structurally formed out of morphemes. A morpheme is the smallest unit of language that conveys meaning. Includes prefixes and suffixes. Example: 'like' → 'dislike' (prefix) or 'liked' (suffix). Language-disordered clients often show morphological errors.
glossary.source: Lecture 1.2.1 — Chapter 1, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def: Located in the frontal lobe. Takes direction from the premotor cortex (including Broca's area). The primary pathway for carrying neural impulses from the brain to the muscles of the body for voluntary movement, including speech.
glossary.source: CSD Ch3 PP (Fogle 3rd ed.)

glossary.def: The plan created by the brain specifying how to produce intended speech. The brain first determines what it wants to say, then creates a motor plan before the four processes of speech production are executed.
glossary.source: Eugene Speech Language Therapy — How We Speak (YouTube, 11xf89bXaCg)

glossary.def: Impaired speech intelligibility caused by a neurological impairment that affects motor programming and planning, coordination, or the strength of the articulators needed for rapid, complex movements in smooth, effortless speech. Umbrella term covering CAS and dysarthria. May result from prenatal, perinatal, or postnatal neurological damage.
glossary.source: Lecture Ch. 6, Kidwell, SJDC

glossary.def: Neurologically-based speech impairments affecting motor planning for speech. Includes childhood apraxia of speech and dysarthria. Can occur in both children and adults. Result from neurological disruptions to the motor planning process.
glossary.source: Lecture 1.2.1 — Chapter 1, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def: Acquired motor speech disorders in adults result from neurological impairments that affect motor planning, programming, neuromuscular control, or execution of speech — or a combination of these. They have a SENSORY component and should be thought of as SENSORIMOTOR in nature, not purely motor dysfunction. Disordered speech is sometimes the first sign of neurological disease. May co-occur with aphasia and cognitive impairments. Two main types: Apraxia of Speech (AOS) and Dysarthria.
glossary.source: Motor_Speech_Disorders.pdf / Ch. 17 PPTX

glossary.def:
Multicultural considerations affect identification, assessment, and treatment of language disorders:
• Children from different cultures may not attend preschool → late identification
• Families may be unaware of SLP services, that they are free, or how to access them
• Standardized tests are often culturally and linguistically biased
• Must assess in the PRIMARY language; use culturally appropriate norms
• Cannot diagnose a language disorder based on second-language difficulties alone
• SLPs must be cautious with EL (English Learner) students — assess both languages; disorder must be present in BOTH to qualify as a disorder
glossary.source: Lecture Ch. 7, Kidwell, SJDC

glossary.def: A progressive autoimmune disease that demyelinates the CNS — causes variable neurological symptoms depending on lesion location. May cause mixed or ataxic dysarthria among other symptoms. Speech may fluctuate with relapse/remission cycles. One of the four neuromuscular disorders commonly associated with dysarthria.
glossary.source: Ch. 17 PPTX — Fogle/SJDC

glossary.def: A hyperfunctional voice disorder in which the voice is adversely affected by excessive muscle tension that ranges from mild to severe; characteristics include a weak voice that lacks intensity, range, and variation; a rough, hoarse, or thin vocal quality that lacks resonance; and a voice that fatigues easily.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: A high-pitched, breathy voice produced by vibration of only the ANTERIOR ONE-THIRD of the vocal folds, with the posterior two-thirds held tightly in a slightly open position. Occurs in young men who persist in using a high-pitched pre-pubertal voice rather than allowing their new, lower voice to emerge during puberty. Primarily an emotional/psychological problem — a fear of growing up or identity conflict. The physical mechanism to produce a lower voice is intact; the client chooses not to use it.
glossary.source: Lecture Ch. 13, Kidwell / Ch. 13 PPTX

glossary.def: An autoimmune neuromuscular disease that impairs communication between nerves and muscles at the neuromuscular junction — causes fatigable muscle weakness. In speech: voice and articulation worsen with continued use (fatigable dysarthria). Hallmark: speech is better at the start of conversation and deteriorates the longer the person speaks. One of the four neuromuscular disorders commonly associated with dysarthria.
glossary.source: Ch. 17 PPTX — Fogle/SJDC

glossary.def: Therapy to correct tongue thrust (abnormal forward tongue movement during swallowing). May be recommended by orthodontists before placing braces. Addresses how tongue thrust can affect both speech production and dentition alignment.
glossary.source: Lecture 2.2 — Ch. 3, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def: Orderly, sequenced relating of accounts or events in conversation. Emerges during the 3–4 year range as children begin to retell stories and recount events in a logical order. An important language and literacy precursor.
glossary.source: Ch. 4 PDF — Prof. Handy, SJDC

glossary.def: Children with SLI and LLD have significant difficulty relating narratives — sequenced stories with connected events. A narrative requires: topic maintenance, sequential organization, cause-effect understanding, inclusion of key details, and coherent structure. Children with narrative disorders may tell stories that are fragmented, out of sequence, missing key characters or events, or incomprehensible to listeners unfamiliar with the topic. The Sariah video is a clinical example: her retelling of Beverly Hills Chihuahua is incomplete, uses simple/incomplete sentences, errors in past tense ('puted' for 'put'), and lacks critical narrative structure.
glossary.source: Lecture Ch. 7, Kidwell / Sariah video transcript

glossary.def: Abnormal flow of the airstream through the nose during production of consonants that require a buildup of intraoral air pressure, caused by a cleft of the hard or soft palate or velopharyngeal dysfunction that results in distortion of speech.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: One of three resonating cavities used in speech production (along with the throat and oral cavity). Sound passes through the nasal cavity to contribute to resonance and voice quality.
glossary.source: Eugene Speech Language Therapy — How We Speak (YouTube, 11xf89bXaCg)

glossary.def: Speech sounds produced with the soft palate remaining DOWN, allowing sound to pass through the nasal cavity for resonance (e.g., /m/, /n/, /ŋ/). Contrasted with oral sounds, where velopharyngeal closure redirects airflow entirely through the mouth.
glossary.source: CSD Ch3 PP (Fogle 3rd ed.)

glossary.def: A minimally invasive procedure commonly used by SLPs for evaluation of velopharyngeal dysfunction that allows visual observation and analysis of the velopharyngeal mechanism during speech.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.term: National Dysphagia Diet — Food Textures and Liquid Consistencies
glossary.def: Standardized by the National Dysphagia Diet Task Force (2002). Four FOOD TEXTURE levels: (1) REGULAR — no restrictions; (2) DYSPHAGIA ADVANCED — ground or chopped tender meat, soft foods; (3) DYSPHAGIA MECHANICALLY ALTERED — ground or finely chopped meat with gravy, mashed potatoes, soft cooked vegetables; (4) DYSPHAGIA PUREED — pureed meat, mashed vegetables, blended/baby-food texture. Four LIQUID CONSISTENCY levels: (1) THIN — no restrictions; (2) NECTAR-LIKE — all liquids thickened to nectar consistency (fruit nectars, V8, eggnog); (3) HONEY-LIKE — all liquids thickened to honey consistency using commercial thickener; (4) SPOON-THICK — all liquids thickened to pudding consistency. SLPs select the appropriate levels for each individual patient based on their specific dysphagia profile.
glossary.source: Fogle, Ch. 18

glossary.def: Perspective that language acquisition is innate, physiologically determined, and genetically transmitted. Newborns are 'prewired' for language — they will acquire it unless there are physical or mental limitations. Language is universal and unique to humans. Clinical application: target age-appropriate language structures not acquired naturally. Limitation: does not fully account for environmental influence.
glossary.source: Lecture 3.2 — Ch. 4, Kidwell / Ch. 4 PDF — Prof. Handy

glossary.def: The death of cells in an organ or tissue due to disease, injury, or failure of the blood supply.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: A combining of sounds that usually follow conventional combinations but is an invented word that appears to serve a linguistic function.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: The brain's ability to reroute neural pathways following injury, allowing recovery from brain damage. The greatest gains occur within the first few weeks or months post-stroke. Recovery may continue at a slower pace for years. Factors affecting recovery: chronological age (younger is generally better), overall physical health, site of lesion (location), and size of lesion (larger lesion = more severe damage). An injury on the right side of the brain affects motor movement on the LEFT side of the body and vice versa.
glossary.source: Ch. 15 PPTX; Kidwell lecture notes

glossary.term: Neurological Voice Disorders — Hypoadduction and Hyperadduction
glossary.def:
Voice disorders caused by neurological problems affecting muscle control and innervation of respiration and phonation:

HYPOADDUCTION (vocal folds do NOT close strongly/long enough):
• Vocal fold PARESIS (weakness) or PARALYSIS
• Unilateral: causes breathiness or DIPLOPHONIA (two simultaneous pitches)
• Bilateral: can PREVENT RESPIRATION — life-threatening

HYPERADDUCTION (vocal folds close TOO tightly or for TOO LONG):
• Results in SPASMODIC DYSPHONIA (SD): rare CNS disorder with strained, strangled, or harsh voice quality
• SD does NOT respond to voice therapy alone
• Treatment: BOTULINUM TOXIN (Botox) injection to weaken the vocal fold muscles OR surgical severing of the recurrent laryngeal nerve
glossary.source: Lecture Ch. 13, Kidwell / Ch. 13 PPTX

glossary.def: A nerve cell — the basic unit of the nervous system. The brain contains approximately 100 billion neurons. Each neuron has thousands of connections to other neurons, creating trillions of connections. Every thought, action, emotion, and memory results from electrochemical reactions between neurons.
glossary.source: Lecture 2.2 — Ch. 3, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def: A permanent sensorineural hearing loss caused by exposure to excessive loud noise, often over long periods of time.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: Aphasia that results from damage to anterior cortical regions (i.e., frontal lobe lesions) and is characterized by sparse, perseverative language with disturbed prosody, misarticulations, errors in syntax, and a reduction in phrase length.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: Communication that does not rely on spoken words. Includes facial expressions, eye contact, body posture, and gestures. Often used simultaneously with verbal communication.
glossary.source: Lecture 1.2.1 — Chapter 1, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def: Disfluencies that are within typical limits and not considered a disorder. Normal disfluencies include: occasional repetitions of syllables or words (once or twice — 'li-li-like this'), hesitations, and use of fillers ('uh,' 'er,' 'um'). They occur most often between 1½ and 5 years of age, tend to come and go, and are often a sign that language is being used in a new way. As an SLP, a key task is determining whether a child is experiencing normal or abnormal disfluencies.
glossary.source: Fluency_Disorders.pdf / Lecture Ch. 12, Kidwell

glossary.term: NPO (Nothing by Mouth) and Enteral Feeding
glossary.def: NPO (nil per os): A patient care instruction advising that a patient is PROHIBITED from orally ingesting food, beverage, or medicine; patients must receive nutrition through enteral feeding. Used when dysphagia is so severe that oral intake cannot be managed safely. ENTERAL FEEDING: The delivery of hydration and nutrition into the gastrointestinal (GI) tract by way of tube feedings — through the nose (NASOGASTRIC TUBE / NG-tube) or through the stomach (GASTROSTOMY TUBE / G-tube). May be temporary or permanent. Common nutrition supplement: Ensure (Pediasure for children) — contains protein, carbohydrates, fat, vitamins, and minerals. Usually after swallowing therapy, patients are able to eat orally again.
glossary.source: Fogle, Ch. 18

glossary.term: NSSLHA (National Student Speech Language Hearing Association)
glossary.def: The student membership division of ASHA. Open to students pursuing degrees in communication sciences and disorders. Website: www.nsslha.org.
glossary.source: Lecture 1.2.2 — Chapter 2: The Professionals, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def: Located at the posterior (back) of the brain. Where visual images are received and processed. Damage can cause visual field deficits or cortical blindness.
glossary.source: Lecture 2.2 — Ch. 3, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def: A partial or complete blockage (occlusion) of a cerebral artery, causing decreased blood supply to brain tissue.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: An articulation error in which a sound is completely left out of a word. Example: 'at' for 'bat' — the initial /b/ is omitted. Often occurs in final position in young children (e.g., 'da' for 'dog').
glossary.source: Lecture 4.2, Kidwell, SJDC

glossary.def: Brain injury that involves a wound caused by a missile (e.g., bullet or shrapnel), instrument (e.g., axe or knife), or force strong enough to fracture the skull and cause brain damage.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.term: Open Head Injury (OHI) and Blast/War Injuries
glossary.def: OPEN HEAD INJURY (OHI / penetrating head injury): the skull and brain are actually penetrated by severe impact or a foreign body (e.g., gunshot wound). Cognitive impairments may last months to years after injury. WAR WOUNDS: usually more extensive because bullets travel at higher velocity and shock waves cause additional damage; shrapnel is hot and the heat itself destroys brain tissue. BLAST INJURIES: described as POLYTRAUMA because they injure multiple body parts and organ systems. PTSD may co-occur — symptoms include intense fear, horror, and helplessness. War-injured patients may present differently from civilian TBI patients.
glossary.source: Ch. 16 PPTX; Kidwell lecture notes

glossary.def: A learning model (Skinner's behavioral theory) in which a behavior is immediately reinforced after it occurs, increasing the likelihood it will recur. In language therapy, used as stimulus-response: a child approximates a word, the SLP reinforces it and shapes it toward the correct form.
glossary.source: Lecture 3.2 — Ch. 4, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def: Difficulty with volitional nonspeech movements of the articulators/face — may co-occur with AOS. The person cannot voluntarily perform movements such as: puffing the cheeks, clicking the tongue, protruding the tongue on command, whistling, or smiling when asked. These same movements may occur automatically/spontaneously. Also called nonverbal apraxia, facial apraxia, or buccofacial apraxia.
glossary.source: Ch. 17 PPTX / Lecture Ch. 17, Kidwell

glossary.term: Oral apraxia (nonverbal apraxia, facial apraxia, buccofacial apraxia)
glossary.def: Difficulty with volitional nonspeech movements of the articulators (e.g., puffing the cheeks, clicking the tongue, protruding the tongue, whistling, or smiling).
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: Difficulty with volitional nonspeech movements of the articulators — may co-occur with CAS. The child (or adult with AOS) cannot voluntarily perform oral movements such as whistling, blowing out candles, smiling on command, or clicking the tongue, even though these movements may occur automatically (e.g., smiling spontaneously in response to something funny).
glossary.source: Ch. 6 PPTX / Lecture Ch. 6, Kidwell

glossary.def: A communication disorder with an identifiable anatomic, physiologic, or neurologic cause. Examples: cleft palate (structural), cerebral palsy (neurologic), hearing loss (physiologic). Contrasted with functional disorder. Can be caused by prenatal, perinatal, or postnatal factors.
glossary.source: Ch. 5 slides — Fogle/SJDC / Lecture 4.2, Kidwell

glossary.def: The three small bones (ossicles) of the middle ear named after their basic shapes: malleus (mallet/hammer), incus (anvil), and stapes (stirrup).
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: Any inflammation or infection of the middle ear; particularly common in childhood and often associated with a common cold, allergies, sinus infections, and sore throats.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: A medical doctor who specializes in diseases of the ears, nose, and throat.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: Typically a hereditary condition that occurs when there is growth of bony tissue around the footplate of the stapes that presses against the oval window, resulting in a conductive hearing loss.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: The adapted, secondary use of a body structure for communication. Humans have learned to use structures designed for biological survival (breathing, eating) and adapt them for speech. Example: lips biologically hold food in → overlaid function is producing speech sounds.
glossary.source: Lecture 2.2 — Ch. 3, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def:
The AUDIBLE characteristics of stuttering — what can be HEARD. Eight types:
1. BLOCKS — complete stoppage of airflow/voicing
2. DYSRHYTHMIC PHONATIONS — disrupted rhythm in sound production
3. INTERJECTIONS — insertion of sounds, syllables, words, or phrases ('uh-uh-uh-uh')
4. PART-WORD REPETITIONS — repetition of part of a word ('b-b-b-baby')
5. PHRASE REPETITIONS — repeating a whole phrase
6. PROLONGATIONS — stretching out a sound
7. REVISIONS — changing direction mid-utterance
8. WHOLE-WORD REPETITIONS — repeating an entire word
glossary.source: Fluency_Disorders.pdf / Lecture Ch. 12, Kidwell

glossary.def: The VISIBLE characteristics of stuttering — physical struggle behaviors that develop when trying NOT to stutter. Also called 'tricks or crutches.' Include: losing eye contact at the moment of stuttering, rapid eye blinking, furrowing the forehead, tensing facial muscles, jerking the head, tensing/raising the shoulders, swinging or jerking an arm, clenching a fist, pressing thumb and fingernail together, tensing chest muscles, moving upper/lower leg, pulling foot toward shin, tapping foot, tensing toes — or any other physical movement distracting to the listener. These behaviors emerge from STRUGGLE, not from the stutter itself.
glossary.source: Fluency_Disorders.pdf / Lecture Ch. 12, Kidwell

glossary.def: A prosthetic device that can be used to raise the soft palate for speech if the soft palate is long enough to achieve velopharyngeal closure but does not move well, often because of central or peripheral neurological impairment.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: Soft, wartlike, benign growths on the vocal folds of children that have a viral origin and may grow to a size that can obstruct the airway.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: Wart-like growths on the vocal folds or larynx caused by a VIRUS (human papillomavirus). An organic voice disorder requiring medical/surgical treatment. Most commonly occur in CHILDREN aged 4–6 years (juvenile papillomatosis). Can recur after surgical removal. The SLP works with the client post-treatment but medical management is primary.
glossary.source: Lecture Ch. 13, Kidwell / Ch. 13 PPTX

glossary.def: Non-verbal contextual elements that contribute to the meaning of a communication beyond the spoken words. Includes facial expressions, body language, clothing, artifacts, and environmental context. Example: knowing someone is the teacher because they have the key, a briefcase, and stand at the front of the class.
glossary.source: Lecture 1.2.1 — Chapter 1, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def: A technique in which a caregiver or clinician names, describes, and explains what the child is experiencing and probably feeling, almost as if the caregiver is the child speaking for them. Provides language concepts and models for the child. A form of inner speech scaffolding. Example: 'You're building a tower. The blocks are tall!'
glossary.source: Ch. 4 PDF — Prof. Handy / Quiz answer key

glossary.def: Loss of voluntary movement (motor function) that may affect one or more groups of muscles.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: Substitution errors in aphasia. LITERAL (PHONEMIC) PARAPHASIA: substituting sounds within a word (e.g., saying 'treen' for 'tree'). VERBAL (SEMANTIC) PARAPHASIA: substituting a related word (e.g., saying 'chair' for 'table'). NEOLOGISMS: making up entirely new words that do not exist. JARGON APHASIA: fluently stringing together nonsense words and real words, but with insufficient relevant content — the output sounds fluent but is semantically meaningless. CIRCUMLOCUTIONS: talking around a word the patient cannot retrieve (e.g., 'the thing you write with' instead of 'pen').
glossary.source: Ch. 15 PPTX; Kidwell lecture notes

glossary.def: A naturally occurring, higher-pitched, slower-paced, melodic speech style that caregivers use when talking to infants. Characterized by frequent pauses, exaggerated intonation, and simplified vocabulary. Infants respond to the tone and melody of parentese before they understand the words.
glossary.source: Lecture 3.2 — Ch. 4, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def: Paresis: partial weakness or reduced strength of a muscle or group of muscles. Paralysis: complete loss of voluntary movement. Both can affect the speech muscles in dysarthria. Dysarthria may involve paresis, paralysis, OR incoordination of the muscles of respiration, phonation, resonation, and articulation.
glossary.source: Ch. 6 PPTX — Fogle/SJDC

glossary.def: Receives and processes sensory information from the body (touch, pressure, temperature, pain). Located behind the frontal lobe. Involved in spatial awareness and integrating sensory input.
glossary.source: Lecture 2.2 — Ch. 3, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def: A progressive neurodegenerative disease of the basal ganglia causing hypokinetic dysarthria. Associated speech characteristics: reduced vocal loudness (hypophonia), monopitch (reduced pitch variation), monoloudness, imprecise consonants, rapid rate with short rushes of speech (festination), and reduced intelligibility. LSVT LOUD (Lee Silverman Voice Treatment) is the most evidence-based treatment for PD-related dysarthria. PD is one of the most commonly associated neuromuscular disorders with dysarthria.
glossary.source: Lecture Ch. 17, Kidwell / Ch. 17 PPTX

glossary.def: Allows the CNS to communicate with the body. Composed of cranial nerves exiting the brainstem and spinal nerves exiting the spinal cord, plus sensory nerves throughout the body. Carries sensory information TO the brain and motor commands FROM the brain to muscles.
glossary.source: Lecture 2.2 — Ch. 3, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def: Automatic and involuntary repetition of a behavior beyond an intended point, including repetition of a word or phrase or hand gesture that may be caused by neurological impairment or psychiatric disorder.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: A frequency modulation (FM) assistive listening device used as part of APD treatment. The teacher or speaker wears a microphone/transmitter; the child wears a receiver that delivers the speaker's voice directly to the child's ears (bypassing distance and background noise). Improves signal-to-noise ratio dramatically. One of the three components of the environmental/assistive APD treatment approach.
glossary.source: Ch. 9 PPTX — Fogle/SJDC

glossary.def: A muscular membranous cavity, approximately 5 inches (12.5 cm) long, behind the nasal cavities and mouth. It may be divided into three areas: the nasal pharynx (top), the oral pharynx (center), and laryngeal pharynx (bottom), which provide passageways for both air and food and functions as a resonating organ in speech.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: The second of four speech production processes. Involves the vocal folds and laryngeal structures to produce voice. Some sounds require vocal fold vibration (voiced: /z, d, b/); others do not (voiceless: /s, t, p/).
glossary.source: Lecture 1.2.1 — Chapter 1, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def: The process of voicing produced by the vibration of the vocal folds. When the vocal folds are pushed together and air from the lungs forces them open, they vibrate at a rapid rate to create sound.
glossary.source: Eugene Speech Language Therapy — How We Speak (YouTube, 11xf89bXaCg)

glossary.def: The smallest unit of sound that is distinct from others in a language. Represented in slashes: /b/, /s/, /æ/. By combining phonemes we create words. Example: /b/ + /æ/ + /g/ = 'bag'. Not to be confused with letters of the alphabet.
glossary.source: Lecture 1.2.1 — Chapter 1, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def: The smallest unit of sound in a language that distinguishes one word from another. English has 26 letters but 45 phonemes (speech sounds). The name of a letter is different from its sound — e.g., the letter 'c' represents two phonemes (/k/ and /s/). SLPs think and work in terms of phonemes, not letters.
glossary.source: Lecture 4.2, Kidwell / Ch. 5 slides

glossary.def: The study of speech-sound production and the special symbols that represent speech sounds. The scientific foundation for analyzing, transcribing, and describing speech. SLPs use phonetics to identify and document errors in speech production.
glossary.source: Ch. 5 slides — Fogle/SJDC

glossary.def: Systematic, rule-based sound simplification patterns children use during speech development. Include: syllable structure processes (e.g., reducing consonant clusters), substitution processes (replacing harder sounds with easier ones), and assimilatory processes (sounds influenced by neighboring sounds). Most resolve by age 5.
glossary.source: Ch. 4 PDF — Prof. Handy, SJDC

glossary.def: Recognition and understanding of sound-letter associations; that individual sounds can be combined to form words; that a single-syllable word can be segmented into its beginning, middle, and ending sounds; and that longer words have more 'middle' sounds.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: A speech disorder characterized by error patterns in sound combinations, rather than individual sound errors. Considered a speech impairment alongside articulation and motor speech disorders.
glossary.source: Lecture 1.2.1 — Chapter 1, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def: Errors of several phonemes that form patterns or clusters, which result from a child simplifying speech sounds and sound combinations used by normally speaking adults. The child has not learned the linguistic rules to produce and combine sounds into syllables — even though they are physically capable of making the motor movements to produce the sounds. Distinguished from articulation disorder by its patterned, rule-based nature.
glossary.source: Quiz answer key + Lecture 4.2, Kidwell / Ch. 5 slides

glossary.def:
Rule-based sound simplification patterns that are normal in young children but become disordered when they persist beyond expected ages. Common processes from Table 5-3:
• Context-sensitive voicing: voiceless sound replaced by voiced (pig→big)
• Final-consonant devoicing: voiced final consonant replaced by voiceless (bag→bat)
• Final-consonant deletion: final consonant omitted (home→hoe)
• Velar fronting: velar consonant /k,g/ replaced by alveolar /t,d/ (key→tea)
• Palatal fronting: /sh,zh/ replaced by alveolar sounds
• Consonant harmony: whole word influenced by single sound (dog→gog)
• Weak-syllable deletion: unstressed syllables omitted (telephone→teffone)
• Cluster reduction: part of consonant cluster omitted (spider→pider, spoon→poon)
• Gliding of liquids: /r,l/ replaced by /w,j/ (red→wed)
• Stopping: fricative/affricate replaced by stop (funny→punny)
glossary.source: Ch. 5 slides — Fogle/SJDC / Lecture 4.2, Kidwell

glossary.def: The study of speech sounds and the rules for using them in a language. Key unit: the phoneme — the smallest unit of sound that is distinct from others. Written using / / notation (e.g., /b/, /s/). Not the same as letters — e.g., 'c' has no phonetic equivalent of its own.
glossary.source: Lecture 1.2.1 — Chapter 1, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def: The study of how body structures function and work. In SLP, physiology refers to how the speech structures move, contract, and coordinate to produce communication. A person can have intact anatomy but impaired physiology.
glossary.source: Lecture 2.2 — Ch. 3, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def: A form of augmentative and alternative communication (AAC) used widely with children and adults with ASD and other disorders. PECS is designed to teach functional communication with an initial focus on spontaneous communication.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: The psychological (perceptual) sensation of frequency — how high or low a voice sounds to a listener. Pitch changes are based on vocal fold tension, mass, and length. Distinct from frequency, which is an objective measurement.
glossary.source: Lecture 2.2 — Ch. 3, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def: Where in the vocal tract the articulators make contact or approach to produce a consonant sound. The seven places of articulation are: Bilabial (both lips), Labiodental (lip + teeth), Interdental (tongue between teeth), Alveolar (tongue + alveolar ridge), Palatal (tongue + hard palate), Velar (tongue + soft palate/velum), Glottal (at the glottis/vocal folds).
glossary.source: Ch. 5 slides — Fogle/SJDC

glossary.def: Swelling of the vocal folds as a result of fluid collection (edema) in the superficial layers of the epithelial tissue.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: A medical term describing the condition of a person who has been subjected to multiple traumatic injuries, such as a serious head injury, severe burns, lacerations, bone fractures, and post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD).
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: Vague or nonspecific symptoms that are a continuation of symptoms seen in the acute phase of a mild TBI that may last for several weeks or months, with physical symptoms such as headache and dizziness, psychological symptoms such as depressed mood and irritability, and cognitive impairments such as memory and concentration difficulties.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: A set of symptoms after exposure to a psychologically extreme traumatic stressor that involves intense fear, horror, or helplessness; symptoms include persistent reexperiencing of the traumatic event, avoidance of stimuli associated with the trauma, and symptoms of increased arousal (hypervigilance).
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: Difficulty with the USE of language in social situations. Pragmatics governs how language is used socially — rules that vary by culture. In the U.S., pragmatic expectations include: appropriate eye contact, personal space, turn-taking, topic maintenance, volume control, and reading social cues. A child with a pragmatic disorder may violate these rules — e.g., interrupting class inappropriately, speaking too loudly, not understanding compliments, not maintaining topic, or not recognizing when a listener has lost interest. Classic example: child with ASD who has correct vocabulary and grammar but inappropriate social language use.
glossary.source: Lecture Ch. 7, Kidwell, SJDC

glossary.def: The rules of use of language in social situations. Concerned with the function of language rather than its structure. Includes verbal and nonverbal cues, physical appearance, body language, timing, and context. Children/adults on the Autism Spectrum often have pragmatic delays — social language does not 'click' naturally.
glossary.source: Lecture 1.2.1 — Chapter 1, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def: The anterior portion of the frontal lobe. Responsible for higher cognitive functions: attention, reasoning, judgment, decision-making, problem-solving, personality, character, philosophies, religious and political beliefs.
glossary.source: CSD Ch3 PP (Fogle 3rd ed.)

glossary.def: Sounds produced by an infant before true words appear (birth to approximately 12 months). Include crying, cooing, babbling, echolalia, vocal play, and jargon. These are preverbal but communicative — the foundation of speech and language development.
glossary.source: Lecture 3.2 — Ch. 4, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def:
Three time-based environmental risk factors for communication disorders:
• Prenatal (during pregnancy): substance abuse, syndromes, low birth weight, prematurity — can cause anatomic/physiologic problems or developmental delay. Maternal substance abuse is the only 100% preventable cause.
• Perinatal (during birth): insufficient oxygen (e.g., cord around neck) → neurologic damage (anoxia/hypoxia)
• Postnatal (after birth): limited environmental stimulation → fewer synaptic connections in brain (e.g., infant left in crib without verbal or visual interaction).
glossary.source: Lecture 4.2, Kidwell, SJDC

glossary.def: Hearing loss associated with old age, usually involving both a loss of hearing sensitivity and a reduction in clarity of speech.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: Age-related dysphonia associated with normal physiological aging, and medical and psychosocial conditions that are common in older age.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: The number of individuals diagnosed with a particular disorder at a given point in time. Distinct from incidence.
glossary.source: Lecture 1.2.1 — Chapter 1, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def: Located in the temporal lobe. Responsible for auditory processing — perceives human speech sounds and environmental sounds. Auditory input is then passed to Wernicke's area for language interpretation.
glossary.source: CSD Ch3 PP (Fogle 3rd ed.)

glossary.def: Unlike other types of aphasia, PPA is DEGENERATIVE — it gets progressively worse over time rather than occurring suddenly. Language capabilities become slowly and progressively impaired, leading to gradual loss of ability to read, write, speak, and understand others. Deterioration can happen over a period of years. Other mental functions such as memory, reasoning, insight, and judgment are usually NOT affected in early stages (distinguishing it from Alzheimer's disease). There is no cure; management focuses on communication tools and support strategies.
glossary.source: Kidwell lecture notes

glossary.def:
PRIMARY language impairment: language IS the primary handicapping condition — no other overarching diagnosis causes it (e.g., SLI). Also called primary language disorder.
SECONDARY language impairment: a more pervasive disability causes the communication disorder. The language impairment exists BECAUSE of the primary condition. Example: a child with Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD) will also have a communication disorder — but the autism is the etiology; the communication disorder is secondary.
glossary.source: Lecture Ch. 7, Kidwell, SJDC

glossary.def: A prediction of the probable course, duration, recovery, or termination of a disease or disorder; a prediction of the outcome of a proposed course of treatment, its effectiveness and duration, and the individual's progress.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: Prompts for Restructuring Oral Muscular Phonetic Targets — a tactile-kinesthetic approach to motor speech therapy in which the clinician uses manual cues on the client's face/jaw/lips to guide correct articulatory placement. Commonly used in CAS treatment as part of a multi-modal approach.
glossary.source: Ch. 6 PPTX — Fogle/SJDC

glossary.def: The melody and rhythm of spoken language — includes stress, timing, pitch, and intonation. Conveys emotional intent and meaning beyond the literal words. Example: the same sentence 'How are you today?' carries different meanings depending on which word is stressed.
glossary.source: Lecture 1.2.1 — Chapter 1, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def: Difficulty recognizing familiar faces, including family members and famous people.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.term: Psychosocial Consequences of Motor Speech Disorders
glossary.def: Individuals with motor speech disorders are often acutely aware of their difficulty being understood. Attempts at communication can be filled with frustration. Consequences include: avoidance of communication, social withdrawal, reduced quality of life, depression and anxiety, difficulty maintaining relationships, and reduced independence. For those with AOS, the harder they try to correct errors, the more difficult speech production becomes — creating a frustrating cycle. SLPs must address emotional and social effects as part of treatment.
glossary.source: Motor_Speech_Disorders.pdf / Lecture Ch. 17, Kidwell

glossary.def: Federal legislation passed in 1975 mandating that all school-aged children with disabilities receive a free and appropriate public education (FAPE) in the least restrictive environment (LRE). A landmark law for special education and communication disorders services.
glossary.source: Lecture 1.2.2 — Chapter 2: The Professionals, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def: A global concept that involves a person's standard of living, personal freedom, and the opportunity to pursue happiness; a measure of a person's ability to cope successfully with the full range of challenges encountered in daily living.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: The biological characteristics shared by a group of people. Distinct from culture, dialect, and language — race is a biological classification, while language and dialect differences are cultural and linguistic, not biological.
glossary.source: Quiz answer key — Ch. 4

glossary.def: The limits to which the articulators can move for speech: how far the mandible can open/close; how far the lips can protrude and retract; how far the tongue can protrude, retract, elevate, lower, and lateralize. Reduced range of motion can impair speech intelligibility.
glossary.source: CSD Ch3 PP (Fogle 3rd ed.)

glossary.def: An inability or difficulty reading that is of neurological origin.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: Difficulty comprehending verbal, written, and/or gestural language.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: The ability to understand spoken or written language — what a person comprehends when others speak. Receptive language delays affect how well a person understands what is being said to them.
glossary.source: Lecture 1.2.1 — Chapter 1, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def:
The process by which a listener INFERS the meaning of an auditory message based on the context of the information and long-term stored memory of words and general knowledge. It is an ACTIVE process. Receptive language = what we understand WITHOUT producing words. Also called language comprehension or auditory comprehension. Children with SLI may struggle with: normal rate of conversation, direct and indirect questions, following directions, comprehension of individual words, and the subtleties of connected speech.
★ Language impairment WITH a receptive component is MORE RESISTANT TO INTERVENTION and carries GREATER RISK for adverse academic outcomes than expressive-only impairment.
glossary.source: Lecture Ch. 7, Kidwell / Ch. 7 slides

glossary.def:
Children with SLI and a receptive component:
• Spoken words come at them too fast — difficulty keeping up with normal conversation rate
• Understand DIRECT statements more easily than INDIRECT versions of the same message
• LITERAL interpretation of idioms, metaphors, similes — cannot grasp figurative language
• Difficulty with abstract concepts
• Difficulty expressing wants, needs, thoughts, knowledge, and feelings
• Vocabulary weaknesses persist through primary grades and junior high school

Adolescent receptive problems: weak vocabulary, difficulty with figurative/abstract language, difficulty following directions and understanding lectures, poor listening skills, misinterpreting facial expressions.
glossary.source: Ch. 7 slides / Lecture Ch. 7, Kidwell

glossary.def: The process by which sound created at the vocal folds travels upward through cavities in the head — the throat, the oral cavity, and the nasal cavity — and is shaped to produce individual voice quality.
glossary.source: Eugene Speech Language Therapy — How We Speak (YouTube, 11xf89bXaCg)

glossary.def: The quality of the voice that results from vibration of sound in the vocal tract — specifically the spaces and tissues of the pharynx, oral cavity, and nasal cavity. Shaped by the position of the soft palate and the structures of the pharyngeal region.
glossary.source: CSD Ch3 PP (Fogle 3rd ed.)

glossary.def: The third of four speech production processes. How sound produced by the larynx is modified by the shape of the vocal tract. Involves airflow through the throat, nose (nasal sounds), and mouth. Nasal sounds like /m/ involve nasal resonance; non-nasal sounds like those in 'soup' do not.
glossary.source: Lecture 1.2.1 — Chapter 1, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def: Caused by abnormal structure or functioning of the hard and soft palates. Results in hypernasal voice (sounds like talking through the nose) or hyponasal voice (sounds lack normal nasal resonance).
glossary.source: Lecture 1.2.1 — Chapter 1, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def: The breathing process that provides the energy source behind speech production. During speech, the inhale-to-exhale ratio shifts dramatically to approximately 10% inhalation and 90% exhalation, compared to the resting ratio of 50/50 or 40/60.
glossary.source: Eugene Speech Language Therapy — How We Speak (YouTube, 11xf89bXaCg)

glossary.def: The first of four speech production processes. Provides the breath support needed to produce speech. Requires lungs, diaphragm, and chest muscles to be correctly formed and functioning. Problems with respiration impact speech sound production and intelligibility.
glossary.source: Lecture 1.2.1 — Chapter 1, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def: Responsible for attention, orientation, emotions, and cognition. Controls all movements for the left side of the body. Works with the left hemisphere via the corpus callosum to comprehend, integrate, and formulate language.
glossary.source: Lecture 2.2 — Ch. 3, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def: Communication and cognitive disorders resulting from damage to the RIGHT hemisphere of the brain. COGNITIVE impairments: attention, perception, memory, orientation, organization, insight, reasoning and problem-solving, executive functions, and ANOSOGNOSIA (lack of awareness or insight into one's own deficits). COMMUNICATION impairments: literal interpretation of messages, metaphors, idioms, and proverbs; pragmatic violations (irrelevant/tangential/inappropriate comments); communication devoid of emotion; APROSODIA (decreased prosody) and FLAT AFFECT; difficulty with topic maintenance and turn-taking. VISUAL-SPATIAL impairments: LEFT-SIDE NEGLECT (not perceiving objects on the left side of the body), driving may be dangerous; PROSOPAGNOSIA (difficulty recognizing faces).
glossary.source: Ch. 16 PPTX; Kidwell lecture notes

glossary.term: Sariah Video — Expressive Language Disorder (Clinical Analysis)
glossary.def:
Sariah's retelling of Beverly Hills Chihuahua is a classroom example of expressive language disorder with the following observable features:
• MORPHOLOGICAL errors: 'puted' for 'put' (irregular past tense error — overgeneralization); 'was trying to get' (correct) but inconsistent
• SYNTACTIC errors: incomplete sentences; pronoun errors ('papy found it' — unclear referent)
• NARRATIVE difficulty: characters introduced without clear identification; events out of sequence; critical plot elements omitted; listener cannot follow without prior knowledge of the movie
• VOCABULARY: some topic vocabulary intact but limited; relies on vague language
• MLU is low for apparent age
This child has expressive language disorder affecting morphology, syntax, and narrative organization — NOT just a speech sound disorder.
glossary.source: Sariah video transcript / Lecture Ch. 7, Kidwell

glossary.def: Support that adults provide to children for them to achieve competence in an activity (e.g., reading and writing), with the support gradually removed until the child is able to perform independently.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: The defined range of professional activities, responsibilities, and services that a licensed SLP or Audiologist is qualified and authorized to perform, as established by ASHA.
glossary.source: Lecture 1.2.2 — Chapter 2: The Professionals, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def: A rare injury in which an individual receives a second concussion before the symptoms of a prior concussion are completely resolved; occurs primarily in teens and young adults who play hard contact sports and can cause diffuse and catastrophic cerebral edema that results in permanent disability or death.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.term: Semantic Problems in SLI (Vocabulary and Concept Development)
glossary.def:
Children with SLI are at high risk for vocabulary and semantic difficulties:
• Delay in using first true words; slow vocabulary growth (fewer than 20 words by 18–24 months)
• Difficulty producing two-word combinations by 24 months
• Poor concept development: adjectives (shape, size, color), quantity, quality; verbs and verb markers (-ed, -ing)
• Word-finding problems — know the word but cannot retrieve it
• Difficulty asking wh- questions (who, what, when, where, why, how)
• Difficulty relating narratives (sequenced stories)
• Difficulty with basic concept words: over, under, next to, first, left, farthest
glossary.source: Ch. 7 slides / Lecture Ch. 7, Kidwell

glossary.def: A perspective of language development that emphasizes the interrelationship between language learning and cognition; that is, the meanings conveyed by a child's productions.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: Perspective that emphasizes the interrelationship between language learning and cognition — the meanings conveyed by a child's productions. Meaning precedes form: children understand what they want to communicate before knowing the correct word or grammar. Clinical application: adjust complexity, amount, or rate of speech. Limitation: does not fully explain how language structure is acquired.
glossary.source: Lecture 3.2 — Ch. 4, Kidwell / Ch. 4 PDF — Prof. Handy

glossary.def: The study of meaning in language. Includes vocabulary, categories, and idioms (figures of speech). Dialectical differences in semantics are not disorders. Example: 'trunk' (US) vs. 'boot' (UK) for the back storage of a car. A delay in vocabulary significantly hinders communication.
glossary.source: Lecture 1.2.1 — Chapter 1, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def: The study of meaning in language. Semantic difficulties in SLI include: vocabulary problems (both receptive and expressive), difficulty with basic concept words (over, under, next to, first), category knowledge, idioms, word definitions, and abstract concepts. Word-finding problems are common — the child 'knows' the word but cannot retrieve it on demand. Poor semantic development is one of the earliest and most consistent markers of SLI.
glossary.source: Lecture Ch. 7, Kidwell, SJDC

glossary.term: Sensorimotor Nature of Motor Speech Disorders
glossary.def: Motor speech disorders have a sensory component — they are SENSORIMOTOR in nature, not purely motor dysfunction. The sensory feedback systems (proprioception, auditory, tactile) that monitor and regulate speech production are also affected. This is why treatment approaches that incorporate multiple sensory modalities (tactile cues, visual feedback, auditory monitoring) are more effective than purely motor-based approaches.
glossary.source: Motor_Speech_Disorders.pdf / Ch. 17 PPTX

glossary.def: A reduction in hearing caused by damage to the cochlea and/or auditory nerve. Distinguished from conductive hearing loss by the location of the damage (inner ear / nerve rather than outer/middle ear).
glossary.source: Lecture 1.2.1 — Chapter 1, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def: Penetration of food or liquid (including saliva) into the larynx and passing below the vocal folds without a protective cough or choking occurring.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def:
The six types of dysarthria (from Table 17-1, Fogle textbook):
1. Spastic — from bilateral UMN damage; hypertonicity; harsh voice, hypernasality, slow rate
2. Flaccid — from LMN/PNS damage; hypotonicity; breathy voice, hypernasality, weak pressure consonants
3. Ataxic — from cerebellar damage; irregular articulatory breakdown; scanning speech; excess/equal stress
4. Hypokinetic — from basal ganglia damage; Parkinson's disease; reduced loudness, monopitch, rapid rate (festination)
5. Hyperkinetic — from basal ganglia damage; irregular involuntary movements; variable rate and loudness
6. Mixed — combination of two or more types; e.g., ALS = flaccid + spastic
glossary.source: Lecture Ch. 17, Kidwell / Ch. 17 PPTX

glossary.term: SLI Red Flags (Late Talker Warning Signs)
glossary.def:
Warning signs that a child may be at risk for SLI — late talkers can alert parents and clinicians:
• Slow development of speech sounds
• Does not say 'Mama' or 'Dada' by 12–18 months
• ★ First true word does not appear until AFTER 18 months
• ★ Two-word combinations not used until AFTER 30 months
• Restricted vocabulary in both comprehension and production
• Reliance on gestures to get needs met
• Infrequent use of verbs; poor verb development
• Lack of yes/no responses to questions
• Difficulty initiating interactions with peers and turn-taking
• Difficulty rhyming words and naming letters
glossary.source: Ch. 7 slides — Fogle/SJDC

glossary.def:
After ENT medical clearance, the SLP assessment includes:
• CASE HISTORY: client interview (voice problem, medications, smoking, alcohol use); reading the ENT medical report
• LISTENING AND OBSERVING: loudness, pitch, quality, throat clearing, tension in voice/face/body
• HEARING SCREENING
• QUANTIFICATION OF THE VOICE using rating scales:
  - CAPE-V (Consensus Auditory-Perceptual-Evaluation of Voice)
  - GRBAS Scale (Grade, Roughness, Breathiness, Asthenic, Strain)
• PERCEPTUAL features: what we hear (loudness, pitch, quality)
• ACOUSTIC features (instrumental): habitual pitch, pitch range, optimal pitch, maximum phonation time
• PHYSIOLOGIC features: aerodynamic features, vibratory behavior, airflow rates/pressures, masses on folds
• INSTRUMENTAL TOOLS: VisiPitch, Computerized Speech Lab (CSL)
• BASELINE established to measure therapy progress
glossary.source: Lecture Ch. 13, Kidwell / Ch. 13 PPTX

glossary.def: Support personnel who perform tasks prescribed, directed, and supervised by ASHA-certified SLPs. Earn an Associate of Science (A.S.) degree from an SLPA training program. Responsibilities are defined on page 32 of the textbook.
glossary.source: Lecture 1.2.2 — Chapter 2: The Professionals, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def: A primary deficit in the social use of nonverbal and verbal communication characterized by difficulty in using language for social purposes, appropriately matching communication to the social context, following rules of the communication context, understanding nonliteral language, and integrating language with nonverbal communicative behaviors.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: Perspective that communication is the basic function of language, developed through caregiver interaction. Prerequisites: caregiver proximity, basic physical needs met, attachment, joint attention, and turn-taking. Clinical application: social games, interactive reading. Limitation: does not explain why all humans develop similar grammar and phonology despite unique social experiences.
glossary.source: Lecture 3.2 — Ch. 4, Kidwell / Ch. 4 PDF — Prof. Handy

glossary.def: The muscular posterior 1/3 of the roof of the mouth, ending in the uvula. At rest it hangs down at the base of the tongue. During speech it raises and moves posteriorly to contact the posterior pharyngeal wall, closing off the nasal cavity (velopharyngeal closure).
glossary.source: Lecture 2.2 — Ch. 3, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def: A rare CENTRAL NERVOUS SYSTEM disorder characterized by involuntary spasms of the laryngeal muscles producing a strained, strangled, or harsh voice quality. Caused by HYPERADDUCTION (vocal folds closing too tightly). Does NOT recover with voice therapy alone. Treatment options: BOTULINUM TOXIN (Botox) injection to weaken or temporarily paralyze the overactive vocal fold muscles, or surgical severing of the recurrent laryngeal nerve.
glossary.source: Lecture Ch. 13, Kidwell / Ch. 13 PPTX

glossary.def: Abnormal excessive muscle tone that interferes with normal relaxed movement of the limbs and body.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: Significant receptive and/or expressive language impairments that CANNOT be attributed to any general or specific cause or condition. SLI is a diagnosis of exclusion — the child has: (1) hearing within normal limits; (2) no obvious perceptual or neurological disorders; (3) nonverbal intelligence within normal limits. SLI is characterized more by the ABSENCE of other disorders than by a clearly identifiable set of observable traits. Present in 7–8% of children starting school (2% more prevalent in boys than girls). The most frequent reason for administering early intervention and special education services to preschool-age and early primary school-age children.
glossary.source: Lecture Ch. 7, Kidwell / Ch. 7 slides

glossary.def: A mode of communication and a motor act of producing oral language. One of the most complicated fine motor skills humans learn. Involves four coordinated processes: respiration, phonation, resonance, and articulation.
glossary.source: Lecture 1.2.1 — Chapter 1, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.term: Speech and Language Disorders from TBI
glossary.def: TBI may produce multiple speech and language disorders — though it can be difficult to determine whether difficulties are due to cognitive/attention/memory problems or true language deficits. Commonly seen: APRAXIA (motor planning disorder), DYSARTHRIA (motor execution disorder), RECEPTIVE language deficits, EXPRESSIVE language deficits (including anomia), PRAGMATIC deficits (especially with frontal lobe injuries), READING and WRITING impairments. SOCIAL (PRAGMATIC) COMMUNICATION DISORDER (SCD): difficulty using and interpreting gestures, facial expressions, speech prosody, and emotions of others; difficulty interpreting abstract language; difficulty following communication rules; difficulty recognizing one's own social communication impairments; possible Theory of Mind deficits.
glossary.source: Ch. 16 PPTX; Kidwell lecture notes

glossary.def: A key component of audiological assessment that uses auditory signals present in everyday communication.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: Per ASHA (2007), CAS characteristics include: (1) Inconsistent errors on consonants and vowels; (2) Lengthened and disrupted coarticulatory transitions between sounds and syllables; (3) Inappropriate prosody (dysprosody); (4) Vowel errors; (5) Increasing errors with increasing length/complexity of utterance; (6) High percentage of omission errors; (7) Groping/silent posturing behaviors; (8) Lack of progress in traditional articulation therapy; (9) Frequent regression.
glossary.source: Lecture Ch. 6, Kidwell / Ch. 6 PPTX

glossary.def: The progressive evolving and shaping of individual sounds and syllables that are used as arbitrary symbols and applied in rule-governed combinations to produce words to communicate a person's wants, needs, thoughts, knowledge, and feelings. Divided into 4 stages: Birth–12 months, 12–24 months, 2–5 years, 6–12 years.
glossary.source: Lecture 3.2 — Ch. 4, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def: Any deviation or abnormality of speech outside the range of acceptable variation in a given environment.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: A licensed professional who identifies, evaluates, diagnoses, and treats communication and swallowing disorders. Requires a Master's degree in Communication Disorders (or Ph.D.), passage of a national exam, and state licensure. Works in schools, hospitals, clinics, private practice, and universities.
glossary.source: Lecture 1.2.2 — Chapter 2: The Professionals, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def: The physiological healing of the brain's damaged tissue, including reduction of cerebral edema, development of collateral blood vessels, and rerouting of neural pathways.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: Whether a child can produce an error sound correctly when it is modeled for them or when they receive instructions on how to produce it. A child is 'stimulable' for a sound if they can produce it in isolation after a model — even if they cannot produce it correctly in words. Stimulability is a positive prognostic indicator for therapy success. Example: a child says 'wat' for 'rat' but can produce /r/ correctly in isolation after demonstration.
glossary.source: Lecture 4.2, Kidwell, SJDC

glossary.def: An opening about the diameter of a finger or thumb made surgically into the neck that allows a person to breathe directly through the trachea.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: Disruption of blood flow to the brain, preventing oxygenated blood and nutrients from reaching brain areas. The second most common cause of death in the world and the most common cause of aphasia in older adults. Men have a higher risk but women are more likely to die from a stroke. Types: OCCLUSIVE (ISCHEMIC) stroke — blocked artery; CEREBRAL THROMBOSIS — artery blocked by debris (atherosclerosis); CEREBRAL EMBOLISM — blood clot lodged in a cerebral blood vessel; CEREBRAL HEMORRHAGE — ruptured blood vessel, most common in those with chronic hypertension. Time is critical — use F.A.S.T. (Face, Arms, Speech, Time).
glossary.source: Ch. 15 PPTX; Kidwell lecture notes

glossary.def:
No single theory explains everything; significant expert disagreement. Three main theoretical categories:
1. BREAKDOWN THEORIES: early environmental stress combined with neurologic predisposition accounts for stuttering
2. REPRESSED NEED THEORIES: stuttering stems from unmet/repressed emotional needs (Kidwell notes this as 'unfounded and antiquated')
3. ANTICIPATORY STRUGGLE BEHAVIOR THEORIES: stuttering results from reactions to penalties applied to what would otherwise be normal disfluencies — fearing and struggling to avoid stuttering CREATES it

★ Kidwell's view: MULTIFACTORIAL is most productive. Believes: neurological mistiming + genetic tendency → stuttering appears under stress → anxiety about stuttering causes avoidance → leads to overt struggle behaviors → more anxiety → more stuttering (vicious cycle).
glossary.source: Lecture Ch. 12, Kidwell, SJDC

glossary.def:
Stuttering is caused by a combination of factors (no single cause is established). Four contributing factors:
1. GENETICS: stuttering runs in families; most children who stutter have a family member who also stutters or stuttered as a child.
2. DEVELOPMENTAL FACTORS: during the preschool years, physical, cognitive, social/emotional, and speech/language skills are all developing rapidly; this rapid development can trigger stuttering in predisposed children — which is why onset most commonly occurs during the preschool years.
3. ENVIRONMENTAL FACTORS: parental attitudes and expectations, the child's speech/language environment, and stressful life events can aggravate stuttering in a child predisposed to it. (These same factors are not harmful to children without a tendency to stutter.)
4. FEAR AND ANXIETY: fear of stuttering can cause it to continue and even worsen in children.
glossary.source: 65_Cause_Treatment_of_Stuttering.pdf — Ducworth, CCC-SLP

glossary.def:
• Affects approximately 1% of the population
• In 90% of those affected, onset is early — typically around 2–6 years of age
• At onset: slightly more common in boys
• By school age: girls more likely to recover naturally; male-to-female ratio becomes approximately 3–4:1 (males outnumber females)
• Tends to RUN IN FAMILIES (genetic component)
• Environmental factors also play a role
• Often co-occurs with other communication disorders, but can be the only disorder
• Stutterers are within normal limits for intelligence
• Do NOT share a specific personality trait — not a symptom of a personality disorder
• Brain function during stuttering differs from brain function when not stuttering (cause vs. compensation unclear)
glossary.source: Lecture Ch. 12, Kidwell, SJDC
