glossary.def: An abnormally high frequency or duration of stoppages in the forward flow of speech affecting its continuity, rhythm, rate, and effortfulness (Guitar, 2014; Fogle). Also known as stammering or disfluency. The most commonly thought-of speech disorder. A complete definition of stuttering must include both OVERT BEHAVIORS (audible and visible characteristics — what we can hear and see) and COVERT REACTIONS (feelings, beliefs, thoughts). Affects approximately 1% of the population. The SLP cannot change the cause of stuttering but can work with the child, parents, and caregivers. Person-first language preferred: 'a person who stutters.'
glossary.source: Fluency_Disorders.pdf / Lecture Ch. 12, Kidwell

glossary.def: A clinical guideline for identifying possible stuttering in children: stuttering on MORE THAN 10 WORDS OUT OF 100 may indicate the child is having a problem. However, this is not a standalone criterion — other factors must also be considered (severity of disfluency, secondary behaviors, family history, impact on communication). Only a speech-language pathologist trained in the diagnosis and treatment of stuttering is qualified to make a formal diagnosis.
glossary.source: 65_Cause_Treatment_of_Stuttering.pdf — Ducworth, CCC-SLP

glossary.def: A therapy approach for children and adult stutterers that requires the speaker to recognize and confront his fears, avoidances, and struggles to escape his stuttering, and the speaker reducing and managing those fears, avoidances, and struggles.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: A therapy approach focused on reducing the negative forces that drive stuttering — fear, avoidance, and escape behaviors — rather than on producing perfectly fluent speech. The goal is COMMUNICATION IMPROVEMENT, and spontaneous fluency emerges as a byproduct of reduced fear and avoidance. THERAPY EMPHASIS: developing insight into attitudes, thoughts, and feelings about stuttering. Client actively participates in identifying and confronting avoidance patterns. Per Gregory (2003): four areas of emphasis — (1) insight into attitudes/thoughts/feelings, (2) awareness of muscular tension through relaxation, (3) speech analysis and modification, (4) building new speech skills.
glossary.source: Lecture Ch. 12, Kidwell / Fluency_Disorders.pdf

glossary.def: The most common formal assessment tool for measuring stuttering. Takes into account four factors: (1) types of disfluencies, (2) rate of disfluencies, (3) physical behaviors (visible overt behaviors), and (4) naturalness of speech. Results place the individual in a range from normal disfluencies to severe stuttering. Used to determine need for therapy, intensity of treatment, and to monitor progress.
glossary.source: Lecture Ch. 12, Kidwell, SJDC

glossary.def:
Working with adolescents and adults who stutter requires specific considerations:
• INTERNAL MOTIVATION is essential — the stutterer must want to change
• Emphasis is on the stutterer's own perception of the problem
• Strong emotions and opinions may exist due to past therapy experiences
• CHANGE (evolution, revolution, resolution) is required for success
• Many stutterers do not complete the full duration of therapy; those who do have a good chance of long-lasting benefits
Per Gregory (2003): four therapy emphasis areas for adolescents/adults:
1. Insight into attitudes, thoughts, and feelings about stuttering
2. Increasing awareness of muscular tension through relaxation exercises
3. Speech analysis and modification
4. Building new speech skills
glossary.source: Lecture Ch. 12, Kidwell, SJDC

glossary.term: Stuttering Therapy — Children (Prevention and Direct Therapy)
glossary.def:
Not all children who are disfluent need direct therapy. For at-risk children:
• SLPs focus on PREVENTION — modifying exposure conditions, changing susceptibility
• Parents and teachers are given verbal and written strategies to improve speech fluency and decrease negative behaviors/emotions
• The STUTTERING FOUNDATION OF AMERICA provides resources and strategies (stutteringhelp.org)

When direct therapy is needed:
• Goals created based on child's individual needs and behaviors
• Open child-clinician relationship essential — building TRUST and CONFIDENCE
• Clinician must be understanding and accepting
• Show parents how to help at home
• Build self-confidence throughout therapy
• Providing SUCCESSFUL SPEAKING ACTIVITIES and OPPORTUNITIES is helpful
glossary.source: Lecture Ch. 12, Kidwell / Fluency_Disorders.pdf

glossary.def: Air pressure that builds up below the closed (adducted) vocal folds. When sufficient pressure builds, it forces the vocal folds open. Bernoulli's law then causes them to snap back shut, producing the vibration cycle that creates voice.
glossary.source: Lecture 2.2 — Ch. 3, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def: An articulation error in which one sound is replaced by another. The most common type of articulation error. Example: 'tat' for 'cat' (/t/ substituted for /k/ in initial position). In IPA notation written as: initial /t/ for initial /k/.
glossary.source: Lecture 4.2, Kidwell, SJDC

glossary.def: A behavioral therapy technique in which attempts toward a target behavior (e.g., a word) are reinforced and gradually shaped toward the correct form. Example: 'poon' → 'spoon' — each closer attempt is reinforced. Used in SLP behavioral therapy.
glossary.source: Ch. 4 PDF — Prof. Handy, SJDC

glossary.def: Stress, timing, emphasis, and tone features of spoken language that span across individual sounds and words. Work together with prosody to convey the true intent and emotional meaning of a spoken message.
glossary.source: Lecture 1.2.1 — Chapter 1, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def: The junction between two neurons where a nerve impulse passes from one cell to another via neurotransmitters. Articulation therapy works by causing changes in neuronal firing and synaptic connections — essentially retraining the brain.
glossary.source: CSD Ch3 PP (Fogle 3rd ed.)

glossary.def: The study of the rules for combining words into sentences to convey meaning. Different languages use different structural rules. Example: 'I am going to the store' vs. 'I am coming from the store' — one or two word changes significantly alters meaning. Non-native English speakers often struggle with syntax.
glossary.source: Lecture 1.2.1 — Chapter 1, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def: Syntax: the rules for combining words into grammatically correct sentences. Morphology: the rules for modifying words using grammatical markers (prefixes, suffixes, inflections). Children with SLI show: incorrect sentence order ('Car fast go'), missing or incorrect morphological markers ('He walking', 'Those childs goed'), omission of auxiliaries ('is', 'am', 'are'), and inability to use complex sentence structures. These errors persist into school age and affect reading and writing.
glossary.source: Lecture Ch. 7, Kidwell, SJDC

glossary.def: Comprehensive assessment of cognitive-communication impairments secondary to TBI includes: ATTENTION and ORIENTATION; MEMORY (working, recent, and long-term/remote); PLANNING and ORGANIZING; ATTENTION TO DETAILS; RECEPTIVE and EXPRESSIVE LANGUAGE; HEARING (if hearing loss suspected); SPEECH SYSTEMS (respiratory, phonatory, resonatory, articulatory) and MOTOR SPEECH if a motor speech disorder is detected; SWALLOWING if a disorder is present or requested; REASONING and PROBLEM-SOLVING for ADLs. Assessment and treatment must take place in REAL-LIFE SITUATIONS including with family and friends. Standardized tools: BTHI, FAVRES, RIPA-2, SCATBI.
glossary.source: Ch. 16 PPTX; Kidwell lecture notes

glossary.def: GOALS of cognitive rehabilitation: improve physical, cognitive, and psychosocial functioning; foster independence; facilitate COMMUNITY INTEGRATION; help progress to the most independent level of functioning possible. ASHA general treatment targets: sensory stimulation responses; family education and interaction training; maintaining attention for basic activities; reducing confusion; ORIENTATION x4 (person, place, time, purpose); improving memory (e.g., memory log); improving problem-solving, reasoning, and organizational skills; social skills in small groups; self-monitoring in hospital/home/community; community outings; vocational rehabilitation. Computer-assisted treatment (CAT) may also be used.
glossary.source: Ch. 16 PPTX; Kidwell lecture notes

glossary.def: A collaborative model of treatment for communication disorders involving multiple professionals and family members. The most important team members are the client and, when applicable, their parent or caregiver.
glossary.source: Lecture 1.2.2 — Chapter 2: The Professionals, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def: Condensed language in which only the essential words are used — typically nouns, verbs, and adjectives — with function words (articles, prepositions) omitted. Example: 'daddy go store' instead of 'Daddy is going to the store.' Characteristic of 2–3 year olds.
glossary.source: Ch. 4 PDF — Prof. Handy / Quiz answer key

glossary.def: TELEGRAPHIC SPEECH: speech condensed to only the most essential content words (nouns, verbs), with functor words (articles a/an/the, prepositions, conjunctions) omitted — like the content of a telegram. Associated with Broca's aphasia. AGRAMMATISM: impaired syntax — difficulty formulating grammatically correct sentences; word order may be wrong, morphological markers omitted. FUNCTOR WORDS (noncontent words): grammatical function words such as 'the,' 'of,' 'was,' 'and' — tend to be omitted in nonfluent aphasias.
glossary.source: Ch. 15 PPTX; Kidwell lecture notes

glossary.def: Responsible for auditory processing. Receives auditory information and sends it to Wernicke's area for language interpretation. Located on the sides of the brain, below the lateral sulcus.
glossary.source: Lecture 2.2 — Ch. 3, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def: The joint connecting the mandible (lower jaw) to the temporal bone of the skull. Allows the jaw to open, close, and move side to side. One of the key skeletal structures of articulation. Problems with the TMJ can affect jaw movement and speech production.
glossary.source: CSD Ch3 PP (Fogle 3rd ed.)

glossary.term: The Four Phases of the Normal Swallow
glossary.def: Each swallow has four phases mediated by the medulla in the brainstem (also involving cortical areas including Broca's area and the precentral gyrus). People swallow approximately 600 times per day (~220,000 per year). (1) ORAL PREPARATORY PHASE: All senses involved; food is chewed (mastication) and formed into a bolus; cheeks tighten to prevent food lodging in sulcus; soft palate drops against tongue base to prevent premature spillage into airway; tongue moves food between teeth. (2) ORAL PHASE: Tongue tip elevates to alveolar ridge; tongue sweeps bolus to posterior oral cavity; soft palate rises to contact posterior pharyngeal wall preventing nasal regurgitation. Takes ~1 second. Voluntary. (3) PHARYNGEAL PHASE: Four activities, all in ~1 second: peristalsis moves bolus down; larynx rises causing epiglottis to drop over airway opening; false and true vocal folds adduct tightly (2nd and 3rd protection from aspiration); upper esophageal sphincter (UEV) relaxes and opens. (4) ESOPHAGEAL PHASE: Bolus passes UEV; peristaltic squeezing carries bolus to lower esophageal valve (LEV); LEV opens briefly then closes to prevent gastric reflux. Takes 8-20 seconds.
glossary.source: Fogle, Ch. 18

glossary.def: The ability to imagine or anticipate the mental states of other people (beliefs, thoughts, intents, desires, feelings, etc.), which may be different from one's own. The ability to see the world from the perspective of other people.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: The upper part of the trunk of the body. Contains the lungs and heart — the principal organs of respiration. Protected by the rib cage, sternum, and vertebrae which form the skeletal framework of the respiratory system.
glossary.source: Lecture 2.2 — Ch. 3, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.term: Three Etiologies of Communication Disorders in Children
glossary.def:
The three general causes of communication disorders in children:
1. Normal Variation: ~70% of children are within normal range; 15% above average, 15% below. SLPs work with children significantly below the mean.
2. Environmental Problems: prenatal (substance abuse, syndromes, prematurity), perinatal (insufficient oxygen/hypoxia at birth), or postnatal (limited stimulation → fewer synaptic connections).
3. Physical Impairments or Neurologic Differences: congenital syndromes, sensory impairments, TBI, CP.
Most children with communication disorders have MULTIPLE causative factors (multifactorial). Single-factor causes are less common.
glossary.source: Lecture 4.2, Kidwell / Ch. 5 slides

glossary.term: Three Prerequisites for Normal Language Development
glossary.def: For language to develop typically, three elements must be integrated: (1) Biological structures and functions must develop within normal limits; (2) Cognitive functioning must be sufficient for language skills to emerge and be cultivated; (3) Social interaction with caregivers must be sufficient to encourage and reward communication attempts.
glossary.source: Lecture 3.2 — Ch. 4, Kidwell / Ch. 4 PDF — Prof. Handy

glossary.def:
1. INATTENTION: difficulty either in selecting what to attend to OR in keeping attention focused for as long as necessary to perform an age-appropriate task. Signs: difficulty concentrating, unrelated thoughts, problems focusing and sustaining attention, appears not to be listening, difficulty planning/organizing/completing tasks on time, problems learning new things, poor self-regulation of behavior.
2. IMPULSIVITY: difficulty properly controlling or regulating one's own behavior. Signs: difficulty thinking before acting (e.g., hitting when frustrated), problems waiting a turn.
3. HYPERACTIVITY: excesses of physical movement, especially those that are purposeless, poorly directed, or have a driven quality. Signs: unable to sit still, squirming, roaming, tapping, fidgety, bounces between activities, tries to do multiple things at once.
glossary.source: ADHD_APD.pdf / Ch. 9 PPTX / Lecture Ch. 9, Kidwell

glossary.def:
Three main categories of voice therapy (Fogle/Kidwell):
1. PHYSIOLOGIC VOICE THERAPY: directly alters the physiology of the vocal mechanism through exercise and manipulation of the respiratory, phonatory, and resonatory systems. Targets the underlying physical cause.
2. HYGIENIC VOICE THERAPY: behavioral approach to decrease VOCAL ABUSE. Often the FIRST STEP in many voice programs. Instills healthy vocal behaviors (hydration, rest). Helps the patient identify and eliminate abusive vocal behaviors (yelling, throat clearing, etc.).
3. SYMPTOMATIC VOICE THERAPY: modifies vocal SYMPTOMS using auditory feedback, counseling, and explaining the voice problem. Trains the patient to consciously decrease and then eliminate vocal abuses and misuses. Based on the premise that most voice disorders are caused by functional misuse/abuse.
glossary.source: Lecture Ch. 13, Kidwell / Ch. 13 PPTX

glossary.term: Three-Way Classification of Voice Disorders (Boone et al.)
glossary.def:
Boone, McFarlane, Von Berg, and Zraick (2014/2020) classify voice disorders into three categories:
1. FUNCTIONAL: faulty use or abuse of the vocal system — no structural or neurological cause
2. NEUROLOGICAL: due to muscle tone and control problems — associated with dysarthrias; affects innervation and muscle control of respiration and phonation
3. ORGANIC: physical abnormality in the larynx — structural changes to the vocal folds or surrounding tissue

Additional useful classifications:
• HYPERFUNCTION: excessive effort and tension on the vocal folds
• HYPOFUNCTION: poor laryngeal muscle tone (insufficient tension) → weak, breathy voice
• ACUTE vs. CHRONIC
glossary.source: Lecture Ch. 13, Kidwell / Ch. 13 PPTX

glossary.def: The largest cartilage of the larynx, commonly known as the 'voice box' or Adam's apple. Part of the laryngeal framework that houses and protects the vocal folds.
glossary.source: Lecture 2.2 — Ch. 3, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def: A subjective noise sensation, often described as a ringing, roaring, or swishing in the ear that may be heard in one or both ears; associated with a variety of hearing disorders in both adults and children.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: The primary articulator for speech. Has 3 biological functions: taste, mastication (chewing), and deglutition (swallowing). Has 8 pairs of muscles — 4 pairs position the tongue, 4 pairs shape it. Critical for sounds: th, t, d, k, g, sh.
glossary.source: Lecture 2.2 — Ch. 3, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.term: Tongue Muscle Pairs — Positioning vs. Shaping
glossary.def: The tongue has 8 pairs of muscles split into two functional groups: 4 pairs that POSITION the tongue (protrude, retract, elevate, depress, lateralize) and 4 pairs that SHAPE the tongue (round, flatten, groove, hump). Both groups must coordinate for precise articulation.
glossary.source: CSD Ch3 PP (Fogle 3rd ed.)

glossary.def: An abnormal swallowing pattern where the tongue thrusts or protrudes forward during swallowing (rather than sweeping back). Can affect both speech and dentition. Treated with myofunctional therapy, sometimes before orthodontic treatment.
glossary.source: Lecture 2.2 — Ch. 3, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def: The airway (windpipe) located below the vocal folds and above the lungs through which air passes during respiration and speech.
glossary.source: Eugene Speech Language Therapy — How We Speak (YouTube, 11xf89bXaCg)

glossary.def: The 'windpipe.' A tube composed of cartilaginous rings that extends from the larynx down to the lungs, where it divides (bifurcates) into the left and right bronchi. Carries air to and from the lungs.
glossary.source: Lecture 2.2 — Ch. 3, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def: A surgical procedure (tracheoesophageal puncture) in which an incision through the trachea and esophageal walls is created to fit a one-way plastic valve (prosthesis) that directs air from the trachea into the esophagus where it can reach the oral cavity and be articulated for speech.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: A nonfluent aphasia with lesion above and around Broca's area. Similar to Broca's aphasia EXCEPT repetition is intact (preserved). Features: nonfluent speech, difficulty spontaneously answering questions, mildly impaired auditory comprehension, but GOOD REPETITION of long utterances. Fluency: −, Comprehension: +, Repetition: +, Naming: −.
glossary.source: Ch. 15 PPTX; Kidwell lecture notes

glossary.def: A fluent aphasia similar to Wernicke's aphasia — impaired auditory comprehension and verbal expression — but with a KEY DIFFERENCE: good ability to repeat words and phrases (repetition is preserved). Patient may repeat questions rather than answer them (echolalia). Rare form of aphasia. Fluency: fluent (some sources list as nonfluent), Comprehension: −, Repetition: +, Naming: −.
glossary.source: Ch. 15 PPTX; Kidwell lecture notes

glossary.def: Voice modification services for transgender and gender-diverse individuals are within the SLP scope of practice. This is NOT a voice disorder — it is helping individuals achieve a voice that better matches their gender identity (transmasculine or transfeminine goals). Requires increased awareness and sensitivity. Standardized training on supporting gender-diverse individuals' communication in SLP graduate curriculum has not yet been developed.
glossary.source: Ch. 13 PPTX — Fogle/SJDC

glossary.def: An episode of cerebral vascular insufficiency with partial occlusion of a cerebral artery by atherosclerotic plaque or an embolus. Disturbances of vision in one or both eyes, dizziness, weakness, numbness, dysphasia, or unconsciousness may occur. TIA usually lasts a few minutes to one hour; beyond one hour it is not considered a TIA but a stroke.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: An acquired brain injury; the most common cause of neurologic disability among people under 50 years old. One-third of survivors have permanent disabilities. Even mild TBI can cause long-lasting damage. Recovery from coma is gradual — not sudden as portrayed in TV/movies — and involves a slow regaining of awareness, feeling, emotions, and the ability to think and learn. The ability to follow simple commands (e.g., 'stick out your tongue') is a critical indicator of rehab potential.
glossary.source: Ch. 15 PPTX; Kidwell lecture notes

glossary.def: Muscles covered with mucous membranes located within the larynx. Biological function: close the airway to prevent food/liquid from entering the trachea. Overlaid function: vibrate to produce voice. Must abduct (open) for breathing and adduct (close) to phonate.
glossary.source: Lecture 2.2 — Ch. 3, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def: A word that meets all three of Locke's (1993) criteria: (1) uttered with clear intention and purpose, (2) recognizably close to an adult's pronunciation of the word, and (3) used consistently by a child in various contexts. First true words emerge around 12 months.
glossary.source: Lecture 3.2 — Ch. 4, Kidwell / Ch. 4 PDF — Prof. Handy

glossary.def: Any abnormal growth of new tissue, either benign or malignant, caused by aberrant increases in the rate of cell reproduction.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: A thin, semitransparent membrane that separates the ear canal from the middle ear and transmits sound vibrations into the middle ear.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: The small, fleshy projection hanging at the posterior end of the soft palate. Moves with the soft palate during speech and swallowing.
glossary.source: Lecture 2.2 — Ch. 3, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def: The movement of the soft palate upward and backward to contact the posterior pharyngeal wall, separating the oral cavity from the nasal cavity during speech. Essential for producing non-nasal sounds. Insufficient closure (e.g., cleft palate) causes hypernasality.
glossary.source: Lecture 2.2 — Ch. 3, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def: A condition where the velopharyngeal valve does not close consistently and/or completely during the production of oral sounds. VPD is a general term that may be used when etiology is not certain or is not specified.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: Neuromotor or physiological disorders that result in poor movement of the velopharyngeal structures.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: An anatomical or structural defect that prevents adequate velopharyngeal closure, including a short velum, and is the most common type of velopharyngeal dysfunction.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: Unintended substitution of one word for another word that is often semantically related to the intended word (e.g., sister for brother and cat for dog); often occur in both speech and writing.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: Communication that uses spoken language — the words and sentences we hear and produce. Speech and language skills form the foundation for reading and writing, which are higher-level language skills.
glossary.source: Lecture 1.2.1 — Chapter 1, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def: The body's system for maintaining balance and spatial orientation, controlled by the inner ear. Audiologists identify, evaluate, diagnose, and treat disorders of vestibular function in addition to hearing disorders.
glossary.source: Lecture 1.2.2 — Chapter 2: The Professionals, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def: The inner ear structures associated with balance and position sense, including the vestibule and semicircular canals of the vestibular mechanism, with interactions of the visual and proprioceptive systems and connection to the cerebellum.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: Harmful acute or chronic vocal behaviors, such as excessive yelling, screaming, cheering, coughing, and hard glottal attacks that are damaging to the vocal folds and the laryngeal and pharyngeal muscles and tissues.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: Unilateral or bilateral loss of laryngeal movements (including vocal fold opening or closing) that may be caused by damage to the brainstem, vagus (X cranial) nerves, recurrent laryngeal nerves, or the neuromuscular junctions, resulting in a weak, breathy voice.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: A pair of tissue structures located above the trachea and lungs. When pushed together and subjected to airflow from the lungs, they vibrate rapidly to produce sound. Also called vocal cords.
glossary.source: Eugene Speech Language Therapy — How We Speak (YouTube, 11xf89bXaCg)

glossary.def: Behaviors that are helpful to achieve and maintain a healthy vocal mechanism and prevent or decrease vocal pathologies.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: Harmful chronic vocal behaviors that may have a cumulative damaging effect on the structure and functioning of the laryngeal mechanism, such as chronic inappropriate loudness or pitch, singing beyond the range of the vocal mechanism, and frequent hard glottal attacks.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: Benign, often BILATERAL vocal fold lesions caused by abuse and misuse of the voice (hyperfunction). Also called 'cheerleaders' nodes,' 'screamers' nodes,' or 'singers' nodes.' Common causes: yelling, talking at too high a pitch, frequent throat clearing, chronic vocal strain. The most common voice disorder in children aged 1–18 (~60% of cases). PREVENTABLE and TREATABLE: SLP helps the client identify and eliminate the abusive behaviors (vocal hygiene), which allows the nodules to heal and disappear. Medical clearance from an ENT is required before SLP treatment.
glossary.source: Lecture Ch. 13, Kidwell / Ch. 13 PPTX

glossary.def: Longer strings of syllables that extend babbling (e.g., baa-da-gi-daa-um-ma). Occurs during the 6–12 month period. The infant experiments with sounds and syllable combinations for social interaction and enjoyment.
glossary.source: Ch. 4 PDF — Prof. Handy, SJDC

glossary.def: Benign vocal fold lesions of various forms with the same causes as vocal nodules — repeated vocal hyperfunction and chronic misuse/abuse. Unlike nodules (which tend to be bilateral), polyps can vary in form and location. A common cause of voice disorders in adults aged 19–60 (12% of cases). GERD (gastroesophageal reflux disorder) can also cause acid laryngitis contributing to polyp formation. Like nodules, polyps are preventable and treatable through vocal hygiene and voice therapy with ENT clearance.
glossary.source: Lecture Ch. 13, Kidwell / Ch. 13 PPTX

glossary.def: A disorder in which voice characteristics (loudness, pitch, quality) fall outside the typical range for a person's age and gender. May make speaking uncomfortable for speaker or listener. Most commonly diagnosed as dysphonia — voice sounds rough, raspy, or hoarse.
glossary.source: Lecture 1.2.1 — Chapter 1, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def: A condition affecting the vocal folds and the quality, pitch, or loudness of voice. Can result from overuse or abuse of the vocal folds, surgery, accidents, or disease. Treated by speech-language pathologists.
glossary.source: Eugene Speech Language Therapy — How We Speak (YouTube, 11xf89bXaCg)

glossary.def:
Any deviation of LOUDNESS, PITCH, or QUALITY that is outside the normal range for a person's age, gender, or geographic/cultural background, AND that interferes with communication, draws unfavorable attention to itself, or adversely affects the speaker or the listener (Fogle). Voice is affected by the anatomy and physiology of the respiratory system, vocal folds, and supraglottal vocal tract (the structures ABOVE the glottis). Voice reflects personality, emotions, and attitudes — a disruption to voice must be treated.

Subjective voice quality terms: hoarse, breathy, harsh, husky, raucous, grating, strident, thin, strained, tense, weak, tremulous, monotone.
glossary.source: Ch. 13 PPTX / Lecture Ch. 13, Kidwell

glossary.term: Voice Disorder Causes by Age Group
glossary.def:
Most common voice disorder causes by age:
• CHILDREN (1–18 years): vocal NODULES (~60%), vocal cysts (~10%), acute laryngitis (~7%)
• ADULTS (19–60 years): functional dysphonia (20.5%), acid laryngitis from GERD (12.5%), vocal polyps (12%)
• ADULTS (60+ years): PRESBYPHONIA (26.5%), functional dysphonia (~17.5%), polypoid degeneration (14%)

Presbyphonia = age-related voice changes.
glossary.source: Ch. 13 PPTX — Fogle/SJDC

glossary.def:
Prevalence of voice disorders:
• Children: approximately 6%
• Individuals aged 0 to 65+: approximately 1%
• Females: ~1%; Males: ~0.5%
• Adults older than 70 years: 2.5%
Most frequent diagnoses: acute laryngitis, nonspecific dysphonia, benign vocal fold lesions (nodules, polyps, contact ulcers), chronic laryngitis. Laryngeal cancer ~2% (mostly males).
★ TEACHERS (including professors) are more likely to have a voice disorder than any other profession.
glossary.source: Ch. 13 PPTX — Fogle/SJDC

glossary.def: The subjective judgment of how a voice sounds. Affected by adequacy of vocal fold closure, efficiency of timing, and amount of tonicity in the folds. A normal voice is not hoarse, breathy, or choppy.
glossary.source: Lecture 2.2 — Ch. 3, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def: Whether the vocal folds are vibrating or not during sound production. Voiced sounds: vocal folds vibrate (e.g., /b/, /d/, /g/, /v/, /z/). Voiceless sounds: vocal folds do not vibrate (e.g., /p/, /t/, /k/, /f/, /s/). All vowels are voiced. Feeling throat vibration is a common way to detect voicing (though pairing with a vowel can be misleading).
glossary.source: Lecture 4.2, Kidwell, SJDC

glossary.def: Voiced speech sounds from the unrestricted passage of the air stream through the mouth without audible stoppage or friction.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

glossary.def: Voiced speech sounds produced by the unrestricted passage of the airstream through the mouth without audible stoppage or friction. All vowels are voiced. The placement and shape of the tongue determine the vowel sound. Described on the Vowel Quadrilateral by height (high/mid/low) and position (front/central/back). All vowels are voiced.
glossary.source: Ch. 5 slides — Fogle/SJDC / Lecture 4.2, Kidwell

glossary.def: A fluent aphasia caused by damage in or around Wernicke's area in the auditory association cortex of the left temporal lobe. Characteristics: impaired AUDITORY COMPREHENSION (cannot grasp meaning of spoken words), impaired verbal expression, impaired reading comprehension, impaired written language. Patient can speak fluently with normal rate and intonation but speech may not make sense; patient may not know their speech is wrong and get frustrated when misunderstood. Features include: literal and verbal paraphasias, neologisms, jargon, difficulty with yes/no questions, difficulty following rapid speech, lacking awareness of errors. Fluency: +, Comprehension: −, Repetition: −, Naming: −.
glossary.source: Ch. 15 PPTX; Kidwell lecture notes

glossary.def: Located in the temporal lobe of the left hemisphere. Involved in comprehension, integration, and formulation of language. Auditory information perceived in the temporal lobe is sent to Wernicke's area for interpretation. Damage causes Wernicke's (receptive/fluent) aphasia.
glossary.source: Lecture 2.2 — Ch. 3, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

glossary.def: The location within a word where an articulation error occurs. Initial position = beginning of the word (e.g., 'cat' → /k/ is initial). Medial position = middle of the word. Final position = end of the word. SLPs document whether a child consistently makes an error in one, two, or all three positions, which influences therapy targeting.
glossary.source: Lecture 4.2, Kidwell, SJDC

glossary.def: Temporary information storage that is limited in capacity and requires rehearsal; often thought of as 'what is on your mind' at any given moment.
glossary.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.front: What are the four core functions of both the SLP and Audiologist?
flashcards.back: IDENTIFY, EVALUATE, DIAGNOSE, and TREAT — disorders within their respective scopes of practice.
flashcards.source: Lecture 1.2.2 — Chapter 2: The Professionals, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

flashcards.front: What degree is required to become a licensed SLP?
flashcards.back: A Master's degree in Communication Disorders (or Ph.D.), plus passage of a national exam and state licensure.
flashcards.source: Lecture 1.2.2 — Chapter 2: The Professionals, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

flashcards.front: What degree is required to become an Audiologist?
flashcards.back: A Ph.D. or Au.D., plus a Clinical Fellowship Year, national exam, state licensure, and ongoing CEUs.
flashcards.source: Lecture 1.2.2 — Chapter 2: The Professionals, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

flashcards.front: What is the largest employment setting for SLPs?
flashcards.back: Public schools are the largest employer of SLPs.
flashcards.source: Lecture 1.2.2 — Chapter 2: The Professionals, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

flashcards.back: That all school-aged children with disabilities receive a free and appropriate public education (FAPE) in the least restrictive environment (LRE).
flashcards.source: Lecture 1.2.2 — Chapter 2: The Professionals, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

flashcards.front: Who can be traced as the origin of the SLP and Audiology professions?
flashcards.back: Alexander Graham Bell, whose father and grandfather were elocutionists in the 1860s and who became interested in people who were deaf and hard of hearing.
flashcards.source: Lecture 1.2.2 — Chapter 2: The Professionals, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

flashcards.front: What does ASHA stand for and what is its role?
flashcards.back: American Speech-Language-Hearing Association. It is the primary professional organization for SLPs and Audiologists in the U.S., establishing the Scope of Practice, Code of Ethics, and maintaining CCC-SLP and CCC-A credentials.
flashcards.source: Lecture 1.2.2 — Chapter 2: The Professionals, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

flashcards.front: What is an SLPA and who supervises them?
flashcards.back: A Speech-Language Pathology Assistant — support personnel who perform tasks prescribed, directed, and supervised by ASHA-certified SLPs. They earn an A.S. degree.
flashcards.source: Lecture 1.2.2 — Chapter 2: The Professionals, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

flashcards.front: What are the six alternative titles used for SLPs?
flashcards.back: Speech-language pathologist, speech-language therapist, speech therapist, speech teacher, speech and hearing specialist, language speech and hearing specialist.
flashcards.source: Lecture 1.2.2 — Chapter 2: The Professionals, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

flashcards.front: What is the student version of ASHA called?
flashcards.back: NSSLHA — National Student Speech Language Hearing Association (www.nsslha.org).
flashcards.source: Lecture 1.2.2 — Chapter 2: The Professionals, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

flashcards.front: What consequences can result from a Code of Ethics violation?
flashcards.back: Loss of ASHA certification or state licensure.
flashcards.source: Lecture 1.2.2 — Chapter 2: The Professionals, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

flashcards.front: What work settings do both SLPs and Audiologists share?
flashcards.back: Public schools, hospitals (acute, subacute, convalescent), inpatient/outpatient clinics, private practices, and universities.
flashcards.source: Lecture 1.2.2 — Chapter 2: The Professionals, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

flashcards.front: In the team approach to treatment, who is considered the most important team member?
flashcards.back: The client themselves, and their parent if applicable.
flashcards.source: Lecture 1.2.2 — Chapter 2: The Professionals, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

flashcards.front: What are the five components of linguistics?
flashcards.back: Phonology, Morphology, Syntax, Semantics, and Pragmatics.
flashcards.source: Lecture 1.2.1 — Chapter 1, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

flashcards.front: What are the four processes of speech production?
flashcards.back: Respiration, Phonation, Resonance, and Articulation — they must all be coordinated.
flashcards.source: Lecture 1.2.1 — Chapter 1, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

flashcards.front: What is the difference between prevalence and incidence?
flashcards.back: Prevalence = number of individuals diagnosed with a disorder at a given time. Incidence = rate at which a disorder appears in a population over a period of time.
flashcards.source: Lecture 1.2.1 — Chapter 1, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

flashcards.front: What is the difference between a speech disorder and a language disorder?
flashcards.back: Speech disorders involve the actual sounds a person produces (e.g., articulation). Language disorders involve the content, form, or use of language (e.g., vocabulary, following directions). Example: trouble with 'r' = speech; trouble following directions = language.
flashcards.source: Lecture 1.2.1 — Chapter 1, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

flashcards.front: What is a phoneme, and how is it notated?
flashcards.back: The smallest unit of sound that is distinct from others. Written in slash notation: /b/, /s/, /æ/. Not the same as a letter — e.g., 'c' is either /k/ or /s/.
flashcards.source: Lecture 1.2.1 — Chapter 1, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

flashcards.front: What is a morpheme? Give an example.
flashcards.back: The smallest unit of language that conveys meaning. Example: in 'walked', the '-ed' is a morpheme signaling past tense. 'Dislike' has two morphemes: 'dis-' (prefix) + 'like'.
flashcards.source: Lecture 1.2.1 — Chapter 1, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

flashcards.front: How did Van Riper (1978) define a communication disorder?
flashcards.back: Communication that deviates from that of others, calls attention to itself, interferes with communication, and causes distress for the speaker and/or listener.
flashcards.source: Lecture 1.2.1 — Chapter 1, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

flashcards.front: What are the two main categories of language delays in children?
flashcards.back: Receptive language delays (understanding what is said) and expressive language delays (producing verbal communication).
flashcards.source: Lecture 1.2.1 — Chapter 1, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

flashcards.front: What are the two main types of hearing loss?
flashcards.back: Conductive (poor conduction through outer/middle ear) and sensorineural (cochlea and/or auditory nerve damage). A mixed loss involves both types.
flashcards.source: Lecture 1.2.1 — Chapter 1, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

flashcards.front: What is the difference between hypernasality and hyponasality?
flashcards.back: Hypernasality = too much nasal airflow, voice sounds like talking through the nose. Hyponasality = too little nasal airflow, nasal sounds lack normal resonance.
flashcards.source: Lecture 1.2.1 — Chapter 1, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

flashcards.front: What are prosody and suprasegmentals, and why are they important?
flashcards.back: Prosody = the melody/rhythm of speech (stress, pitch, timing). Suprasegmentals = features of stress, emphasis, and tone that span across words. Together they convey emotional intent and meaning beyond the literal words — e.g., you can hear if Mom is happy or angry on the phone.
flashcards.source: Lecture 1.2.1 — Chapter 1, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

flashcards.front: What population is most associated with pragmatic language difficulties?
flashcards.back: Children and adults on the Autism Spectrum — social language does not 'click' naturally for them.
flashcards.source: Lecture 1.2.1 — Chapter 1, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

flashcards.front: What are the eight categories of speech and language disorders?
flashcards.back: Articulation, Language, Fluency, Voice, Resonance, Cognition, Literacy, and Hearing. Note: swallowing disorders are a major SLP area but are NOT classified as communication disorders.
flashcards.source: Lecture 1.2.1 — Chapter 1, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

flashcards.front: What is disfluency and what are its main features?
flashcards.back: A fluency disorder (preferred term over 'stuttering') involving: unusual repetitions of sounds/syllables/words/phrases, prolongations of sounds, abnormal silent blocks, visible tension, and struggle behaviors.
flashcards.source: Lecture 1.2.1 — Chapter 1, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

flashcards.front: Why is hearing critical to speech and language development?
flashcards.back: Normal hearing is essential for normal speech and language development. If speech sounds are not perceived normally during developmental stages, children may not understand others' speech or develop their own speech correctly.
flashcards.source: Lecture 1.2.1 — Chapter 1, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

flashcards.front: What are the four processes of speech production in order?
flashcards.back: Respiration → Phonation → Resonance → Articulation
flashcards.source: Eugene Speech Language Therapy — How We Speak (YouTube, 11xf89bXaCg)

flashcards.front: What is the inhale-to-exhale ratio at rest vs. during speech?
flashcards.back: At rest: ~50/50 (or 40/60). During speech: 10% inhale / 90% exhale.
flashcards.source: Eugene Speech Language Therapy — How We Speak (YouTube, 11xf89bXaCg)

flashcards.front: Why does exhalation become active during speech?
flashcards.back: The diaphragm pushes upward and abdominal muscles contract to force air out, supporting the long exhalations needed for speech and singing.
flashcards.source: Eugene Speech Language Therapy — How We Speak (YouTube, 11xf89bXaCg)

flashcards.front: Where are the vocal folds located?
flashcards.back: Above the trachea, which is above the lungs.
flashcards.source: Eugene Speech Language Therapy — How We Speak (YouTube, 11xf89bXaCg)

flashcards.front: What are the three resonating cavities involved in speech?
flashcards.back: The throat (pharyngeal cavity), the mouth (oral cavity), and the nose (nasal cavity).
flashcards.source: Eugene Speech Language Therapy — How We Speak (YouTube, 11xf89bXaCg)

flashcards.back: The tongue. Other articulators include the lips, teeth, cheeks, and palate.
flashcards.source: Eugene Speech Language Therapy — How We Speak (YouTube, 11xf89bXaCg)

flashcards.front: What role does an SLP play in respiration?
flashcards.back: SLPs work with clients to strengthen respiratory muscles to increase speech loudness.
flashcards.source: Eugene Speech Language Therapy — How We Speak (YouTube, 11xf89bXaCg)

flashcards.back: Overuse or abuse of the vocal folds, surgery, accidents, or disease.
flashcards.source: Eugene Speech Language Therapy — How We Speak (YouTube, 11xf89bXaCg)

flashcards.front: What happens in the brain before speech production begins?
flashcards.back: The brain determines what it wants to say and creates a motor plan before the four processes are executed.
flashcards.source: Eugene Speech Language Therapy — How We Speak (YouTube, 11xf89bXaCg)

flashcards.front: What is phonation and where does it occur?
flashcards.back: Phonation is voicing — the vibration of the vocal folds located above the trachea. Air from the lungs forces the folds open, causing rapid vibration that creates sound.
flashcards.source: Eugene Speech Language Therapy — How We Speak (YouTube, 11xf89bXaCg)

flashcards.front: What is the difference between biological function and overlaid function? Give one example of each for the lips.
flashcards.back: Biological function = the life-sustaining primary purpose. Overlaid function = the adapted use for communication. Lips: biological = hold food/liquid in the oral cavity; overlaid = shape speech sounds (e.g., /p/, /b/, /m/).
flashcards.source: Lecture 2.2 — Ch. 3, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

flashcards.front: What is the inhalation-to-exhalation ratio at rest vs. during speech?
flashcards.back: At rest: ~50/50 (or 40/60). During speech: 10% inhalation / 90% exhalation — a quick short breath in followed by a long sustained breath out.
flashcards.source: Lecture 2.2 — Ch. 3, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

flashcards.front: What are the four cartilages that make up the larynx?
flashcards.back: Cricoid cartilage, thyroid cartilage ('voice box'), two arytenoid cartilages, and the epiglottis.
flashcards.source: Lecture 2.2 — Ch. 3, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

flashcards.front: What is the biological function of the true vocal folds vs. the overlaid function?
flashcards.back: Biological: close the airway to prevent food/liquid from entering the trachea (airway protection). Overlaid: vibrate to produce voice.
flashcards.source: Lecture 2.2 — Ch. 3, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

flashcards.front: What is the difference between frequency and pitch? Between intensity and loudness?
flashcards.back: Frequency = objective cycles/sec (Hz); Pitch = subjective perception of frequency. Intensity = objective physical force (dB); Loudness = subjective perception of intensity.
flashcards.source: Lecture 2.2 — Ch. 3, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

flashcards.front: What is velopharyngeal closure and why does it matter for SLPs?
flashcards.back: The soft palate rising and moving posteriorly to contact the pharyngeal wall, separating the oral and nasal cavities during speech. Without it (e.g., cleft palate), air leaks into the nasal cavity causing hypernasality.
flashcards.source: Lecture 2.2 — Ch. 3, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

flashcards.front: What is the primary articulator, and what are its three biological functions?
flashcards.back: The tongue is the primary articulator. Three biological functions: taste, mastication (chewing), and deglutition (swallowing).
flashcards.source: Lecture 2.2 — Ch. 3, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

flashcards.front: What are the six movements the lips produce for speech?
flashcards.back: Opening, closing, rounding, flattening, protruding, and retracting.
flashcards.source: Lecture 2.2 — Ch. 3, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

flashcards.front: What structures make up the CNS vs. the PNS?
flashcards.back: CNS: brain, cerebellum, brainstem, and spinal cord. PNS: cranial nerves (from brainstem), spinal nerves (from spinal cord), and sensory nerves throughout the body.
flashcards.source: Lecture 2.2 — Ch. 3, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

flashcards.front: What are the primary functions of the left hemisphere vs. the right hemisphere?
flashcards.back: Left: dominant for receptive and expressive language/speech; processes rapidly changing info sequentially; controls right body movement. Right: attention, orientation, emotions, cognition; controls left body movement.
flashcards.source: Lecture 2.2 — Ch. 3, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

flashcards.front: Where is Broca's area located and what does it do?
flashcards.back: Lower posterior portion of the left hemisphere's frontal lobe. Plans, sequences, coordinates, and initiates motor movements of the articulators for speech.
flashcards.source: Lecture 2.2 — Ch. 3, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

flashcards.front: Where is Wernicke's area and what does it do?
flashcards.back: Located in the temporal lobe of the left hemisphere. Responsible for comprehension, integration, and formulation of language. Auditory info is perceived in the temporal lobe and sent to Wernicke's area for interpretation.
flashcards.source: Lecture 2.2 — Ch. 3, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

flashcards.front: What are the four lobes of the brain and their primary functions?
flashcards.back: Frontal: cognition, reasoning, judgment, Broca's area. Temporal: auditory processing, Wernicke's area. Parietal: body sensation. Occipital: visual processing.
flashcards.source: Lecture 2.2 — Ch. 3, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

flashcards.front: Which seven cranial nerves are most important for speech?
flashcards.back: V (Trigeminal), VII (Facial), VIII (Vestibulocochlear/Auditory), IX (Glossopharyngeal), X (Vagus), XI (Accessory), XII (Hypoglossal).
flashcards.source: Lecture 2.2 — Ch. 3, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

flashcards.front: When do primary teeth erupt, and when do permanent teeth erupt?
flashcards.back: Primary (baby) teeth: begin erupting 6–9 months; all 20 erupted by 18–24 months. Permanent teeth: 32 total, erupt between 6–13 years of age.
flashcards.source: Lecture 2.2 — Ch. 3, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

flashcards.front: What is the primary muscle of respiration, and is it under conscious control?
flashcards.back: The diaphragm. It is NOT under conscious control — it contracts and lowers automatically during inhalation.
flashcards.source: Lecture 2.2 — Ch. 3, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

flashcards.front: What is the hard palate vs. soft palate — location and composition?
flashcards.back: Hard palate: bony anterior 2/3 of the roof of the mouth, within the maxilla. Soft palate (velum): muscular posterior 1/3, ending in the uvula. Hard = fixed; soft = moves during speech and swallowing.
flashcards.source: Lecture 2.2 — Ch. 3, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

flashcards.front: What role does the corpus callosum play in communication?
flashcards.back: It connects the left and right hemispheres, allowing them to work together to analyze information and produce a timely, coordinated response. Full language comprehension involves all lobes of both hemispheres.
flashcards.source: Lecture 2.2 — Ch. 3, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

flashcards.front: What is the average fundamental frequency (f0) for adult males vs. adult females?
flashcards.back: Adult males: approximately 120 Hz. Adult females: approximately 220 Hz. These are the baseline rates of vocal fold vibration during normal voicing.
flashcards.source: CSD Ch3 PP (Fogle 3rd ed.)

flashcards.front: What are the four tongue muscle pairs that POSITION the tongue vs. the four that SHAPE it?
flashcards.back: POSITION (4 pairs): protrude, retract, elevate, depress/lateralize. SHAPE (4 pairs): round, flatten, groove, hump. Both sets must coordinate for precise articulation.
flashcards.source: CSD Ch3 PP (Fogle 3rd ed.)

flashcards.front: What is intonation and how does it differ from pitch?
flashcards.back: Intonation = variations in pitch across syllables, words, and phrases to give stress, emphasis, and meaning. Pitch = the perceptual sensation of a single frequency. Intonation is the pattern of pitch changes across an utterance — it's how you convey questions, sarcasm, excitement, etc.
flashcards.source: CSD Ch3 PP (Fogle 3rd ed.)

flashcards.front: What is the neuron transmission sequence?
flashcards.back: Neuron → Axon → Dendrite → Neuron. Every thought, action, emotion, and memory results from electrochemical reactions along this pathway. The brain has ~100 billion neurons forming trillions of connections via synapses.
flashcards.source: CSD Ch3 PP (Fogle 3rd ed.)

flashcards.front: What percentage of the heart's blood output goes to the brain?
flashcards.back: 20–25% of blood pumped from the heart goes to the brain, despite the brain being only ~3 pounds. Disruption to this blood supply causes anoxia and brain tissue death.
flashcards.source: CSD Ch3 PP (Fogle 3rd ed.)

flashcards.front: What is Heschl's gyrus and where is it located?
flashcards.back: The primary auditory cortex, located in the temporal lobe. Perceives human and environmental sounds. Auditory input is then sent to Wernicke's area for language interpretation.
flashcards.source: CSD Ch3 PP (Fogle 3rd ed.)

flashcards.front: What are executive functions and which brain region governs them?
flashcards.back: Frontal lobes (prefrontal cortex). Executive functions include: anticipation, goal selection, planning, initiation of activity, self-regulation/self-monitoring, and using feedback to adjust future responses.
flashcards.source: CSD Ch3 PP (Fogle 3rd ed.)

flashcards.front: What is the difference between the premotor cortex / Broca's area and the motor cortex?
flashcards.back: Broca's area (premotor cortex) = PLANS, sequences, coordinates, and initiates motor speech movements. Motor cortex = EXECUTES — takes direction from the premotor cortex and carries neural impulses out to the muscles.
flashcards.source: CSD Ch3 PP (Fogle 3rd ed.)

flashcards.front: Video mnemonic: How do you remember that Wernicke's area handles vocabulary/comprehension?
flashcards.back: Wernicke (German name, pronounced with a 'V') = Vocabulary. Two V's match. Broca's area is then the other one = grammar and language production. Temporal lobe = listening/auditory processing → comprehension. Frontal lobe = planning → production.
flashcards.source: Language and the Brain video transcript (KAtalentsearch)

flashcards.front: During phonation, what shape do the vocal folds form when they blow open?
flashcards.back: A football shape — the anterior and posterior areas remain closed while the middle blows open, allowing a puff of air to escape. The folds then close again and the cycle repeats, producing the vibration that creates voice.
flashcards.source: CSD Ch3 PP (Fogle 3rd ed.)

flashcards.front: What are the soft palate positions for oral sounds vs. nasal sounds?
flashcards.back: Oral sounds: soft palate moves SUPERIORLY and POSTERIORLY (up and back) to contact the posterior pharyngeal wall → velopharyngeal closure → nasal cavity sealed off. Nasal sounds (/m/, /n/): soft palate stays DOWN → air passes through nasal cavity for resonance.
flashcards.source: CSD Ch3 PP (Fogle 3rd ed.)

flashcards.front: What is the TMJ and why does it matter for SLPs?
flashcards.back: The temporomandibular joint — connects the mandible to the temporal bone of the skull. Allows jaw movement (open, close, lateral). One of the key skeletal structures of articulation. TMJ dysfunction can restrict jaw range of motion and impair speech.
flashcards.source: CSD Ch3 PP (Fogle 3rd ed.)

flashcards.front: What are the four stages of speech and language development, with their age ranges?
flashcards.back:
Stage I: Birth–12 months (Infancy)
Stage II: 12–24 months (Toddlerhood)
Stage III: 2–5 years (Early Childhood)
Stage IV: 6–12 years (Middle Childhood to Early Adolescence)
Note: stages overlap — a child may begin the next stage while still finishing the previous one.
flashcards.source: Lecture 3.2 — Ch. 4, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

flashcards.front: What is the key difference between cooing and babbling?
flashcards.back: Cooing = vowel-like sounds only (/u/, /oo/) — NO consonant-vowel combination. Babbling = consonant AND vowel in the same syllable (ba-ba, da, ga). If there's no consonant in the syllable, it's cooing, not babbling.
flashcards.source: Lecture 3.2 — Ch. 4, Kidwell / Quiz answer key

flashcards.front: What are Locke's (1993) three criteria for a 'true word'?
flashcards.back:
(1) Uttered with clear intention and purpose
(2) Recognizably close to an adult's pronunciation
(3) Used consistently by the child in various contexts
All three must be met. If a caregiver cannot identify what word the child intends, it is not a true word.
flashcards.source: Lecture 3.2 — Ch. 4, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

flashcards.front: What are the three prerequisites for normal language development?
flashcards.back:
(1) Biological structures and functions must develop within normal limits
(2) Cognitive functioning must be sufficient for language to emerge
(3) Social interaction with caregivers must be sufficient to encourage and reward communication attempts
flashcards.source: Lecture 3.2 — Ch. 4, Kidwell / Ch. 4 PDF — Prof. Handy

flashcards.front: What are the four theories of language development and who is associated with the behavioral theory?
flashcards.back:
1. Behavioral Theory (B.F. Skinner) — operant conditioning, imitation, reinforcement
2. Nativist Theory — innate, prewired for language
3. Semantic-Cognitive Theory — meaning before form; language tied to cognition
4. Social-Pragmatic Theory — communication is the basic function; caregiver interaction essential
flashcards.source: Lecture 3.2 — Ch. 4, Kidwell / Ch. 4 PDF — Prof. Handy

flashcards.front: What is holophrastic language? Give an example.
flashcards.back: Use of a single word to express a complete thought. Example: saying 'juice?' with rising intonation to mean 'Can I have more juice please?' Characteristic of 12–18 month toddlers. Early word categories: nouns (ball, mommy), action words (go, bye-bye), modifiers (hot), personal-social words (no, please).
flashcards.source: Ch. 4 PDF — Prof. Handy / Quiz answer key

flashcards.front: What is MLU and what is it roughly equivalent to?
flashcards.back: Mean Length of Utterance — the average number of morphemes in a young child's individual utterances. Roughly equivalent to the child's chronological age. A 2-year-old has an MLU of approximately 2; a 3-year-old approximately 3, etc.
flashcards.source: Ch. 4 PDF — Prof. Handy / Quiz answer key

flashcards.front: What are the intelligibility benchmarks by age?
flashcards.back:
2 years: 75% intelligible (2–3 word sentences)
3 years: 90% intelligible (3–4 word sentences)
4 years: 95% intelligible
6–8 years: 95–100% intelligible
9–12 years: Speech fully mastered
flashcards.source: Lecture 3.2 — Ch. 4, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

flashcards.front: What is code-switching and is it a disorder?
flashcards.back: Code-switching is substituting sounds, words, or grammatical elements from one language into speech in another language. It is a NORMAL stage in bilingual language development — NOT a disorder. Example: 'I cayo here' (cayo = 'I fall' in Spanish).
flashcards.source: Lecture 3.2 — Ch. 4, Kidwell / Quiz answer key

flashcards.front: When should you be concerned about a baby's hearing based on prelinguistic development?
flashcards.back: If prelinguistic vocalizations (babbling, cooing) do not begin by 8 months. Babies with significant hearing loss will coo and may start to babble, then STOP — because they cannot hear themselves. Deaf babies stop vocalizing around 8 months when self-auditory feedback becomes the reinforcer.
flashcards.source: Lecture 3.2 — Ch. 4, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

flashcards.front: What speech sounds are produced by 90% of infants (0–12 months)?
flashcards.back:
Stops: /p, b, t, d, k, g/
Nasals: /m, n/
Glides: /w, j, hw/
Fricative: /s/
These are the earliest-emerging sound classes in speech development.
flashcards.source: Ch. 4 PDF — Prof. Handy, SJDC

flashcards.front: What sounds are established by age 3? What sounds emerge by age 5?
flashcards.back:
By age 3: /m, n, p, b, d, k, g, h, f, w/
By age 5: Most sounds emerged including /r, l, s, z, sh, ch, dz, j, v/, 'ng', 'th', and consonant clusters (blends). May not yet be consistent.
flashcards.source: Ch. 4 PDF — Prof. Handy, SJDC

flashcards.front: What is the clinical significance of language problems in school-age children (6–12 years)?
flashcards.back: Language IS the tool for learning at this age — language problems = learning problems. Communication abilities determine educational success, social success, and future employment. By 11–12 years, language delays affect both language demands AND academic (school) demands. Peak time for figurative language development.
flashcards.source: Ch. 4 PDF — Prof. Handy, SJDC

flashcards.front: What is the distinction between a language difference and a language disorder in bilingual/multilingual children?
flashcards.back: A language disorder must occur in the child's PRIMARY language (most proficient/used). Difficulty in a second language only is NOT a disorder. A child with a true language disorder will have difficulty in BOTH their primary language AND the second language. Dialects and accents are differences, never disorders.
flashcards.source: Lecture 3.2 — Ch. 4, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

flashcards.front: What are the three tips from the 'When Will My Baby Speak?' video for encouraging early language?
flashcards.back:
Tip 1: TALK — narrate what your child is doing, where you're going, what's there. Respond to and imitate their vocalizations.
Tip 2: TEACH IMITATION — peekaboo, clapping, waving. Teaches turn-taking, the foundation of conversation.
Tip 3: MAKE IT FUN — sing, play, read books every day. Maintain joint attention and engagement.
Bonus: Always check HEARING first — many speech problems relate to hearing difficulties.
flashcards.source: When Will My Baby Speak video transcript

flashcards.front: What is jargon, and how is it different from babbling?
flashcards.back: Jargon = strings of syllables produced with adult-like STRESS and INTONATION that mimic real speech — but no true words. The child sounds like they're speaking a real language. Babbling = CV syllable combinations without the full prosodic pattern of speech. Jargon comes AFTER babbling and is more sophisticated — it mimics the melody of conversation.
flashcards.source: Lecture 3.2 — Ch. 4, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

flashcards.front: What is the key distinction between an articulation disorder and a phonological disorder?
flashcards.back:
ARTICULATION disorder: incorrect production of a specific sound due to faulty motor movement (placement, timing, direction, pressure, speed, coordination). The problem is at the MOTOR/PRODUCTION level — the child cannot physically make the sound correctly.

PHONOLOGICAL disorder: patterned errors across multiple phonemes caused by simplifying sound combinations. The child IS capable of making the motor movements — the problem is at the LINGUISTIC/RULE level. They haven't learned the phonological rules of the language.
flashcards.source: Lecture 4.2, Kidwell / Quiz answer key

flashcards.front: What are the 4 types of articulation errors? (SODA) Give an example of each.
flashcards.back:
S — SUBSTITUTION: one sound replaces another. Example: 'tat' for 'cat' (/t/ for /k/). Most common error type.
O — OMISSION: a sound is left out. Example: 'at' for 'bat' (initial /b/ omitted).
D — DISTORTION: sound produced inaccurately but no standard sound replaces it. Example: interdental lisp on /s/ (sounds like 'th' but isn't).
A — ADDITION: an extra sound is inserted. Example: 'animamal' for 'animal'.

Errors can occur in initial, medial, or final word position.
flashcards.source: Lecture 4.2, Kidwell, SJDC

flashcards.front: What are the three distinctive features used to describe consonants? Describe each.
flashcards.back:
1. PLACE — where in the vocal tract articulators come together:
  Bilabial, Labiodental, Interdental, Alveolar, Palatal, Velar, Glottal

2. MANNER — how the airstream is modified:
  Stop, Nasal, Glide, Fricative, Lateral, Affricate, Rhotic, Flap

3. VOICE — whether vocal folds vibrate:
  Voiced (folds vibrate) = /b, d, g, v, z/
  Voiceless (folds don't vibrate) = /p, t, k, f, s/
flashcards.source: Lecture 4.2, Kidwell / Ch. 5 slides

flashcards.front: What are cognates? Give two examples.
flashcards.back:
Cognates are two sounds that differ ONLY in voicing — place and manner are identical.

Examples:
/p/ and /b/ — both bilabial stops; /p/ voiceless, /b/ voiced
/f/ and /v/ — both labiodental fricatives; /f/ voiceless, /v/ voiced
/s/ and /z/ — both alveolar fricatives; /s/ voiceless, /z/ voiced
/t/ and /d/ — both alveolar stops; /t/ voiceless, /d/ voiced
flashcards.source: Quiz answer key — Ch. 5 / Lecture 4.2, Kidwell

flashcards.front: What are the three etiologies of communication disorders in children? What is the most important single etiology?
flashcards.back:
1. Normal Variation: 70% within normal range; 15% above, 15% below. SLPs work with the 15% significantly below the mean.
2. Environmental Problems: prenatal (substance abuse — the only 100% preventable cause), perinatal (oxygen deprivation → neurologic damage), postnatal (limited stimulation → fewer synaptic connections).
3. Physical Impairments / Neurologic Differences: congenital syndromes, hearing loss, TBI, CP.

Most children have MULTIPLE (multifactorial) causes. Hearing loss is the MOST CRUCIAL single influence on speech and language development.
flashcards.source: Lecture 4.2, Kidwell, SJDC

flashcards.front: What is stimulability and why does it matter clinically?
flashcards.back:
Stimulability = whether a child can produce an error sound correctly when the clinician models it or gives placement instructions — even if they misarticulate it in words.

Clinical significance:
• Positive prognostic indicator — suggests the sound is likely to be more easily established in therapy
• Tells the SLP whether to target the sound in isolation first
• Example: child says 'wat' for 'rat' in all contexts, but CAN produce /r/ in isolation when shown how — they are stimulable for /r/ in isolation
flashcards.source: Lecture 4.2, Kidwell, SJDC

flashcards.front: How is an articulation disorder different from a dialect/accent difference? Why does this matter for assessment?
flashcards.back:
Dialect/accent = a DIFFERENCE, not a disorder. Some sound substitutions or distortions are predictable based on a speaker's native language and should NOT be scored as errors.

Example: Spanish does not use /v/ the same way as English — a Spanish-speaking child saying 'base' for 'vase' is a language difference, not an error. The /sh/ sound does not exist in Spanish, so difficulty with it in English is expected.

Clinical rule: Errors must be assessed relative to the child's PRIMARY language. When possible, use assessment tools in the child's native language. This is also the LAW in public schools.
flashcards.source: Lecture 4.2, Kidwell, SJDC

flashcards.front: What is the difference between CAS and dysarthria?
flashcards.back:
CAS (Childhood Apraxia of Speech):
• Motor PLANNING disorder — problem with planning/programming/sequencing the motor movements
• NOT caused by muscle weakness
• Affects articulation and prosody
• Inconsistent errors; more difficulty with longer/complex words

DYSARTHRIA:
• Motor EXECUTION disorder — paresis, paralysis, or incoordination of speech muscles
• CAUSED by CNS/PNS damage (weakness is present)
• Can affect ALL speech subsystems: respiration, phonation, resonation, articulation, prosody
• Associated with dysphagia
• Most common cause in children: Cerebral Palsy
flashcards.source: Ch. 5 slides — Fogle/SJDC

flashcards.front: What is cluster reduction and why is it clinically important?
flashcards.back:
Cluster reduction = a phonological process in which part of a consonant cluster (blend) is omitted.
Example: 'poon' for 'spoon', 'pider' for 'spider', 'pill' for 'spill', 'pot' for 'spot'.

Key clinical feature: the child only omits the sound when it's in a BLEND — NOT when the same sound precedes a vowel. A child with cluster reduction will say 'song' and 'same' correctly (no omission) but omit /s/ in 'spoon', 'spill', 'spot'.

This patterned nature is what distinguishes it as a PHONOLOGICAL (not articulation) error — it is context-dependent and rule-governed.
flashcards.source: Lecture 4.2, Kidwell, SJDC

flashcards.front: What is AAC? Distinguish augmentative from alternative approaches.
flashcards.back:
AAC (Augmentative and Alternative Communication): any approach designed to support, enhance, or supplement communication for individuals who are not independent verbal communicators.

AUGMENTATIVE: ENHANCES existing communication — supports verbal speech. Example: using a device alongside attempts at speech.

ALTERNATIVE: REPLACES verbal communication entirely. Example: using a speech-generating device as the primary communication method.

Types: High-tech (electronic, speech-generating devices) and low-tech (communication boards, picture exchange). Selection methods: direct (pointing) or scanning. Requires a multidisciplinary team.
flashcards.source: Ch. 5 slides — Fogle/SJDC

flashcards.front: How many letters vs. phonemes does English have? What is the IPA and why do SLPs use it?
flashcards.back:
English: 26 LETTERS → 45 PHONEMES (speech sounds)
Letters do not represent sounds 1:1 — e.g., 'c' makes /k/ OR /s/; 'th' is one digraph for two different phonemes.

IPA (International Phonetic Alphabet): specially devised symbols that represent the individual sounds of ALL languages. SLPs use it because:
• Standard spelling is inadequate for capturing exact sound errors
• Allows precise transcription of speech
• Universal system across all languages
• The letter 'c' doesn't exist in IPA — we use /k/ or /s/
flashcards.source: Lecture 4.2, Kidwell / Ch. 5 slides

flashcards.front: What is the goal of articulation therapy and what is the general hierarchy of treatment?
flashcards.back:
END GOAL: GENERALIZATION (carryover) — the correctly produced sound is used in all words, sentences, and conversations in all settings (home, school, playground, everywhere).

Therapy hierarchy (traditional):
1. Auditory discrimination (ear training — perceiving the target sound)
2. Recognizing the misarticulation
3. Production at increasing levels of difficulty:
   • Isolation (/s/ by itself)
   • Syllable ('sa', 'os')
   • Word ('sun', 'bus')
   • Phrase ('a sunny day')
   • Sentence
   → Spontaneous conversational speech
4. Generalization to all contexts
flashcards.source: Lecture 4.2, Kidwell, SJDC

flashcards.front: What is the single most important distinction between CAS and dysarthria?
flashcards.back:
MUSCLE WEAKNESS:
• CAS: occurs in the ABSENCE of muscle weakness — the muscles are fine; the problem is motor PLANNING/PROGRAMMING
• DYSARTHRIA: caused by paresis, paralysis, or incoordination — muscle WEAKNESS IS PRESENT

Both are motor speech disorders; both interfere with articulation and prosody; but the underlying mechanism is completely different.
flashcards.source: Lecture Ch. 6, Kidwell, SJDC

flashcards.front: List at least 5 speech characteristics of CAS.
flashcards.back:
Per ASHA (2007):
1. Inconsistent errors (hallmark)
2. Lengthened/disrupted transitions between sounds and syllables
3. Inappropriate prosody (dysprosody)
4. Vowel errors
5. Errors increase with increasing length/complexity
6. High rate of omission errors
7. Groping/silent posturing behavior
8. Lack of progress in traditional articulation therapy
9. Frequent regression
flashcards.source: Ch. 6 PPTX / Lecture Ch. 6, Kidwell

flashcards.front: What are the four types of cerebral palsy? Which is most common?
flashcards.back:
1. SPASTIC CP (spasticity) — most common; hypertonicity; stiff, jerky movements
2. ATHETOID CP (athetosis) — involuntary writhing movements; fluctuating muscle tone
3. ATAXIC CP (ataxia) — balance/coordination problems; unsteady
4. MIXED CP — combination (most often spastic + athetoid)

CP is nonprogressive (does not get worse over time) and is the most frequent cause of dysarthria in children.
flashcards.source: Ch. 6 PPTX / Lecture Ch. 6, Kidwell

flashcards.front: What does dysarthria affect, and why does it sound 'mushy'?
flashcards.back:
Dysarthria can affect any or all speech subsystems:
• Respiratory: low intensity, short phrases, frequent pauses
• Phonatory: breathiness from vocal fold paresis/paralysis
• Resonatory: hypernasality from soft palate weakness
• Articulatory: imprecise, distorted consonants from reduced ROM/strength/coordination/rate of jaw, lips, tongue

Sounds 'mushy' because of distorted consonants and vowels caused by weak/uncoordinated muscles.
flashcards.source: Ch. 6 PPTX / Lecture Ch. 6, Kidwell

flashcards.front: What are the CAS treatment principles?
flashcards.back:
• Intensive, individualized therapy
• Motor learning principles
• Proprioception and multiple modalities (tactile, visual, auditory)
• Drill — many repetitions
• Incremental increases in difficulty
• PROMPTS (tactile-kinesthetic cues)
• Traditional articulation therapy alone is INSUFFICIENT for CAS
• AAC may be needed for highly unintelligible children
flashcards.source: Ch. 6 PPTX / Lecture Ch. 6, Kidwell

flashcards.front: Why is CAS described as a 'diagnosis of exclusion'?
flashcards.back: CAS is diagnosed by ruling out other explanations first. Articulation disorders and phonological disorders must be excluded before CAS is diagnosed. A definitive diagnosis of CAS may not always be possible — the SLP may instead describe the child as having 'CAS characteristics' or document what the child can and cannot do. Assessment begins with articulation and phonological assessment, then progresses to comprehensive oral motor and motor speech evaluation.
flashcards.source: Ch. 6 PPTX / Lecture Ch. 6, Kidwell

flashcards.front: What is the most common cause of Apraxia of Speech in adults? How does this differ from CAS in children?
flashcards.back:
Adults: Most common cause is STROKE with damage to or around BROCA'S AREA (left frontal lobe). Acquired — the person previously had normal speech.

CAS (children): NOT specifically linked to Broca's area. Etiology is neurological, secondary to a neurobehavioral disorder, OR idiopathic. Developmental — the child may never have spoken correctly.

Both: absence of muscle weakness; affects motor planning/programming; hallmark is inconsistency.
flashcards.source: Lecture Ch. 17, Kidwell

flashcards.front: What is the hallmark of AOS and what does inconsistency look like clinically?
flashcards.back:
HALLMARK: INCONSISTENCY — errors are unpredictable and variable.

Clinical examples:
• May produce a complex multisyllabic word correctly, then struggle on a simple one-syllable word
• Same word produced differently on repeated attempts
• Can count to 10 automatically, but cannot say '9' on demand
• Can sing a familiar song, but cannot say individual words volitionally
• 'Islands of fluency' (correct phrases) amid otherwise struggling speech

This is why automatic speech (counting, songs, common phrases) is preserved while volitional speech fails.
flashcards.source: Lecture Ch. 17, Kidwell / Ch. 17 PPTX

flashcards.front: List the four neuromuscular disorders most commonly associated with dysarthria in adults. What type of dysarthria does each cause?
flashcards.back:
1. PARKINSON'S DISEASE (PD) — Hypokinetic dysarthria: reduced loudness (hypophonia), monopitch, rapid rate (festination). Treat with LSVT LOUD.

2. ALS (Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis / Lou Gehrig's disease) — Mixed dysarthria (flaccid + spastic): progressive to anarthria; eventually requires AAC.

3. MULTIPLE SCLEROSIS (MS) — Mixed or ataxic dysarthria: variable with relapse/remission.

4. MYASTHENIA GRAVIS — Fatigable dysarthria: speech worsens with continued use; better at start of conversation.
flashcards.source: Lecture Ch. 17, Kidwell / Ch. 17 PPTX

flashcards.front: What are the six types of dysarthria?
flashcards.back:
1. SPASTIC — bilateral UMN damage; harsh voice, hypernasality, slow rate, imprecise consonants
2. FLACCID — LMN/PNS damage; breathy voice, hypernasality, weak pressure consonants
3. ATAXIC — cerebellar damage; irregular articulatory breakdown; excess/equal stress; 'scanning speech'
4. HYPOKINETIC — basal ganglia (Parkinson's); reduced loudness, monopitch, rapid short rushes
5. HYPERKINETIC — basal ganglia; involuntary movements; variable rate/loudness
6. MIXED — combination; e.g., ALS = flaccid + spastic
flashcards.source: Lecture Ch. 17, Kidwell / Ch. 17 PPTX

flashcards.front: Contrast AOS and dysarthria across: muscle weakness, error consistency, error type, and speech quality.
flashcards.back:
MUSCLE WEAKNESS:
• AOS: ABSENT — planning disorder, muscles fine
• Dysarthria: PRESENT — paresis/paralysis/incoordination

ERROR CONSISTENCY:
• AOS: INCONSISTENT — variable, unpredictable
• Dysarthria: CONSISTENT — related to specific weakened systems

ERROR TYPE:
• AOS: substitutions predominate; groping behavior
• Dysarthria: distortions predominate; 'mushy/slurred'

SPEECH QUALITY:
• AOS: effortful, struggling, with islands of fluency
• Dysarthria: consistently weak/slurred across all speech

AUTOMATIC SPEECH:
• AOS: preserved (counting, songs, phrases)
• Dysarthria: also affected in severe cases
flashcards.source: Lecture Ch. 17, Kidwell / Motor_Speech_Disorders.pdf

flashcards.front: What are the two treatment approaches for dysarthria and when is each used?
flashcards.back:
1. RESTORATIVE APPROACH: improves the underlying impairment — muscle tone, ROM, strength, rate of movement. Best for mild-to-moderate dysarthria. Goal: natural-sounding speech.

2. COMPENSATORY APPROACH: teaches strategies to work around weakness — rate control, overarticulation, postural adjustments. Used when full restoration is not possible. Goal: intelligibility and efficiency.

Primary goal for BOTH: maximize effectiveness, efficiency, and naturalness of communication.

Severe dysarthria: AAC may be required.
flashcards.source: Lecture Ch. 17, Kidwell / Ch. 17 PPTX

flashcards.front: What is the ASHA definition of a language disorder? What three components can be involved?
flashcards.back:
Language disorder (ASHA): the impaired comprehension and/or use of spoken, written (graphic), and/or other symbol systems.

Three components (any combination):
1. FORM — phonology, morphology, syntax
2. CONTENT — semantics
3. FUNCTION/USE — pragmatics/social communication

SLPs treat receptive AND expressive language disorders involving all three in spoken AND written modalities.
flashcards.source: Ch. 7 slides — Fogle/SJDC

flashcards.front: What is SLI? What THREE criteria must be present for a child to be diagnosed with SLI?
flashcards.back:
SLI (Specific Language Impairment): significant receptive and/or expressive language impairments that CANNOT be attributed to any general or specific cause or condition.

Three required criteria (all must be present):
1. Hearing WITHIN NORMAL LIMITS
2. No obvious perceptual or neurological disorders
3. Nonverbal intelligence WITHIN NORMAL LIMITS

SLI is a DIAGNOSIS OF EXCLUSION — defined by what is NOT present.
Present in 7–8% of school-entering children; 2% more common in boys.
Most frequent reason for early intervention and special education services.
flashcards.source: Ch. 7 slides / Lecture Ch. 7, Kidwell

flashcards.front: What is the difference between language disorder, language delay, and language difference?
flashcards.back:
LANGUAGE DISORDER: child will NOT catch up with peers; gaps WIDEN over time. Requires SLP diagnosis and intervention.

LANGUAGE DELAY: slow start — child is expected to eventually catch up ('slow talker', 'late bloomer'). Some mild delays resolve; most children still risk residual impairment.
