flashcards.source: Quiz answer key — Ch. 5

flashcards.back: A second pair of folds above the true vocal folds. They do NOT vibrate during speech. Their function is to close during swallowing to help prevent material from entering the trachea. Also called ventricular folds.
flashcards.source: Lecture 2.2 — Ch. 3, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

flashcards.back: Non-literal language forms including idioms ('raining cats and dogs'), metaphors, similes, and verbal humor. Peak period for learning figurative language is 11–12 years (adolescence). Difficulty with figurative language is clinically significant and affects social and academic success.
flashcards.source: Ch. 4 PDF — Prof. Handy, SJDC

flashcards.back: The continuity, smoothness, rate, and effort in speech production. Fluent speech flows forward without disruption. Fluency disorders are among the most recognized and most researched areas in speech-language pathology — stuttering is commonly the first thing people think of when they think of a speech disorder.
flashcards.source: Fluency_Disorders.pdf / Lecture Ch. 12, Kidwell

flashcards.back:
Assessment of stuttering in children includes:
• Parent interview (foremost — requires interviewing and counseling skills)
• General speech and language evaluation
• Formal and informal observations in multiple settings (therapy room, classroom, recess, small/large group — variety is essential)
• Analysis of: types of disfluencies, frequency of disfluencies, conditions that increase/decrease stuttering (e.g., emotional topics), physical behaviors, child's covert reactions
• STUTTERING SEVERITY INSTRUMENT-4 (SSI-4): most common formal tool; measures types of disfluencies, rate of disfluencies, physical behaviors, and naturalness of speech
• Diagnosis determines: whether stuttering is normal or abnormal, severity, disfluency rate, types and patterns
flashcards.source: Lecture Ch. 12, Kidwell, SJDC

flashcards.back:
A structured behavioral approach to stuttering therapy that trains a client to produce fluent speech using specific physiologic techniques:
• Relaxed RESPIRATION during speech
• Relaxed VOCAL FOLDS
• Relaxed ARTICULATORY muscles
• Slow/easy speech and smooth exhalation exercises
• Gradual increase toward natural rate
• Cancellations: stopping after a stuttered word, pausing, and saying it fluently
• Pull-outs: changing from a stutter to a smooth production mid-word
Goal: build entirely new speech pattern. THERAPY EMPHASIS: increasing awareness of muscular tension through relaxation; analyzing and modifying speech; building new speech skills.
flashcards.source: Lecture Ch. 12, Kidwell / Fluency_Disorders.pdf

flashcards.back:
Two dominant approaches to stuttering therapy:
1. FLUENCY SHAPING (FS): teaches the person to speak with relaxed respiration, vocal folds, and articulatory muscles. Highly structured with behavioral modification. Uses a series of exercises (slow/easy speech, smooth exhalation). Teaches the client to recognize and confront fears, avoidance, and struggle behaviors. Techniques include cancellations and pull-outs. Goal: build fluent speech from the ground up.
2. STUTTERING MODIFICATION (SM): focuses more on COMMUNICATION IMPROVEMENT than on fluent speech. Targets negative forces (fear, avoidance, escape behaviors) for reduction, which creates spontaneous fluency. Develops insight into attitudes, thoughts, and feelings about stuttering.

Both approaches may be used together.
flashcards.source: Lecture Ch. 12, Kidwell / Fluency_Disorders.pdf

flashcards.front: Define: Form, Content, and Use (Three Components of Language)
flashcards.back:
Language can be described by three dimensions:
• FORM: the structure and rules of the language system — includes phonology (sound system), morphology (word structure rules), and syntax (sentence structure rules)
• CONTENT: the meaning conveyed by language — semantics (vocabulary, concepts, word relationships)
• USE/FUNCTION: how language is used in social communication — pragmatics (turn-taking, topic maintenance, eye contact, implied meaning)
A language disorder may involve any one or any combination of these three components.
flashcards.source: Lecture Ch. 7, Kidwell / Ch. 7 slides

flashcards.front: Define: Four Neuromuscular Disorders Commonly Associated with Dysarthria
flashcards.back:
The four neuromuscular disorders most commonly associated with dysarthria in adults (per Fogle/ASHA):
1. Parkinson's Disease (PD) — hypokinetic dysarthria; reduced loudness, monopitch, rapid rate
2. ALS (Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis / Lou Gehrig's disease) — mixed (flaccid + spastic) dysarthria; progressive to anarthria
3. Multiple Sclerosis (MS) — mixed or ataxic dysarthria; variable with relapse/remission
4. Myasthenia Gravis — fatigable dysarthria; worsens with use
flashcards.source: Ch. 17 PPTX / Lecture Ch. 17, Kidwell

flashcards.front: Define: Free and Appropriate Public Education (FAPE)
flashcards.back: A right established under Public Law 94-142 (1975) guaranteeing that all school-aged children with disabilities receive educational services at no cost in an appropriate setting. Remains a significant legal standard today.
flashcards.source: Lecture 1.2.2 — Chapter 2: The Professionals, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

flashcards.back: A fold of mucous tissue connecting the floor of the mouth to the midline underside of the tongue. A short or tight frenum (tongue-tie / ankyloglossia) can restrict tongue mobility and potentially affect articulation — though clinical significance varies. An anatomic structure assessed during oral motor evaluation.
flashcards.source: Quiz answer key — Ch. 5

flashcards.back: The number of complete cycles of vocal fold opening and closing per second. Measured in Hertz (Hz). The fundamental frequency is the basic rate of vocal fold vibration. An objective, physical measurement.
flashcards.source: Lecture 2.2 — Ch. 3, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

flashcards.back: Essential for cognition, attention, reasoning, judgment, decision-making, and problem-solving. Anterior portion governs personality, character, beliefs, goal-setting, and self-regulation. Posterior portion contains Broca's area for motor speech control.
flashcards.source: Lecture 2.2 — Ch. 3, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

flashcards.back: A communication disorder with no identifiable anatomic, physiologic, or neurologic cause. The child has difficulty producing speech sounds, but examination reveals no structural or neurological reason. Contrasted with organic disorder. The cause is unknown. Also called 'idiopathic.'
flashcards.source: Ch. 5 slides — Fogle/SJDC / Lecture 4.2, Kidwell

flashcards.front: Define: Functional Dysphonia / Muscle Tension Dysphonia (MTD)
flashcards.back: A voice disorder with no organic, physical, or neurological cause — results from EXTREME TENSION in the laryngeal region (muscle tension dysphonia). One of the most common adult voice disorders (20.5% of adults aged 19–60). Voice sounds strained, tense, or pressed. Responds well to voice therapy targeting tension reduction, relaxation, and vocal technique modification.
flashcards.source: Lecture Ch. 13, Kidwell / Ch. 13 PPTX

flashcards.back: Words that give order and grammatical structure to sentences rather than carrying primary meaning themselves. Include articles (a, the), prepositions (in, on), conjunctions (and, but), and auxiliaries (is, have). Emerge around 3–5 years. Absent in telegraphic speech.
flashcards.source: Ch. 4 PDF — Prof. Handy, SJDC

flashcards.back: American English that is not associated with any regional dialect — the variety heard on radio and television. Derived from British English. Composed of 26 letters representing 45 speech sounds (phonemes). The reference standard used when evaluating articulation and phonological disorders.
flashcards.source: Ch. 5 slides — Fogle/SJDC / Lecture 4.2, Kidwell

flashcards.back: The end goal of articulation/phonological therapy — the transfer of a correctly learned sound from the therapy room to all words, sentences, and conversations in all settings (home, playground, classroom, etc.). Therapy progresses from isolation → syllable → word → phrase → sentence → spontaneous conversation, always working toward generalization.
flashcards.source: Lecture 4.2, Kidwell, SJDC

flashcards.back: The space between the open (abducted) vocal folds. When the glottis is open, air passes freely for breathing. When the vocal folds adduct and close, air pressure builds below the glottis to initiate phonation.
flashcards.source: Lecture 2.2 — Ch. 3, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

flashcards.back: A hallmark characteristic of CAS in which the child visibly struggles and makes trial-and-error movements (silent posturing) while attempting to find the correct articulatory placement for a sound. The child knows what they want to say but cannot successfully execute the motor plan — the articulators appear to 'search' for the correct position.
flashcards.source: Ch. 6 PPTX — Fogle/SJDC

flashcards.back: Habilitation refers to developing communication skills in individuals who never had them (e.g., children born deaf). Rehabilitation refers to restoring communication skills lost due to injury or disease. Audiologists assess potential for both.
flashcards.source: Lecture 1.2.2 — Chapter 2: The Professionals, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

flashcards.front: Define: Home Strategies for Parents of Children Who Stutter
flashcards.back:
Key home strategies (Ducworth, Handy Handout #65):
• Use a SMOOTH, RELAXED RATE when speaking to the child — the most important thing a parent can do
• Speak in SIMPLE, SHORT SENTENCES; pause slightly before responding
• LISTEN without interrupting or finishing sentences
• Make sure the child knows they are being UNDERSTOOD
• SLOW the pace of the household; minimize excitement levels
• When difficulty occurs, it is OK to calmly acknowledge it: 'You had a little trouble getting that out'
• Inform ALL people who interact with the child (family members, teachers) to use smooth, relaxed speech
• Behavioral treatment approaches help children develop more POSITIVE ATTITUDES toward communication

Resources: Stuttering Foundation (stutteringhelp.org); ASHA board-certified fluency specialists (stutteringspecialists.org)
flashcards.source: 65_Cause_Treatment_of_Stuttering.pdf — Ducworth, CCC-SLP

flashcards.back: A U-shaped bone in the neck that supports the tongue and larynx. The only bone in the body that does not articulate directly with any other bone. Acts as an anchor for tongue muscles and plays a role in swallowing and speech.
flashcards.source: CSD Ch3 PP (Fogle 3rd ed.)

flashcards.back: Hypotonicity: abnormally low muscle tone — muscles are floppy/weak. Hypertonicity: abnormally high muscle tone — muscles are rigid/stiff. Both affect speech production in dysarthria. The type present depends on the site and extent of neurological damage. CP can present with either type.
flashcards.source: Ch. 6 PPTX — Fogle/SJDC

flashcards.back: Inadequate oxygenation of the blood leading to tachycardia (rapid heart rate) and rapid, shallow breathing that quickly affects the brain and other organs and systems. Less severe than anoxia (complete oxygen absence) but still causes neurological damage. A perinatal risk factor for communication disorders.
flashcards.source: Quiz answer key — Ch. 5

flashcards.back: CAS that occurs spontaneously with no apparent or identifiable cause. One of the three recognized etiological categories of CAS (neurological, secondary to a neurobehavioral disorder, or idiopathic). Even when no cause can be found, treatment is still indicated.
flashcards.source: Ch. 6 PPTX / Lecture Ch. 6, Kidwell

flashcards.back: The rate at which a disorder appears in a population over a period of time. Distinct from prevalence.
flashcards.source: Lecture 1.2.1 — Chapter 1, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

flashcards.back: At rest the ratio is approximately 50:50 (or 40:60). During speech and singing, it shifts dramatically to approximately 10% inhalation and 90% exhalation — a quick short breath in, then a long sustained breath out to support speech.
flashcards.source: Lecture 2.2 — Ch. 3, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

flashcards.back: The internal monologue humans use to think, plan, and make decisions privately before expressing thoughts verbally. A uniquely human form of self-directed communication.
flashcards.source: Lecture 1.2.1 — Chapter 1, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

flashcards.back: The process of drawing air into the lungs. During inspiration, the diaphragm contracts and moves downward, the rib cage rises and expands, thoracic volume increases, air pressure decreases, and air flows in through the nose/mouth down the trachea into the alveolar sacs.
flashcards.source: CSD Ch3 PP (Fogle 3rd ed.)

flashcards.back: The degree to which speech can be understood by a listener. Speech-language pathologists work with clients to improve intelligibility across all four processes of speech production.
flashcards.source: Eugene Speech Language Therapy — How We Speak (YouTube, 11xf89bXaCg)

flashcards.back: The physical force with which the vocal folds open and close and the amount of air escaping between them. Measured in decibels (dB). The objective physical measurement underlying the perception of loudness.
flashcards.source: Lecture 2.2 — Ch. 3, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

flashcards.back: Muscles located between the ribs (internal and external). Important for both inhalation and exhalation during breathing and speech. Part of the 26 pairs of muscles involved in respiration beyond the diaphragm.
flashcards.source: Lecture 2.2 — Ch. 3, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

flashcards.back: A type of distortion error on /s/ (and sometimes /z/) in which the tongue protrudes between the teeth during production, resulting in a sound that resembles 'th' but is neither a correct /s/ nor a correct /th/. Classified as a distortion because there is no exact standard symbol for the altered sound.
flashcards.source: Lecture 4.2, Kidwell, SJDC

flashcards.back: Brief periods of fluent, error-free speech that occur amid otherwise effortful, struggling communication in Apraxia of Speech. Stereotypic phrases (well-rehearsed expressions) may be produced correctly even in severe AOS. For example, a person with severe/profound AOS may be able to say 'hello' or 'I love you' perfectly but cannot produce volitional speech on command.
flashcards.source: Ch. 17 PPTX / Lecture Ch. 17, Kidwell

flashcards.back: When a caregiver and infant simultaneously attend to the same object, person, or event. The foundation of early communication and turn-taking. Infants learn to take turns in conversation because they observe when the other person stops and understand it is their turn to 'speak.'
flashcards.source: Lecture 3.2 — Ch. 4, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

flashcards.front: Define: Kidwell's Analogy: APD and Second Language Processing
flashcards.back: Kidwell explains APD using a second-language analogy: 'I cannot listen to Spanish radio while talking to someone and then report what was just said. I require careful listening, context cues, a slower rate of speech, and a quiet background to understand Spanish.' A person with APD experiences the same difficulty — but in their PRIMARY language. The ear functions correctly; the problem is that the brain cannot make sense of the incoming signals in difficult or competing listening conditions.
flashcards.source: Lecture Ch. 9, Kidwell, SJDC

flashcards.back:
The work or activity that leads to a clinical decision (the diagnosis). Assessment must be based on valid and reliable measurements. Steps:
1. REFERRAL and SCREENING — parent, teacher, or caregiver refers; SLP screens to determine if full assessment is warranted
2. FORMAL assessment — standardized tests, normative comparison; required for school eligibility (CA standard: 1.5 SD or more below norm on 2+ tests in 1 area)
3. INFORMAL assessment — language samples, observation, dynamic assessment; used especially for culturally/linguistically diverse children
4. DIAGNOSIS and ELIGIBILITY determination
5. Treatment plan and PROGNOSIS
★ Standardized tests are biased — assess in PRIMARY language. If one language is WNL, child is not language disordered.
flashcards.source: Lecture Ch. 7, Kidwell, SJDC

flashcards.back: The progressive growth of a receptive and expressive communication system for representing concepts using arbitrary symbols (sounds and words) and rule-governed combinations of those symbols (grammar). Involves both receptive (understanding) and expressive (speaking) language.
flashcards.source: Lecture 3.2 — Ch. 4, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

flashcards.back: An impairment of receptive and/or expressive linguistic symbols that affects learning and educational achievement. Closely related to SLI — many SLPs consider them the same condition. However, per Fogle: a Learning Disability cannot formally be identified until the child has had the opportunity to learn (read, write, complete oral tasks in school) and is struggling — i.e., school-age diagnosis. Language-learning disabilities run in families. Longitudinal studies show 50%+ of children with SLI pre-K or K develop reading disabilities; 20% more become poor readers.
flashcards.source: Lecture Ch. 7, Kidwell / Ch. 7 slides

flashcards.back:
Evidence-based therapy for language disorders must be: effective, functional, and attainable. Key principles:
• Goals selected based on the child's specific needs
• Normative language data used as a developmental guide
• Progress from SIMPLE to COMPLEX language goals
• Long-term goals: desired end result; short-term goals: incremental steps
• Well-organized and tightly structured sessions, especially for young children
• Flexibility within structure — clinician must adapt in the moment
• Tangible reinforcers may be used (stickers, stamps, bubbles)
• Goals should be functionally meaningful — what will most help the child in real life
flashcards.source: Lecture Ch. 7, Kidwell, SJDC

flashcards.back: A life-threatening organic disease of the larynx requiring medical and surgical management. Approximately 2% of voice disorders; mostly affects males. SMOKING is a major cause of larynx, lung, pharynx, and oral structure cancers. ★ Any person with a voice disorder must be seen by an OTOLARYNGOLOGIST (ENT) to RULE OUT MALIGNANCY before SLP treatment begins. Presbyphonia (age-related voice change) and polypoid degeneration are the most common voice concerns in adults 60+.
flashcards.source: Lecture Ch. 13, Kidwell / Ch. 13 PPTX

flashcards.back: The anterior (front) point of the thyroid cartilage that is visible and palpable in the neck. More prominent in males due to the larger larynx. Commonly referred to as the 'Adam's apple.'
flashcards.source: CSD Ch3 PP (Fogle 3rd ed.)

flashcards.back:
Inflammation of the larynx causing hoarseness. Two types:
• ACUTE (TRAUMATIC) LARYNGITIS: sudden onset — from a cold, allergies, or voice strain at concerts/sporting events. Alcohol worsens the problem by causing dehydration which dries out the vocal folds.
• CHRONIC LARYNGITIS: persistent inflammation from ongoing vocal abuse, smoking, or other repeated causes.
Laryngitis is among the most frequent voice disorder diagnoses. A functional etiology — caused by faulty voice use or abuse.
flashcards.source: Lecture Ch. 13, Kidwell / Ch. 13 PPTX

flashcards.back: A disorder in one or more of the basic psychological processes involved in understanding or using language, spoken or written, that may manifest as difficulty with: listening, speaking, reading, writing, spelling, mathematical computations, reasoning, and/or problem solving. NOT diagnosed until school age (when the child has had the opportunity to attempt academic tasks). Often has roots in an underlying language disorder. Approximately 15% of children with LDs also have difficulty with gross and fine motor skills and coordination.
flashcards.source: Ch. 7 slides — Fogle/SJDC / Lecture Ch. 7, Kidwell

flashcards.back: A legal requirement under Public Law 94-142 that children with disabilities be educated alongside non-disabled peers to the maximum extent appropriate. Remains a significant standard in special education today.
flashcards.source: Lecture 1.2.2 — Chapter 2: The Professionals, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

flashcards.back: The dominant hemisphere for receptive and expressive language and speech. Processes rapidly changing information sequentially. Controls movement and sensation on the right side of the body. Contains Broca's area and receives input from Wernicke's area.
flashcards.source: Lecture 2.2 — Ch. 3, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

flashcards.back: A person's vocabulary — the total set of words known and used. Between 12–18 months, toddlers develop an expressive vocabulary (lexicon) of approximately 90 words. By 2 years: ~300 words. Vocabulary continues to grow throughout life.
flashcards.source: Ch. 4 PDF — Prof. Handy, SJDC

flashcards.back: Impaired volitional movement of the limbs (arms, hands) that may co-occur with CAS or acquired AOS. A person with limb apraxia cannot perform purposeful limb movements on command — similar to oral apraxia but affecting limbs rather than articulators.
flashcards.source: Ch. 6 PPTX — Fogle/SJDC

flashcards.back: The scientific study of language. Divides language into five components: phonology, morphology, syntax, semantics, and pragmatics.
flashcards.source: Lecture 1.2.1 — Chapter 1, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

flashcards.back: The perceptual (subjective) correlate of intensity — how loud a sound seems to a listener. Measured in decibels (dB) when objective. Distinct from intensity, which is a physical measurement.
flashcards.source: Lecture 2.2 — Ch. 3, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

flashcards.front: Define: LSVT LOUD (Lee Silverman Voice Treatment)
flashcards.back: A research-based voice treatment approach used in dysarthria therapy that focuses on increasing vocal loudness as the primary target. Based on the principle that increasing loudness through intensive vocal effort improves overall speech system function. Originally developed for Parkinson's disease but applicable to other dysarthria populations.
flashcards.source: Ch. 6 PPTX — Fogle/SJDC

flashcards.back: The lower jaw. Opens and closes the mouth during speech and eating. During speech, jaw movement (how open or closed) is controlled and varies by sound. One of the two primary bones of concern to an SLP.
flashcards.source: Lecture 2.2 — Ch. 3, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

flashcards.back: How the outgoing airstream is modified during consonant production. The eight manners of articulation are: Stop (airflow completely stopped), Nasal (air through nasal cavity), Glide (smooth gradual movement), Fricative (continuous turbulent airflow), Lateral/Liquid (/l/ sounds), Affricate (stop + fricative combined), Rhotic (/r/ sounds), Flap (brief tap of tongue).
flashcards.source: Ch. 5 slides — Fogle/SJDC

flashcards.back: The act of chewing. One of the three biological functions of the tongue. Also involves the mandible, teeth, and facial muscles.
flashcards.source: Lecture 2.2 — Ch. 3, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

flashcards.back: The upper jaw bone. Contains the hard palate and upper teeth. One of the two primary bones of concern to an SLP (along with the mandible). Fuses during fetal development; disruption causes cleft palate.
flashcards.source: Lecture 2.2 — Ch. 3, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

flashcards.back: ★ SLPs NEVER treat voice disorders without MEDICAL CLEARANCE from an otolaryngologist (ENT/ear-nose-throat physician). The reason: an SLP cannot determine the cause of a hoarse voice simply by listening — structural, neurological, and organic causes must be ruled out, including malignancy (cancer). The ENT sees the client FIRST and may use: indirect laryngoscopy (laryngeal mirror), endoscopy (rigid or flexible fiber-optic), direct laryngoscopy, and/or biopsy. Only after the ENT examination does the SLP proceed with assessment and treatment.
flashcards.source: Lecture Ch. 13, Kidwell, SJDC

flashcards.back: Metalinguistics = awareness of language as an object/tool — 'talking about language.' Problems include difficulty with: multiple meanings of words, making inferences, figurative language, sequencing, understanding similarities/differences, distinguishing literal vs. figurative meanings, and working with sentence types (declarative, interrogative, imperative, exclamatory, compound/complex). Children with SLI and LLD are particularly challenged in this area as school demands for metalinguistic skills increase with grade level.
flashcards.source: Ch. 7 slides — Fogle/SJDC

flashcards.back: A hearing loss that involves both conductive and sensorineural components simultaneously.
flashcards.source: Lecture 1.2.1 — Chapter 1, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

flashcards.front: Define: Morphological and Syntactic Problems in SLI
flashcards.back:
Children with SLI are late in developing syntactic structures and using them consistently. Key indicators:
• Low MLU (Mean Length of Utterance) for age — missing morphological markers like plural -s, verb -ed/-ing, possessive 's
• Speak in short, incomplete sentences ('Milk') or simple declarative sentences ('Me want milk')
• Incorrect sentence order: 'Why you not play with me?' or 'Car fast go'
• Incorrect grammar: 'Those childs goed to the park' or 'Her is stand in the water'
• Interferes with ability to convey messages even when vocabulary is adequate
flashcards.source: Lecture Ch. 7, Kidwell / Ch. 7 slides

flashcards.back: The study of the way words are structurally formed out of morphemes. A morpheme is the smallest unit of language that conveys meaning. Includes prefixes and suffixes. Example: 'like' → 'dislike' (prefix) or 'liked' (suffix). Language-disordered clients often show morphological errors.
flashcards.source: Lecture 1.2.1 — Chapter 1, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

flashcards.back: Located in the frontal lobe. Takes direction from the premotor cortex (including Broca's area). The primary pathway for carrying neural impulses from the brain to the muscles of the body for voluntary movement, including speech.
flashcards.source: CSD Ch3 PP (Fogle 3rd ed.)

flashcards.back: The plan created by the brain specifying how to produce intended speech. The brain first determines what it wants to say, then creates a motor plan before the four processes of speech production are executed.
flashcards.source: Eugene Speech Language Therapy — How We Speak (YouTube, 11xf89bXaCg)

flashcards.back: Impaired speech intelligibility caused by a neurological impairment that affects motor programming and planning, coordination, or the strength of the articulators needed for rapid, complex movements in smooth, effortless speech. Umbrella term covering CAS and dysarthria. May result from prenatal, perinatal, or postnatal neurological damage.
flashcards.source: Lecture Ch. 6, Kidwell, SJDC

flashcards.back: Neurologically-based speech impairments affecting motor planning for speech. Includes childhood apraxia of speech and dysarthria. Can occur in both children and adults. Result from neurological disruptions to the motor planning process.
flashcards.source: Lecture 1.2.1 — Chapter 1, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

flashcards.back: Acquired motor speech disorders in adults result from neurological impairments that affect motor planning, programming, neuromuscular control, or execution of speech — or a combination of these. They have a SENSORY component and should be thought of as SENSORIMOTOR in nature, not purely motor dysfunction. Disordered speech is sometimes the first sign of neurological disease. May co-occur with aphasia and cognitive impairments. Two main types: Apraxia of Speech (AOS) and Dysarthria.
flashcards.source: Motor_Speech_Disorders.pdf / Ch. 17 PPTX

flashcards.front: Define: Multicultural Factors in Language Assessment
flashcards.back:
Multicultural considerations affect identification, assessment, and treatment of language disorders:
• Children from different cultures may not attend preschool → late identification
• Families may be unaware of SLP services, that they are free, or how to access them
• Standardized tests are often culturally and linguistically biased
• Must assess in the PRIMARY language; use culturally appropriate norms
• Cannot diagnose a language disorder based on second-language difficulties alone
• SLPs must be cautious with EL (English Learner) students — assess both languages; disorder must be present in BOTH to qualify as a disorder
flashcards.source: Lecture Ch. 7, Kidwell, SJDC

flashcards.back: A progressive autoimmune disease that demyelinates the CNS — causes variable neurological symptoms depending on lesion location. May cause mixed or ataxic dysarthria among other symptoms. Speech may fluctuate with relapse/remission cycles. One of the four neuromuscular disorders commonly associated with dysarthria.
flashcards.source: Ch. 17 PPTX — Fogle/SJDC

flashcards.back: An autoimmune neuromuscular disease that impairs communication between nerves and muscles at the neuromuscular junction — causes fatigable muscle weakness. In speech: voice and articulation worsen with continued use (fatigable dysarthria). Hallmark: speech is better at the start of conversation and deteriorates the longer the person speaks. One of the four neuromuscular disorders commonly associated with dysarthria.
flashcards.source: Ch. 17 PPTX — Fogle/SJDC

flashcards.back: Therapy to correct tongue thrust (abnormal forward tongue movement during swallowing). May be recommended by orthodontists before placing braces. Addresses how tongue thrust can affect both speech production and dentition alignment.
flashcards.source: Lecture 2.2 — Ch. 3, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

flashcards.back: Orderly, sequenced relating of accounts or events in conversation. Emerges during the 3–4 year range as children begin to retell stories and recount events in a logical order. An important language and literacy precursor.
flashcards.source: Ch. 4 PDF — Prof. Handy, SJDC

flashcards.back: Children with SLI and LLD have significant difficulty relating narratives — sequenced stories with connected events. A narrative requires: topic maintenance, sequential organization, cause-effect understanding, inclusion of key details, and coherent structure. Children with narrative disorders may tell stories that are fragmented, out of sequence, missing key characters or events, or incomprehensible to listeners unfamiliar with the topic. The Sariah video is a clinical example: her retelling of Beverly Hills Chihuahua is incomplete, uses simple/incomplete sentences, errors in past tense ('puted' for 'put'), and lacks critical narrative structure.
flashcards.source: Lecture Ch. 7, Kidwell / Sariah video transcript

flashcards.back: One of three resonating cavities used in speech production (along with the throat and oral cavity). Sound passes through the nasal cavity to contribute to resonance and voice quality.
flashcards.source: Eugene Speech Language Therapy — How We Speak (YouTube, 11xf89bXaCg)

flashcards.back: Speech sounds produced with the soft palate remaining DOWN, allowing sound to pass through the nasal cavity for resonance (e.g., /m/, /n/, /ŋ/). Contrasted with oral sounds, where velopharyngeal closure redirects airflow entirely through the mouth.
flashcards.source: CSD Ch3 PP (Fogle 3rd ed.)

flashcards.back: Perspective that language acquisition is innate, physiologically determined, and genetically transmitted. Newborns are 'prewired' for language — they will acquire it unless there are physical or mental limitations. Language is universal and unique to humans. Clinical application: target age-appropriate language structures not acquired naturally. Limitation: does not fully account for environmental influence.
flashcards.source: Lecture 3.2 — Ch. 4, Kidwell / Ch. 4 PDF — Prof. Handy

flashcards.front: Define: Neurological Voice Disorders — Hypoadduction and Hyperadduction
flashcards.back:
Voice disorders caused by neurological problems affecting muscle control and innervation of respiration and phonation:

HYPOADDUCTION (vocal folds do NOT close strongly/long enough):
• Vocal fold PARESIS (weakness) or PARALYSIS
• Unilateral: causes breathiness or DIPLOPHONIA (two simultaneous pitches)
• Bilateral: can PREVENT RESPIRATION — life-threatening

HYPERADDUCTION (vocal folds close TOO tightly or for TOO LONG):
• Results in SPASMODIC DYSPHONIA (SD): rare CNS disorder with strained, strangled, or harsh voice quality
• SD does NOT respond to voice therapy alone
• Treatment: BOTULINUM TOXIN (Botox) injection to weaken the vocal fold muscles OR surgical severing of the recurrent laryngeal nerve
flashcards.source: Lecture Ch. 13, Kidwell / Ch. 13 PPTX

flashcards.back: A nerve cell — the basic unit of the nervous system. The brain contains approximately 100 billion neurons. Each neuron has thousands of connections to other neurons, creating trillions of connections. Every thought, action, emotion, and memory results from electrochemical reactions between neurons.
flashcards.source: Lecture 2.2 — Ch. 3, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

flashcards.back: Communication that does not rely on spoken words. Includes facial expressions, eye contact, body posture, and gestures. Often used simultaneously with verbal communication.
flashcards.source: Lecture 1.2.1 — Chapter 1, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

flashcards.back: Disfluencies that are within typical limits and not considered a disorder. Normal disfluencies include: occasional repetitions of syllables or words (once or twice — 'li-li-like this'), hesitations, and use of fillers ('uh,' 'er,' 'um'). They occur most often between 1½ and 5 years of age, tend to come and go, and are often a sign that language is being used in a new way. As an SLP, a key task is determining whether a child is experiencing normal or abnormal disfluencies.
flashcards.source: Fluency_Disorders.pdf / Lecture Ch. 12, Kidwell

flashcards.front: Define: NSSLHA (National Student Speech Language Hearing Association)
flashcards.back: The student membership division of ASHA. Open to students pursuing degrees in communication sciences and disorders. Website: www.nsslha.org.
flashcards.source: Lecture 1.2.2 — Chapter 2: The Professionals, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

flashcards.back: Located at the posterior (back) of the brain. Where visual images are received and processed. Damage can cause visual field deficits or cortical blindness.
flashcards.source: Lecture 2.2 — Ch. 3, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

flashcards.back: An articulation error in which a sound is completely left out of a word. Example: 'at' for 'bat' — the initial /b/ is omitted. Often occurs in final position in young children (e.g., 'da' for 'dog').
flashcards.source: Lecture 4.2, Kidwell, SJDC

flashcards.back: A learning model (Skinner's behavioral theory) in which a behavior is immediately reinforced after it occurs, increasing the likelihood it will recur. In language therapy, used as stimulus-response: a child approximates a word, the SLP reinforces it and shapes it toward the correct form.
flashcards.source: Lecture 3.2 — Ch. 4, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

flashcards.front: Define: Oral Apraxia (Nonverbal / Buccofacial / Facial Apraxia)
flashcards.back: Difficulty with volitional nonspeech movements of the articulators/face — may co-occur with AOS. The person cannot voluntarily perform movements such as: puffing the cheeks, clicking the tongue, protruding the tongue on command, whistling, or smiling when asked. These same movements may occur automatically/spontaneously. Also called nonverbal apraxia, facial apraxia, or buccofacial apraxia.
flashcards.source: Ch. 17 PPTX / Lecture Ch. 17, Kidwell

flashcards.back: Difficulty with volitional nonspeech movements of the articulators — may co-occur with CAS. The child (or adult with AOS) cannot voluntarily perform oral movements such as whistling, blowing out candles, smiling on command, or clicking the tongue, even though these movements may occur automatically (e.g., smiling spontaneously in response to something funny).
flashcards.source: Ch. 6 PPTX / Lecture Ch. 6, Kidwell

flashcards.back: A communication disorder with an identifiable anatomic, physiologic, or neurologic cause. Examples: cleft palate (structural), cerebral palsy (neurologic), hearing loss (physiologic). Contrasted with functional disorder. Can be caused by prenatal, perinatal, or postnatal factors.
flashcards.source: Ch. 5 slides — Fogle/SJDC / Lecture 4.2, Kidwell

flashcards.back: The adapted, secondary use of a body structure for communication. Humans have learned to use structures designed for biological survival (breathing, eating) and adapt them for speech. Example: lips biologically hold food in → overlaid function is producing speech sounds.
flashcards.source: Lecture 2.2 — Ch. 3, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

flashcards.back: The VISIBLE characteristics of stuttering — physical struggle behaviors that develop when trying NOT to stutter. Also called 'tricks or crutches.' Include: losing eye contact at the moment of stuttering, rapid eye blinking, furrowing the forehead, tensing facial muscles, jerking the head, tensing/raising the shoulders, swinging or jerking an arm, clenching a fist, pressing thumb and fingernail together, tensing chest muscles, moving upper/lower leg, pulling foot toward shin, tapping foot, tensing toes — or any other physical movement distracting to the listener. These behaviors emerge from STRUGGLE, not from the stutter itself.
flashcards.source: Fluency_Disorders.pdf / Lecture Ch. 12, Kidwell

flashcards.back: Wart-like growths on the vocal folds or larynx caused by a VIRUS (human papillomavirus). An organic voice disorder requiring medical/surgical treatment. Most commonly occur in CHILDREN aged 4–6 years (juvenile papillomatosis). Can recur after surgical removal. The SLP works with the client post-treatment but medical management is primary.
flashcards.source: Lecture Ch. 13, Kidwell / Ch. 13 PPTX

flashcards.back: Non-verbal contextual elements that contribute to the meaning of a communication beyond the spoken words. Includes facial expressions, body language, clothing, artifacts, and environmental context. Example: knowing someone is the teacher because they have the key, a briefcase, and stand at the front of the class.
flashcards.source: Lecture 1.2.1 — Chapter 1, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

flashcards.back: A technique in which a caregiver or clinician names, describes, and explains what the child is experiencing and probably feeling, almost as if the caregiver is the child speaking for them. Provides language concepts and models for the child. A form of inner speech scaffolding. Example: 'You're building a tower. The blocks are tall!'
flashcards.source: Ch. 4 PDF — Prof. Handy / Quiz answer key

flashcards.back: A naturally occurring, higher-pitched, slower-paced, melodic speech style that caregivers use when talking to infants. Characterized by frequent pauses, exaggerated intonation, and simplified vocabulary. Infants respond to the tone and melody of parentese before they understand the words.
flashcards.source: Lecture 3.2 — Ch. 4, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

flashcards.back: Paresis: partial weakness or reduced strength of a muscle or group of muscles. Paralysis: complete loss of voluntary movement. Both can affect the speech muscles in dysarthria. Dysarthria may involve paresis, paralysis, OR incoordination of the muscles of respiration, phonation, resonation, and articulation.
flashcards.source: Ch. 6 PPTX — Fogle/SJDC

flashcards.back: Receives and processes sensory information from the body (touch, pressure, temperature, pain). Located behind the frontal lobe. Involved in spatial awareness and integrating sensory input.
flashcards.source: Lecture 2.2 — Ch. 3, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

flashcards.back: A progressive neurodegenerative disease of the basal ganglia causing hypokinetic dysarthria. Associated speech characteristics: reduced vocal loudness (hypophonia), monopitch (reduced pitch variation), monoloudness, imprecise consonants, rapid rate with short rushes of speech (festination), and reduced intelligibility. LSVT LOUD (Lee Silverman Voice Treatment) is the most evidence-based treatment for PD-related dysarthria. PD is one of the most commonly associated neuromuscular disorders with dysarthria.
flashcards.source: Lecture Ch. 17, Kidwell / Ch. 17 PPTX

flashcards.back: Allows the CNS to communicate with the body. Composed of cranial nerves exiting the brainstem and spinal nerves exiting the spinal cord, plus sensory nerves throughout the body. Carries sensory information TO the brain and motor commands FROM the brain to muscles.
flashcards.source: Lecture 2.2 — Ch. 3, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

flashcards.back: A frequency modulation (FM) assistive listening device used as part of APD treatment. The teacher or speaker wears a microphone/transmitter; the child wears a receiver that delivers the speaker's voice directly to the child's ears (bypassing distance and background noise). Improves signal-to-noise ratio dramatically. One of the three components of the environmental/assistive APD treatment approach.
flashcards.source: Ch. 9 PPTX — Fogle/SJDC

flashcards.back: The study of speech-sound production and the special symbols that represent speech sounds. The scientific foundation for analyzing, transcribing, and describing speech. SLPs use phonetics to identify and document errors in speech production.
flashcards.source: Ch. 5 slides — Fogle/SJDC

flashcards.back: Systematic, rule-based sound simplification patterns children use during speech development. Include: syllable structure processes (e.g., reducing consonant clusters), substitution processes (replacing harder sounds with easier ones), and assimilatory processes (sounds influenced by neighboring sounds). Most resolve by age 5.
flashcards.source: Ch. 4 PDF — Prof. Handy, SJDC

flashcards.front: Define: Phonological Processes (Common in Typical Development)
flashcards.back:
Rule-based sound simplification patterns that are normal in young children but become disordered when they persist beyond expected ages. Common processes from Table 5-3:
• Context-sensitive voicing: voiceless sound replaced by voiced (pig→big)
• Final-consonant devoicing: voiced final consonant replaced by voiceless (bag→bat)
• Final-consonant deletion: final consonant omitted (home→hoe)
• Velar fronting: velar consonant /k,g/ replaced by alveolar /t,d/ (key→tea)
• Palatal fronting: /sh,zh/ replaced by alveolar sounds
• Consonant harmony: whole word influenced by single sound (dog→gog)
• Weak-syllable deletion: unstressed syllables omitted (telephone→teffone)
• Cluster reduction: part of consonant cluster omitted (spider→pider, spoon→poon)
• Gliding of liquids: /r,l/ replaced by /w,j/ (red→wed)
• Stopping: fricative/affricate replaced by stop (funny→punny)
flashcards.source: Ch. 5 slides — Fogle/SJDC / Lecture 4.2, Kidwell

flashcards.back: The study of speech sounds and the rules for using them in a language. Key unit: the phoneme — the smallest unit of sound that is distinct from others. Written using / / notation (e.g., /b/, /s/). Not the same as letters — e.g., 'c' has no phonetic equivalent of its own.
flashcards.source: Lecture 1.2.1 — Chapter 1, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

flashcards.back: The study of how body structures function and work. In SLP, physiology refers to how the speech structures move, contract, and coordinate to produce communication. A person can have intact anatomy but impaired physiology.
flashcards.source: Lecture 2.2 — Ch. 3, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

flashcards.back: The psychological (perceptual) sensation of frequency — how high or low a voice sounds to a listener. Pitch changes are based on vocal fold tension, mass, and length. Distinct from frequency, which is an objective measurement.
flashcards.source: Lecture 2.2 — Ch. 3, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

flashcards.back: Where in the vocal tract the articulators make contact or approach to produce a consonant sound. The seven places of articulation are: Bilabial (both lips), Labiodental (lip + teeth), Interdental (tongue between teeth), Alveolar (tongue + alveolar ridge), Palatal (tongue + hard palate), Velar (tongue + soft palate/velum), Glottal (at the glottis/vocal folds).
flashcards.source: Ch. 5 slides — Fogle/SJDC

flashcards.front: Define: Pragmatic Disorder / Social Communication Disorder
flashcards.back: Difficulty with the USE of language in social situations. Pragmatics governs how language is used socially — rules that vary by culture. In the U.S., pragmatic expectations include: appropriate eye contact, personal space, turn-taking, topic maintenance, volume control, and reading social cues. A child with a pragmatic disorder may violate these rules — e.g., interrupting class inappropriately, speaking too loudly, not understanding compliments, not maintaining topic, or not recognizing when a listener has lost interest. Classic example: child with ASD who has correct vocabulary and grammar but inappropriate social language use.
flashcards.source: Lecture Ch. 7, Kidwell, SJDC

flashcards.back: The rules of use of language in social situations. Concerned with the function of language rather than its structure. Includes verbal and nonverbal cues, physical appearance, body language, timing, and context. Children/adults on the Autism Spectrum often have pragmatic delays — social language does not 'click' naturally.
flashcards.source: Lecture 1.2.1 — Chapter 1, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

flashcards.back: The anterior portion of the frontal lobe. Responsible for higher cognitive functions: attention, reasoning, judgment, decision-making, problem-solving, personality, character, philosophies, religious and political beliefs.
flashcards.source: CSD Ch3 PP (Fogle 3rd ed.)

flashcards.back: Sounds produced by an infant before true words appear (birth to approximately 12 months). Include crying, cooing, babbling, echolalia, vocal play, and jargon. These are preverbal but communicative — the foundation of speech and language development.
flashcards.source: Lecture 3.2 — Ch. 4, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

flashcards.back:
Three time-based environmental risk factors for communication disorders:
• Prenatal (during pregnancy): substance abuse, syndromes, low birth weight, prematurity — can cause anatomic/physiologic problems or developmental delay. Maternal substance abuse is the only 100% preventable cause.
• Perinatal (during birth): insufficient oxygen (e.g., cord around neck) → neurologic damage (anoxia/hypoxia)
• Postnatal (after birth): limited environmental stimulation → fewer synaptic connections in brain (e.g., infant left in crib without verbal or visual interaction).
flashcards.source: Lecture 4.2, Kidwell, SJDC

flashcards.back: Prompts for Restructuring Oral Muscular Phonetic Targets — a tactile-kinesthetic approach to motor speech therapy in which the clinician uses manual cues on the client's face/jaw/lips to guide correct articulatory placement. Commonly used in CAS treatment as part of a multi-modal approach.
flashcards.source: Ch. 6 PPTX — Fogle/SJDC

flashcards.front: Define: Psychosocial Consequences of Motor Speech Disorders
flashcards.back: Individuals with motor speech disorders are often acutely aware of their difficulty being understood. Attempts at communication can be filled with frustration. Consequences include: avoidance of communication, social withdrawal, reduced quality of life, depression and anxiety, difficulty maintaining relationships, and reduced independence. For those with AOS, the harder they try to correct errors, the more difficult speech production becomes — creating a frustrating cycle. SLPs must address emotional and social effects as part of treatment.
flashcards.source: Motor_Speech_Disorders.pdf / Lecture Ch. 17, Kidwell

flashcards.back: The biological characteristics shared by a group of people. Distinct from culture, dialect, and language — race is a biological classification, while language and dialect differences are cultural and linguistic, not biological.
flashcards.source: Quiz answer key — Ch. 4

flashcards.back: The limits to which the articulators can move for speech: how far the mandible can open/close; how far the lips can protrude and retract; how far the tongue can protrude, retract, elevate, lower, and lateralize. Reduced range of motion can impair speech intelligibility.
flashcards.source: CSD Ch3 PP (Fogle 3rd ed.)

flashcards.back:
The process by which a listener INFERS the meaning of an auditory message based on the context of the information and long-term stored memory of words and general knowledge. It is an ACTIVE process. Receptive language = what we understand WITHOUT producing words. Also called language comprehension or auditory comprehension. Children with SLI may struggle with: normal rate of conversation, direct and indirect questions, following directions, comprehension of individual words, and the subtleties of connected speech.
★ Language impairment WITH a receptive component is MORE RESISTANT TO INTERVENTION and carries GREATER RISK for adverse academic outcomes than expressive-only impairment.
flashcards.source: Lecture Ch. 7, Kidwell / Ch. 7 slides

flashcards.front: Define: Receptive Language Characteristics in SLI
flashcards.back:
Children with SLI and a receptive component:
• Spoken words come at them too fast — difficulty keeping up with normal conversation rate
• Understand DIRECT statements more easily than INDIRECT versions of the same message
• LITERAL interpretation of idioms, metaphors, similes — cannot grasp figurative language
• Difficulty with abstract concepts
• Difficulty expressing wants, needs, thoughts, knowledge, and feelings
• Vocabulary weaknesses persist through primary grades and junior high school

Adolescent receptive problems: weak vocabulary, difficulty with figurative/abstract language, difficulty following directions and understanding lectures, poor listening skills, misinterpreting facial expressions.
flashcards.source: Ch. 7 slides / Lecture Ch. 7, Kidwell

flashcards.back: The process by which sound created at the vocal folds travels upward through cavities in the head — the throat, the oral cavity, and the nasal cavity — and is shaped to produce individual voice quality.
flashcards.source: Eugene Speech Language Therapy — How We Speak (YouTube, 11xf89bXaCg)

flashcards.back: The third of four speech production processes. How sound produced by the larynx is modified by the shape of the vocal tract. Involves airflow through the throat, nose (nasal sounds), and mouth. Nasal sounds like /m/ involve nasal resonance; non-nasal sounds like those in 'soup' do not.
flashcards.source: Lecture 1.2.1 — Chapter 1, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

flashcards.back: Caused by abnormal structure or functioning of the hard and soft palates. Results in hypernasal voice (sounds like talking through the nose) or hyponasal voice (sounds lack normal nasal resonance).
flashcards.source: Lecture 1.2.1 — Chapter 1, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

flashcards.back: The breathing process that provides the energy source behind speech production. During speech, the inhale-to-exhale ratio shifts dramatically to approximately 10% inhalation and 90% exhalation, compared to the resting ratio of 50/50 or 40/60.
flashcards.source: Eugene Speech Language Therapy — How We Speak (YouTube, 11xf89bXaCg)

flashcards.back: The first of four speech production processes. Provides the breath support needed to produce speech. Requires lungs, diaphragm, and chest muscles to be correctly formed and functioning. Problems with respiration impact speech sound production and intelligibility.
flashcards.source: Lecture 1.2.1 — Chapter 1, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

flashcards.back: Responsible for attention, orientation, emotions, and cognition. Controls all movements for the left side of the body. Works with the left hemisphere via the corpus callosum to comprehend, integrate, and formulate language.
flashcards.source: Lecture 2.2 — Ch. 3, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

flashcards.front: Define: Sariah Video — Expressive Language Disorder (Clinical Analysis)
flashcards.back:
Sariah's retelling of Beverly Hills Chihuahua is a classroom example of expressive language disorder with the following observable features:
• MORPHOLOGICAL errors: 'puted' for 'put' (irregular past tense error — overgeneralization); 'was trying to get' (correct) but inconsistent
• SYNTACTIC errors: incomplete sentences; pronoun errors ('papy found it' — unclear referent)
• NARRATIVE difficulty: characters introduced without clear identification; events out of sequence; critical plot elements omitted; listener cannot follow without prior knowledge of the movie
• VOCABULARY: some topic vocabulary intact but limited; relies on vague language
• MLU is low for apparent age
This child has expressive language disorder affecting morphology, syntax, and narrative organization — NOT just a speech sound disorder.
flashcards.source: Sariah video transcript / Lecture Ch. 7, Kidwell

flashcards.back: The defined range of professional activities, responsibilities, and services that a licensed SLP or Audiologist is qualified and authorized to perform, as established by ASHA.
flashcards.source: Lecture 1.2.2 — Chapter 2: The Professionals, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

flashcards.front: Define: Semantic Problems in SLI (Vocabulary and Concept Development)
flashcards.back:
Children with SLI are at high risk for vocabulary and semantic difficulties:
• Delay in using first true words; slow vocabulary growth (fewer than 20 words by 18–24 months)
• Difficulty producing two-word combinations by 24 months
• Poor concept development: adjectives (shape, size, color), quantity, quality; verbs and verb markers (-ed, -ing)
• Word-finding problems — know the word but cannot retrieve it
• Difficulty asking wh- questions (who, what, when, where, why, how)
• Difficulty relating narratives (sequenced stories)
• Difficulty with basic concept words: over, under, next to, first, left, farthest
flashcards.source: Ch. 7 slides / Lecture Ch. 7, Kidwell

flashcards.back: Perspective that emphasizes the interrelationship between language learning and cognition — the meanings conveyed by a child's productions. Meaning precedes form: children understand what they want to communicate before knowing the correct word or grammar. Clinical application: adjust complexity, amount, or rate of speech. Limitation: does not fully explain how language structure is acquired.
flashcards.source: Lecture 3.2 — Ch. 4, Kidwell / Ch. 4 PDF — Prof. Handy

flashcards.back: The study of meaning in language. Includes vocabulary, categories, and idioms (figures of speech). Dialectical differences in semantics are not disorders. Example: 'trunk' (US) vs. 'boot' (UK) for the back storage of a car. A delay in vocabulary significantly hinders communication.
flashcards.source: Lecture 1.2.1 — Chapter 1, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

flashcards.back: The study of meaning in language. Semantic difficulties in SLI include: vocabulary problems (both receptive and expressive), difficulty with basic concept words (over, under, next to, first), category knowledge, idioms, word definitions, and abstract concepts. Word-finding problems are common — the child 'knows' the word but cannot retrieve it on demand. Poor semantic development is one of the earliest and most consistent markers of SLI.
flashcards.source: Lecture Ch. 7, Kidwell, SJDC

flashcards.front: Define: Sensorimotor Nature of Motor Speech Disorders
flashcards.back: Motor speech disorders have a sensory component — they are SENSORIMOTOR in nature, not purely motor dysfunction. The sensory feedback systems (proprioception, auditory, tactile) that monitor and regulate speech production are also affected. This is why treatment approaches that incorporate multiple sensory modalities (tactile cues, visual feedback, auditory monitoring) are more effective than purely motor-based approaches.
flashcards.source: Motor_Speech_Disorders.pdf / Ch. 17 PPTX

flashcards.back: A reduction in hearing caused by damage to the cochlea and/or auditory nerve. Distinguished from conductive hearing loss by the location of the damage (inner ear / nerve rather than outer/middle ear).
flashcards.source: Lecture 1.2.1 — Chapter 1, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

flashcards.front: Define: SLI Red Flags (Late Talker Warning Signs)
flashcards.back:
Warning signs that a child may be at risk for SLI — late talkers can alert parents and clinicians:
• Slow development of speech sounds
• Does not say 'Mama' or 'Dada' by 12–18 months
• ★ First true word does not appear until AFTER 18 months
• ★ Two-word combinations not used until AFTER 30 months
• Restricted vocabulary in both comprehension and production
• Reliance on gestures to get needs met
• Infrequent use of verbs; poor verb development
• Lack of yes/no responses to questions
• Difficulty initiating interactions with peers and turn-taking
• Difficulty rhyming words and naming letters
flashcards.source: Ch. 7 slides — Fogle/SJDC

flashcards.back:
After ENT medical clearance, the SLP assessment includes:
• CASE HISTORY: client interview (voice problem, medications, smoking, alcohol use); reading the ENT medical report
• LISTENING AND OBSERVING: loudness, pitch, quality, throat clearing, tension in voice/face/body
• HEARING SCREENING
• QUANTIFICATION OF THE VOICE using rating scales:
  - CAPE-V (Consensus Auditory-Perceptual-Evaluation of Voice)
  - GRBAS Scale (Grade, Roughness, Breathiness, Asthenic, Strain)
• PERCEPTUAL features: what we hear (loudness, pitch, quality)
• ACOUSTIC features (instrumental): habitual pitch, pitch range, optimal pitch, maximum phonation time
• PHYSIOLOGIC features: aerodynamic features, vibratory behavior, airflow rates/pressures, masses on folds
• INSTRUMENTAL TOOLS: VisiPitch, Computerized Speech Lab (CSL)
• BASELINE established to measure therapy progress
flashcards.source: Lecture Ch. 13, Kidwell / Ch. 13 PPTX

flashcards.back: Perspective that communication is the basic function of language, developed through caregiver interaction. Prerequisites: caregiver proximity, basic physical needs met, attachment, joint attention, and turn-taking. Clinical application: social games, interactive reading. Limitation: does not explain why all humans develop similar grammar and phonology despite unique social experiences.
flashcards.source: Lecture 3.2 — Ch. 4, Kidwell / Ch. 4 PDF — Prof. Handy

flashcards.back: A mode of communication and a motor act of producing oral language. One of the most complicated fine motor skills humans learn. Involves four coordinated processes: respiration, phonation, resonance, and articulation.
flashcards.source: Lecture 1.2.1 — Chapter 1, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

flashcards.back: The progressive evolving and shaping of individual sounds and syllables that are used as arbitrary symbols and applied in rule-governed combinations to produce words to communicate a person's wants, needs, thoughts, knowledge, and feelings. Divided into 4 stages: Birth–12 months, 12–24 months, 2–5 years, 6–12 years.
flashcards.source: Lecture 3.2 — Ch. 4, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

flashcards.back:
No single theory explains everything; significant expert disagreement. Three main theoretical categories:
1. BREAKDOWN THEORIES: early environmental stress combined with neurologic predisposition accounts for stuttering
2. REPRESSED NEED THEORIES: stuttering stems from unmet/repressed emotional needs (Kidwell notes this as 'unfounded and antiquated')
3. ANTICIPATORY STRUGGLE BEHAVIOR THEORIES: stuttering results from reactions to penalties applied to what would otherwise be normal disfluencies — fearing and struggling to avoid stuttering CREATES it

★ Kidwell's view: MULTIFACTORIAL is most productive. Believes: neurological mistiming + genetic tendency → stuttering appears under stress → anxiety about stuttering causes avoidance → leads to overt struggle behaviors → more anxiety → more stuttering (vicious cycle).
flashcards.source: Lecture Ch. 12, Kidwell, SJDC

flashcards.back:
• Affects approximately 1% of the population
• In 90% of those affected, onset is early — typically around 2–6 years of age
• At onset: slightly more common in boys
• By school age: girls more likely to recover naturally; male-to-female ratio becomes approximately 3–4:1 (males outnumber females)
• Tends to RUN IN FAMILIES (genetic component)
• Environmental factors also play a role
• Often co-occurs with other communication disorders, but can be the only disorder
• Stutterers are within normal limits for intelligence
• Do NOT share a specific personality trait — not a symptom of a personality disorder
• Brain function during stuttering differs from brain function when not stuttering (cause vs. compensation unclear)
flashcards.source: Lecture Ch. 12, Kidwell, SJDC

flashcards.front: Define: Stuttering Clinical Threshold — 10 Words per 100
flashcards.back: A clinical guideline for identifying possible stuttering in children: stuttering on MORE THAN 10 WORDS OUT OF 100 may indicate the child is having a problem. However, this is not a standalone criterion — other factors must also be considered (severity of disfluency, secondary behaviors, family history, impact on communication). Only a speech-language pathologist trained in the diagnosis and treatment of stuttering is qualified to make a formal diagnosis.
flashcards.source: 65_Cause_Treatment_of_Stuttering.pdf — Ducworth, CCC-SLP

flashcards.back: A therapy approach focused on reducing the negative forces that drive stuttering — fear, avoidance, and escape behaviors — rather than on producing perfectly fluent speech. The goal is COMMUNICATION IMPROVEMENT, and spontaneous fluency emerges as a byproduct of reduced fear and avoidance. THERAPY EMPHASIS: developing insight into attitudes, thoughts, and feelings about stuttering. Client actively participates in identifying and confronting avoidance patterns. Per Gregory (2003): four areas of emphasis — (1) insight into attitudes/thoughts/feelings, (2) awareness of muscular tension through relaxation, (3) speech analysis and modification, (4) building new speech skills.
flashcards.source: Lecture Ch. 12, Kidwell / Fluency_Disorders.pdf

flashcards.back: The most common formal assessment tool for measuring stuttering. Takes into account four factors: (1) types of disfluencies, (2) rate of disfluencies, (3) physical behaviors (visible overt behaviors), and (4) naturalness of speech. Results place the individual in a range from normal disfluencies to severe stuttering. Used to determine need for therapy, intensity of treatment, and to monitor progress.
flashcards.source: Lecture Ch. 12, Kidwell, SJDC

flashcards.front: Define: Stuttering Therapy — Adolescents and Adults
flashcards.back:
Working with adolescents and adults who stutter requires specific considerations:
• INTERNAL MOTIVATION is essential — the stutterer must want to change
• Emphasis is on the stutterer's own perception of the problem
• Strong emotions and opinions may exist due to past therapy experiences
• CHANGE (evolution, revolution, resolution) is required for success
• Many stutterers do not complete the full duration of therapy; those who do have a good chance of long-lasting benefits
Per Gregory (2003): four therapy emphasis areas for adolescents/adults:
1. Insight into attitudes, thoughts, and feelings about stuttering
2. Increasing awareness of muscular tension through relaxation exercises
3. Speech analysis and modification
4. Building new speech skills
flashcards.source: Lecture Ch. 12, Kidwell, SJDC

flashcards.front: Define: Stuttering Therapy — Children (Prevention and Direct Therapy)
flashcards.back:
Not all children who are disfluent need direct therapy. For at-risk children:
• SLPs focus on PREVENTION — modifying exposure conditions, changing susceptibility
• Parents and teachers are given verbal and written strategies to improve speech fluency and decrease negative behaviors/emotions
• The STUTTERING FOUNDATION OF AMERICA provides resources and strategies (stutteringhelp.org)

When direct therapy is needed:
• Goals created based on child's individual needs and behaviors
• Open child-clinician relationship essential — building TRUST and CONFIDENCE
• Clinician must be understanding and accepting
• Show parents how to help at home
• Build self-confidence throughout therapy
• Providing SUCCESSFUL SPEAKING ACTIVITIES and OPPORTUNITIES is helpful
flashcards.source: Lecture Ch. 12, Kidwell / Fluency_Disorders.pdf

flashcards.back: Air pressure that builds up below the closed (adducted) vocal folds. When sufficient pressure builds, it forces the vocal folds open. Bernoulli's law then causes them to snap back shut, producing the vibration cycle that creates voice.
flashcards.source: Lecture 2.2 — Ch. 3, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

flashcards.back: An articulation error in which one sound is replaced by another. The most common type of articulation error. Example: 'tat' for 'cat' (/t/ substituted for /k/ in initial position). In IPA notation written as: initial /t/ for initial /k/.
flashcards.source: Lecture 4.2, Kidwell, SJDC

flashcards.back: A behavioral therapy technique in which attempts toward a target behavior (e.g., a word) are reinforced and gradually shaped toward the correct form. Example: 'poon' → 'spoon' — each closer attempt is reinforced. Used in SLP behavioral therapy.
flashcards.source: Ch. 4 PDF — Prof. Handy, SJDC

flashcards.back: The junction between two neurons where a nerve impulse passes from one cell to another via neurotransmitters. Articulation therapy works by causing changes in neuronal firing and synaptic connections — essentially retraining the brain.
flashcards.source: CSD Ch3 PP (Fogle 3rd ed.)

flashcards.back: The study of the rules for combining words into sentences to convey meaning. Different languages use different structural rules. Example: 'I am going to the store' vs. 'I am coming from the store' — one or two word changes significantly alters meaning. Non-native English speakers often struggle with syntax.
flashcards.source: Lecture 1.2.1 — Chapter 1, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

flashcards.front: Define: Syntax and Morphology (Language Disorder Context)
flashcards.back: Syntax: the rules for combining words into grammatically correct sentences. Morphology: the rules for modifying words using grammatical markers (prefixes, suffixes, inflections). Children with SLI show: incorrect sentence order ('Car fast go'), missing or incorrect morphological markers ('He walking', 'Those childs goed'), omission of auxiliaries ('is', 'am', 'are'), and inability to use complex sentence structures. These errors persist into school age and affect reading and writing.
flashcards.source: Lecture Ch. 7, Kidwell, SJDC

flashcards.back: Condensed language in which only the essential words are used — typically nouns, verbs, and adjectives — with function words (articles, prepositions) omitted. Example: 'daddy go store' instead of 'Daddy is going to the store.' Characteristic of 2–3 year olds.
flashcards.source: Ch. 4 PDF — Prof. Handy / Quiz answer key

flashcards.back: Responsible for auditory processing. Receives auditory information and sends it to Wernicke's area for language interpretation. Located on the sides of the brain, below the lateral sulcus.
flashcards.source: Lecture 2.2 — Ch. 3, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

flashcards.back: The upper part of the trunk of the body. Contains the lungs and heart — the principal organs of respiration. Protected by the rib cage, sternum, and vertebrae which form the skeletal framework of the respiratory system.
flashcards.source: Lecture 2.2 — Ch. 3, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

flashcards.front: Define: Three Etiologies of Communication Disorders in Children
flashcards.back:
The three general causes of communication disorders in children:
1. Normal Variation: ~70% of children are within normal range; 15% above average, 15% below. SLPs work with children significantly below the mean.
2. Environmental Problems: prenatal (substance abuse, syndromes, prematurity), perinatal (insufficient oxygen/hypoxia at birth), or postnatal (limited stimulation → fewer synaptic connections).
3. Physical Impairments or Neurologic Differences: congenital syndromes, sensory impairments, TBI, CP.
Most children with communication disorders have MULTIPLE causative factors (multifactorial). Single-factor causes are less common.
flashcards.source: Lecture 4.2, Kidwell / Ch. 5 slides

flashcards.front: Define: Three Prerequisites for Normal Language Development
flashcards.back: For language to develop typically, three elements must be integrated: (1) Biological structures and functions must develop within normal limits; (2) Cognitive functioning must be sufficient for language skills to emerge and be cultivated; (3) Social interaction with caregivers must be sufficient to encourage and reward communication attempts.
flashcards.source: Lecture 3.2 — Ch. 4, Kidwell / Ch. 4 PDF — Prof. Handy

flashcards.back: The largest cartilage of the larynx, commonly known as the 'voice box' or Adam's apple. Part of the laryngeal framework that houses and protects the vocal folds.
flashcards.source: Lecture 2.2 — Ch. 3, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

flashcards.back: An abnormal swallowing pattern where the tongue thrusts or protrudes forward during swallowing (rather than sweeping back). Can affect both speech and dentition. Treated with myofunctional therapy, sometimes before orthodontic treatment.
flashcards.source: Lecture 2.2 — Ch. 3, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

flashcards.back: The airway (windpipe) located below the vocal folds and above the lungs through which air passes during respiration and speech.
flashcards.source: Eugene Speech Language Therapy — How We Speak (YouTube, 11xf89bXaCg)

flashcards.back: Voice modification services for transgender and gender-diverse individuals are within the SLP scope of practice. This is NOT a voice disorder — it is helping individuals achieve a voice that better matches their gender identity (transmasculine or transfeminine goals). Requires increased awareness and sensitivity. Standardized training on supporting gender-diverse individuals' communication in SLP graduate curriculum has not yet been developed.
flashcards.source: Ch. 13 PPTX — Fogle/SJDC

flashcards.back: The small, fleshy projection hanging at the posterior end of the soft palate. Moves with the soft palate during speech and swallowing.
flashcards.source: Lecture 2.2 — Ch. 3, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

flashcards.back: Communication that uses spoken language — the words and sentences we hear and produce. Speech and language skills form the foundation for reading and writing, which are higher-level language skills.
flashcards.source: Lecture 1.2.1 — Chapter 1, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

flashcards.back: The body's system for maintaining balance and spatial orientation, controlled by the inner ear. Audiologists identify, evaluate, diagnose, and treat disorders of vestibular function in addition to hearing disorders.
flashcards.source: Lecture 1.2.2 — Chapter 2: The Professionals, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

flashcards.back: A pair of tissue structures located above the trachea and lungs. When pushed together and subjected to airflow from the lungs, they vibrate rapidly to produce sound. Also called vocal cords.
flashcards.source: Eugene Speech Language Therapy — How We Speak (YouTube, 11xf89bXaCg)

flashcards.back: Benign, often BILATERAL vocal fold lesions caused by abuse and misuse of the voice (hyperfunction). Also called 'cheerleaders' nodes,' 'screamers' nodes,' or 'singers' nodes.' Common causes: yelling, talking at too high a pitch, frequent throat clearing, chronic vocal strain. The most common voice disorder in children aged 1–18 (~60% of cases). PREVENTABLE and TREATABLE: SLP helps the client identify and eliminate the abusive behaviors (vocal hygiene), which allows the nodules to heal and disappear. Medical clearance from an ENT is required before SLP treatment.
flashcards.source: Lecture Ch. 13, Kidwell / Ch. 13 PPTX

flashcards.back: Longer strings of syllables that extend babbling (e.g., baa-da-gi-daa-um-ma). Occurs during the 6–12 month period. The infant experiments with sounds and syllable combinations for social interaction and enjoyment.
flashcards.source: Ch. 4 PDF — Prof. Handy, SJDC

flashcards.back: Benign vocal fold lesions of various forms with the same causes as vocal nodules — repeated vocal hyperfunction and chronic misuse/abuse. Unlike nodules (which tend to be bilateral), polyps can vary in form and location. A common cause of voice disorders in adults aged 19–60 (12% of cases). GERD (gastroesophageal reflux disorder) can also cause acid laryngitis contributing to polyp formation. Like nodules, polyps are preventable and treatable through vocal hygiene and voice therapy with ENT clearance.
flashcards.source: Lecture Ch. 13, Kidwell / Ch. 13 PPTX

flashcards.back:
Any deviation of LOUDNESS, PITCH, or QUALITY that is outside the normal range for a person's age, gender, or geographic/cultural background, AND that interferes with communication, draws unfavorable attention to itself, or adversely affects the speaker or the listener (Fogle). Voice is affected by the anatomy and physiology of the respiratory system, vocal folds, and supraglottal vocal tract (the structures ABOVE the glottis). Voice reflects personality, emotions, and attitudes — a disruption to voice must be treated.

Subjective voice quality terms: hoarse, breathy, harsh, husky, raucous, grating, strident, thin, strained, tense, weak, tremulous, monotone.
flashcards.source: Ch. 13 PPTX / Lecture Ch. 13, Kidwell

flashcards.front: Define: Voice Disorder Causes by Age Group
flashcards.back:
Most common voice disorder causes by age:
• CHILDREN (1–18 years): vocal NODULES (~60%), vocal cysts (~10%), acute laryngitis (~7%)
• ADULTS (19–60 years): functional dysphonia (20.5%), acid laryngitis from GERD (12.5%), vocal polyps (12%)
• ADULTS (60+ years): PRESBYPHONIA (26.5%), functional dysphonia (~17.5%), polypoid degeneration (14%)

Presbyphonia = age-related voice changes.
flashcards.source: Ch. 13 PPTX — Fogle/SJDC

flashcards.back:
Prevalence of voice disorders:
• Children: approximately 6%
• Individuals aged 0 to 65+: approximately 1%
• Females: ~1%; Males: ~0.5%
• Adults older than 70 years: 2.5%
Most frequent diagnoses: acute laryngitis, nonspecific dysphonia, benign vocal fold lesions (nodules, polyps, contact ulcers), chronic laryngitis. Laryngeal cancer ~2% (mostly males).
★ TEACHERS (including professors) are more likely to have a voice disorder than any other profession.
flashcards.source: Ch. 13 PPTX — Fogle/SJDC

flashcards.back: The subjective judgment of how a voice sounds. Affected by adequacy of vocal fold closure, efficiency of timing, and amount of tonicity in the folds. A normal voice is not hoarse, breathy, or choppy.
flashcards.source: Lecture 2.2 — Ch. 3, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

flashcards.back: Whether the vocal folds are vibrating or not during sound production. Voiced sounds: vocal folds vibrate (e.g., /b/, /d/, /g/, /v/, /z/). Voiceless sounds: vocal folds do not vibrate (e.g., /p/, /t/, /k/, /f/, /s/). All vowels are voiced. Feeling throat vibration is a common way to detect voicing (though pairing with a vowel can be misleading).
flashcards.source: Lecture 4.2, Kidwell, SJDC

flashcards.back: Voiced speech sounds produced by the unrestricted passage of the airstream through the mouth without audible stoppage or friction. All vowels are voiced. The placement and shape of the tongue determine the vowel sound. Described on the Vowel Quadrilateral by height (high/mid/low) and position (front/central/back). All vowels are voiced.
flashcards.source: Ch. 5 slides — Fogle/SJDC / Lecture 4.2, Kidwell

flashcards.back: A fluent aphasia caused by damage in or around Wernicke's area in the auditory association cortex of the left temporal lobe. Characteristics: impaired AUDITORY COMPREHENSION (cannot grasp meaning of spoken words), impaired verbal expression, impaired reading comprehension, impaired written language. Patient can speak fluently with normal rate and intonation but speech may not make sense; patient may not know their speech is wrong and get frustrated when misunderstood. Features include: literal and verbal paraphasias, neologisms, jargon, difficulty with yes/no questions, difficulty following rapid speech, lacking awareness of errors. Fluency: +, Comprehension: −, Repetition: −, Naming: −.
flashcards.source: Ch. 15 PPTX; Kidwell lecture notes

flashcards.back: The location within a word where an articulation error occurs. Initial position = beginning of the word (e.g., 'cat' → /k/ is initial). Medial position = middle of the word. Final position = end of the word. SLPs document whether a child consistently makes an error in one, two, or all three positions, which influences therapy targeting.
flashcards.source: Lecture 4.2, Kidwell, SJDC

flashcards.back: The opening of the vocal folds.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: Damage to hearing from a transient, high-intensity sound.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: A disorder that begins after an individual has developed normal communication abilities, such as a hearing loss from loud noise exposure or a speech, language, or cognitive disorder caused by a traumatic brain injury.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: Tasks performed in a typical day that allow independent living in the home, community, and work and school environments, such as dressing, hygiene, and self-feeding, with more advanced ADLs including cooking, performing household chores, doing laundry, telephoning, using transportation, shopping, and managing personal finances.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: A hospital where patients are treated for brief but severe episodes of illness, injury, trauma, or during recovery from surgery.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: An abrupt, intense, and usually relatively brief inflammation of the mucous membrane lining in the larynx, accompanied by edema of the vocal folds with hoarseness and loss of voice that is often caused by severe vocal abuse.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: Intense and of short duration, usually referring to a disease or injury.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: The ability to act as independently and responsibly as other people of the same age and cultural background; includes conceptual skills (e.g., language and literacy, time, and number concepts), social skills (e.g., interpersonal skills, social problem solving, and the ability to follow rules and obey laws); and practical skills (e.g., personal care, occupational skills, basic healthcare, travel/transportation, schedules/routines, safety, use of money, use of the telephone).
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: The insertion of a sound or sounds not part of the word itself, such as animamal for animal.
