flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: The closing of the vocal folds.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: Lymphoid tissue found on the pharyngeal wall in the nasal-pharyngeal region. They begin to develop at about 6 months of age and may continue to grow and possibly interfere with the Eustachian tubes. When enlarged, adenoids may block the passage of air from the nasal cavity into the pharynx, preventing normal nasal breathing. Sometimes surgically removed to allow nasal breathing and help prevent middle ear infections.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: Relating to, arising from, or influencing feelings or emotions; affect is revealed by facial expressions, body posture and gestures, tone of voice, and choice of words.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: Impairment of grammar and syntax and the ability to produce words in their correct sequence and with all necessary morphemes.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: An acquired disorder of writing resulting in difficulty writing letters, words, and sentences.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: The transmission of sound to the inner ear through the external auditory canal and the structures of the middle ear.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: Impaired ability to read as the result of neurological damage.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: Letters and combinations of letters represent speech sounds; speech can be turned into print; and print can be turned into speech.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: The spongy tissue of the lungs where gas exchange takes place. Walls of alveoli are one cell thick and porous, allowing rapid transfer of fresh oxygen into the capillary bed surrounding the alveoli, and of carbon dioxide from the capillary bed into the alveoli to be exhaled.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: A neurological disease that causes progressive dementia that usually begins after age 65 and in many cases is related to genetic factors that cause microscopic changes to both gray and white matter, resulting in cerebral atrophy, and is characterized by decline in memory, intellect, disorientation, delusions, personality changes, and communication impairments.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: Background sounds that are present in an environment or location, such as wind, water, birds, crowds, office noises, traffic, and so on.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: A manual system of communication commonly used by members of the Deaf culture in the United States; sometimes referred to as Ameslan.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.front: Define: Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS; Lou Gehrig's disease)
flashcards.back: A rare, rapidly progressive degenerative disease of motor neurons that control movement of all muscle systems, including the speech systems.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: A complete or near complete loss of the ability to articulate speech.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: A localized dilation of a blood vessel wall, resulting in the thinning of the wall at that site and increasing the likelihood of hemorrhaging, especially with high blood pressure.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: A complete loss of voice followed by whispering for oral communication that typically has psychological causes such as emotional stress.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: An applied science devoted to developing procedures that produce measurable changes in behavior using principles of operant conditioning, classical conditioning, and social learning theory (formerly, behavior modification).
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: Difficulty recognizing, interpreting, and conveying prosody (rhythm, pitch, stress, intonation) that is usually secondary to neurological impairment.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: A common disorder characterized by thickening, hardening, and loss of elasticity of arterial walls as a result of buildup of atherosclerotic plaque, leading to decreased blood supply.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: An acute inflammation of the lungs caused by foreign material (e.g., food or liquid) entering the lung tissue and resulting in infection; alveolar and bronchiole congestion with substances discharged from the alveolar walls as a protection against foreign material.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: Material (food, liquid, saliva, etc.) penetrating the larynx and entering the airway below the true vocal folds; food or liquid entering the lungs rather than the stomach after the swallow.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: Amplification systems, other than conventional hearing aids, that are designed for specific listening situations (e.g., personal amplifiers, telephone amplifiers, personal text telephones, and personal FM systems).
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: A common disorder characterized by yellowish plaques of cholesterol and other lipids (fats) in the inner layers of the walls of arteries; often associated with tobacco use, obesity, hypertension, diabetes mellitus, and aging.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: A standard graph used to record pure-tone hearing thresholds with air- and bone-conduction thresholds graphed by frequency in hertz (Hz) and hearing level (HL) in decibels (dB).
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: An electronic device designed to measure the sensitivity of hearing of pure-tone frequencies (hertz, Hz) and intensities (decibels, dB); sounds are delivered to the ears either through earphones or free field in an audiometry booth.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: An electrophysiological response to sound that consists of five to seven identifiable peaks that represent neural function of auditory pathways.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: The understanding of spoken messages, as distinguished from producing messages.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: Aural rehabilitation methods designed to optimize use of residual hearing by structured practice in listening, altering the environment, and use of hearing aids to increase sound awareness, sound discrimination, identification of words, and comprehension of spoken messages.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: Intervention for people who have not developed listening, speech, and language skills, and may include diagnosis of communication and hearing-related difficulties, speech perception training, speech and language therapy, manual communication, educational management, and patient and family counseling. Sometimes used synonymously with aural rehabilitation.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: A broad term that involves intervention aimed at minimizing and alleviating the communication difficulties associated with hearing loss, including identification and diagnosis, patient and family counseling and education, selection and fitting amplification devices, communication training strategies, speech perception training, speech-language therapy, promotion of literacy, and educational management.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: A lifelong complex behavioral syndrome that appears by 3 years of age, with children having a markedly absent interest in social interactions and relationships, severely impaired communication skills, and repetitive, stereotyped movements, combined with restricted interests that are often obsessive or fixated.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: Over-learned sequences of words that can be recited without much conscious thought, such as counting to 10; saying the days of the week, months of the year, and the alphabet; and singing some songs.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: Respect for the patient's right to determine his or her own choices in making healthcare decisions.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: A feeling of fullness or pressure in the middle ear as a result of ascending or descending with changes in altitude. Symptoms may include general ear discomfort, fullness or stuffiness in the ear, difficulty hearing, and dizziness. Prolonged barotrauma may result in ear pain, nosebleed, moderate to severe hearing loss, and in severe cases, rupture of the tympanic membrane.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: Procedures based on the best available evidence (research and expert opinion); procedures that are accepted or prescribed as being correct or most effective.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: The attempt to understand and resolve ethical issues and problems in a healthcare setting.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: The process of becoming aware of various physiological functions of the body using instruments that provide information on the activity of muscles or systems being monitored, with the goal of being able to control or manipulate those muscles or systems voluntarily.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: A semipermeable membrane barrier that prevents circulating blood in the capillaries and other blood vessels from making direct contact with the cells of the nervous system, but allows oxygen and nutrients to pass through the membrane to supply the nervous system tissue.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: Transmission of sound to the inner ear through vibration applied to the bones of the skull; allows determination of the cochlea's hearing sensitivity while bypassing any outer or middle ear abnormalities.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: Incomplete closure of the vocal folds during phonation that results in excessive unvibrated air escaping.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: A malignant neoplasm (new growth) characterized by uncontrolled growth of cells that tend to invade surrounding tissue and metastasize (spread) to distant body sites.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: Firm, fibrous, and strong connective tissue that does not contain blood vessels.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: Either of the two halves of the brain that contains a frontal lobe, parietal lobe, occipital lobe, and temporal lobe.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: A yellowish or brownish waxy secretion produced by ceruminous glands in the ear canal that protects the ear canal from intrusion by insects.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: A persistent laryngitis lasting more than 10 days with inflammation of the mucous membrane lining in the larynx, accompanied by edema of the vocal folds with hoarseness and loss of voice that is often caused by heavy smoking, coughing, allergies and chemical irritants, as well as ongoing vocal abuse and misuse.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: Of long duration with slow progress, usually in reference to a disease or disorder.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: The actual age of a person that is derived from date of birth and expressed in days, months, and years.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: The application of information based on actual observation of a person, combined with objective and subjective data that lead to a decision or conclusion.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: Healthcare, rehabilitation, and educational professionals, such as physicians, nurses, physical therapists, occupational therapists, speech-language pathologists, audiologists, psychiatrists, or psychologists, involved in clinical practice who base their practice on direct observation and treatment of patients and clients.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: The pronunciation of a sound that is affected by the sound preceding and/or following the sound. The influence of one phoneme on another in perception or production.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: The part of the inner ear containing the sensory mechanism of hearing; a spiral tunnel with 2¾ turns about 30 mm long, resembling a tiny snail shell.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: A device that enables individuals with profound hearing loss to perceive sound through an array of electrodes that are surgically implanted in the cochlea and deliver electrical signals to the vestibulocochlear (VIII cranial) nerve, and an external amplifier that activates the electrode array.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: Two sounds that differ only in voicing (e.g., /p/ /b/).
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: An impairment of attention, perception of stimuli (auditory, visual, tactile, taste, smell), memory, reasoning, judgment, and problem solving.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: Individualized programs focused on specific cognitive areas (e.g., improving memory and language processing) to help brain injured individuals restore abilities or compensate for cognitive deficits to promote independent functioning in daily living.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: A model of counseling designed to help individuals recognize and examine problematic beliefs and replace them with more adaptive and flexible ways of thinking.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: A prolonged period of unconsciousness in which a patient has minimal, if any, purposeful responses to stimuli.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: An apparatus, electronic device, book, or simple board with letters, words, numbers, pictures, and/or symbols used by children and adults with very limited functional verbal communication abilities.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: Existing simultaneously with and usually independent of another condition, impairment, or disorder.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: The presence of two or more conditions, disorders, or disabilities.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: A mild traumatic brain injury caused by a violent jarring or shaking, or blunt instrument injury to the skull that may or may not result in loss of consciousness or seizures, but commonly causes temporary (days to weeks) mild cognitive impairments.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: A disorder that is present at birth.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: Speech sounds articulated by either stopping the outgoing air stream or creating a narrow opening of resistance using the articulators.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: The outer layer of brain tissue containing nerve cell bodies (neurons).
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: Shrinkage and wasting away of cortical tissue that is common in dementia.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: An applied social science and a helping, interpersonal relationship in which the clinician's intentions are to assist a person or family member to understand a hearing, communication, or swallowing disorder and its impact on their lives, and ways of preventing, managing, adjusting to, or coping with these disorders.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: Feelings and thoughts such as frustration, anxiety, anger, guilt, hostility, shame, and expectations of difficulty talking, which lead to inhibitory and avoidance behaviors in individuals who stutter.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: The understanding and appropriate responding to the unique combination of cultural variables—including ability, age, beliefs, ethnicity, experience, gender, gender identity, linguistic background, national origin, race, religion, sexual orientation, and socioeconomic status—that the professional and client/patient bring to interactions.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: Individuals who are deaf or hard of hearing who share a common language (usually manual communication), common experiences and values, and a common way of interacting with each other and with hearing people.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: Ideology, beliefs, and customs shared by many individuals with prelinguistic deafness that may include communication, social protocol, entertainment, art, recreation, worship, and other aspects of culture.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: A basic unit of measure of the intensity (loudness) of sound.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: Any condition that causes gradual deterioration of normal body functions.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: The process of narrowing possibilities and reaching conclusions about the nature of a deficit.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: Two distinct pitches perceived simultaneously during phonation that is caused by the two vocal folds vibrating under different degrees of tension or vibration of the ventricular folds concurrently with the true vocal folds.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: Any restriction or lack of ability to perform an activity in the manner or within the range considered normal for a human being; the impairment, loss, or absence of a physical or intellectual function.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: The most common genetic chromosome disorder that causes lifelong intellectual disability and developmental delays, and may cause some health problems.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: A developmental motor and/or literacy disorder that affects a child's or adult's ability to write, characterized by messy or illegible handwriting, misspellings, and difficulty with grammar and organizing sentences; note agraphia is a loss of ability to write resulting from injury to the brain.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: A general neurological term for a variety of problems characterized by excessive contraction of muscles with associated abnormal movements and postures.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: Accumulation of excessive fluid in tissue that is associated with inflammatory conditions and results in swelling of the tissue.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: An electronic (battery powered) device used by laryngectomees that produces a vibrated mechanical sound that is held against the neck, with the sound entering the pharynx and oral cavity where it is articulated for speech.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: Early skills developed in the preschool years that precede or are presumed prerequisites for later-developing reading and writing skills.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: An uncontrolled and often rapidly changing expression of emotions or mood swings sometimes occurring with neurological impairments such as TBIs and strokes that indicate a loss of inhibitory control, most frequently seen as crying easily for no apparent reason or inappropriate laughter.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: Methods of viewing the velopharyngeal mechanism, vocal tract, or both that use a rigid endoscope introduced intraorally or flexible fiberoptic endoscope transnasally with a camera lens and fiber-optic light source at the tip of the scope.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: The compression of air within the oropharynx and injection of it into the esophagus, followed by the rapid expelling of the air out of the esophagus that causes it to vibrate the upper esophageal valve, which produces a low-pitched, monotone 'voice' that is shaped by the articulators to produce speech.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: The cause of an occurrence (e.g., a medical problem that results in a disorder or disability).
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: A tube lined with mucous membrane that joins the nasopharynx and the middle ear cavity that is normally closed but opens during yawning, chewing, and swallowing to allow equalization of air pressure in the middle ear with atmospheric pressure.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: A disruption of executive functions that regulate, control, and manage cognitive processes, behaviors, and emotions.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: Difficulty formulating verbal, written, and/or gestural language.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: Paired, thick folds of mucous membranes with few muscle fibers that lie just above the true vocal folds in the larynx. They do not vibrate during normal voicing but close tightly during swallowing to prevent material from entering the trachea.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: Models of counseling and service delivery that focus on variables regarding the family unit as a whole, where each family member is part of a system, each member affects the others, and the system is interdependent.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: The result of a mother's use of alcohol during pregnancy that may cause a fetus to develop abnormally slowly, have facial abnormalities, and have problems with the central nervous system, including intellectual disability.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.front: Define: Fiber-optic endoscopic evaluation of swallowing (FEES)
flashcards.back: A procedure used to provide visual information about the pharyngeal phase of swallowing using a flexible endoscope passed through the nasal passageway into the nasopharynx.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: Movements that require a high degree of dexterity, control, and precision of the small muscles of the body that enable such functions as grasping small objects, drawing shapes, cutting with scissors, fastening clothing, and writing.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: Aphasia that results from damage to posterior regions of the cortex (i.e., temporal or temporal-parietal lesions) and is characterized by language with normal pauses and relatively normal articulation, but with frequent syntactic errors, paraphasias, and circumlocutions.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: A fold of mucous tissue connecting the floor of the mouth to the midline underside of the tongue.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: Forms of behavior that express wants, needs, feelings, thoughts, knowledge, and preferences that others can understand.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: The results of treatment intended to clearly show a person's improved ability to communicate in the hospital setting, at home, in the community, and on the job.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: The rules of the use of morphology and syntax in a language.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: Involvement of the large muscles of the body (e.g., trunk, legs, arms) that require tone, strength, and coordination that enable such functions as standing, lifting, walking, and throwing a ball.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: Forceful approximation (closing) of the vocal folds during the initiation of phonation.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: A 'rough' sounding vocal quality resulting from a combination of hard glottal attacks, low pitch, and high intensity caused by overadduction (hyperfunction) of the vocal folds.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: Any battery powered electronic device designed to amplify and deliver sound to the ear; consists of a microphone, amplifier, and receiver.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: In audiometry, the level at which a stimulus sound, such as a pure tone, is barely perceptible; usual clinical criteria demand that the level be just high enough for the subject to be aware of the sound at least 50% of the time it is presented.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: A common dysphonia that is a combination of breathiness and harshness that may affect loudness (usually decreased), pitch (usually a low pitch with reduced pitch range), and quality (usually decreased 'pleasantness' of the sound of the voice).
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: Difficulty with the vocal folds closing too tightly or for too long causing the voice to sound tense and strained, and tending to fatigue with use as a result of hypertonicity.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: A pervasive pattern of excessive effort and tension that affects many different structures and muscles in the phonatory system; signs of hyperfunction include a tense-sounding voice and hard glottal attacks.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: Excessive tone or tension of a muscle or group of muscles.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: Difficulty making the vocal folds close strongly enough or long enough for normal phonation that results in a weak, breathy voice that often deteriorates with increasing amounts of vocal use throughout the day.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: Inadequate muscle tone in the laryngeal mechanism and associated structures, including the muscles of respiration; signs of hypofunction include breathiness because of inadequate closure of the vocal folds, weak vocal power, and reduced vocal endurance.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: Absence of normal tone or tension of a muscle or group of muscles.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: Any disorder, disease, or condition that occurs spontaneously or for which the cause is unknown.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: Any loss or abnormality of psychological, physiological, or anatomical structure or function.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: A localized area of necrotic (dead) tissue resulting from lack of blood supply and oxygen to the tissue.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: The nearly constant internal monologue a person has with himself at a conscious or semiconscious level that involves thinking in words; a conversation with oneself.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: A disability characterized by significant limitations in both intellectual functioning (intelligence) and adaptive behavior, and originates before 18 years of age.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: A group of professionals from various disciplines who work together to coordinate the care of a patient through collaboration, interaction, communication, and cooperation.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: Continuous but unintelligible speech with various combinations of literal and verbal paraphasias and neologisms, with little or no conveying of information, often with the speaker having little awareness of the problem.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: An active process in which, from instant to instant, a listener infers the meaning of an auditory message based on the context of the information and long-term stored memory of words and general knowledge.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: An abnormal slowness in developing language skills that may result in incomplete language development.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: An impairment of receptive and/or expressive linguistic symbols that affects learning and educational achievement and, consequentially, possible occupational and professional choices and success, in addition to emotional and social development.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: Surgical removal of the larynx because of cancer; includes the trachea being brought forward and sutured to the skin in the lower midline of the neck to create a permanent stoma.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: Impaired responsiveness, usually caused by diffuse brain dysfunction or damage to the brainstem and the reticular activating system; duration is often used as a measure of traumatic brain injury severity.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: Misalignment or incorrect relation between the teeth of the maxillary (upper) and mandibular (lower) dental arches when the dental arches close.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: A chronic disease of the inner ear characterized by recurrent episodes of vertigo, tinnitus, and sensorineural hearing loss that may be bilateral.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: The ability to think about and talk about language.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: An air-filled chamber located within the temporal bone of the skull; beginning at the inner side of the tympanic membrane and attaching the ossicular chain to the oval window of the cochlea.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: A dynamic imaging radiographic (X-ray) procedure focusing on the mouth, pharynx, larynx, and cervical esophagus used to examine the process of swallowing.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: A hyperfunctional voice disorder in which the voice is adversely affected by excessive muscle tension that ranges from mild to severe; characteristics include a weak voice that lacks intensity, range, and variation; a rough, hoarse, or thin vocal quality that lacks resonance; and a voice that fatigues easily.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: Abnormal flow of the airstream through the nose during production of consonants that require a buildup of intraoral air pressure, caused by a cleft of the hard or soft palate or velopharyngeal dysfunction that results in distortion of speech.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: A minimally invasive procedure commonly used by SLPs for evaluation of velopharyngeal dysfunction that allows visual observation and analysis of the velopharyngeal mechanism during speech.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: The death of cells in an organ or tissue due to disease, injury, or failure of the blood supply.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: A combining of sounds that usually follow conventional combinations but is an invented word that appears to serve a linguistic function.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: A permanent sensorineural hearing loss caused by exposure to excessive loud noise, often over long periods of time.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: Aphasia that results from damage to anterior cortical regions (i.e., frontal lobe lesions) and is characterized by sparse, perseverative language with disturbed prosody, misarticulations, errors in syntax, and a reduction in phrase length.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: A partial or complete blockage (occlusion) of a cerebral artery, causing decreased blood supply to brain tissue.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: Brain injury that involves a wound caused by a missile (e.g., bullet or shrapnel), instrument (e.g., axe or knife), or force strong enough to fracture the skull and cause brain damage.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.front: Define: Oral apraxia (nonverbal apraxia, facial apraxia, buccofacial apraxia)
flashcards.back: Difficulty with volitional nonspeech movements of the articulators (e.g., puffing the cheeks, clicking the tongue, protruding the tongue, whistling, or smiling).
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: The three small bones (ossicles) of the middle ear named after their basic shapes: malleus (mallet/hammer), incus (anvil), and stapes (stirrup).
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: Any inflammation or infection of the middle ear; particularly common in childhood and often associated with a common cold, allergies, sinus infections, and sore throats.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: A medical doctor who specializes in diseases of the ears, nose, and throat.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: Typically a hereditary condition that occurs when there is growth of bony tissue around the footplate of the stapes that presses against the oval window, resulting in a conductive hearing loss.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: A prosthetic device that can be used to raise the soft palate for speech if the soft palate is long enough to achieve velopharyngeal closure but does not move well, often because of central or peripheral neurological impairment.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: Soft, wartlike, benign growths on the vocal folds of children that have a viral origin and may grow to a size that can obstruct the airway.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: Loss of voluntary movement (motor function) that may affect one or more groups of muscles.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: Automatic and involuntary repetition of a behavior beyond an intended point, including repetition of a word or phrase or hand gesture that may be caused by neurological impairment or psychiatric disorder.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: A muscular membranous cavity, approximately 5 inches (12.5 cm) long, behind the nasal cavities and mouth. It may be divided into three areas: the nasal pharynx (top), the oral pharynx (center), and laryngeal pharynx (bottom), which provide passageways for both air and food and functions as a resonating organ in speech.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: Recognition and understanding of sound-letter associations; that individual sounds can be combined to form words; that a single-syllable word can be segmented into its beginning, middle, and ending sounds; and that longer words have more 'middle' sounds.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: A form of augmentative and alternative communication (AAC) used widely with children and adults with ASD and other disorders. PECS is designed to teach functional communication with an initial focus on spontaneous communication.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: Swelling of the vocal folds as a result of fluid collection (edema) in the superficial layers of the epithelial tissue.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: A medical term describing the condition of a person who has been subjected to multiple traumatic injuries, such as a serious head injury, severe burns, lacerations, bone fractures, and post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD).
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: Vague or nonspecific symptoms that are a continuation of symptoms seen in the acute phase of a mild TBI that may last for several weeks or months, with physical symptoms such as headache and dizziness, psychological symptoms such as depressed mood and irritability, and cognitive impairments such as memory and concentration difficulties.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: A set of symptoms after exposure to a psychologically extreme traumatic stressor that involves intense fear, horror, or helplessness; symptoms include persistent reexperiencing of the traumatic event, avoidance of stimuli associated with the trauma, and symptoms of increased arousal (hypervigilance).
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: Hearing loss associated with old age, usually involving both a loss of hearing sensitivity and a reduction in clarity of speech.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: Age-related dysphonia associated with normal physiological aging, and medical and psychosocial conditions that are common in older age.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: A prediction of the probable course, duration, recovery, or termination of a disease or disorder; a prediction of the outcome of a proposed course of treatment, its effectiveness and duration, and the individual's progress.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: Difficulty recognizing familiar faces, including family members and famous people.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: A global concept that involves a person's standard of living, personal freedom, and the opportunity to pursue happiness; a measure of a person's ability to cope successfully with the full range of challenges encountered in daily living.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: An inability or difficulty reading that is of neurological origin.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: Difficulty comprehending verbal, written, and/or gestural language.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: Support that adults provide to children for them to achieve competence in an activity (e.g., reading and writing), with the support gradually removed until the child is able to perform independently.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: A rare injury in which an individual receives a second concussion before the symptoms of a prior concussion are completely resolved; occurs primarily in teens and young adults who play hard contact sports and can cause diffuse and catastrophic cerebral edema that results in permanent disability or death.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: A perspective of language development that emphasizes the interrelationship between language learning and cognition; that is, the meanings conveyed by a child's productions.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: Penetration of food or liquid (including saliva) into the larynx and passing below the vocal folds without a protective cough or choking occurring.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: A primary deficit in the social use of nonverbal and verbal communication characterized by difficulty in using language for social purposes, appropriately matching communication to the social context, following rules of the communication context, understanding nonliteral language, and integrating language with nonverbal communicative behaviors.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: Abnormal excessive muscle tone that interferes with normal relaxed movement of the limbs and body.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: A key component of audiological assessment that uses auditory signals present in everyday communication.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: Any deviation or abnormality of speech outside the range of acceptable variation in a given environment.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: The physiological healing of the brain's damaged tissue, including reduction of cerebral edema, development of collateral blood vessels, and rerouting of neural pathways.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: An opening about the diameter of a finger or thumb made surgically into the neck that allows a person to breathe directly through the trachea.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: A therapy approach for children and adult stutterers that requires the speaker to recognize and confront his fears, avoidances, and struggles to escape his stuttering, and the speaker reducing and managing those fears, avoidances, and struggles.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: The ability to imagine or anticipate the mental states of other people (beliefs, thoughts, intents, desires, feelings, etc.), which may be different from one's own. The ability to see the world from the perspective of other people.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: A subjective noise sensation, often described as a ringing, roaring, or swishing in the ear that may be heard in one or both ears; associated with a variety of hearing disorders in both adults and children.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: A surgical procedure (tracheoesophageal puncture) in which an incision through the trachea and esophageal walls is created to fit a one-way plastic valve (prosthesis) that directs air from the trachea into the esophagus where it can reach the oral cavity and be articulated for speech.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: An episode of cerebral vascular insufficiency with partial occlusion of a cerebral artery by atherosclerotic plaque or an embolus. Disturbances of vision in one or both eyes, dizziness, weakness, numbness, dysphasia, or unconsciousness may occur. TIA usually lasts a few minutes to one hour; beyond one hour it is not considered a TIA but a stroke.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: Any abnormal growth of new tissue, either benign or malignant, caused by aberrant increases in the rate of cell reproduction.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: A thin, semitransparent membrane that separates the ear canal from the middle ear and transmits sound vibrations into the middle ear.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: A condition where the velopharyngeal valve does not close consistently and/or completely during the production of oral sounds. VPD is a general term that may be used when etiology is not certain or is not specified.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: Neuromotor or physiological disorders that result in poor movement of the velopharyngeal structures.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: An anatomical or structural defect that prevents adequate velopharyngeal closure, including a short velum, and is the most common type of velopharyngeal dysfunction.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: Unintended substitution of one word for another word that is often semantically related to the intended word (e.g., sister for brother and cat for dog); often occur in both speech and writing.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: The inner ear structures associated with balance and position sense, including the vestibule and semicircular canals of the vestibular mechanism, with interactions of the visual and proprioceptive systems and connection to the cerebellum.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: Harmful acute or chronic vocal behaviors, such as excessive yelling, screaming, cheering, coughing, and hard glottal attacks that are damaging to the vocal folds and the laryngeal and pharyngeal muscles and tissues.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: Unilateral or bilateral loss of laryngeal movements (including vocal fold opening or closing) that may be caused by damage to the brainstem, vagus (X cranial) nerves, recurrent laryngeal nerves, or the neuromuscular junctions, resulting in a weak, breathy voice.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: Behaviors that are helpful to achieve and maintain a healthy vocal mechanism and prevent or decrease vocal pathologies.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: Harmful chronic vocal behaviors that may have a cumulative damaging effect on the structure and functioning of the laryngeal mechanism, such as chronic inappropriate loudness or pitch, singing beyond the range of the vocal mechanism, and frequent hard glottal attacks.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: Voiced speech sounds from the unrestricted passage of the air stream through the mouth without audible stoppage or friction.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.back: Temporary information storage that is limited in capacity and requires rehearsal; often thought of as 'what is on your mind' at any given moment.
flashcards.source: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. — Textbook Glossary

flashcards.front: Describe all four phases of the normal swallow in sequence. What happens in each phase and how long does each take?
flashcards.back:
PHASE 1 — ORAL PREPARATORY:
All senses involved; food chewed (mastication) and formed into bolus; cheeks tighten; soft palate drops to tongue base; tongue manipulates food. No set time — varies with food.

PHASE 2 — ORAL PHASE (~1 second, VOLUNTARY):
Tongue tip elevates to alveolar ridge; tongue sweeps bolus posteriorly; soft palate rises to contact posterior pharyngeal wall (prevents nasal regurgitation).

PHASE 3 — PHARYNGEAL PHASE (~1 second):
4 activities simultaneously:
1. Pharyngeal peristalsis moves bolus downward
2. Larynx rises → epiglottis drops over airway opening (prevents aspiration) → UEV begins to open
3. False AND true vocal folds adduct tightly (2nd and 3rd protection)
4. Upper esophageal valve (UEV) relaxes and opens

PHASE 4 — ESOPHAGEAL PHASE (8-20 seconds):
Bolus passes UEV; esophageal peristalsis carries bolus to lower esophageal valve (LEV); LEV opens briefly then closes (prevents gastric reflux).

KEY: Phases 1-2 are voluntary; phases 3-4 are involuntary/reflexive.
flashcards.source: Fogle, Ch. 18

flashcards.front: What is the difference between aspiration and silent aspiration? Why is silent aspiration particularly dangerous?
flashcards.back:
ASPIRATION:
Food, liquid, or saliva penetrates the larynx and enters the airway BELOW the true vocal folds (enters the lungs instead of the stomach).
Risk: aspiration pneumonia.
Normal response: protective cough and/or choking to clear the material.

SILENT ASPIRATION:
Same penetration occurs BUT with NO protective cough or choking.
Cause: patient has reduced or absent sensation in the laryngeal region.
The brain does not receive a signal that something entered the airway.

WHY PARTICULARLY DANGEROUS:
• Material enters the lungs completely undetected
• Without coughing, nothing clears the material from the airway
• Patient and caregivers may have NO IDEA aspiration is occurring
• Can cause repeated, silent aspiration pneumonia
• Cannot be detected at the bedside without instrumental evaluation (MBSS or FEES)
• The bedside evaluation's check for a strong protective cough is specifically designed to screen for this risk
flashcards.source: Fogle, Ch. 18

flashcards.front: Compare MBSS and FEES as instrumental evaluation tools for dysphagia. What does each assess and how is each performed?
flashcards.back:
MODIFIED BARIUM SWALLOW STUDY (MBSS) — 'Gold Standard':
Assesses: ALL FOUR PHASES (oral preparatory, oral, pharyngeal, esophageal)
Method: Dynamic radiographic (X-ray) video in radiology suite; SLP and radiologist team; patient swallows liquids mixed with barium and foods coated with barium; real-time video of bolus from mouth to stomach
Strengths: Most thorough; shows complete swallowing physiology; can test compensatory techniques during the study
Limitations: Radiation exposure; must be done in radiology suite; less portable

FIBER-OPTIC ENDOSCOPIC EVALUATION OF SWALLOWING (FEES):
Assesses: PHARYNGEAL PHASE primarily (top-down view)
Method: Flexible endoscope passed transnasally to hypopharynx; food/liquid dyed with food coloring; SLP views pharynx, larynx, and upper trachea in color
Strengths: No radiation; portable (can be done bedside); shows vocal fold movement and anatomy in color; identifies penetration/aspiration events
Limitations: Cannot view oral phases; the swallow itself (when larynx rises) briefly blocks the view (white-out)

Both are used to confirm aspiration and guide treatment decisions.
flashcards.source: Fogle, Ch. 18

flashcards.front: What are the three dysphagia treatment approaches? Give examples of specific techniques within each.
flashcards.back:
1. COMPENSATORY TREATMENT TECHNIQUES
Optimize use of CURRENT swallowing abilities; do not change underlying physiology.
Taught to staff and family.
Examples:
• Postural techniques: chin tuck (reduces aspiration risk), head rotation to weaker side, head tilt to stronger side
• Food placement: place food on stronger side; control bolus size (~5cc/1 tsp)
• Best for patients with cognitive/communication problems (easiest to learn)

2. FACILITATION TREATMENT TECHNIQUES
Designed to IMPROVE function of the swallowing mechanism.
Examples:
• Increasing vocal fold adduction (breath-hold maneuvers)
• Intentional breath-hold just prior to and during swallow
• Oral-motor exercises to strengthen articulatory and swallowing muscles
• Swallow maneuvers (e.g., Mendelsohn maneuver, supraglottic swallow)

3. DIET MODIFICATION TECHNIQUES
Modify food texture and/or liquid consistency using National Dysphagia Diet levels.
Four food textures: Regular → Dysphagia Advanced → Mechanically Altered → Pureed
Four liquid consistencies: Thin → Nectar → Honey → Spoon-thick
Choice based on individual patient's specific phase of disorder.
flashcards.source: Fogle, Ch. 18

flashcards.front: What are the steps of the clinical (bedside) swallow evaluation? What three reasons prompt a referral to an SLP for dysphagia evaluation?
flashcards.back:
CLINICAL (BEDSIDE) SWALLOW EVALUATION — 6 STEPS:
1. Review medical chart carefully
2. Screen/interview patient — perceptions of the problem; assess strength of PROTECTIVE COUGH (essential to detect silent aspiration risk)
3. Oral-mechanism examination — anatomy and physiology of articulatory structures
4. Observe patient swallowing different consistencies:
   • Liquids: thin, nectar, honey, pudding
   • Foods: regular, mechanically altered, puree
5. Determine risk for aspiration and need for instrumental evaluation
6. Document all assessment procedures and results in medical chart

THREE REASONS FOR REFERRAL:
1. Patient appears at risk for aspiration based on MEDICAL DIAGNOSIS
2. OBSERVED DIFFICULTIES with feeding and intake of food or liquid
3. Patient appears NOT to be taking in ADEQUATE NOURISHMENT

Note: The bedside evaluation CANNOT confirm aspiration definitively — only instrumental evaluation (MBSS or FEES) can do that.
flashcards.source: Fogle, Ch. 18

questions.text: Describe the educational pathway and credentialing requirements to become a licensed Speech-Language Pathologist in the United States.
questions.answer: 1. Earn a Bachelor's degree in Communication Disorders or a related field. 2. Complete a Master's degree in Communication Disorders (or Ph.D.). 3. Pass a national examination. 4. Obtain ASHA certification (CCC-SLP). 5. Become state licensed in the state where you practice. Ongoing CEUs are required to maintain licensure.
questions.source: Lecture 1.2.2 — Chapter 2: The Professionals, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

questions.text: Compare and contrast the roles, education requirements, and scope of practice of an SLP versus an Audiologist.
questions.answer: SLP: Master's or Ph.D. required; treats communication AND swallowing disorders; largest employer = public schools; also works in hospitals, clinics, private practice, universities; ASHA credential = CCC-SLP. Audiologist: Ph.D. or Au.D. required + Clinical Fellowship Year; treats auditory and vestibular disorders; assesses hearing loss, amplification, habilitation/rehabilitation; same work settings; credential = CCC-A. Both: must pass national exam, obtain state licensure, follow ASHA Code of Ethics, and earn CEUs. Key difference: SLPs treat communication/swallowing; Audiologists focus on hearing and balance.
questions.source: Lecture 1.2.2 — Chapter 2: The Professionals, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

questions.text: Why is the team approach important in treating communication disorders, and who are the key members of that team?
questions.answer: Communication disorders often require input from multiple disciplines — speech, medical, educational, and family. The team approach ensures that all aspects of the client's needs are addressed collaboratively. Key members include professionals (SLP, Audiologist, teachers, physicians) and family members. The most important team member is the client themselves, and their parent if applicable, as the client's needs and goals must be central to all decisions.
questions.source: Lecture 1.2.2 — Chapter 2: The Professionals, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

questions.text: Explain the difference between receptive and expressive language disorders and give a clinical example of each.
questions.answer: Receptive language = understanding what is being said. A receptive delay means the person struggles to comprehend spoken or written language (e.g., cannot follow directions, does not understand questions). Expressive language = how a person produces language verbally. An expressive delay means the person struggles to communicate their thoughts and needs (e.g., limited vocabulary, short/incomplete sentences, difficulty naming objects). An SLP must assess both domains separately, as a client may have delays in one or both areas.
questions.source: Lecture 1.2.1 — Chapter 1, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

questions.text: Describe the five components of linguistics and explain why each is important for an SLP to understand.
questions.answer: 1. Phonology — speech sounds and their rules; SLPs assess and treat sound errors. 2. Morphology — word structure and meaning units; language-disordered clients often show morphological errors (e.g., missing -ed, -s). 3. Syntax — sentence structure rules; errors affect intelligibility and academic language. 4. Semantics — vocabulary and meaning; vocabulary delays significantly hinder communication. 5. Pragmatics — social language use; especially important for clients with autism spectrum disorders. Understanding all five allows the SLP to pinpoint the exact nature and level of a client's language breakdown.
questions.source: Lecture 1.2.1 — Chapter 1, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

questions.text: Using Van Riper's 1978 definition, explain what makes something a communication disorder, and discuss the emotional and social impact these disorders may have.
questions.answer: Van Riper (1978) defined a communication disorder as communication that: (1) deviates from that of others, (2) calls attention to itself, (3) interferes with communication, and (4) causes distress for the speaker and/or listener. All four criteria help distinguish true disorders from dialectical differences or temporary disruptions. Impact: communication disorders can affect quality of life, emotional wellbeing, academic performance, social relationships, and family dynamics. Because the family is also affected, SLPs must develop strong counseling and people skills and educate caregivers about the nature of the disorder. The SLP's goal is to improve the client's quality of communication and quality of life.
questions.source: Lecture 1.2.1 — Chapter 1, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

questions.text: Explain why the exhalation phase of breathing must become active during speech, and describe the muscles involved.
questions.answer: At rest, exhalation is passive. During speech, long sustained exhalation is needed to support sentences and singing. The diaphragm actively pushes upward and the abdominal muscles contract to force air out through the vocal folds. SLPs may work with clients to strengthen these muscles to improve speech loudness.

questions.text: Describe the role of an SLP across all four processes of speech production, using examples from the video.
questions.answer: SLPs work across all four processes: (1) Respiration — strengthening respiratory muscles to increase loudness; (2) Phonation — treating voice disorders caused by overuse, surgery, accidents, or disease; (3) Resonance — addressing how sound quality is shaped through the cavities; (4) Articulation — the most commonly known area, helping clients shape sounds correctly. SLPs also work with patients post-stroke, brain injury, or with progressive diseases to improve intelligibility.
questions.source: Eugene Speech Language Therapy — How We Speak (YouTube, 11xf89bXaCg)

questions.text: Explain the respiratory process for speech, describing how the breathing pattern changes from quiet breathing to speaking.
questions.answer: At rest, inhalation and exhalation are roughly equal (50/50). For speech, the pattern shifts dramatically: we take a quick, short inhalation (~10% of capacity) followed by a long, sustained exhalation (~90%). During quiet breathing, exhalation is passive (lungs and rib cage recoil). For speech and singing, exhalation becomes active — the diaphragm and abdominal muscles contract to push air out steadily. The brainstem sends signals to initiate the cycle: the diaphragm contracts and lowers, the rib cage expands, thoracic volume increases, and air pressure drops, drawing air in through the nose/mouth and down the trachea into the alveoli. As the diaphragm relaxes, the rib cage lowers, thoracic volume decreases, and air is pushed out through the trachea and mouth/nose to power speech.
questions.source: Lecture 2.2 — Ch. 3, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

questions.text: Explain how the vocal folds produce voice, including the role of subglottic air pressure and Bernoulli's law.
questions.answer: To produce voice, the vocal folds must adduct (come together). Once closed, air continues to flow upward from the lungs, building subglottic air pressure below the folds. When this pressure is sufficient, it forces the folds apart (abduction). As air rushes through the narrow opening at high speed, Bernoulli's law causes a drop in air pressure between the folds, which pulls them back together (adduction). Subglottic pressure then builds again and the cycle repeats — this rapid opening and closing creates vibration, and vibration creates sound (voice). The rate of this cycle per second is the fundamental frequency (measured in Hz), perceived by the listener as pitch.
questions.source: Lecture 2.2 — Ch. 3, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

questions.text: Compare and contrast the functions of the left and right hemispheres of the brain, and explain why damage to the left hemisphere tends to result in language disorders.
questions.answer: Left hemisphere: dominant for receptive and expressive language and speech; processes rapidly changing sequential information; contains Broca's area (motor speech planning) and receives input from Wernicke's area (language comprehension); controls right-body movement and sensation. Right hemisphere: responsible for attention, orientation, emotions, and cognition; controls left-body movement. The two are connected by the corpus callosum. Because language and speech are lateralized to the left hemisphere — with Broca's area coordinating motor speech output and Wernicke's area interpreting auditory language — damage to the left hemisphere disproportionately disrupts these functions, resulting in aphasia, motor speech disorders, and related communication impairments depending on the location and extent of injury.
questions.source: Lecture 2.2 — Ch. 3, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

questions.text: Discuss why a person could have severe speech, voice, AND swallowing problems following damage to the brainstem.
questions.answer: The brainstem (pons and medulla oblongata) controls the face, mouth, and larynx for speech production and serves as the pathway connecting the brain to the spinal cord. It also houses the nuclei of multiple cranial nerves critical to speech and swallowing — including CN V (jaw movement), CN VII (facial muscles), CN IX (pharynx, swallowing), CN X (voice, swallowing, respiration), and CN XII (tongue). Because all of these functions share brainstem control, a single brainstem lesion can simultaneously disrupt articulation (tongue, lips, jaw), phonation (larynx/vocal folds via CN X), resonance (soft palate), and the swallowing mechanism — producing a wide-ranging impairment profile often seen in conditions like brainstem stroke.
questions.source: Lecture 2.2 — Ch. 3, Kidwell, SJDC Comm Dis 1A

pairs: Diaphragm contracts and lowers; rib cage rises and expands
Thoracic volume increases → air pressure decreases
Air flows in through nose → trachea → alveolar sacs
questions.answer: 1=brainstem signal, 2=diaphragm lowers/ribs expand, 3=volume up/pressure down, 4=air flows in, 5=alveoli expand

questions.text: Explain executive functions: what they are, which brain region governs them, and why damage to this area would severely affect a person's quality of life.
questions.answer: Executive functions are a composite of higher-order cognitive activities related to goal completion, governed by the prefrontal cortex (anterior frontal lobe). They include: anticipation of consequences, goal selection, planning, initiation of activity, self-regulation and self-monitoring, and the use of feedback to adjust future behavior. This area also controls personality, judgment, reasoning, decision-making, and beliefs. Damage to the frontal lobe/prefrontal cortex would severely affect quality of life because the person would struggle to plan and execute daily tasks, regulate their own behavior, make sound judgments, and interact appropriately in social situations. Their personality and character may change dramatically. Because Broca's area (also in the frontal lobe) controls motor speech planning, frontal lobe damage can simultaneously impair both executive function AND speech production.

questions.text: Which of the following correctly distinguishes cooing from babbling?
options: Cooing = vowel-like sounds only; babbling = consonant + vowel in the same syllable
Cooing = consonant + vowel combinations; babbling = vowel-like sounds only
Cooing = long syllable strings with prosody; babbling = single consonant sounds
Cooing and babbling are the same — both include CV syllable combinations
questions.answer: Cooing = vowel-like sounds only; babbling = consonant + vowel in the same syllable

questions.answer: The language difficulty is also present in Spanish (the primary language)

questions.text: Which vocalization type is characterized by strings of syllables with adult-like stress and intonation that mimic real speech, but contain no true words?
By approximately what age should most speech sounds be mastered according to the developmental stages?
questions.source: Ch. 4 PDF — Prof. Handy / Quiz answer key

questions.text: Jargon contains true words spoken with adult-like intonation.
A child who speaks only Spanish at home and no English is considered to have a language disorder because they cannot communicate in English.
Mean Length of Utterance (MLU) is roughly equivalent to a young child's chronological age.
Deaf babies never produce cooing or babbling because they cannot hear from birth.
Code-switching in bilingual children is a normal stage of language development and is not classified as a disorder.
questions.source: Lecture 3.2 — Ch. 4, Kidwell / Quiz answer key

questions.text: By ages 11–12, children reach a peak period for learning figurative language such as idioms and metaphors.
__________ is the production of vowel-like sounds by infants when feeling comfort or pleasure and interacting with a caregiver.
Children must first experience objects, events, or relationships and understand the words that relate to them before they can use the words meaningfully in __________ language.
__________ language is the use of a single word to express a complete thought.
__________ speech is condensed language in which only the essential words (nouns, verbs, adjectives) are used.
__________ switching is a normal occurrence for bilingual individuals in which elements from one language are included when speaking another.
Every new stimulus from any of the five senses creates new __________ pathways and connections in the brain.
Communicative __________ is the ability to use language and to know when, where, and with whom to use it.
Match each vocalization type to its correct description.
pairs: First vocalization of a newborn; reflexive prelinguistic sound
Vowel-like sounds (/u/, /oo/) — no CV combination; comfort/pleasure
Consonant + vowel in the same syllable (ba-ba, da, ga)
Syllable strings with adult-like prosody/intonation — no true words
Immediate, automatic imitation/reproduction of speech heard
questions.answer: Crying=first neonate sound, Cooing=vowels only, Babbling=CV syllables, Jargon=prosodic strings no words, Echolalia=imitation

questions.text: Match each language development theory to its core clinical application.
pairs: Stimulus-response therapy; imitation and reinforcement of correct sounds
Target age-appropriate structures not yet acquired naturally
Adjust complexity, amount, or rate of speech to match child's meaning
Social games, interactive reading, caregiver coaching
