Recommend MBSS or FEES when: aspiration risk is suspected but not confirmed clinically; patient has a weak or absent protective cough (silent aspiration risk); want to identify the specific phase of dysfunction; want to trial compensatory techniques and observe their effectiveness directly. The MBSS is preferred when all four phases need to be examined; FEES is preferred when bedside evaluation is needed or when repeat evaluation of pharyngeal phase is required.

notes.title: Ch. 18 — Dysphagia: Treatment, Diet Modification, and Emotional Effects
notes.body:
SOURCE: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. Chapter 18, pp. 390-407.

DYSPHAGIA TREATMENT TEAM (SLP = HEAD/COORDINATOR)
CNAs, Dentists, Dietitians, Family, Gastroenterologists, Neurologists, Nurses, OTs, Otolaryngologists,
Pharmacists, PTs, Physicians, Pulmonologists, Radiologists, Respiratory therapists, Social workers, SLPs

ASHA ESSENTIAL SKILLS (2002-2007):
1. Identify people at risk for dysphagia
2. Conduct and interpret clinical assessments
3. Conduct and interpret instrumental-based evaluations
4. Develop intervention strategies
5. Document care and discharge planning
6. Provide education, counseling, and training

THREE TREATMENT APPROACHES:

1. COMPENSATORY TECHNIQUES (optimize current abilities; does NOT change underlying physiology)
   Postural techniques: chin tuck, head rotation — control bolus flow, reduce aspiration risk
   Best for patients with cognitive/communication problems (easiest to learn)
   Food placement: place on stronger side; limit bolus to ~5cc (1 teaspoon)
   Taught to family and staff

2. FACILITATION TECHNIQUES (improve underlying physiological function)
   Vocal fold adduction training
   Breath-hold just prior to/during swallow
   Oral-motor exercises to strengthen swallowing muscles
   Swallow maneuvers (Mendelsohn, supraglottic swallow)

3. DIET MODIFICATION (National Dysphagia Diet Task Force, 2002)

FOOD TEXTURES (4 levels):
  Regular: No restrictions
  Dysphagia Advanced: Ground/chopped tender meat, soft foods
  Mechanically Altered: Ground/finely chopped with gravy, mashed, soft cooked
  Pureed: Blended/baby-food texture

LIQUID CONSISTENCIES (4 levels):
  Thin: No restrictions
  Nectar-like: Nectar consistency (fruit nectars, V8, eggnog)
  Honey-like: Honey consistency (commercial thickener)
  Spoon-thick: Pudding consistency (commercial thickener)

NPO (Nothing by Mouth) — Nil Per Os:
Used when dysphagia so severe no oral intake is safe
Patients receive nutrition via ENTERAL FEEDING:
  NG-tube (nasogastric) — through nose
  G-tube (gastrostomy) — through stomach
Common supplement: Ensure (adults) / Pediasure (children)
Usually temporary — therapy may allow return to oral feeding

EVIDENCE-BASED PRACTICE:
TREATMENT EFFICACY: beneficial under OPTIMAL/IDEAL conditions
TREATMENT EFFECTIVENESS: beneficial under TYPICAL/REAL-WORLD conditions
BEST PRACTICES: based on best available evidence
Third-party payers want: can patient go home, recover, return to work — at minimum cost/time
Long-term goal: 'Patient will achieve safe and efficient swallowing to sustain adequate nutrition and hydration orally'
Early identification and intervention reduces aspiration risk, shortens hospitalization, improves QOL
Cost of ONE aspiration pneumonia hospitalization: ~$27,000-$28,000

EMOTIONAL AND SOCIAL EFFECTS:
Food and eating have STRONG EMOTIONAL SIGNIFICANCE — eating is social
Eating alone or being fed takes away enjoyment → patients may eat inadequately

QUALITY OF LIFE:
Patient QOL is ALWAYS most important consideration
Decreasing food texture or increasing liquid viscosity CHANGES FLAVOR
Food has 5 properties: appearance, aroma, flavor, texture, nutrition (nutrition cannot be sensed)
Patients on modified diets often decrease consumption even understanding nutritional need

BIOETHICS AND AUTONOMY:
Bioethics: resolving ethical issues in healthcare
Autonomy: respecting patient's right to make their own healthcare decisions
SLP can inform, advise, recommend — but patient can DEMAND
Must balance safety recommendations with patient's right to self-determination

ANXIETIES AND FEARS:
Embarrassment in front of family/friends during meals
Fear of aspiration pneumonia or death
Discomfort from suctioning procedures
Fear of never eating favorite foods normally again

MULTICULTURAL CONSIDERATIONS:
All cultures have food preferences, rituals, religious/dietary taboos
Hospital food may be totally foreign to patients from other cultures
Family members may bring 'ethnic' food with wrong texture/consistency (aspiration risk)
SLP must educate patient AND family about dietary hazards
Recognize that patients and professionals come from diverse linguistic and cultural backgrounds

notes.title: Ch. 18 — Dysphagia: Normal Swallow, Disorders, and Assessment
notes.body:
SOURCE: Fogle, P.T. (2019). Essentials of Communication Sciences & Disorders, 3rd ed. Chapter 18, pp. 390-407.

DYSPHAGIA OVERVIEW
Difficulty swallowing affecting any of four phases of the normal swallow
Connected to speech systems (respiratory, phonatory, resonatory, articulatory)
SLPs are recognized as PRIMARY professionals for dysphagia evaluation and treatment
Within SLPs' scope of practice per ASHA (2016)
Dysphagia is a MAJOR area of acute care hospital work for SLPs
Post-stroke dysphagia incidence: 13-94%

CAUSES
Children: prematurity, low birth weight, heart disease, meningitis, encephalopathy, cerebral palsy
Adults: stroke (most common), TBI, Parkinson's, MS, ALS, muscular dystrophy, Alzheimer's
Also: oral/pharyngeal/laryngeal/esophageal cancer and its treatment
Medications (e.g., muscle relaxants) may affect swallowing muscle tone

THE NORMAL SWALLOW (600x/day = ~220,000/year)
Mediated by medulla in brainstem + cortical areas (including Broca's area, precentral gyrus)

PHASE 1 — ORAL PREPARATORY:
All 5 senses involved; cognitive awareness that food is available
Bolus = food mass formed in the mouth
Cheeks tighten; soft palate drops to tongue base; tongue moves food between teeth
No set time

PHASE 2 — ORAL PHASE (~1 second, VOLUNTARY):
Tongue tip elevates to alveolar ridge
Tongue sweeps bolus posteriorly
Soft palate rises to contact posterior pharyngeal wall (prevents nasal regurgitation)

PHASE 3 — PHARYNGEAL PHASE (~1 second, INVOLUNTARY):
4 activities simultaneously:
1. Pharyngeal peristalsis moves bolus downward
2. Larynx rises → epiglottis drops → shields airway from aspiration → UEV begins to open
3. FALSE + TRUE vocal folds adduct tightly (2nd and 3rd layer of protection)
4. Upper esophageal valve (UEV) fully relaxes and opens

PHASE 4 — ESOPHAGEAL PHASE (8-20 seconds, INVOLUNTARY):
Bolus passes UEV; sphincter closes
Esophageal peristalsis carries bolus to lower esophageal valve (LEV)
LEV opens briefly → bolus enters stomach → LEV closes (prevents reflux)

DISORDERS BY PHASE:

ORAL PREPARATORY:
  Decreased cognition (unaware of food in front of them)
  Impulsivity (TBI — dangerously large bites, packing food)
  Weak mandible, lip, tongue → poor bolus control

ORAL:
  Anterior spillage (drooling) — poor lip seal
  Premature spillage — poor posterior seal
  Weak tongue tip — discoordinated posterior movement
  Pocketed food in sulcus — residue/stasis
  Insufficient tongue propulsive force

PHARYNGEAL:
  Delayed swallow response (seconds to many seconds)
  Impaired timing/coordination
  Weak soft palate → nasal regurgitation
  Weak laryngeal elevation → epiglottis fails to close → penetration/aspiration
  UEV may not open → food can't enter esophagus
  Weak vocal fold closure → aspiration
  SILENT ASPIRATION — no protective cough or choking (no sensation)

ESOPHAGEAL:
  UEV does not open/remain open long enough
  Slow/absent esophageal peristalsis → residue, discomfort, infection
  LEV does not relax → food cannot enter stomach

ASSESSMENT

THREE REFERRAL REASONS:
1. At risk for aspiration based on medical diagnosis
2. Observed feeding difficulties
3. Not taking in adequate nourishment

CLINICAL (BEDSIDE) SWALLOW EVALUATION:
1. Review medical chart
2. Screen/interview — protective cough strength is KEY
3. Oral-mechanism examination
4. Trial swallows — thin/nectar/honey/spoon-thick liquids; regular/mech.altered/pureed foods
5. Determine aspiration risk and need for instrumental evaluation
6. Document in medical chart
Note: Bedside CANNOT definitively confirm aspiration

MBSS (Modified Barium Swallow Study) — GOLD STANDARD:
All 4 phases; dynamic X-ray video; SLP + radiologist team; barium-coated food/liquid
Most thorough; can test compensatory techniques during study
Limitations: radiation; must be in radiology suite

FEES (Fiber-Optic Endoscopic Evaluation of Swallowing):
Pharyngeal phase focus; flexible endoscope transnasally; food dyed with food coloring
No radiation; portable/bedside capable; color view of anatomy and vocal fold movement
Limitation: oral phases not visible; brief white-out during swallow itself

notes.title: Ch. 16 — Right-Hemisphere Syndrome and Dementia
notes.body:
SOURCE: Ch. 16 PPTX (Fogle textbook); Kidwell lecture notes, Comm Dis 1A, SJDC

RIGHT-HEMISPHERE SYNDROME
Results from damage to the RIGHT hemisphere of the brain
Causes impairments in multiple domains:

COGNITIVE IMPAIRMENTS:
  Attention, perception, memory, orientation, organization
  Insight — including ANOSOGNOSIA (unaware of own deficits — makes treatment challenging)
  Reasoning and problem-solving
  Executive functions

COMMUNICATION IMPAIRMENTS:
  LITERAL INTERPRETATION of messages, metaphors, idioms, and proverbs
    Example: takes 'break a leg' to mean physically break one's leg
  PRAGMATIC VIOLATIONS: irrelevant, tangential, inappropriate comments
  APROSODIA: flat, monotone speech with reduced emotional prosody
  FLAT AFFECT: reduced emotional expression overall
  Difficulty with topic maintenance and turn-taking
  May have difficulty naming categories or describing pictures

VISUAL-SPATIAL IMPAIRMENTS:
  LEFT-SIDE NEGLECT: fails to perceive objects/stimuli on left side of body — driving is dangerous
  PROSOPAGNOSIA: difficulty recognizing familiar faces

ASSESSMENT: MIRBI-2, RICE-R, RHLB-2
TREATMENT: similar principles to aphasia and cognitive disorder therapy; assess attention and visual-perceptual disturbances

EMOTIONAL EFFECTS: damage to self-image and self-confidence; family members significantly affected

DEMENTIA

DEFINITIONS:
  DEMENTIA: degenerative neurological functioning MORE SEVERE than normal aging
    Decline in intellect, memory, communication, and personality
    Must interfere with daily functioning in work or social life
  SENILITY: NORMAL loss of cognition due to aging — NOT a disorder
    Key distinction: dementia is pathological; senility is normal

TYPES of DEMENTIA: vascular disease dementia, multi-infarct dementia, frontotemporal dementia, Alzheimer's disease

ALZHEIMER'S DISEASE (AD / DAT):
  Progressive neurological disease with decline in intellect, memory, communication, and personality
  MRI may reveal CORTICAL ATROPHY
  Definitive diagnosis requires BRAIN AUTOPSY — cannot be confirmed in a living patient
  Preceded by PRECLINICAL Alzheimer's (brain changes, no clinical symptoms yet)

STAGES OF ALZHEIMER'S DISEASE:

STAGE I — Mild / Early / Forgetfulness:
  Signs subtle and difficult to recognize; patient frustrated and angry at increasing impairments
  Impaired working memory
  Difficulty completing familiar tasks
  Forgetting common names
  Misplacing items in inappropriate places
  Sudden unexplained mood/behavior changes
  Indifference to personal appearance
  Disorientation to person, place, time, and purpose
  Getting lost while driving on familiar streets

STAGE II — Moderate / Middle / Confusion:
  More apparent; DIAGNOSIS OFTEN MADE AT THIS STAGE (family seeks medical evaluation)
  Most difficult stage — PERSONAL SAFETY is the greatest concern; constant monitoring needed
  Increased loss of working memory
  Significantly diminished vocabulary
  Conversation increasingly empty and meaningless
  Loss of reading and writing ability
  Difficulty with skilled movements (tying shoelaces, using utensils)
  Aggressiveness, outbursts of anger, or withdrawal
  Inappropriate public behavior
  Hallucinations or delusions
  Nursing home placement common when safety/hygiene cannot be maintained

STAGE III — Severe / Late / Terminal:
  Patient can no longer think, reason, or communicate
  Usually NOT a good candidate for speech therapy
  Little or no memory
  Minimal verbal communication
  No emotional expression
  Cannot recognize others or themselves in a mirror
  Total ADL dependence
  DYSPHAGIA with risk for aspiration pneumonia, weight loss, dehydration

Note: Stages overlap — no exact timing. Called 'the long goodbye.'

SLP ROLE IN DEMENTIA:
  DOES NOT diagnose dementia — that is physicians' and psychologists' role
  Purpose: determine cognitive/communication/swallowing functioning
  Determine candidacy for therapy
  Cannot cure — uses strategies to MAINTAIN best communication possible
  Goal: maximize QUALITY OF LIFE
  Tools: ABCDD, Communicative Activities of Daily Living 2nd ed., RIPA-G:2

COMMUNICATION TIPS FOR DEMENTIA PATIENTS:
  Allow extra time; minimize distractions; sit face-to-face (catty-corner, no table)
  Speak slowly (not sing-song), clearly, with sufficient loudness
  Use short simple words and sentences (rule of 5s); don't be condescending
  Stick to one topic at a time; write down and frequently summarize instructions
  Listen without interrupting; allow time for questions
  Memory aids at home: whiteboard on refrigerator for daily schedule

EMOTIONAL EFFECTS:
  TBI: personality changes, loss of skills/emotions, anger, isolation
  Right-hemisphere: self-image and confidence affected
  Dementia: anxiety, agitation, depression; 'the long goodbye'
  All: family systems affected; emotional and financial toll
  SLP must address impact on the ENTIRE FAMILY SYSTEM

notes.title: Ch. 16 — Traumatic Brain Injury: Types, Impairments, and Treatment
notes.body:
SOURCE: Ch. 16 PPTX (Fogle textbook); Kidwell lecture notes, Comm Dis 1A, SJDC

COGNITIVE-LINGUISTIC DISORDERS — OVERVIEW
Language problem exists as a SECONDARY issue to a cognitive problem
Most common causes: TBI, right-hemisphere damage, dementia
Distinguished from aphasia where the language system itself is primarily damaged

TYPES OF TBI

CLOSED HEAD INJURY (CHI) — most common civilian TBI:
  Skull NOT penetrated
  Damage from blunt force OR deceleration (brain strikes skull from inside)
  Most common causes: motor vehicle accidents, assaults
  COUP-CONTRECOUP: brain damaged at site of impact (coup) AND opposite side (contrecoup)
  Significant swelling may last days to weeks — causes additional damage
  Blood-brain barrier (BBB) may be disrupted

CONCUSSION (mild TBI / mTBI):
  Usually no loss of consciousness (LOC), but may occur
  Problems at home and work; relationships deteriorate
  POSTCONCUSSION SYNDROME (PCS): long-lasting subtle impairments
  Even mild TBI causes long-term structural changes in white and grey matter

CHRONIC TRAUMATIC ENCEPHALOPATHY (CTE):
  Degenerative brain disease from REPEATED mild TBIs (sports concussions)
  Symptoms begin 8–10 YEARS after repeated mTBIs
  Symptoms: headaches, mood disorders, cognitive difficulties, speech/language difficulties, aggressive behavior, dementia, suicidal tendencies
  ONLY diagnosed by BRAIN AUTOPSY — cannot be confirmed in a living patient
  Discovered by Dr. Bennet Omalu (worked in Stockton, CA)
  Reason professional athletes are now given recovery time after concussions

OPEN HEAD INJURY (OHI / penetrating):
  Skull and brain penetrated by foreign object
  Cognitive impairments may last months to years
  WAR WOUNDS: more extensive due to high velocity, shock waves, and heat from shrapnel
  BLAST INJURIES: polytrauma — injures multiple body parts and organ systems
  PTSD may co-occur: intense fear, horror, helplessness

IMPAIRMENTS FROM TBI

COGNITIVE:
  Attention deficits
  Memory (working, recent, long-term/remote)
  Orientation (person, place, time, purpose — orientation x4)
  Reasoning and problem-solving
  Executive functions: abstract thinking, goal-setting, planning, implementing strategies, evaluating, emotion regulation
  When executive dysfunction present, everyday activities negatively impacted

SPEECH AND LANGUAGE:
  Apraxia, dysarthria, receptive/expressive language, pragmatics, reading, writing
  Difficult to separate cognitive vs. language deficits in TBI
  SOCIAL (PRAGMATIC) COMMUNICATION DISORDER (SCD):
    Using/interpreting gestures, facial expressions, prosody, emotions of others
    Interpreting abstract language
    Following communication rules
    Recognizing own communication impairments
    Theory of Mind deficits possible
  Swallowing disorders may also occur

TBI ASSESSMENT (Evidence-Based Practice):
  Attention and orientation
  Memory (working, recent, long-term/remote)
  Planning and organizing
  Attention to details
  Receptive and expressive language
  Hearing (if suspected)
  Speech systems and motor speech (if detected)
  Swallowing (if present or requested)
  Reasoning and problem-solving for ADLs
  Assessment/treatment in REAL-LIFE SITUATIONS with family and friends
  Standardized tools: BTHI, FAVRES, RIPA-2, SCATBI

TBI TREATMENT (Evidence-Based Practice):
  GOALS: improve physical/cognitive/psychosocial functioning; foster independence; community integration
  ASHA TARGETS: sensory stimulation; family training; maintaining attention for basic activities; reducing confusion; ORIENTATION x4; memory strategies (memory log); problem-solving and reasoning; social skills in small groups; self-monitoring; community outings; vocational rehabilitation
  Computer-assisted treatment (CAT) may be used

notes.title: Ch. 15 — Aphasia Assessment and Treatment
notes.body:
SOURCE: Ch. 15 PPTX (Fogle textbook); Kidwell lecture notes, Comm Dis 1A, SJDC; Handy Handouts #177 and #435

ASSESSMENT OF APHASIA (Evidence-Based Practice)
Step 1: Review medical chart
Step 2: Interview patient and family
Step 3: Formal assessment of receptive and expressive language

RECEPTIVE ABILITIES:
Hearing acuity (with/without hearing aids)
Visual acuity (with/without glasses)
Pointing to objects or pictures named by clinician
Answering simple to complex questions
Following simple to complex spoken directions
Answering questions about spoken discourse
Following conversational speech

EXPRESSIVE ABILITIES:
Evaluation of speech systems (respiratory, phonatory, resonatory, articulatory)
Confrontation naming: naming common objects
Verb association: providing the action associated with objects
Responsive naming: completing phrases (e.g., 'salt and ___')
Word fluency: e.g., name all animals in 1 minute
Repetition: words, phrases, sentences of varying length
Sentence production using a target word
Picture description (discourse)
Conversational speech
Nonverbal communication: imitation of facial expressions and gestures; following simple nonverbal commands
Automatic speech: days of week, months of year

READING: Matching; word-to-picture matching; reading aloud (numbers, letters, words, sentences); answering printed questions; reading paragraphs
WRITING: Copying; writing to dictation; biographical information; object names and functions; written narratives

MULTICULTURAL CONSIDERATIONS:
Standardized tests often not translated into other languages
Use interpreters and translators
Interpret results cautiously considering cultural/linguistic background
Recognize different beliefs about health and disability
Avoid multicultural stereotyping
Consider whether own background is influencing assessment

TREATMENT OF APHASIA (Evidence-Based Practice)
FACTORS AFFECTING TREATMENT SUCCESS:
Age, prior language/literacy skills, site and size of lesion
Severity of impairments
Timing, length, and intensity of therapy
Appropriateness and effectiveness of goals and procedures
Family involvement

TWO PRIMARY APPROACHES:
RESTORATIVE: aims to improve the underlying impaired process; retrain damaged skills
COMPENSATORY: teaches strategies to cope with persistent deficits; maximize daily function
Example: severe word-finding → try restorative word retrieval drills first; if insufficient → teach compensatory gestures or communication board

GENERAL THERAPY GOAL ORDER:
1. Basic language comprehension and communication
2. Receptive language
3. Expressive language
4. Reading
5. Writing

SPECIFIC THERAPY AREAS:
Receptive: auditory comprehension (words → sentences → discourse); following directions; answering questions
Expressive: word-finding; sentence formulation; discourse; conversation
Reading: letter/word recognition → sentences → paragraph-length material → recreational/occupational reading
Writing: individual letters → words → sentences → narratives

COMMUNICATION PARTNER TRAINING:
Speak slowly; pause between topics; use short utterances
Repeat and rephrase; use gestures and facial expressions
Use multiple modalities; create equality in conversation
Do NOT finish sentences; do NOT shout; do NOT use figurative language
Ask yes/no questions; avoid using numbers
Write key words for comprehension support
Do not treat patient as cognitively impaired

ANOMIA TREATMENT (Handy Handout #435):
Goal: INCREASE INDEPENDENCE — patient does as much as possible on their own
SLP: preserve existing skills, restore lost skills, discover new communication strategies
Activities: writing/gestures to cue retrieval; flash cards; word repetition; computer programs
Word retrieval cueing strategies: first sound/letter; meaning questions; picture pointing; fill-in-the-blank sentences
CAREGIVER CAUTION: providing words for patient creates cycle of dependence — patience and positive reinforcement are essential

PERSON-CENTERED CARE / LIFE PARTICIPATION APPROACH TO APHASIA (LPAA):
Emphasizes competence and inclusion in ADLs and community reintegration
Integrate patient back into community as primary goal
Goes beyond impairment-level treatment to quality of life

EMOTIONAL AND SOCIAL EFFECTS:
Patient: loss of language, identity, independence; altered appearance; dependency
Family: passive (medical) → active helper (rehab) → permanent adjustment
Social: job loss, friendship loss, financial strain
Kübler-Ross 5 stages of grief: Denial → Anger → Bargaining → Depression → Acceptance
Family systems theory and family-centered service models apply

notes.title: Ch. 15 — Aphasia Classification and Types
notes.body:
SOURCE: Ch. 15 PPTX (Fogle textbook); Kidwell lecture notes, Comm Dis 1A, SJDC; Handy Handouts #177 and #435

APHASIA DEFINED
Acquired language impairment resulting from neurological damage in the absence of sensory, motor, intellectual, or cognitive impairments
May affect ALL input (auditory, visual, tactile) and output (speaking, writing, gesturing) modalities
Note: technically 'dysphasia' (partial loss) but 'aphasia' is clinical convention
Affects 1 in 250 people; more than 100,000 new cases per year in U.S.
More common than cerebral palsy, Parkinson's disease, or muscular dystrophy

APHASIA SYNDROME CHARACTERISTICS TABLE:
Type                    | Fluency | Comprehension | Repetition | Naming
Wernicke's              |   +     |      -        |     -      |   -
Anomic                  |   +     |      +        |     +      |   -
Transcortical Sensory   |   +     |      -        |     +      |   -
Conduction              |   +     |      +        |     -      |   -
Broca's                 |   -     |      +        |     -      |   -
Transcortical Motor     |   -     |      +        |     +      |   -
Global                  |   -     |      -        |     -      |   -

KEY RULE: Naming (-) in ALL types. Transcortical types ALWAYS have preserved repetition (+).

FLUENT APHASIAS (lesions posterior to central sulcus / left temporal lobe)

WERNICKE'S APHASIA:
Impaired auditory comprehension, verbal expression, reading, and writing
Speech is fluent but meaningless — jargon, neologisms, paraphasias
Patient unaware of errors; gets frustrated when misunderstood
Features: literal paraphasias, verbal paraphasias, neologisms, jargon, poor yes/no reliability

ANOMIC APHASIA:
Most common RESIDUAL symptom after any aphasia
Persistent severe word-retrieval problem — other areas relatively intact
Circumlocutions, generic fillers ('you know,' 'uh'), nonspecific pronouns ('thing,' 'they')
Good comprehension and repetition
Anomic aphasia exists as a standalone type but anomia is a symptom in all aphasia types

THREE TYPES OF ANOMIA (Handy Handout #435):
1. Lexical anomia: knows and can select object, cannot name it
2. Phonological anomia (= Conduction aphasia): knows the word, produces wrong sounds
3. Semantic anomia: meaning is lost; cannot select even when given the name

TRANSCORTICAL SENSORY APHASIA:
Similar to Wernicke's but repetition is PRESERVED
May repeat questions rather than answer (echolalia)
Rarest of fluent aphasias

CONDUCTION APHASIA:
Damage to arcuate fasciculus (connecting Wernicke's area to Broca's area)
Fluent speech, good comprehension, BUT severely impaired repetition
Phonemic paraphasias; more errors with longer utterances

NONFLUENT APHASIAS (lesions in/around Broca's area / left frontal lobe)

BROCA'S APHASIA (most common nonfluent):
Effortful, halting, trial-and-error articulatory groping
Telegraphic speech: content words preserved; functors (a, an, the) omitted
Agrammatism: impaired syntax
Patient knows what they want to say but cannot produce it
Often limited to <4 words; clumsy sound formation
Poor repetition; relatively preserved comprehension
May co-occur with apraxia of speech or dysarthria

TRANSCORTICAL MOTOR APHASIA:
Similar to Broca's EXCEPT repetition is INTACT
Nonfluent; difficulty spontaneously answering questions
Mildly impaired auditory comprehension

GLOBAL APHASIA:
Most severe form; large lesion affecting multiple language areas
All modalities severely impaired: comprehension, expression, reading, writing
Poor prognosis; may retain nonverbal expression through intonation and gestures

PRIMARY PROGRESSIVE APHASIA (PPA):
Degenerative — gets WORSE over time (unlike other aphasia types)
Slow, progressive loss of reading, writing, speaking, and understanding
Other cognitive functions (memory, reasoning) typically spared early
No cure; management with tools and support

VERBAL EXPRESSION IMPAIRMENT TERMINOLOGY:
Circumlocutions: talking around an unretrievable word
Literal (phonemic) paraphasia: wrong sounds within a word
Verbal (semantic) paraphasia: substituting a related word
Neologisms: made-up words
Jargon aphasia: fluent but meaningless output
Telegraphic speech: functor words omitted
Agrammatism: impaired syntax
Alexia: reading impairment
Agraphia: writing impairment

notes.title: Ch. 15 — Language Disorders in Adults: Etiologies and Stroke
notes.body:
SOURCE: Ch. 15 PPTX (Fogle textbook); Kidwell lecture notes, Comm Dis 1A, SJDC

ETIOLOGIES OF NEUROGENIC COMMUNICATION DISORDERS
Young adults: most common = traumatic brain injury (TBI)
Older adults (65+): most common = stroke, tumor, or degenerative disorder
Increased prevalence due to improved medical care and longer life spans
SLP works in: convalescent hospitals, acute/subacute hospitals, inpatient/outpatient clinics, rehab centers, home health, private practice, university clinics

STROKE (Cerebrovascular Accident / CVA)
2nd most common cause of death in the world
Men: higher RISK. Women: more likely to DIE from stroke
Blood flow to the brain is disrupted — oxygenated blood and nutrients cannot reach the area
Strokes can occur in: brain, cerebellum, or brainstem
Location and SIZE of lesion determine the characteristics and extent of damage

FOUR TYPES OF STROKE:
1. CEREBRAL THROMBOSIS (most common occlusive): artery blocked by local debris — atherosclerosis plaque
2. CEREBRAL EMBOLISM (occlusive): traveling clot lodges in a cerebral blood vessel
3. CEREBRAL HEMORRHAGE (hemorrhagic): blood vessel RUPTURES — most common with chronic hypertension
4. TIA (Transient Ischemic Attack): temporary disruption; symptoms resolve; MAJOR warning sign of future stroke

F.A.S.T. ACRONYM:
Face — droop on one side?
Arms — one drifts down?
Speech — slurred or confused?
Time — CALL 911 IMMEDIATELY

WOMEN'S UNIQUE STROKE RISK FACTORS:
Oral contraceptives (especially with age, smoking, hypertension, diabetes)
Migraine headaches with aura (2.5x increased risk)
Pregnancy (increased blood pressure, cardiac stress)
Hormone replacement therapy (progestin + estrogen)

Women's unique symptoms also include: fainting, general weakness, shortness of breath, confusion/disorientation, behavioral change, agitation, hallucinations, nausea, seizures

RECOVERY FACTORS:
Greatest gains: first weeks to months post-stroke
Neural plasticity: brain reroutes pathways — continues for years
Factors: age (younger generally better), overall physical health, site and size of lesion
Right hemisphere lesion → left side of body affected (and vice versa)

OTHER ETIOLOGIES:
TBI: most common neurologic disability in people under 50; 1/3 have permanent disability; even mild TBI = lasting damage
Tumors (neoplasms): benign or malignant; site and size determine impairment
Toxins: alcohol is most common; damage to much of brain = poor prognosis for rehab
Degenerative diseases: Alzheimer's, ALS, Parkinson's disease

notes.title: Chapter 13 — Voice Disorders in Children and Adults
notes.body:
SOURCES: Lecture 10.2 (Kidwell, SJDC) + Ch. 13 PPTX (Fogle)

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
FOUNDATIONS
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Voice = mirror of the person; reflects personality, emotions, attitudes
Voice disorders recorded for 3500+ years
SLPs treat speaking voice disorders

VOICE DISORDER DEFINITION:
  Any deviation of LOUDNESS, PITCH, or QUALITY outside normal range for age/gender/background
  AND interferes with communication, draws unfavorable attention, or adversely affects speaker/listener
  Subjective terms: hoarse, breathy, harsh, husky, raucous, strained, tense, weak, tremulous, monotone

PREVALENCE:
  Children: ~6% | Adults 0–65: ~1% | Females: ~1% | Males: ~0.5% | 70+: 2.5%
  ★ TEACHERS have highest rate of any profession
  Most frequent diagnoses: acute laryngitis, nonspecific dysphonia, benign lesions (nodules, polyps, contact ulcers)
  Laryngeal cancer: ~2% (mostly males)

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
CLASSIFICATION (Boone et al.)
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
1. FUNCTIONAL: faulty use/abuse — no structural or neurological cause
2. NEUROLOGICAL: muscle tone and control problems; innervation of respiration/phonation
3. ORGANIC: physical abnormality in the larynx

Also:
• HYPERFUNCTION: excessive effort/tension on vocal folds
• HYPOFUNCTION: poor laryngeal muscle tone → weak, breathy voice
• ACUTE vs. CHRONIC

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
CAUSES BY AGE
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Children 1–18: ★ NODULES (~60%), cysts (~10%), laryngitis (~7%)
Adults 19–60: functional dysphonia (20.5%), GERD/acid laryngitis (12.5%), polyps (12%)
Adults 60+: PRESBYPHONIA (26.5%), functional dysphonia (17.5%), polypoid degeneration (14%)

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
FUNCTIONAL VOICE DISORDERS
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
LARYNGITIS: acute (cold, straining, alcohol) or chronic
VOCAL NODULES: benign bilateral lesions; abuse/misuse; most common in children
  ★ PREVENTABLE + TREATABLE via vocal hygiene; eliminate abusive behaviors → nodules heal
  Also called cheerleaders' nodes, screamers' nodes, singers' nodes
VOCAL POLYPS: benign; same causes as nodules; various forms
CONTACT ULCERS: posterior vocal fold region; hard glottal attacks/hyperfunction
FUNCTIONAL DYSPHONIA / MUSCLE TENSION DYSPHONIA (MTD): extreme laryngeal tension; no organic cause
FUNCTIONAL APHONIA: speaks in whisper; CAN produce voice (laughs, coughs normally); emotional/conversion reaction
MUTATIONAL FALSETTO (PUBERPHONIA): young men persist in pre-pubertal high pitch; anterior 1/3 vibrates, posterior 2/3 tightly open; emotional/fear of growing up

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
ORGANIC VOICE DISORDERS
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
PAPILLOMAS: viral wart-like growths; most common in children 4–6 (juvenile papillomatosis)
LARYNGEAL CANCER: life-threatening; mostly males; SMOKING is major cause
  ★ Any voice disorder → rule out malignancy (ENT required)
PRESBYPHONIA: age-related voice changes (most common in 60+)

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
NEUROLOGICAL VOICE DISORDERS
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
HYPOADDUCTION (folds don't close enough):
  Vocal fold PARESIS or PARALYSIS
  Unilateral → breathiness or DIPLOPHONIA
  Bilateral → can PREVENT RESPIRATION (life-threatening)

HYPERADDUCTION (folds close too tightly):
  SPASMODIC DYSPHONIA (SD): rare CNS disorder; strained/strangled voice
  Does NOT respond to voice therapy alone
  Tx: BOTOX injection OR surgical severing of recurrent laryngeal nerve

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
MEDICAL CLEARANCE RULE
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
★ NEVER treat voice disorders without MEDICAL CLEARANCE
SLP cannot determine cause by listening — must rule out malignancy
ENT (OTOLARYNGOLOGIST) sees client FIRST:
  Indirect laryngoscopy (laryngeal mirror)
  Endoscopy (rigid or flexible fiber-optic)
  Direct laryngoscopy
  Biopsy

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
ASSESSMENT (after ENT clearance)
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Case history + ENT report review
Listening + observing (loudness, pitch, quality, throat clearing, tension)
Hearing screening
Voice quantification:
  CAPE-V (Consensus Auditory-Perceptual-Evaluation of Voice)
  GRBAS Scale (Grade, Roughness, Breathiness, Asthenic, Strain)
Acoustic features: habitual pitch, pitch range, optimal pitch, max phonation time
Physiologic: aerodynamic, vibratory behavior, airflow rates
Instruments: VisiPitch, Computerized Speech Lab (CSL)
Baseline established for measuring therapy progress

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
VOICE THERAPY — THREE APPROACHES
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
1. HYGIENIC VOICE THERAPY: ★ FIRST STEP; decrease vocal abuse; hydration; eliminate abusive behaviors
2. PHYSIOLOGIC VOICE THERAPY: exercise to alter physiology of respiratory/phonatory/resonatory systems
3. SYMPTOMATIC VOICE THERAPY: modify symptoms via auditory feedback, counseling, patient education

Requires: anatomy/physiology knowledge, good ear, psychology/counseling skills

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
LARYNGECTOMY + ALARYNGEAL SPEECH
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Total laryngectomy (usually for cancer):
  Trachea → sutured to neck skin → permanent STOMA
  Pharynx closed as separate swallowing tract
  No vocal sound possible

3 ALARYNGEAL SPEECH OPTIONS:
1. TEP (Tracheoesophageal Prosthesis): air trachea → esophagus → oral cavity; most natural-sounding
2. ESOPHAGEAL SPEECH: push air oropharynx → esophagus → vibrates upper esophageal valve; 'burp-like'; no device
3. ELECTROLARYNX: device at neck; transmits sound through neck; ALL sounds voiced

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
SPECIAL TOPICS
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
TRANSGENDER VOICE: NOT a disorder; SLP helps achieve desired voice identity (transmasculine/transfeminine)

EMOTIONAL/SOCIAL EFFECTS BY AGE:
  Young children: unaware/unconcerned; parent counseling essential first
  Adolescents: abuse voice by yelling/cheering; lack motivation until chronic; texting helps give voice rest
  Adults: better coping skills; quality of life considerations; holistic approach

notes.title: Chapter 12 — Fluency Disorders in Children and Adults
notes.body:
SOURCES: Lecture 9.2 (Kidwell, SJDC) + Essentials_of_Fluency_Disorders.pdf (Fogle Ch. 12)

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
FOUNDATIONS
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
FLUENCY: continuity, smoothness, rate, and effort in speech production
Most researched disorder in SLP; most commonly thought-of speech disorder
Person-first language: 'a person who stutters' (though some researchers self-identify as 'stutterer')

NORMAL DISFLUENCY:
  • Occasional syllable/word repetitions (1–2x), hesitations, fillers ('uh,' 'er,' 'um')
  • Most common: 1½–5 years of age; come and go
  • Often signal language being used in new ways
  SLP task: determine if disfluencies are NORMAL or ABNORMAL

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
STUTTERING DEFINITION
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
An abnormally high frequency or duration of stoppages in the forward flow of speech affecting its CONTINUITY, RHYTHM, RATE, and EFFORTFULNESS
Also known as: stammering, disfluency
★ Complete definition requires TWO elements:
  1. OVERT BEHAVIORS: audible (what we hear) + visible (what we see)
  2. COVERT REACTIONS: feelings, beliefs, thoughts

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
OVERT BEHAVIORS — AUDIBLE
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
1. BLOCKS — complete airflow/voicing stoppage
2. DYSRHYTHMIC PHONATIONS — disrupted rhythm
3. INTERJECTIONS — added sounds/syllables/words ('uh-uh-uh')
4. PART-WORD REPETITIONS — 'b-b-b-baby'
5. PHRASE REPETITIONS
6. PROLONGATIONS — sound stretched out
7. REVISIONS — change direction mid-utterance
8. WHOLE-WORD REPETITIONS

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
OVERT BEHAVIORS — VISIBLE
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
'Tricks or crutches' used in struggle to avoid stuttering:
  Eye contact loss, rapid eye blinking
  Forehead furrowing, facial muscle tension, head jerking
  Shoulder tensing/raising, arm swinging/jerking, fist clenching
  Chest muscle tension
  Leg movements, foot tapping/pulling, toe tensing
★ Trying NOT to stutter → physical struggle behaviors develop

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
COVERT REACTIONS
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Feelings: frustration, anxiety, anger, guilt, hostility, shame
★ CAN BE MOST SERIOUS CHALLENGES in life and therapy

Anxiety/fear → AVOIDANCE BEHAVIORS:
  • Not speaking in class; not asking questions
  • Refusing/minimizing oral presentations
  • Avoiding social activities (fear of embarrassment/rejection)
  • Avoiding people hardest to talk to
  • Substituting non-feared words for feared words
  • Some develop large vocabularies just to avoid feared words

Life choices impacted:
  • Career chosen to minimize communication demands
  • Long-term relationships affected
  • Self-imposed isolation and loneliness

★ THE CYCLE:
  Negative emotions → tension → MORE stuttering → more negative emotions (self-reinforcing)

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
PREVALENCE AND DEMOGRAPHICS
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
★ 1% of population
90% onset between ages 2–6
At onset: slightly more common in boys
By school age: girls more likely to recover naturally
★ School-age ratio: 3–4 MALES to 1 FEMALE
Runs in families; environmental factors also contribute
Intelligence: WITHIN NORMAL LIMITS
No specific personality trait; NOT a personality disorder
Brain function during stuttering differs from non-stuttering (cause vs. compensation unclear)
Often co-occurs with other communication disorders, but can be the only disorder

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
ETIOLOGY (THEORIES)
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
No single cause; significant expert disagreement
1. BREAKDOWN THEORIES: environmental stress + neurologic predisposition
2. REPRESSED NEED THEORIES: unmet emotional needs (Kidwell: unfounded/antiquated)
3. ANTICIPATORY STRUGGLE: fearing/avoiding stuttering CREATES it
★ MULTIFACTORIAL (Kidwell's view): neurological mistiming + genetic tendency + stress → anxiety → avoidance → struggle behaviors → more anxiety → cycle

CONDITIONS THAT INCREASE STUTTERING:
  Own name/'I'; waiting to speak; telephone; authority figures; high stress

CONDITIONS THAT DECREASE STUTTERING:
  ★ SINGING — almost always fluent while singing
  Metronome/beat; shadowing; reading in unison
  ★ DAF (Delayed Auditory Feedback) — delays own voice playback; now fits like hearing aid

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
CLUTTERING
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Related disorder — DIFFERENT from stuttering:
  ★ LACKS repetitions and blocks
  Abnormally fast OR irregular speech (or both)
  Lacks normal sound, syllable, phrase, pausing patterns
  Speech appears DISORGANIZED (not disfluent)
  Listener: 'they have something to say but I can't follow it'
  Also a speech AND language disorder
  Different incidence from stuttering

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
ASSESSMENT
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Foremost: PARENT INTERVIEW + general speech/language eval
  Requires interview AND counseling skills
Formal + informal assessment in MULTIPLE SETTINGS
  (therapy room, classroom, recess, small/large group)
Analyze: disfluency types, frequency, conditions, physical behaviors, covert reactions
★ SSI-4 (Stuttering Severity Instrument-4): most common formal tool
  Measures: disfluency types, rate, physical behaviors, naturalness
  Ranges: normal disfluencies → severe stuttering

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
THERAPY
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
CHILDREN:
  Not all disfluent children need direct therapy → PREVENTION first
  Stuttering Foundation of America: stutteringhelp.org (great resource)
  Parents/teachers given verbal + written strategies
  Direct therapy if indicated: open relationship, trust, confidence
  Clinician: understanding, accepting; show parents how to help
  ★ Successful speaking activities + opportunities = helpful

2 DOMINANT APPROACHES:
  FLUENCY SHAPING (FS):
    Teaches NEW speech pattern (relaxed respiration, VFs, articulators)
    Highly structured; behavioral modification
    Exercises: slow/easy speech, smooth exhalation
    Techniques: CANCELLATIONS (stop → pause → retry fluently); PULL-OUTS (modify mid-stutter)
    Therapy emphasis: muscular tension awareness, speech analysis, building new skills

  STUTTERING MODIFICATION (SM):
    Goal: COMMUNICATION IMPROVEMENT (not perfect fluency)
    Targets fear, avoidance, escape → spontaneous fluency emerges
    Therapy emphasis: insight into attitudes/thoughts/feelings about stuttering

Gregory (2003) — 4 therapy areas for adolescents/adults:
  1. Insight into attitudes, thoughts, feelings about stuttering
  2. Muscular tension awareness through relaxation
  3. Speech analysis and modification
  4. Building new speech skills

ADOLESCENTS/ADULTS:
  ★ INTERNAL MOTIVATION is essential
  Emphasis on stutterer's own perception of the problem
  May have strong emotions from past therapy
  Change required: evolution, revolution, resolution
  Those who complete therapy → good chance of long-lasting benefit

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
EMOTIONAL/SOCIAL EFFECTS
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
More sensitive temperaments; anxiety (primary emotion)
Low self-esteem; excessive need for social approval
Habitual avoidance of speaking
Career choices influenced; low-communication jobs
Self-imposed isolation; relationship impacts
Parents: guilt, embarrassment, wonder if they caused it
★ Many successful, happy, famous people stutter — 'Stutterers Are in Good Company' (Fogle)

notes.title: Chapter 9 — ADHD and Auditory Processing Disorders
notes.body:
SOURCES: Lecture 7.2.2 (Kidwell, SJDC) + Ch. 9 PPTX (Fogle) + ADHD_APD.pdf

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
ADHD OVERVIEW
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
First described 1845; accepted as disorder 1902
DSM-5 (2013): problems in 3 areas — inattention, impulsivity, hyperactivity
Prevalence: ~3–5% of classrooms; ~5.3% worldwide; ~9% US parental reports (4–17 yrs)
More common in boys
Avg first diagnosis: age 7 (physician), age 6 (psychiatrist/psychologist)
★ SLPs CANNOT diagnose ADHD

3 PRIMARY COMPONENTS:
  1. INATTENTION: difficulty selecting what to attend to OR sustaining attention for age-appropriate tasks
     Signs: trouble concentrating, unrelated thoughts, not listening, planning/organizing difficulty, poor self-regulation
  2. IMPULSIVITY: difficulty controlling/regulating own behavior
     Signs: acts before thinking, cannot wait turn
  3. HYPERACTIVITY: excess purposeless physical movement
     Signs: squirming, roaming, tapping, fidgety, bounces between activities, multitasks poorly

DSM-5 DIAGNOSTIC CRITERIA (all 4 required):
  1. Symptoms present ≥ 6 MONTHS
  2. Onset BEFORE AGE 12
  3. In TWO OR MORE SETTINGS (home + school)
  4. Symptoms INTERFERE with social, academic, or occupational functioning

EXECUTIVE DYSFUNCTION:
  Many ADHD symptoms = executive function problems
  Controlled by FRONTAL LOBE
  Includes: attention, time management, task switching, planning, organizing, remembering details, multitasking
  Two categories: ORGANIZATION (gather/structure info) + REGULATION (adjust behavior)

COMORBIDITIES:
  APD, learning disabilities, behavioral disorders, anxiety, depression, ASD, TBI, motor problems, sleep problems, ODD
  Children with speech/language disorders → higher ADHD rates
  ★ Incarcerated individuals: ~40% ADHD prevalence (5x higher than general population)

ASSESSMENT (SLP role):
  Diagnosis = physicians + psychologist/neuropsychologist
  SLP: observe, interview, assess speech/language, evaluate pragmatics/narrative/planning
  Formal + informal measures; parental/teacher behavioral rating scales

TREATMENT:
  Team approach
  Behavioral management + stimulant medication
  Help family/child understand ADHD
  Classroom environmental modifications
  SLP: language/study skills; teacher support; therapy accommodations

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
AUDITORY PROCESSING (APD / CAPD)
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Auditory Processing (ASHA, 2005): perceptual processing of auditory information in the CNS + neurobiological activity underlying the processing
  = What the brain does with what it hears:
    • Understanding what was heard
    • Remembering it
    • Maintaining sequence
    • Combining with visual info
    • Effectively using the info
  ★ HEARING occurs in the BRAIN — not the ear
  Ear = sensor only (sound waves → fluid waves → electrochemical signals → brain)

APD DEFINITION (ASHA, 2005): difficulty using auditory information to communicate and learn WITHOUT peripheral hearing loss
  ★ Ear/peripheral hearing = INTACT
  ★ Problem = CNS cannot properly process what ear correctly received
  Kidwell's view: APD = a type of RECEPTIVE LANGUAGE DISORDER
  Paucity (limited) of research

Anology: listening to Spanish radio while talking → brain can't process competing language → APD patients experience this in their PRIMARY language

APD INDICATIONS:
  ★ Difficulty understanding speech in NOISY environments (auditory figure-ground difficulty)
  • Misunderstands messages; inappropriate/inconsistent responses
  • Frequently asks 'What?' or 'Huh?'
  • Difficulty attending, avoiding distraction
  • Delayed responses
  • Cannot follow complex/multi-step directions
  • Reduced musical/singing skills
  • Reading, spelling, learning problems
  • Unusual reactions to loud/sudden sounds
  • Difficulty with names, dates, times
  • Poor relationships; few friends
  • Slow/inappropriate responses; appears confused

SPEECH/LANGUAGE PROBLEMS WITH APD:
  Auditory figure-ground; difficulty with subtle sound differences; speech/reading/spelling/writing problems;
  following conversations; answering questions; jokes; multi-part directions; rapid speech; memory for info

ADHD vs. APD DISTINCTION:
  APD: noisy settings, oral directions, listening, auditory association → HEARING is the problem
  ADHD: inattentive, hyperactive, impulsive, interrupts → ATTENTION/BEHAVIOR is the problem
  Both may co-occur!

ASSESSMENT:
  ★ ONLY AUDIOLOGISTS can diagnose APD (sound-treated booth; cortex-level testing)
  SLP: FIRST to recognize; SCREEN and REFER
  APD screening tools: SCAN-3:C, ASA (Geffner & Goldman), DST-P (Richard & Ferre)
  Further: TAPS-3, DTAP, comprehensive language evaluation

TREATMENT (3-component approach):
  1. ENVIRONMENTAL MODIFICATIONS: reduce noise, preferential seating, improve acoustics
  2. PERSONAL FM SYSTEM: teacher wears mic/transmitter; child wears receiver → voice delivered directly
  3. COMPENSATORY STRATEGIES: teach child to manage difficult listening
  + Direct auditory training: phoneme discrimination, temporal processing, pattern recognition
  Treatment must be FREQUENT + INTENSE; computer software supplements

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
EMOTIONAL/SOCIAL EFFECTS
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
ADHD: conduct disorder, ODD, anxiety, depression, school suspension, trouble with police
  ★ ~40% of incarcerated individuals have ADHD (5x general population)
APD: paucity of research; similar to language disorder impacts; poor relationships, few friends, academic underachievement

notes.title: Chapter 7 — Language Disorders in Children
notes.body:
SOURCES: Lecture 6.2 (Kidwell, SJDC) + Ch. 7 slides (Fogle) + Sariah video transcript
TEXTBOOK: Essentials of Communication Sciences and Disorders, 3rd ed. — Fogle

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
FOUNDATIONS
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Language disorder: impaired comprehension and/or use of spoken, written (graphic), and/or other symbol systems (ASHA)

Three components of language (any combination can be affected):
  FORM: phonology + morphology + syntax
  CONTENT: semantics
  USE/FUNCTION: pragmatics/social communication

SLP scope: treat receptive AND expressive disorders in spoken AND written modalities

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
KEY DISTINCTIONS
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Language DISORDER vs. DELAY:
  ★ DISORDER: will NOT catch up; gaps WIDEN over time
  ★ DELAY: slow start; may catch up ('slow talker', 'late bloomer'); some mild delays resolve
  ★ Do NOT use interchangeably!

Language DIFFERENCE vs. DISORDER:
  Difference = influenced by culture/linguistics (ESL, dialect) — NOT a disorder
  No dialect is superior; assess in PRIMARY language
  If ONE language WNL → NOT a language disorder
  Learning a 2nd language is NOT a disability

Primary vs. Secondary Language Impairment:
  PRIMARY: language IS the main handicapping condition (e.g., SLI)
  SECONDARY: another diagnosis causes the language impairment (e.g., ASD → communication disorder)

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
SPECIFIC LANGUAGE IMPAIRMENT (SLI)
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Definition: significant receptive and/or expressive language impairments NOT attributed to any cause or condition
Present in 7–8% of school-entering children (2% more in boys)
★ DIAGNOSIS OF EXCLUSION — defined by the ABSENCE of other disorders
Most frequent reason for early intervention and special education services

Three required criteria:
  1. Hearing WITHIN NORMAL LIMITS
  2. No obvious perceptual or neurological disorders
  3. Nonverbal intelligence WITHIN NORMAL LIMITS

★ RED FLAGS / Late Talker Warning Signs:
  • Slow speech sound development
  • No 'Mama'/'Dada' by 12–18 months
  ★ First true word AFTER 18 months
  ★ Two-word combinations AFTER 30 months
  • Restricted vocabulary in comprehension AND production
  • Reliance on gestures
  • Infrequent/poor verb development
  • No yes/no responses
  • Difficulty with peer interaction and turn-taking
  • Difficulty rhyming and letter naming

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
RECEPTIVE LANGUAGE
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Definition: active process — listener INFERS meaning from context + long-term stored memory; language we UNDERSTAND

Children with SLI receptive problems:
  • Speech comes too fast — difficulty keeping up
  • Better with DIRECT than INDIRECT questions/statements
  • LITERAL interpretation — miss idioms, metaphors, similes
  • Difficulty with abstract concepts
  • Poor comprehension of individual words

★ RECEPTIVE impairment = MORE RESISTANT to intervention = GREATER RISK for adverse academic outcomes
★ 50%+ of SLI pre-K/K children develop reading disabilities; 20% more are poor readers

Adolescent receptive problems: weak vocabulary; figurative/abstract language; following directions; lecture comprehension; misreading facial expressions

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
EXPRESSIVE LANGUAGE — FIVE PROBLEM AREAS
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
1. ARTICULATION/PHONOLOGY: poor intelligibility affects interaction

2. MORPHOLOGY/SYNTAX: grammatically incomplete/incorrect
   • Low MLU for age
   • Missing markers: plural -s, verb -ed/-ing, possessive 's
   • Short/incomplete sentences ('Milk') or incorrect structure ('Why you not play?')
   • 'Those childs goed' / 'Her is stand in water'

3. VOCABULARY/SEMANTICS (SLI risk):
   • First words late; <20 words by 18–24 months
   • Two-word combos not by 24 months
   • Word-finding problems
   • Poor concept development (adjectives, verbs, basic concepts)
   • Difficulty with wh- questions and NARRATIVES

4. METALINGUISTICS: language as a tool — multiple meanings, figurative language, inferences, sentence types

5. PRAGMATICS: social language use — turn-taking, topic maintenance, eye contact, personal space, volume

Adolescent expressive: incomplete/inappropriate communication; low-content words ('stuff', 'things'); simple syntax; verb tense errors; perceived as 'abrasive'

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
SARIAH VIDEO — CLINICAL ANALYSIS
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Expressive language disorder — Beverly Hills Chihuahua retelling:
  • 'Puted' for 'put' → irregular past tense error (morphology)
  • Characters not identified clearly → referential/narrative problem
  • Events out of sequence → narrative organization deficit
  • Short, simple sentences → low MLU/syntactic complexity
  • Listener cannot follow without prior movie knowledge
NOT primarily a speech sound disorder — intelligibility is sufficient
Affects: morphology, syntax, narrative organization

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
LEARNING DISABILITIES
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
SLI → school-age → Language Learning Disability (LLD)
Language Learning Disability: impairment of receptive/expressive linguistic symbols affecting learning and educational achievement
Learning Disability (LD): disorder in basic psychological processes for understanding/using language; may manifest as reading, writing, spelling, math, reasoning difficulties
★ NOT diagnosed until school age (must have had opportunity to learn)
LLD runs in families; ~15% of children with LD also have motor coordination difficulties

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
ASSESSMENT
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Screening → Full Assessment if indicated
Formal assessment: standardized tests (CA standard: 1.5 SD below norm on 2+ tests in 1 area for eligibility)
Informal assessment: language sample (50+ utterances), observation
Language sample prompts: OPEN-ENDED ('Tell me about your dog') not closed ('Do you have a dog?')
Dynamic assessment: teach skill → observe learning rate
Standardized tests are biased → assess in PRIMARY language
★ BOTH languages must show impairment for diagnosis

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
THERAPY
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Evidence-based, effective, functional, attainable
Goals: specific to child's needs; based on normative data
Progression: simple → complex language goals
Long-term goals (end result) + short-term goals (baby steps)
Well-organized, tightly structured; flexibility built in
Tangible reinforcers for young children
★ Early identification and treatment CRITICAL for long-term school and life outcomes

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
EMOTIONAL/SOCIAL/LONG-TERM EFFECTS
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Language disorder → school struggles → poor self-image → giving up
Long-term impacts: education, career, relationships, psychiatric disorders
Early identification reduces these long-term consequences significantly
SLPs must address emotional and social effects in therapy

notes.title: Chapter 17 — Motor Speech Disorders in Adults
notes.body:
SOURCES: Lecture 5.2.2 (Kidwell, SJDC) + Ch. 17 PPTX (Fogle) + Motor Speech Disorders PDF

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
OVERVIEW
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
★ Disordered speech is sometimes the FIRST SIGN of neurological disease
Motor speech disorders = SENSORIMOTOR (not purely motor) — sensory feedback also impaired
Result from neurological impairments affecting motor planning, programming, neuromuscular control, or execution of speech
May co-occur with aphasia and cognitive impairments (stroke, TBI, degenerative disease)
Two types: Apraxia of Speech (AOS) and Dysarthria

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
APRAXIA OF SPEECH (AOS)
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Definition: deficit in neural motor planning/programming of articulatory muscles for voluntary speech — in the ABSENCE of muscle weakness; primarily affects articulation and prosody

Cause: damage to/around BROCA'S AREA — most commonly STROKE
Computer analogy: muscles fine (monitor/mouse/hard drive work); but the APP (motor program) won't load correctly

★ AOS CHARACTERISTICS (not all needed for diagnosis):
  1. INCONSISTENCY — hallmark (variable, unpredictable errors)
  2. Errors NOT from muscle weakness
  3. SUBSTITUTIONS > distortions, omissions, additions
  4. CONSONANT errors > vowel errors
  5. INITIAL consonant errors most common
  6. CONSONANT CLUSTERS more affected than single consonants
  7. Errors INCREASE with word length
  8. GROPING — trial-and-error articulatory searching
  9. FRONT-of-mouth sounds (/b,d,z/) better than BACK (/k,g,ch,sh/)
  10. 'ISLANDS OF FLUENCY' — fluent phrases amid struggling speech
  11. Adequate isolated movements; overtaxed in complex speech sequences
  12. Disfluencies (stutter-like)
  13. AUTOMATIC SPEECH INTACT — counting, songs, common phrases, cursing

Oral Apraxia (Buccofacial/Nonverbal Apraxia): co-occurring difficulty with volitional nonspeech movements — cannot whistle, puff cheeks, protrude tongue on command (though these occur automatically)
Limb Apraxia: may also co-occur

Assessment: Apraxia Battery for Adults (Dabul, 2000); Quick Assessment for AOS (Tanner & Culbertson); Test of Oral and Limb Apraxia (Helm-Estabrooks)

Treatment:
  Primary goal: maximize effectiveness, efficiency, and naturalness of communication
  • DRILL — most effective method
  • Multimodal approach (mirrors, tactile, visual, auditory cues)
  • Start simple, increase complexity
  • Leverage automatic speech/islands of fluency
  ★ The harder they try → the harder it gets → frustration → avoidance
  • AAC for severe/profound cases

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
DYSARTHRIA (ADULTS)
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Definition: neurological speech disorders from weakness/incoordination in muscles controlling respiration, phonation, resonation, and articulation
★ KEY: MUSCLE WEAKNESS IS PRESENT (unlike AOS)

SPEECH BY SYSTEM:
  Respiratory: low intensity; short phrases; frequent pauses to breathe
  Phonatory: breathiness from VF paresis/paralysis
  Resonatory: HYPERNASALITY from soft palate weakness
  Articulatory: distorted consonants; reduced ROM, strength, coordination, rate

6 TYPES OF DYSARTHRIA:
  1. SPASTIC — bilateral UMN damage; harsh voice, hypernasality, slow rate
  2. FLACCID — LMN/PNS damage; breathy, weak pressure consonants
  3. ATAXIC — cerebellar damage; irregular, scanning speech, excess/equal stress
  4. HYPOKINETIC — basal ganglia (Parkinson's); ↓ loudness, monopitch, fast rushes
  5. HYPERKINETIC — basal ganglia; involuntary movements; variable rate/loudness
  6. MIXED — combination; ALS = flaccid + spastic

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
4 NEUROMUSCULAR DISORDERS ASSOCIATED WITH DYSARTHRIA
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
1. PARKINSON'S DISEASE (PD)
   Type: Hypokinetic dysarthria
   Features: ↓ loudness (hypophonia), monopitch, monoloudness, rapid short rushes (festination)
   Treatment: ★ LSVT LOUD (Lee Silverman Voice Treatment)

2. ALS (Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis — Lou Gehrig's Disease)
   Type: Mixed flaccid-spastic dysarthria
   Features: progressive weakness → anarthria; requires AAC eventually
   Note: PROGRESSIVE — no cure

3. MULTIPLE SCLEROSIS (MS)
   Type: Mixed or ataxic dysarthria
   Features: variable with relapse/remission cycles

4. MYASTHENIA GRAVIS
   Type: Fatigable dysarthria
   Features: speech worsens with use; better at start of conversation

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
ASSESSMENT & DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Differential diagnosis: rank possibilities → narrow down
AOS tools: Apraxia Battery for Adults, Quick Assessment for AOS, Test of Oral and Limb Apraxia
Dysarthria tools: Assessment of Intelligibility of Dysarthric Speech; Dysarthria Examination Battery; Frenchay Dysarthria Assessment; Quick Assessment for Dysarthria
All four speech subsystems must be assessed

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
TREATMENT — DYSARTHRIA
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Primary goal: maximize effectiveness, efficiency, and naturalness of communication
  RESTORATIVE: muscle tone, ROM, strength, rate → mild/moderate; goal = natural speech
  COMPENSATORY: rate control, overarticulation, posture → when restoration limited; goal = intelligibility
  AAC if severe

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
PSYCHOSOCIAL CONSEQUENCES
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Highly aware of difficulty being understood
Frustration → avoidance of communication
With AOS: trying harder makes it worse
SLP must address emotional/social effects in therapy
Impacts: verbal communication, interpersonal interactions, quality of life, independence

notes.title: Chapter 6 — Motor Speech Disorders in Children
notes.body:
SOURCES: Lecture 5.2.1 (Kidwell, SJDC) + Ch. 6 PPTX (Fogle) + Motor Speech Disorders PDF

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
OVERVIEW
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Motor Speech Disorder: impaired intelligibility from neurological impairment affecting motor programming/planning, coordination, or articulatory strength needed for rapid, complex speech movements
Two types in children: CAS and Dysarthria
★ Both can occur with aphasia and cognitive disorders
Disordered speech can be the FIRST sign of neurological disease
Motor speech disorders are SENSORIMOTOR — not just motor

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
CHILDHOOD APRAXIA OF SPEECH (CAS)
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Definition: motor speech disorder in the ABSENCE of muscle weakness affecting planning, programming, sequencing, coordinating, and initiating motor movements of the articulators; interferes with articulation AND prosody

Etiology (3 types):
  1. Neurological damage
  2. Primary or secondary sign of neurobehavioral disorder
  3. Idiopathic (spontaneous, no known cause)

CAS vs. Adult AOS:
  • Adult AOS: damage to/around Broca's area (typically stroke)
  • CAS: NOT specifically Broca's — often idiopathic; child may never have spoken correctly

★ CAS CHARACTERISTICS (ASHA, 2007):
  1. INCONSISTENT errors (hallmark)
  2. Lengthened/disrupted transitions between sounds and syllables
  3. Inappropriate prosody (DYSPROSODY)
  4. Vowel errors
  5. Errors INCREASE with increasing length/complexity
  6. High rate of OMISSION errors
  7. GROPING/silent posturing behavior
  8. LACK OF PROGRESS in traditional articulation therapy
  9. Frequent REGRESSION

Associated types:
  • Oral apraxia: difficulty with volitional nonspeech movements (whistling, blowing, smiling on command)
  • Limb apraxia: difficulty with volitional limb movements

ASSESSMENT:
  • CAS = DIAGNOSIS OF EXCLUSION — rule out artic/phonological disorders first
  • Definitive diagnosis may not always be possible
  • Comprehensive oral mechanism + motor speech exams
  • Sequential/alternating movement repetitions (diadochokinesis)
  • Tools: KSPT, STDAS-2, SRT, DEMSS

TREATMENT:
  • Intensive + individualized
  • Motor learning principles + proprioception
  • DRILL — many repetitions
  • Incremental increases in difficulty
  • Multiple modalities (tactile, visual, auditory)
  • PROMPTS (tactile-kinesthetic cues)
  • Traditional artic therapy ALONE is INEFFECTIVE
  • AAC if child is highly unintelligible

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
CHILDHOOD (DEVELOPMENTAL) DYSARTHRIA
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Definition: group of motor speech disorders caused by PARESIS (weakness), PARALYSIS (complete loss), or INCOORDINATION of speech muscles from CNS/PNS damage; may affect respiration, phonation, resonation, articulation, and prosody
★ Sounds 'MUSHY' — distorted consonants and vowels
★ KEY DIFFERENCE from CAS: MUSCLE WEAKNESS IS PRESENT

Etiology: prenatal, perinatal, postnatal, or childhood CNS/PNS damage
May also cause DYSPHAGIA (swallowing disorder)
Most common pediatric cause: CEREBRAL PALSY

Muscle tone types:
  • Hypotonicity: low/floppy muscle tone
  • Hypertonicity: high/rigid muscle tone

SPEECH CHARACTERISTICS BY SYSTEM:
  Respiratory: low intensity; short phrases; frequent pauses
  Phonatory: breathiness (unilateral/bilateral VF paresis/paralysis)
  Resonatory: HYPERNASALITY (soft palate weakness)
  Articulatory: imprecise, distorted consonants; reduced ROM, strength, coordination, rate

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
CEREBRAL PALSY (CP)
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Definition: heterogeneous group of NONPROGRESSIVE, permanent disorders of movement and postural development; often with abnormal muscle tone, loss of selective motor control, weakness, impaired balance
Cause: widespread CNS damage
★ MOST COMMON CAUSE OF DYSARTHRIA IN CHILDREN

4 TYPES:
  1. SPASTIC CP — most common; hypertonicity; stiff, jerky
  2. ATHETOID CP (athetosis) — involuntary writhing; fluctuating tone
  3. ATAXIC CP (ataxia) — balance/coordination problems
  4. MIXED CP — combination (usually spastic + athetoid)

Associated problems:
  • Hearing/vision impairment
  • Seizure disorders
  • Cognitive impairments/Intellectual Disability
  • Receptive/expressive language delays
  • Dysphagia

ASSESSMENT:
  • No formal standardized test for children under 12
  • Perceptual/clinical assessment
  • Assess all speech systems
  • Tool: FDA-2 (Frenchay Dysarthria Assessment, 2nd ed.)

TREATMENT:
  Primary goal: MAXIMIZE COMMUNICATIVE POTENTIAL
  • Increase physiologic support (muscle strength, ROM)
  • Develop phonetic repertoire
  • Improve loudness/pitch/quality (LSVT LOUD)
  • Prosody training
  • Interaction strategies (eye contact, facial expressions)
  • Compensatory strategies (rate control, posture)
  • Language therapy; preliteracy/literacy
  • Family/caregiver education
  • Self-confidence and self-esteem strategies
  • AAC if needed

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
AAC/AT REMINDER
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Used when functional verbal communication cannot be established
Augmentative: ENHANCES verbal communication
Alternative: REPLACES verbal communication
Low-tech → high-tech (communication boards → electronic devices)
Requires multidisciplinary team; individualized to client
4 foundational AAC elements:
  • Active communicators
  • Peers as competent communication partners
  • Families as involved collaborators
  • Educators as effective facilitators

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
EMOTIONAL/SOCIAL EFFECTS
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Children with motor speech disorders have MORE significant negative impacts than those with artic/phonology delays
Affects: social interactions with family, peers, adults
SLP must address negative social implications in therapy
Children are aware of their difficulty being understood → frustration → avoidance of communication

notes.title: Chapter 5 — Articulation and Phonological Disorders in Children
notes.body:
SOURCES: Lecture 4.2 (Kidwell, SJDC) + Ch. 5 PDF slides (Fogle/SJDC) + Quiz answer key + Ch. 5 discussion post
TEXTBOOK: Essentials of Communication Sciences and Disorders, 3rd ed. — Fogle

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
FOUNDATIONS
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Articulation and phonological disorders = MOST COMMON communication disorders in children
May occur in isolation OR with: language disorders, intellectual disability, brain injury, orofacial anomalies (cleft palate)

General American English (GAE):
  • No regional dialect — radio/TV English
  • 26 LETTERS → 45 PHONEMES
  ★ We talk in SOUNDS, not letters; letters represent sounds (not vice versa)

Developmental vs. Acquired disorders:
  • Developmental: child has NEVER spoken correctly
  • Acquired: person LOSES previously established speech skill (accident/disease) — more common in adults

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
PHONETICS & IPA
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Phonetics: study of speech-sound production and the symbols that represent them
IPA: specially devised symbols to represent sounds of ALL languages
  • Letter 'c' does NOT exist in IPA → use /k/ or /s/
  • 'cat' = /kæt/
Allophone: slight variation in phoneme production based on position in word or surrounding sounds

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
VOWELS
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
• ALL vowels are VOICED
• Produced by UNRESTRICTED passage of airstream through mouth
• Tongue PLACEMENT and SHAPE determine the vowel
• Vowel Quadrilateral: High/Mid/Low × Front/Central/Back
• Diphthong: two vowel sounds gliding together in one syllable (bite, boy, bout)

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
CONSONANTS: THREE DISTINCTIVE FEATURES
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
★ PLACE — where articulators meet:
  Bilabial (both lips) → /p, b, m/
  Labiodental (lip + teeth) → /f, v/
  Interdental (tongue between teeth) → /θ, ð/ (th)
  Alveolar (tongue + alveolar ridge) → /t, d, n, s, z, l/
  Palatal (tongue + hard palate) → /sh, zh, ch, j, r/
  Velar (tongue + velum/soft palate) → /k, g, ng/
  Glottal (at glottis) → /h/

★ MANNER — how airstream is modified:
  Stop: airflow completely stops (p, b, t, d, k, g)
  Nasal: air through nasal cavity (m, n, ng)
  Glide: smooth movement (w, y)
  Fricative: continuous turbulent air (f, v, s, z, sh, zh, h, th)
  Lateral/Liquid: /l/
  Affricate: stop + fricative (ch, j)
  Rhotic: /r/ sounds
  Flap: brief tap of tongue

★ VOICE — vocal fold vibration:
  Voiced: folds vibrate (b, d, g, v, z, m, n, ng...)
  Voiceless: folds do NOT vibrate (p, t, k, f, s, h...)
  ALL VOWELS are voiced

COGNATES: two sounds differing ONLY in voicing (same place + manner):
  /p/-/b/, /t/-/d/, /k/-/g/, /f/-/v/, /s/-/z/, /sh/-/zh/, /ch/-/j/

Distinctive Feature: smallest difference needed to distinguish two phonemes

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
ETIOLOGIES (THREE CAUSES)
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
1. NORMAL VARIATION: 70% in normal range; 15% above, 15% below
   SLPs work with the 15% significantly BELOW the mean
   ★ Don't say 'Sloan is doing great' — say 'Sloan is making great progress'

2. ENVIRONMENTAL PROBLEMS:
   Prenatal: substance abuse (★ ONLY 100% preventable cause), syndromes, prematurity
   Perinatal: insufficient O2 → neurologic damage (anoxia = complete lack; hypoxia = inadequate)
   Postnatal: limited stimulation → fewer synaptic brain connections; neglect

3. PHYSICAL IMPAIRMENTS / NEUROLOGIC:
   Congenital syndromes, hearing loss (★ most crucial influence on S&L development), TBI, CP

★ MOST CHILDREN have MULTIPLE (multifactorial) causes — single-factor is less common
★ Causes are often UNKNOWN

Functional vs. Organic:
  Functional: NO identifiable anatomic/physiologic/neurologic cause
  Organic: HAS an identifiable cause

Failure to thrive: abnormal retardation of growth/development from conditions interfering with metabolism, appetite, activity

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
ARTICULATION DISORDERS: SODA
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
★ S — SUBSTITUTION: one sound replaces another (most common type)
   Example: 'tat' for 'cat' → /t/ for /k/ in initial position
★ O — OMISSION: sound left out entirely
   Example: 'at' for 'bat' → /b/ omitted
★ D — DISTORTION: sound altered, no standard symbol for error
   Example: interdental lisp on /s/; distorted /r/
★ A — ADDITION: extra sound inserted
   Example: 'animamal' for 'animal'; 'suh-poon' for 'spoon'

Errors can occur in INITIAL, MEDIAL, or FINAL position
A child may have multiple error types on the same word

Frenum: fold of mucous tissue connecting floor of mouth to underside of tongue

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
PHONOLOGICAL DISORDERS
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
• PATTERNED errors across multiple phonemes
• Child persists in using patterns appropriate for younger age OR uses atypical patterns
• Child IS CAPABLE of the motor movement — problem is LINGUISTIC (hasn't learned rules)
• Simplifies sound combinations systematically

Common Phonological Processes (Table 5-3):
  Context-sensitive voicing: pig→big
  Final-consonant devoicing: bag→bat
  Final-consonant deletion: home→hoe, car→ca
  ★ Velar fronting: key→tea, give→div (k,g → t,d)
  Palatal fronting: ship→sip
  Consonant harmony: dog→gog
  Weak-syllable deletion: telephone→teffone, banana→nana
  ★ CLUSTER REDUCTION: spoon→poon, spider→pider, spill→pill, spot→pot
    (only omits when in a BLEND — 'song' and 'same' are produced correctly)
  Gliding of liquids: red→wed, leg→weg
  Stopping: funny→punny, jump→dump

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
ASSESSMENT
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Steps:
  1. Caregiver questionnaire/interview
  2. Establish rapport
  3. ★ HEARING SCREENING (always — hearing is most crucial influence)
  4. Language screening (co-morbid disorders)
  5. Spontaneous speech sample (intelligibility, error types, patterns, consistency)
  6. Oral motor evaluation (anatomy + physiology of articulators)
  7. Structured standardized articulation assessment (standard scores, percentiles)
     → Needed for school eligibility determination
  8. Phonological assessment if patterned errors found
  9. Stimulability testing

Stimulability: can child produce error sound after model/instruction?
  • Stimulable = positive prognostic indicator
  • Even if says 'wat' for 'rat' in all words, may be stimulable for /r/ in isolation

Emerging sound: inconsistent errors may = sound is emerging → respond well to therapy

Multicultural factors in assessment (required by LAW in public schools):
  • Language differences ≠ disorders
  • Spanish: 'v' used differently → 'base'/'vase' NOT scored as error for Spanish speakers
  • Spanish lacks /sh/ → difficulty expected in English
  • Test in primary language when possible
  • ASHA resources available for phoneme inventories in other languages

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
THERAPY
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
SLPs must be: Scientists + Humanists + Artists
Foundation: Evidence-Based Practice (EBP) — guiding principle of ALL assessment and treatment

Therapy hierarchy (traditional):
  1. Auditory discrimination / ear training
  2. Recognize misarticulation
  3. Production at increasing levels:
     Isolation → Syllable → Word → Phrase → Sentence → Spontaneous speech
  4. ★ GOAL: GENERALIZATION (carryover) — correct in ALL words, ALL settings, ALL conversations

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
MOTOR SPEECH DISORDERS
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
CAS (Childhood Apraxia of Speech):
  • Motor PLANNING disorder — NOT caused by muscle weakness
  • Affects planning, programming, sequencing, coordinating, initiating motor movements
  • Inconsistent errors; more difficulty with longer/complex words
  • Interferes with articulation AND prosody

DYSARTHRIA (Childhood):
  • Group of motor EXECUTION disorders
  • Caused by paresis/paralysis/incoordination → CNS or PNS damage
  • Can affect ALL speech subsystems (respiration, phonation, resonation, articulation, prosody)
  • Associated with dysphagia
  • Most common pediatric cause: CEREBRAL PALSY

CEREBRAL PALSY (CP):
  • Neuromotor impairment from widespread CNS damage
  • Most frequent cause of dysarthria in children
  • Associated: receptive/expressive language delays, cognitive impairments, ID
  • Associated conditions: hearing/vision impairments, seizure disorders
  • AAC/AT often used

AAC / AT:
  • Augmentative: ENHANCES communication
  • Alternative: REPLACES communication
  • High-tech (electronic) + low-tech (communication boards)
  • Selection: direct or scanning
  • Requires multidisciplinary team

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
EMOTIONAL/SOCIAL EFFECTS
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Children with speech disorders:
  • Often teased, labeled, excluded
  • May become frustrated, develop poor self-image
  • May choose to remain silent rather than communicate
  • Difficulty with social competence (parent reports)

Poor communication impacts quality of life:
  • Verbal communication
  • Interpersonal interactions
  • Advanced learning (literacy)
  • Applied/general learning (handling stress)
  • Basic learning (copying, rehearsing)

notes.title: Chapter 4 — Speech and Language Development
notes.body:
SOURCES: Lecture 3.2 (Kidwell, SJDC) + Ch. 4 PDF (Prof. Handy, SJDC) + When Will My Baby Speak video + Quiz answer key
TEXTBOOK: Essentials of Communication Sciences and Disorders, 3rd ed. — Fogle

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
CORE DEFINITIONS
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Speech Development: progressive evolving and shaping of sounds/syllables used as arbitrary symbols to communicate
Language Development: progressive growth of a receptive AND expressive communication system using arbitrary symbols and rule-governed combinations

3 PREREQUISITES FOR NORMAL LANGUAGE DEVELOPMENT (★ exam concept):
  1. Biological structures and functions must develop within normal limits
  2. Cognitive functioning must be sufficient for language to emerge
  3. Social interaction with caregivers must encourage and reward communication

Most language basics complete by age 7–8
Vocabulary continues growing for life
Humor and figurative language expand beyond childhood

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
FOUR THEORIES OF LANGUAGE DEVELOPMENT
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
1. BEHAVIORAL (B.F. Skinner)
   Core: language = verbal behaviors learned through operant conditioning
   Mechanisms: reinforcement, imitation, successive approximation
   Clinical: stimulus-response therapy, shaping approximations
   Limitation: cannot explain novel sentences or untaught vocabulary

2. NATIVIST
   Core: language is innate, prewired, genetically transmitted
   All humans will acquire language unless physical/mental limitations exist
   Language is universal and unique to humans
   Clinical: target age-appropriate structures not naturally acquired
   Limitation: underplays environmental role

3. SEMANTIC-COGNITIVE
   Core: language tied to cognition; meaning precedes form
   Child knows WHAT to communicate before knowing HOW
   Clinical: adjust complexity/amount/rate of speech
   Limitation: doesn't fully explain structural acquisition

4. SOCIAL-PRAGMATIC
   Core: communication is the BASIC FUNCTION of language
   Prerequisites: caregiver proximity, basic needs met, attachment, joint attention, turn-taking
   Clinical: social games, interactive reading, caregiver coaching
   Limitation: doesn't explain why all humans develop same grammar/phonology
★ Reality: it's a MIX of all four theories

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
SPEECH DEVELOPMENT STAGES
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Stage I: BIRTH–12 MONTHS (Infancy)
Birth–6 months (prelinguistic):
  • Crying (first vocalization — neonate)
  • Cooing: vowel-like sounds /u/, /oo/ — NO consonant — comfort/pleasure
  • Babbling: CV in SAME syllable (ba-ba, da, ga) — ★ MUST have consonant
  • Echolalia: immediate, automatic imitation of speech
6–12 months:
  • Vocal Play: longer syllable strings extending babbling
  • Jargon: syllable strings with ADULT PROSODY — no true words
  • 90% of infant sounds: Stops /p,b,t,d,k,g/ + Nasals /m,n/ + Glides /w,j,hw/ + Fricative /s/
★ If prelinguistic vocalizations absent by 8 months → evaluate HEARING
★ Deaf babies: coo/babble then STOP ~8 months (can no longer self-reinforce)

Stage II: 12–24 MONTHS (Toddlerhood)
12 months: First TRUE WORD
  True word criteria (Locke, 1993):
    ✓ Clear intention/purpose
    ✓ Close to adult pronunciation
    ✓ Used consistently in various contexts
18 months: ~50 intelligible words; stressed syllables (nana/banana); final /t/ emerges
24 months: Two-word utterances (mommy up, no juice); final consonants /t,p,k,n/ present

Stage III: 2–5 YEARS (Early Childhood)
  2 yrs: 75% intelligible; 2–3 word sentences; speech = primary communication mode
  3 yrs: 90% intelligible; 3–4 word sentences; vowels complete
  4 yrs: 95% intelligible; blends emerging
  5 yrs: Most sounds emerged (r, l, s, z, sh, ch, j, v, ng, th, blends) — may not be consistent
  Age 3 sounds established: /m, n, p, b, d, k, g, h, f, w/
  Phonological processes: syllable structure, substitution, assimilation

Stage IV: 6–12 YEARS (Middle Childhood → Early Adolescence)
  6–8 yrs: 95–100% intelligible
  9–12 yrs: Speech FULLY MASTERED; adjust communication by audience

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
LANGUAGE DEVELOPMENT STAGES
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Stage I: BIRTH–12 MONTHS (Infancy)
  • Five senses = sources of environmental stimuli
  • Each new stimulus → new NEURAL PATHWAYS in brain
  • 6–12 months: learns to communicate via crying/smiling to get needs met
