  • Forms emotional bonds; uses facial expressions/body language for turn-taking

Stage II: 12–24 MONTHS (Toddlerhood)
  12–18 months (one-word / holophrastic):
    • LEXICON: ~90 expressive words
    • HOLOPHRASTIC language: one word = complete thought
    • Early word types: nouns (ball, mommy), action words (go), modifiers (hot), personal-social (no)
    • Uses inflection to signal questions ('juice?')
  18–24 months (two-word):
    • COMMUNICATIVE COMPETENCE: knows when/where/with whom to use language
    • MLU (Mean Length of Utterance): avg morphemes ≈ chronological age
    • Articles (a, the), copulas (is, be), auxiliary verbs (have, can) emerging

Stage III: 2–5 YEARS (Early Childhood)
  2 yrs: ~300-word vocabulary; 'No!' favorite word; tantrums
  3–4 yrs:
    • PARALLEL SPEECH: caregiver narrates what child is doing (scaffolding)
    • COGNITIVE DEVELOPMENT: perception, memory, imagination, reasoning
    • FUNCTOR words: articles, prepositions, conjunctions give order to sentences
    • TELEGRAPHIC SPEECH: essential words only (no function words)
    • NARRATIVE: orderly sequenced accounts of events
    • DISCOURSE: extended verbal exchange; pragmatics (topic initiation, turn-taking, repair)
  5 yrs: Declarative sentences and basic questions; contractions

Stage IV: 6–12 YEARS (Middle Childhood → Adolescence)
  6–8 yrs: Compound/complex sentences; variety of morphemes
  ★ Language = TOOL FOR LEARNING → language problems = learning problems
  9–10 yrs: Increased complexity; social/pragmatic skills
  Communication abilities → educational + social + employment success
  11–12 yrs (Adolescence): PEAK for FIGURATIVE LANGUAGE (idioms, metaphors, similes, humor)
  Slang and jargon = foundation of teen social communication

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
CULTURAL LINGUISTIC DIVERSITY (CLD)
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
• 7,000+ languages in the world; most have dialects
• No dialect is superior to another
• Standard dialect associated with higher education levels
• Dialect / accent = DIFFERENCE, not disorder
• Disorder must occur in PRIMARY LANGUAGE to be classified as a disorder
• A true language disorder → problems in BOTH primary AND second language

Key terms:
  ESL / ELL: English learned AFTER primary language established
  DLL (Dual-Language Learner): two+ languages learned SIMULTANEOUSLY
  Bilingual: primary language at home; English at school
  Code-Switching: NORMAL bilingual behavior — NOT a disorder
    (substitutes known word from L1 when L2 word unknown)
  Race: biological characteristics shared by a group
  Culture: philosophies, values, beliefs, customs shared and passed generationally
  Dialect: regional/group speech form differing in pronunciation, vocab, grammar
  Accent: pronunciation/inflection of nonnative speakers

Poverty + neglect → reduced caregiver interaction → negatively impacts language development
Children in poverty hear fewer words → affects language and cognitive development

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
★ VIDEO TIPS: WHEN WILL MY BABY SPEAK?
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
First: always check HEARING — many speech problems relate to hearing difficulties
Tip 1: TALK — narrate constantly; respond to and imitate baby's vocalizations
Tip 2: TEACH IMITATION — peekaboo, clapping, waving → teaches TURN-TAKING
Tip 3: MAKE IT FUN — sing, play, read books every day → maintain JOINT ATTENTION
First true word typically emerges around 12 months
Model language BEFORE 12 months — it's never too early

notes.title: Chapter 3 Supplement — PowerPoint + Brain Video Key Details
notes.body:
SOURCES: CSD_Ch3_PP.pptx (Fogle 3rd ed.) + Language and the Brain video (KAtalentsearch)
NOTE: This supplements the main Ch. 3 lecture notes — new details only

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
RESPIRATION — ADDED DETAIL
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Inspiration = drawing air IN
Expiration = breathing air OUT

Diaphragm movement:
  • Inspiration → moves DOWN → thoracic volume ↑ → pressure ↓ → air IN
  • Expiration → moves UP → thoracic volume ↓ → pressure ↑ → air OUT
Intercostals: RAISE rib cage (inspiration) and LOWER it (expiration)
Abdominal muscles: forced expiration (active speech/singing)

Quiet Inspiration Step-by-Step:
  1. Brainstem signals muscles
  2. Diaphragm contracts & lowers; rib cage rises & expands
  3. Thoracic volume ↑ → air pressure ↓
  4. Air flows: nose → trachea → alveolar sacs (expand like balloon)
Quiet Expiration:
  5. Alveolar sacs relax/deflate
  6. Thoracic cavity relaxes → intrathoracic pressure ↑
  7. Air forced out: lungs → trachea → nose or mouth

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
PHONATION — ADDED DETAIL
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Hyoid bone: U-shaped, only bone not articulating with another bone; anchors tongue and larynx
Laryngeal prominence (Adam's apple): anterior point of thyroid cartilage

True vocal folds:
  • Look pearly white
  • 'V' point is ANTERIOR
  • Blow open in a FOOTBALL SHAPE (mid-section opens; anterior/posterior stay closed)
  • Puffs of air that escape = what is heard as voice

False (ventricular) folds:
  • Just ABOVE true folds
  • Help prevent food below true folds during swallowing
  • In NORMAL phonation → remain OPEN (do not vibrate)

★ FREQUENCY VALUES TO MEMORIZE:
  Adult males:   ~120 Hz
  Adult females: ~220 Hz
  Fundamental frequency (f0) = rate during normal voicing of 'ah'

Intonation: pitch variations across syllables/words/phrases → conveys stress and meaning

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
RESONATION — ADDED DETAIL
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Resonance = vibration of sound in vocal tract (pharynx + oral + nasal cavities)

Structures involved:
  Laryngeal pharynx (above vocal folds)
  Nasal pharynx → soft palate, nasal cavity
  Oral pharynx → maxilla, hard palate, alveolar ridge, tongue

Soft palate position:
  ORAL sounds: velum moves SUPERIORLY + POSTERIORLY → contacts pharyngeal wall
             → velopharyngeal closure → nasal cavity SEALED OFF
  NASAL sounds (/m/, /n/): velum stays DOWN → sound resonates through NASAL cavity
★ Intact structures can still cause disorder if WEAK (physiology ≠ anatomy)

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
ARTICULATION — ADDED DETAIL
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Skeletal structures: maxilla, alveolar ridge, mandible, TMJ (temporomandibular joint)

TMJ: connects mandible to temporal bone; allows open/close/lateral jaw movement

Tongue muscle pairs:
  POSITIONING (4 pairs): protrude, retract, elevate, depress/lateralize
  SHAPING (4 pairs): round, flatten, groove, hump
  Articulation = rapid (milliseconds) and PRECISE coordination of all these

Range of Motion = limits of articulator movement for speech
  Mandible: open/close
  Lips: protrude/retract
  Tongue: protrude, retract, elevate, lower, lateralize

Dentition:
  Deciduous (baby) teeth = 20 total
    Erupt: 6–9 months → fully erupted by 18–24 months
    Shed: 6–13 years
  Permanent teeth = 32 total

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
NERVOUS SYSTEM — ADDED DETAIL
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Neuron anatomy:
  Axon: carries impulse AWAY from cell body
  Dendrite: receives impulse FROM other neurons
  Synapse: junction between neurons (electrochemical transmission)
  Sequence: neuron → axon → dendrite → neuron
  Articulation therapy = changes neuronal firing + synaptic connections

Blood supply:
  Brain = ~3 lbs
  Receives 20–25% of blood pumped from the heart
  Cerebrovascular system = brain's blood circulation
  Anoxia: lack of O2 from asphyxiation (drowning/choking) or inadequate circulation (heart attack)
          → unconsciousness + death of brain tissue

Frontal lobe (expanded):
  Prefrontal cortex (anterior): personality, judgment, beliefs, cognition
  Executive functions: anticipation, goal selection, planning, initiation,
                       self-regulation, use of feedback
  Motor cortex: EXECUTES movements → sends neural impulses to muscles
  Broca's area (premotor cortex): PLANS motor speech → directs motor cortex

Temporal lobe (expanded):
  Primary auditory cortex = Heschl's gyrus (perceives sounds)
  Wernicke's area: language processing, auditory comprehension,
                   integrates language with other modalities (visual, tactile, taste, smell),
                   formulates language for verbal expression
  ★ Impaired cognition → impaired language processing (and vice versa)

Parietal lobe:
  Left parietal: receives input from RIGHT side of body
  Right parietal: receives input from LEFT side of body
  Anterior: tactile (touch) detection
  Posterior: interprets + integrates bodily sensations

Occipital lobe: retina → optic nerves → optic chiasm → optic tracts → occipital lobes

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
★ VIDEO MNEMONICS (Language and the Brain)
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Wernicke (German 'W' = 'V' sound) → VOCABULARY → comprehension
Broca → the other one → grammar + language PRODUCTION
Frontal → PLANNING ('what am I going to say?') → PRODUCTION
Temporal → listening/AUDITORY → COMPREHENSION
Parietal → sensorimotor cortex → FINE MOTOR CONTROL for tongue/lips/signing
Occipital → VISUAL → reading, writing, lip-reading, gesture
Cerebellum + Brainstem → breathing, memory, keeping you alive WHILE speaking
★ Full language = WHOLE BRAIN, but LEFT hemisphere specializes in oral communication

notes.title: Chapter 3 — Anatomy & Physiology of Speech and Language
notes.body:
SOURCE: Lecture 2.2 | Kidwell, Susan M.S. CCC-SLP | Comm Dis 1A, SJDC
TEXTBOOK: Essentials of Communication Sciences and Disorders, 3rd ed. — Fogle
SEE ALSO: Discussion questions in COM_DIS_2_3.docx

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
CORE CONCEPTS
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
• Anatomy = the structures (parts of the body)
• Physiology = how those structures function
★ Key insight: You can have INTACT ANATOMY but IMPAIRED PHYSIOLOGY
• Intelligibility = how well a speaker is understood → primary goal of speech therapy
• Normal speech does NOT involve conscious thought (until a disorder disrupts it)

BIOLOGICAL vs. OVERLAID FUNCTION (★ major exam concept)
  Every speech structure has a life-sustaining biological function
  Humans have ADAPTED these structures for communication (overlaid function)
  Examples:
    Lips:        biological = hold food in mouth | overlaid = shape speech sounds
    Vocal folds: biological = protect airway    | overlaid = produce voice
    Tongue:      biological = taste/chew/swallow | overlaid = primary articulator
    Respiratory system: biological = O2/CO2 exchange | overlaid = produce voice

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
RESPIRATION
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Framework: rib cage + sternum + vertebrae
Principal organs: lungs + heart (in thorax)
Trachea: cartilaginous rings, 'windpipe', extends larynx → lungs

Primary muscle: DIAPHRAGM (NOT under conscious control)
Other muscles (26 pairs): intercostals (inhalation/exhalation), abdominals (forced exhalation), pectoralis major/minor, rectus abdominus

★ BREATHING RATIO:
  At rest:       ~50% inhale / 50% exhale
  During speech: ~10% inhale / 90% exhale
  → Quick short IN, long sustained OUT
  → Exhalation changes from PASSIVE (rest) to ACTIVE (speech)

Respiratory cycle sequence:
  1. Brainstem signals muscles
  2. Diaphragm contracts & lowers → rib cage expands/rises
  3. Thoracic volume ↑ → lung pressure ↓
  4. Air flows in through nose → trachea → alveoli
  5. Alveolar sacs deflate → diaphragm relaxes
  6. Rib cage lowers → thoracic volume ↓ → air pressure ↑
  7. Air flows out through trachea → nose or mouth

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
PHONATION (VOICE)
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Larynx = organ of phonation
Location: atop trachea, below root of tongue
Cartilages: cricoid, thyroid (voice box/Adam's apple), 2 arytenoids, epiglottis

Vocal Folds:
  TRUE folds: vibrate → voice (overlaid) | close airway → protect trachea (biological)
  FALSE folds: do NOT vibrate; close during swallowing
  Glottis = space between OPEN vocal folds
  Folds attach POSTERIORLY to arytenoids
  Folds = muscles covered in mucous membranes

Voice Production Mechanism:
  1. Vocal folds ADDUCT (close)
  2. Subglottic air pressure builds
  3. Pressure forces folds APART (abduct)
  4. Bernoulli's law → folds snap back together
  5. Cycle repeats → vibration → sound

Voice Characteristics:
  Frequency (Hz) = cycles/sec of vocal fold vibration [OBJECTIVE]
  Pitch = perceptual sensation of frequency [SUBJECTIVE] — varies with fold tension, mass, length
  Intensity (dB) = physical force of fold opening/closing [OBJECTIVE]
  Loudness = perceptual correlate of intensity [SUBJECTIVE]
  Quality = subjective judgment — normal voice is NOT hoarse, breathy, or choppy

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
RESONATION & ARTICULATION
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Palatal Development (★ 1st trimester critical period):
  • Lips formed by 8 weeks gestation
  • Hard & soft palate fused by 3rd month
  → Disruption → cleft lip/palate

Hard Palate: bony, anterior 2/3, within maxilla, surrounded by alveolar ridge
Soft Palate (velum): muscular, posterior 1/3, ends in uvula
  • At rest: hangs down, rests at base of tongue
  • During speech: RAISES + moves POSTERIORLY → contacts pharyngeal wall
  → VELOPHARYNGEAL CLOSURE = separates oral from nasal cavity
  ★ Insufficient closure (cleft palate) → hypernasality

Bones: maxilla (upper jaw) + mandible (lower jaw) = 2 primary SLP concerns
Facial muscles: orbicularis oris (lips), masseter/buccinator (cheeks), tongue

ARTICULATORS:
  TONGUE (primary articulator):
    Biological: taste, mastication, deglutition
    8 pairs of muscles: 4 position, 4 shape
    Key sounds: th, t, d, k, g, sh
    /t/ = tongue tip to alveolar ridge
    /k/ = tongue back rises

  LIPS (2nd most important articulator):
    Biological: hold food/liquid in oral cavity
    6 movements: opening, closing, rounding, flattening, protruding, retracting
    Key sounds: p, b, m, w, f, v

  TEETH (Dentition):
    Primary: 20 teeth, erupt 6 months–24 months
    Permanent: 32 teeth, erupt 6–13 years
    Types: incisors, cuspids, bicuspids, premolars, molars

  MYOFUNCTIONAL THERAPY:
    Tongue thrust = forward tongue protrusion during swallowing
    Can affect speech AND dentition
    May be required before orthodontic treatment

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
CENTRAL NERVOUS SYSTEM (CNS)
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Brain: ~3 pounds, ~100 billion neurons, trillions of connections
All thoughts, actions, emotions, memories = electrochemical reactions
Transmission: neuron → axon → dendrite → neuron
Anoxia = brain damage from lack of oxygen (birth, stroke, chemicals, etc.)

CNS = brain + cerebellum + brainstem + spinal cord
PNS = cranial nerves (from brainstem) + spinal nerves + sensory nerves

HEMISPHERES:
  LEFT (dominant for language):
    • Receptive & expressive language/speech
    • Processes rapidly changing info, sequentially
    • Controls right-body movement
    • Contains: Broca's area + Wernicke's area

  RIGHT:
    • Attention, orientation, emotions, cognition
    • Controls left-body movement

  Connected by CORPUS CALLOSUM → allows hemispheres to work together

FOUR LOBES:
  Frontal:   cognition, reasoning, judgment, problem-solving, Broca's area
  Temporal:  auditory processing → Wernicke's area → language comprehension
  Parietal:  body sensations (touch, pressure)
  Occipital: visual processing

SPECIAL STRUCTURES:
  Broca's area: lower posterior LEFT FRONTAL lobe
    → plans, sequences, coordinates, initiates motor speech movements
  Wernicke's area: LEFT TEMPORAL lobe
    → comprehension, integration, formulation of language

CEREBELLUM: coordinates muscle groups, balance and equilibrium
BRAINSTEM (pons + medulla oblongata):
  → Connects brain to spinal cord
  → Controls face, mouth, larynx for speech
  ★ Brainstem damage → severe speech, voice, AND swallowing problems

CRANIAL NERVES FOR SPEECH (7 key ones):
  V   Trigeminal
  VII Facial
  VIII Vestibulocochlear / Auditory
  IX  Glossopharyngeal
  X   Vagus
  XI  Accessory
  XII Hypoglossal

SPINAL CORD: conducts sensory/motor impulses; controls many reflexes

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
★ DISCUSSION STUDY QUESTIONS (COM_DIS_2_3)
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Knowledge: Define anatomy / physiology / inspiration / expiration
Knowledge: Describe true vocal folds and their function
Knowledge: Explain hard palate vs. soft palate anatomic relationship
Knowledge: Left hemisphere functions vs. right hemisphere functions
Application: How would poor inspiration affect voice?
Application: How to explain respiration/phonation to an 8-year-old?
Application: How to explain soft palate movement to an adult client?
Application: How would frontal lobe damage affect quality of life?
Application: Why does brainstem damage cause speech/voice/swallowing problems?
Analysis: Why must SLPs explain these processes in non-technical terms?
Analysis: Importance of the concept that speech requires perfect timing and coordination
Analysis: CNS vs. PNS — compare and contrast

notes.title: The Four Processes of Speech Production
notes.body:
SOURCE: Eugene Speech Language Therapy — YouTube (11xf89bXaCg)

OVERVIEW
Speech production involves four sequential processes that begin after the brain forms its intended message and creates a motor plan.

1. RESPIRATION (Breath Support)
- The energy source behind speech
- At rest: inhale/exhale ratio = ~50/50
- During speech: ratio shifts to 10% inhale / 90% exhale
- Exhalation becomes ACTIVE during speech (vs. passive at rest)
- Muscles involved: diaphragm (pushes up) + abdominal muscles (contract)
- SLP role: strengthen these muscles to increase speech loudness

2. PHONATION (Voicing)
- Produced by the vocal folds (vocal cords)
- Location: above the trachea, above the lungs
- Mechanism: vocal folds pushed together → airflow forces them open → rapid vibration → sound
- SLP role: treat voice disorders caused by overuse/abuse, surgery, accidents, or disease

3. RESONANCE
- Sound created at the vocal folds travels upward through three cavities:
  • Pharyngeal cavity (throat)
  • Oral cavity (mouth)
  • Nasal cavity (nose)
- These spaces can be manipulated to change voice quality
- What makes each person's voice unique

4. ARTICULATION
- Shaping sound into recognizable words/language
- Occurs when articulators touch or interact
- Primary articulator: TONGUE
- Other articulators: lips, teeth, cheeks, palate
- Most commonly associated area of SLP work

KEY CONCEPTS TO FLAG
★ The brain creates a MOTOR PLAN before any speech process begins
★ Normal resting breathing ≠ speech breathing — the ratio shifts dramatically
★ SLPs work across ALL FOUR processes, not just articulation
★ Conditions that bring clients to SLP: voice disorders, brain injury, stroke, progressive disease
★ Goal of SLP intervention often includes improving INTELLIGIBILITY
notes.source: Eugene Speech Language Therapy — How We Speak (YouTube, 11xf89bXaCg)

notes.title: Chapter 1 — Essentials of Communication and Its Disorders
notes.body:
SOURCE: Lecture 1.2.1 | Kidwell, Susan M.S. CCC-SLP | Comm Dis 1A, SJDC
TEXTBOOK: Essentials of Communication Sciences and Disorders, 2nd ed. — Fogle

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
WHAT IS COMMUNICATION?
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
• Definition (Fogle): Any means by which individuals relate their wants, needs, thoughts, knowledge, and feelings to another person
• A basic human need — often taken for granted until disrupted
• 'We cannot not communicate' — communication occurs in any modality
• Dr. Fogle's analogy: communication is like a dance — we take turns leading, stay in step, decide if we want to 'dance' again
• Communication is a basic human necessity — cave paintings & hieroglyphs show 30,000+ year history

Types:
  • Verbal: spoken language — words, sentences; foundation for reading & writing
  • Nonverbal: facial expressions, eye contact, body posture, gestures
  • Inner speech: talking to ourselves internally before expressing outwardly

Modalities:
  INPUT: auditory, visual, tactile
  OUTPUT: speaking, gesturing, writing
★ Disruption to ANY modality = communication disorder

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
SPEECH — THE FOUR PROCESSES
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Speech = motor act of producing oral language; one of the most complex fine motor skills
All four processes must be coordinated:

1. RESPIRATION — breath support (lungs, diaphragm, chest muscles)
2. PHONATION — vocal fold vibration; voiced (/z,d,b/) vs. voiceless (/s,t,p/)
3. RESONANCE — shape of vocal tract; nasal vs. oral airflow
   • Nasal sounds: /m/ — plug nose and say 'mom', feel the vibration
   • Oral sounds: /s/ — say 'soup' holding nose, no nasal vibration
4. ARTICULATION — jaw, lip, tongue movements to form sounds
   • /m,p,b/ = bilabial (both lips)
   • /k,g/ = tongue rises at the back

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
LANGUAGE
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Definition (Owens 2015 / Fogle): A socially shared code or conventional system for representing concepts through arbitrary symbols and rule-governed combinations
→ If you know the code, you understand the message
→ ~7,000 known living languages + unknown extinct
→ Each code is arbitrary — no code is better than another

Spoken language also conveys:
  • Prosody — melody/rhythm; stress, pitch, timing; conveys emotion
  • Suprasegmentals — stress, emphasis, tone across words/sentences
  • Paralinguistic cues — facial expression, body language, clothing, artifacts, environment

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
THE FIVE COMPONENTS OF LINGUISTICS
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
1. PHONOLOGY — speech sounds & rules for using them
   • Phoneme = smallest unit of sound distinct from others
   • Written in / / notation: /b/, /s/, /æ/
   • Not letters! No 'c' in phonetic alphabet ('c' = /k/ or /s/)

2. MORPHOLOGY — word structure from morphemes
   • Morpheme = smallest unit of language that conveys meaning
   • Prefixes & suffixes: 'dislike', 'liked'
   • '-ed' on 'walked' = past tense morpheme
   • Language-disordered clients often show morphological errors

3. SYNTAX — rules for combining words into sentences
   • 'I am going to the store' vs 'I am coming from the store'
   • Non-native speakers often struggle with syntax

4. SEMANTICS — meaning: vocabulary, categories, idioms
   • Dialectical differences ≠ disorder
   • 'Trunk' (US) vs 'boot' (UK) — same object, different code

5. PRAGMATICS — social rules for language use
   • Verbal AND nonverbal; function over structure
   • Polite / professional / intimate registers — we switch all day
★ Children on the Autism Spectrum often have pragmatic delays
★ Typically developing children learn pragmatics naturally and quickly

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
COMMUNICATION DISORDERS
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Van Riper (1978) Definition — disorder that:
  ✓ Deviates from that of others
  ✓ Calls attention to itself
  ✓ Interferes with communication
  ✓ Causes distress for speaker and/or listener

Prevalence = number with disorder at a given time
Incidence = rate disorder appears in population over time

Use 'disability' or 'impairment' — NOT 'handicapped'

8 CATEGORIES (Figure 1-3):
  1. Articulation — improper sound production, often tongue placement
     • Phonological disorder = error patterns in sound combinations
     • Motor speech (apraxia/dysarthria) = neurological motor planning
  2. Language — receptive (understanding) & expressive (producing)
     • Reading & writing = higher-level language skills
     • Aphasia = acquired adult language disorder
  3. Fluency — disfluency (preferred over 'stuttering')
     • Repetitions, prolongations, silent blocks, visible tension
     • Cluttering = fast speech with compressed words
  4. Voice — loudness, pitch, quality outside typical range
     • Dysphonia = rough, raspy, or hoarse voice
  5. Resonance — hard/soft palate structure or function
     • Hypernasality = too much nasal air ('talking through nose')
     • Hyponasality = too little nasal air
  6. Cognition — perceiving, memory, reasoning, judgment
  7. Literacy — reading & writing (higher-level language)
  8. Hearing — foundation for speech & language development
     • Conductive = outer/middle ear (eardrum, ossicles, ear canal)
     • Sensorineural = cochlea and/or auditory nerve
     • Mixed = both types

★ IMPORTANT: Swallowing disorders are a MAJOR SLP area but are NOT classified as communication disorders
★ SLP role: improve client's quality of communication AND quality of life
★ Good people skills and counseling skills are essential — the family is affected too

notes.title: Chapter 2 — The Professionals: SLP & Audiology
notes.body:
SOURCE: Lecture 1.2.2 | Kidwell, Susan M.S. CCC-SLP | Comm Dis 1A, SJDC
TEXTBOOK: Essentials of Communication Sciences and Disorders, 2nd ed. — Fogle

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
HISTORICAL ORIGINS
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
• Traced to Alexander Graham Bell — father & grandfather were elocutionists (1860s)
• Bell & father became interested in deaf/hard of hearing individuals
• 1900s: Speech correction groups formed → became American Speech and Hearing Association
• Now known as: American Speech-Language-Hearing Association (ASHA)
• 1960s: Special education became federally mandated
• 1975: Public Law 94-142 — FAPE in the LRE for all children with disabilities

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
ASHA — THE GOVERNING BODY
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
• Primary professional org for SLPs & Audiologists in the U.S.
• Establishes: Scope of Practice + Code of Ethics
• Maintains credentials: CCC-SLP and CCC-A
• Website: www.asha.org
• Student org: NSSLHA — www.nsslha.org
★ Code of Ethics violations = loss of certification or licensure
★ Primary ethical obligation: PUT THE CLIENT'S NEEDS FIRST

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
SPEECH-LANGUAGE PATHOLOGIST (SLP)
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Core functions: IDENTIFY → EVALUATE → DIAGNOSE → TREAT
Focus: Communication AND swallowing disorders

Education pathway:
  1. Bachelor's in Communication Disorders (or related field)
  2. Master's in Communication Disorders (or Ph.D.)
  3. Pass national exam
  4. Earn CCC-SLP from ASHA
  5. Obtain state licensure

Work settings (largest first):
  • Public schools ← LARGEST EMPLOYER
  • Hospitals: acute, subacute, convalescent
  • Inpatient/outpatient clinics
  • Private practices
  • Universities

Alternate titles:
  speech-language pathologist | speech-language therapist | speech therapist
  speech teacher | speech & hearing specialist | language speech & hearing specialist

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
AUDIOLOGIST
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Core functions: IDENTIFY → EVALUATE → DIAGNOSE → TREAT
Focus: Auditory AND vestibular disorders

Education pathway:
  1. Bachelor's in related field
  2. Ph.D. or Au.D.
  3. Complete Clinical Fellowship Year
  4. Pass national exam
  5. Earn CCC-A from ASHA
  6. State licensure + ongoing CEUs

Scope of practice includes:
  • Determining type and extent of hearing loss
  • Assessing benefits of amplification (hearing aids)
  • Assessing potential for habilitation or rehabilitation

Work settings: same as SLP (schools, hospitals, clinics, private practice, universities)

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
SUPPORT PERSONNEL
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
SLPA (Speech-Language Pathology Assistant):
  • Tasks prescribed, directed & supervised by CCC-SLP
  • Earns A.S. degree from SLPA training program
  • See p. 32 in textbook for full list of responsibilities

Audiology Assistant:
  • Duties directed & supervised by licensed Audiologist
  • Requires high school diploma + competency-based training
  • Assists with routine tasks only

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
TEAM APPROACH
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
• Often required for treatment of communication disorders
• Involves professionals + family members
★ Most important team member = THE CLIENT (and parent if applicable)

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
★ KEY CONCEPTS TO REMEMBER
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
★ SLPs work from conception (prenatal care) to death
★ An individual is NOT defined by their disorder
★ You may need to fight for what is right for your client
★ Movies featuring SLPs: My Fair Lady, The King's Speech, Children of a Lesser God
★ FAPE and LRE remain significant legal standards today
★ The SLP is the supervisor of the SLPA — always
